topics.title: ROS Graph
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT525 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** ROS Graph

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ROS Graph (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> The **ROS Graph** is a peer-to-peer network of ROS 2 processes (Nodes) that are simultaneously processing data together. It encompasses all the nodes connected at runtime, the topic/service/action relationships between them, and the parameters governing their behavior. It represents the complete, dynamic topology of a running ROS 2 system.

The ROS Graph is the fundamental abstraction in ROS 2 that captures **who is running, what they are doing, and how they are exchanging data** at any given moment. Unlike ROS 1, which relied on a central master (roscore), the **ROS 2 Graph uses DDS (Data Distribution Service)** middleware to allow true decentralized, peer-to-peer discovery using the **RTPS (Real-Time Publish-Subscribe)** protocol.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PERUT525 Module 1):**
> The ROS Graph forms the foundational layer for all higher-level ROS concepts including topic-based communication, service-based request-response, action-based long-running tasks, and parameter management. Every robot application built on ROS 2 is, at its core, a graph of communicating nodes.

### 1.2 The Five Primary Graph Elements

| # | Element | Functional Role | Persistent? |
|---|---------|-----------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Nodes** | Computational processes | Process lifetime |
| 2 | **Topics** | Named async data buses | Always available |
| 3 | **Services** | Named sync RPC endpoints | Always available |
| 4 | **Actions** | Long-running goal-oriented tasks | Goal-to-Result lifecycle |
| 5 | **Parameters** | Dynamic configuration values | Server-managed |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Tower

Imagine the **ROS Graph as a busy international airport**:

- **Nodes** are individual **aircraft** (each with its own pilot, computer, and mission).
- **Topics** are **radio broadcast frequencies** (any aircraft tuned to a frequency can hear all messages on it, but only those broadcasting are heard).
- **Services** are **direct radio calls** to a specific air-traffic controller (you must call, wait for reply, no broadcasting).
- **Actions** are **flight plans submitted to ATC** (you submit, get continuous updates, can be cancelled, finally land).
- **Parameters** are **published airport rules** (taxi speed, runway closure, current weather settings) that any plane can read.
- **DDS Discovery** is the **automatic radar handshake** that allows aircraft to find each other without a central tower.

Just as no single component "owns" the airport, no single node "owns" the ROS Graph — it emerges from cooperation.

### 1.4 KTU 2024 Standard Metrics

- **Default Discovery Protocol:** **DDS-RTPS** (OMG Standard)
- **Default Discovery Time:** **< 1 second** for local network
- **Default QoS Policy:** **Reliable, Volatile, Keep-Last(10)**
- **Max Practical Nodes (Single Host):** **~200 nodes** before discovery overhead dominates
- **Max Topics/Node:** **Unbounded**, practically limited by socket/file descriptors (**1024** default on Linux)

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** In KTU 2024 exams, when asked to "define ROS Graph," students must explicitly mention the **peer-to-peer DDS-based architecture**. Merely saying "a collection of nodes" loses 1 mark.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Anatomy of a ROS Graph — Layered Breakdown

**Layer 1 — The Computation Layer (Nodes)**
A **Node** is an executable program that uses ROS 2 client libraries (rclcpp for C++, rclpy for Python) to communicate with other nodes. Each node must declare a **unique name** within the graph to prevent collisions.

- **Why?** Multiple nodes of the same type (e.g., 2 camera drivers) must coexist with different identities.
- **How?** Namespaces (e.g., `/front_camera`, `/rear_camera`) and remapping rules (`__name:=new_name`) resolve conflicts.

**Layer 2 — The Communication Layer (Topics / Services / Actions)**

- **Topics** use **anonymous publish-subscribe** semantics. Publishers and subscribers are decoupled in time, space, and identity. This is the **most common** communication pattern in ROS.
- **Services** use **synchronous request-reply** semantics. A client blocks until the service server responds. Best for deterministic, short computations.
- **Actions** use **asynchronous goal-feedback-result** semantics with built-in preemption. Best for long-running tasks (e.g., "navigate to kitchen").

**Layer 3 — The Configuration Layer (Parameters)**
Parameters are typed, named, dynamically reconfigurable values stored in a **Parameter Server** (often colocated with a node). They enable runtime tuning without recompilation.

**Layer 4 — The Discovery Layer (DDS/RTPS)**
This invisible layer handles automatic peer discovery using **multicast UDP** (default) or **shared memory** (intra-process). Each node periodically announces its presence, publications, and subscriptions.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Notation | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| Topic Bandwidth | $B_{topic} = S_{msg} \times f_{msg}$ | Per-topic data rate | bits/sec (bps) |
| Graph Total Throughput | $B_{graph} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} B_{topic_i}$ | Sum of all topic rates | bits/sec (bps) |
| End-to-End Latency | $t_{e2e} = t_{pub} + t_{net} + t_{sub} + t_{proc}$ | Time from publish to callback | milliseconds (ms) |
| Discovery Convergence | $T_{disc} \approx \frac{2 \times TTL_{multicast}}{R_{heartbeat}}$ | Time for new node to be visible | seconds (s) |
| Subscriber Count Subsystem | $C_{sub} = \sum_{j=1}^{M} 1_{active}(j)$ | Active listeners on a topic | unitless |
| Message Loss Probability | $P_{loss} = 1 - e^{-\lambda \times \tau}$ | For Poisson arrival at rate $\lambda$ | dimensionless |
| Effective QoS Reliability | $R_{eff} = 1 - P_{loss}^{retransmits}$ | After N retransmissions | dimensionless |
| Service Round-Trip Time | $RTT = 2 \times t_{net} + t_{server}$ | Synchronous call latency | milliseconds (ms) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Rule (KTU 2024):** Where $S_{msg}$ is message size in **bits**, $f_{msg}$ is publication frequency in **Hz**, $TTL$ is multicast Time-To-Live, and $R_{heartbeat}$ is DDS participant heartbeat rate (default **1 Hz**).

### 2.3 Engineering Utility

- **Multi-Robot Systems:** Each robot runs its own ROS Graph; **namespaces** (`/robot1/`, `/robot2/`) prevent collision when merged.
- **Fleet Management:** Tools like `ros2 topic list` and `rqt_graph` provide **real-time introspection** of the running system.
- **Debugging:** Visualizing the ROS Graph reveals orphan nodes, broken topic chains, and bandwidth bottlenecks.
- **Industrial Automation:** Factory AGVs use ROS 2 graphs to coordinate perception, planning, and control across hundreds of devices.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Industrial Use Case:** Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs) in warehouses (e.g., Amazon Kiva) use ROS 2 graphs to share map data, battery status, and traffic coordination across a fleet of 100+ robots simultaneously.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Graph Bandwidth Consumption

**Given:** A mobile robot ROS Graph has the following active topics:

- `/camera/image_raw` → publishes compressed images at **15 Hz**, each **250 KB**
- `/lidar/scan` → publishes 360° scan at **20 Hz**, each **120 KB**
- `/imu/data` → publishes orientation at **100 Hz**, each **48 B**
- `/odom` → publishes pose at **50 Hz**, each **200 B**

**Find:** Total graph bandwidth $B_{graph}$ in Mbps and the percentage contribution of each topic.

**Step 1 — Convert all sizes to bits.**

$$
S_{camera} = 250 \, \text{KB} \times 8 \times 1024 = 2{,}048{,}000 \, \text{bits}
$$

$$
S_{lidar} = 120 \, \text{KB} \times 8 \times 1024 = 983{,}040 \, \text{bits}
$$

$$
S_{imu} = 48 \, \text{B} \times 8 = 384 \, \text{bits}
$$

$$
S_{odom} = 200 \, \text{B} \times 8 = 1{,}600 \, \text{bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply per-topic bandwidth formula $B_{topic} = S_{msg} \times f_{msg}$.**

$$
B_{camera} = 2{,}048{,}000 \times 15 = 30{,}720{,}000 \, \text{bps} = 30.72 \, \text{Mbps}
$$

$$
B_{lidar} = 983{,}040 \times 20 = 19{,}660{,}800 \, \text{bps} = 19.66 \, \text{Mbps}
$$

$$
B_{imu} = 384 \times 100 = 38{,}400 \, \text{bps} = 0.0384 \, \text{Mbps}
$$

$$
B_{odom} = 1{,}600 \times 50 = 80{,}000 \, \text{bps} = 0.08 \, \text{Mbps}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum for total graph throughput.**

$$
B_{graph} = 30.72 + 19.66 + 0.0384 + 0.08 = 50.50 \, \text{Mbps}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute percentage contributions.**

$$
\%_{camera} = \frac{30.72}{50.50} \times 100 \approx 60.83\%
$$

$$
\%_{lidar} = \frac{19.66}{50.50} \times 100 \approx 38.93\%
$$

$$
\%_{imu} = \frac{0.0384}{50.50} \times 100 \approx 0.076\%
$$

$$
\%_{odom} = \frac{0.08}{50.50} \times 100 \approx 0.158\%
$$

**Conclusion:** The camera and LIDAR topics dominate **~99.76%** of the bandwidth budget, justifying the use of compression and lower publish rates for these sensors in bandwidth-constrained wireless deployments.

### 3.2 Derivation: End-to-End Latency for a Topic Chain

**Given:** A perception-to-planning pipeline with:
- $t_{pub} = 2$ ms (publish overhead)
- $t_{net} = 5$ ms (Wi-Fi transport)
- $t_{sub} = 1$ ms (subscribe dispatch)
- $t_{proc} = 15$ ms (planning node processing)

**Find:** Total end-to-end latency $t_{e2e}$.

**Step 1 — Write the additive model:**

$$
t_{e2e} = t_{pub} + t_{net} + t_{sub} + t_{proc}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
t_{e2e} = 2 + 5 + 1 + 15 = 23 \, \text{ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Check against real-time constraints:**

For a robot operating at **50 Hz control loop**, the control period is:

$$
T_{control} = \frac{1}{50} = 20 \, \text{ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare:**

$$
t_{e2e} = 23 \, \text{ms} > T_{control} = 20 \, \text{ms}
$$

**Conclusion:** The pipeline **misses one control cycle** every iteration. Optimization is required (e.g., reduce $t_{proc}$ to 12 ms or use a faster transport like Ethernet).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Communication Pattern Selection

| Scenario | Topic | Service | Action | Why? |
|----------|-------|---------|--------|------|
| Continuous sensor stream | ✅ Best | ❌ Overkill | ❌ Overkill | Many subscribers, no reply needed |
| Query robot's current pose once | ⚠️ Wasteful | ✅ Best | ❌ Overkill | One-shot, sync response |
| "Navigate to coordinates X,Y" | ❌ Cannot cancel | ❌ Blocks caller | ✅ Best | Long task, needs feedback, can preempt |
| Update PID gain at runtime | ❌ Wrong tool | ⚠️ Pollutes | ✅ Param | Persistent config value |
| Emergency stop trigger | ✅ Possible | ✅ Possible | ❌ Overkill | Critical, low-latency, both OK |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level ROS Graph Topology

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SENSORS["SENSOR LAYER"]
        nodeCam["Camera Driver Node"]
        nodeLidar["LIDAR Driver Node"]
        nodeIMU["IMU Driver Node"]
    end

    subgraph PERCEPTION["PERCEPTION LAYER"]
        nodePerc["Object Detection Node"]
        nodeLocal["Localization Node"]
    end

    subgraph DECISION["DECISION LAYER"]
        nodePlan["Path Planning Node"]
        nodeCtrl["Motion Control Node"]
    end

    subgraph ACTUATOR["ACTUATOR LAYER"]
        nodeDrive["Motor Driver Node"]
        nodeDisp["Telemetry Display Node"]
    end

    nodeCam -- "/camera/image_raw" --> nodePerc
    nodeLidar -- "/lidar/points" --> nodePerc
    nodeIMU -- "/imu/data" --> nodeLocal
    nodeCam -- "/camera/info" --> nodeLocal

    nodePerc -- "/detected/objects" --> nodePlan
    nodeLocal -- "/amcl_pose" --> nodePlan

    nodePlan -- "/plan/trajectory" --> nodeCtrl
    nodeCtrl -- "/cmd_vel" --> nodeDrive

    nodeCtrl -- "/diagnostics" --> nodeDisp
    nodeLocal -. "/trigger_relocalize" .-> nodeLocal
    nodePlan == "/navigate_to_pose" ==> nodeCtrl

    style SENSORS fill:#E3F2FD
    style PERCEPTION fill:#FFF3E0
    style DECISION fill:#F3E5F5
    style ACTUATOR fill:#E8F5E9
```

> **Diagram Legend:**
> - Solid arrows (`-->`): **Topics** (asynchronous publish-subscribe)
> - Dotted arrows (`-.->`): **Services** (synchronous request-reply)
> - Thick arrows (`==>`): **Actions** (long-running goal-feedback-result)

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Robot Power ON] --> B[DDS Discovery Phase]
    B --> C{All Required Nodes Alive?}
    C -- No --> D[Wait for Heartbeat]
    D --> C
    C -- Yes --> E[ROS Graph Established]
    E --> F[Publishers Start Streaming]
    F --> G[Subscribers Receive Data]
    G --> H[Services Available for Calls]
    H --> I[Actions Accept Goals]
    I --> J[Parameters Readable Writable]
    J --> K[Continuous Graph Operation]

    style A fill:#FFEBEE
    style B fill:#FFF9C4
    style E fill:#C8E6C9
    style K fill:#B3E5FC
```

### 4.3 Discovery Sequence in the ROS Graph

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant NodeA as Node A (Publisher)
    participant DDS as DDS Discovery Layer
    participant NodeB as Node B (Subscriber)

    NodeA->>DDS: Announce Participant (multicast UDP)
    NodeA->>DDS: Announce Publication on /cmd_vel
    NodeB->>DDS: Announce Participant
    NodeB->>DDS: Announce Subscription on /cmd_vel
    DDS-->>NodeA: Match found with NodeB
    DDS-->>NodeB: Match found with NodeA
    NodeA->>NodeB: Begin publishing messages
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2]**
**Define the ROS Graph. List any FOUR elements that constitute the ROS Graph.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **ROS Graph** is a peer-to-peer network of ROS 2 processes (nodes) that are simultaneously exchanging data at runtime, built on the **DDS-RTPS middleware** for decentralized discovery.

**Four elements of the ROS Graph:**

1. **Nodes** — Executable processes performing computation.
2. **Topics** — Named asynchronous data buses using publish-subscribe.
3. **Services** — Named synchronous request-reply endpoints.
4. **Parameters** — Dynamically configurable values stored in the parameter server.

*(Acceptable alternative: Actions, Messages — award 0.5 each for any two correct)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between Topics and Services in ROS 2. (Any 3 points)**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| # | Aspect | Topics | Services |
|---|--------|--------|----------|
| 1 | Pattern | Asynchronous publish-subscribe | Synchronous request-reply |
| 2 | Coupling | Anonymous, decoupled | Tightly coupled, named |
| 3 | Cardinality | Many-to-many | One-to-one |
| 4 | Reply | No reply | Mandatory reply |
| 5 | Use Case | Continuous sensor streams | Discrete operations |

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of the **ROS 2 Graph**. List the role of **DDS** in this architecture. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concepts of **Topics, Services, and Actions** in ROS 2 with one real-world example for each. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

The **ROS 2 Graph** is a fully distributed peer-to-peer network that eliminates the central master present in ROS 1. It consists of multiple **Nodes** that discover each other automatically via the **DDS (Data Distribution Service)** standard.

**Architecture Layers (4 Marks):**

1. **Application Layer:** User-written nodes using rclcpp / rclpy.
2. **ROS 2 Client Library (RCL):** Language-agnostic common API.
3. **RMW (ROS Middleware) Layer:** Vendor-agnostic abstraction (e.g., CycloneDDS, Fast DDS).
4. **DDS / RTPS Layer:** Performs actual discovery, serialization, and transport over UDP, TCP, or shared memory.
5. **Transport Layer:** Physical network (Wi-Fi, Ethernet, intra-process).

**Role of DDS (3 Marks):**

- **Discovery:** Multicast UDP-based automatic peer detection (no central roscore).
- **Quality of Service (QoS):** Configurable reliability, durability, deadline, lifespan policies.
- **Transport Flexibility:** Supports UDP, TCP, shared memory, and inter-process communication.
- **Real-Time Capability:** Deterministic message delivery for industrial robotics.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Topics (2.5 Marks):**
Asynchronous, many-to-many, named data bus using publish-subscribe.
- **Example:** A camera node publishes `/camera/image_raw`; multiple nodes (object detector, recorder, UI viewer) subscribe to it simultaneously without coupling.

**Services (2.5 Marks):**
Synchronous, one-to-one, request-reply RPC.
- **Example:** A client node calls `/reset_odometry` service to zero out the robot's pose; blocks until the server responds with success/failure.

**Actions (2 Marks):**
Asynchronous, long-running tasks with goal, continuous feedback, final result, and preemption support.
- **Example:** Sending a "navigate to kitchen" goal; receives progress feedback every second, can be cancelled, and ultimately returns success/failure.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Alternate Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain **Nodes, Publishers, and Subscribers** in ROS 2. How do they together form the core of the ROS Graph? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the **parameter server** and **message** concepts in ROS 2. State the syntax for declaring a parameter in a C++ node. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Nodes (2 Marks):**
A **node** is an executable process that uses ROS 2 client libraries to participate in the ROS Graph. Each node is designed to perform a single, modular purpose (e.g., wheel control, laser driver).

**Publishers (2 Marks):**
A **publisher** is an object created by a node to **send** messages on a specific topic. Multiple publishers can coexist on the same topic, and they are anonymous — they do not know who subscribes.

**Subscribers (2 Marks):**
A **subscriber** is an object created by a node to **receive** messages from a topic. It registers a **callback function** that executes whenever a new message arrives.

**Forming the ROS Graph (1 Mark):**
When publishers and subscribers across multiple nodes establish matching topic connections, they form the **edges** of the ROS Graph, with **nodes** as the vertices. DDS middleware automatically weaves these edges at runtime.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Parameter Server (3 Marks):**
The **parameter server** is a distributed, multi-vendor dictionary of configuration values accessible to all nodes. Parameters can be integers, floats, strings, booleans, arrays, or nested structures. They allow runtime reconfiguration without code recompilation.
- **Example:** A motor controller node reads `max_rpm` parameter (default 100) at startup; an operator can later set it to 150 via CLI.

**Messages (2 Marks):**
A **message** is a strongly-typed data structure (defined in `.msg` files) used for topic communication. It consists of primitive types and nested fields.
- **Example:** `sensor_msgs/msg/Imu` containing `linear_acceleration`, `angular_velocity`, `orientation`.

**C++ Parameter Declaration Syntax (2 Marks):**
```cpp
this->declare_parameter<int32_t>("max_rpm", 100);
int32_t rpm = this->get_parameter("max_rpm").as_int();
```
*(Note: C++ syntax is permitted here as PERUT525 is a CS-aligned ROS course.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention DDS:** Many students define the ROS Graph as just "nodes and topics" without crediting **DDS-RTPS**. **−1.5 marks** in 14-mark answers.
> 2. **Confusing Topics with Services:** Stating that "services are also asynchronous" costs full marks for that sub-part.
> 3. **Missing the Publish-Subscribe decoupling:** Examiners specifically look for the phrase "**anonymous, many-to-many**" in topic answers.
> 4. **No diagram in 7-mark parts:** Always draw a **labeled block diagram** of the ROS Graph for full credit. Hand-drawn or printed, but mandatory.
> 5. **Ignoring Parameters:** A surprisingly common omission. Parameters are a **core graph element** per KTU 2024 syllabus.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- ✅ The **ROS Graph** is the **complete runtime topology** of all communicating ROS 2 processes.
- ✅ Built on **DDS-RTPS** middleware — **fully decentralized**, no roscore in ROS 2.
- ✅ **Nodes** are vertices; **Topics / Services / Actions** are the edges connecting them.
- ✅ **Topics** = async, many-to-many, publish-subscribe, **no reply**.
- ✅ **Services** = sync, one-to-one, request-reply, **blocks caller**.
- ✅ **Actions** = async, one-to-one, goal-feedback-result, **preemptible**, long tasks.
- ✅ **Parameters** = dynamic config values, typed, runtime-modifiable.
- ✅ **DDS QoS** policies control reliability, durability, deadline, and lifespan.
- ✅ Default discovery uses **multicast UDP** with default heartbeat at **1 Hz**.
- ✅ **Visualization tools:** `rqt_graph`, `ros2 node list`, `ros2 topic list`, `ros2 service list`.
- ✅ **Namespacing** (`/robot1/camera`) prevents collision in multi-robot graphs.
- ✅ Total graph bandwidth = **sum of per-topic data rates** $B_{graph} = \sum S_{msg} \times f_{msg}$.
- ✅ End-to-end latency is **additive**: $t_{e2e} = t_{pub} + t_{net} + t_{sub} + t_{proc}$.
- ✅ Discovery convergence time: typically **< 1 second** for local network segments.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ROS Master
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT525 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** ROS Master

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**ROS Master** is the central coordination node (a name service and registry) in a Robot Operating System (ROS) computation graph that provides naming, registration, and lookup services to all other nodes (processes) in the ROS network. It maintains a global view of publishers, subscribers, services, and parameters, enabling decentralized peer-to-peer communication between ROS nodes without requiring direct configuration of endpoints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim from Syllabus):** The ROS Master is the single point of coordination in a ROS system, implementing a name service and parameter server. It enables loose coupling between nodes by maintaining a registry of topics, services, and the URIs of all active publishers and subscribers.

### 1.1.1 Core Responsibilities of ROS Master

The ROS Master performs **four primary functions** that are essential to the operation of any ROS system:

1. **Name Registration & Lookup Service:** Tracks all active nodes and their published/subscribed topics in a centrally accessible table.
2. **Connection Negotiation:** Facilitates the handshake between publishers and subscribers by exchanging XML-RPC URIs.
3. **Parameter Server Hosting:** Maintains a shared, multi-variate, network-accessible dictionary of parameters that all nodes can read/write.
4. **Service Discovery:** Handles the request/response architecture for service-client communication.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Telephone Exchange

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of ROS Master as the **"Telephone Exchange"** of a 1940s city.

| Element in Analogy | ROS Master Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The Telephone Exchange Operator | **ROS Master** process |
| Telephone Numbers | **Topic Names** (e.g., `/cmd_vel`) |
| People making calls (publishers) | **Publisher Nodes** |
| People receiving calls (subscribers) | **Subscriber Nodes** |
| The actual phone lines between people | **TCP/IP connections** (peer-to-peer) |
| The directory of subscribers | **Registration Table** |

When Person A (Publisher) wants to call Person B (Subscriber), they do not dial directly. They tell the **Exchange Operator** (Master) "I want to reach anyone subscribed to `/camera/image`". The Operator checks the **registry**, finds Person B's number (URI), and then **tells both parties how to connect directly** to each other on a dedicated line. The Operator then **steps aside** — the actual conversation happens peer-to-peer.

> [!TIP]
> **Crucial Insight for KTU Exam:** The Master is a **matchmaker, not a messenger**. Once the connection is negotiated, the Master does not pass any data between nodes. The actual message streams flow directly between publishers and subscribers using **TCP/IP** protocols (typically using port **11311** by default).

## 1.3 Why ROS Master is Central to the Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decoupling Principle:** By introducing the Master, ROS achieves **logical decoupling** of nodes. A publisher node does not need to know the IP address, port, or even the existence of a subscriber at compile time. This makes the system modular, hot-swappable, and inherently distributed.

### 1.3.1 Default Network Configuration

- **Default Master URI:** `http://localhost:11311` (resides on the same machine where `roscore` is launched)
- **Default Port:** **11311** (XML-RPC port)
- **Environment Variable:** `ROS_MASTER_URI` (used to point nodes to a remote master)

## 1.4 Key Terminology Locked-In (KTU Examiner Vocabulary)

The following terms **must be used precisely** in KTU 2024 Scheme answers:

- **Node:** A process performing computation. The basic unit of execution in ROS.
- **Master:** The name service and registry (the topic of this module).
- **Topic:** A named bus over which nodes exchange messages (anonymous, one-to-many, many-to-many).
- **Service:** Synchronous request/reply communication using a strict service definition.
- **Parameter Server:** A shared multi-variate dictionary hosted by the Master.
- **XML-RPC:** The lightweight XML-based protocol used by ROS for registry and metadata communication.
- **Graph Resource Name:** A hierarchical, slash-delimited name like `/turtle1/cmd_vel`.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture of ROS Master

The ROS Master is implemented as a single executable (`rosmaster` in ROS 2 / `master` in ROS 1) that bundles together three core services:

### 2.1.1 The Three Logical Sub-Components

1. **The Registrant Table (XML-RPC Server)**
   - Listens on port `11311` for incoming registrations.
   - Stores the API URI of every publisher, subscriber, service, and action server.
   - Responds to lookup requests from other nodes.

2. **The Parameter Server (Shared Dictionary)**
   - Implemented as an in-memory key-value store.
   - Supports types: integers, floats, booleans, strings, lists, dictionaries, and binary blobs (encoded as base64).
   - Globally accessible via `rosparam` command-line tool.

3. **The Connection Negotiation Logic (Publisher-Subscriber Linker)**
   - Monitors new topic subscriptions and triggers the XML-RPC "subscriberUpdate" callback to publishers.
   - The XML-RPC connection provides the URI; the actual data stream is established via **TCP/IP** using **TCPROS protocol**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Protocol Stack Distinction (High-Yield for KTU):** ROS uses a **two-protocol architecture**:
> - **XML-RPC** for control plane (registration, discovery, parameter get/set)
> - **TCPROS (TCP-based)** for data plane (actual high-throughput message streams)

## 2.2 The Publisher–Subscriber Registration Flow (Step-by-Step Logic)

The operational flow when a publisher and subscriber come online can be broken down into **7 logical steps**:

1. **Master Boot:** Master starts, binds to port `11311`, and becomes reachable at `ROS_MASTER_URI`.
2. **Publisher Registration:** Publisher node calls Master via XML-RPC: `registerPublisher(topic_name, topic_type, node_uri)`.
3. **Subscriber Registration:** Subscriber node calls Master: `registerSubscriber(topic_name, topic_type, node_uri)`.
4. **Lookup Trigger:** Master detects that a matching publisher exists for the subscriber's topic.
5. **URI Exchange:** Master calls back the **publisher** with the subscriber's XML-RPC URI via the `publisherUpdate` API.
6. **Handshake:** Publisher contacts the subscriber via XML-RPC to negotiate a TCP connection (using `requestTopic` API).
7. **Data Stream:** Publisher and subscriber establish a raw **TCP socket** for message data; Master is **bypassed** for actual payload transfer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Highlight:** The Master is **NOT in the data path**. It only handles the metadata. Failing to state this clearly is one of the most common reasons for partial mark deductions in KTU ESE answers.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Reference Sheet

The table below summarizes the critical network addresses, ports, equations, and protocols associated with ROS Master. **No pipe characters are used inside table cells to prevent rendering issues.**

| Parameter | Symbol / Default Value | Protocol Used | Purpose | Notes for KTU |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Default Master Port | **11311** | XML-RPC / HTTP | Master listens for registrations | Configurable via `--port` flag |
| Default Master URI | `http://localhost:11311` | HTTP | Address of the Master | Set by `ROS_MASTER_URI` env var |
| Publisher Registration API | `registerPublisher` | XML-RPC | Registers a publishing endpoint | Called once at node startup |
| Subscriber Registration API | `registerSubscriber` | XML-RPC | Registers a subscribing endpoint | Called once at node startup |
| Topic Lookup API | `lookupTopic` | XML-RPC | Returns topic type & list of publishers | Used by tools like `rostopic` |
| Parameter Server API | `setParam` / `getParam` | XML-RPC | R/W shared dictionary | Accessible globally |
| Publisher-Subscriber Data | TCPROS | TCP (raw socket) | Actual message transport | Master not involved |
| UDP Variant | UDPROS | UDP (lossy, low-latency) | Optional for sensors | Rarely used in KTU exams |
| Graph Resource Name | `/ns/topic_name` | N/A | Unique name in the namespace tree | Must start with `/` |
| Message Latency Equation | $L = L_{\text{transmit}} + L_{\text{propagation}} + L_{\text{queue}}$ | N/A | Total transport latency | Simplified for KTU |

### 2.3.1 Transport Latency Expression (Conceptual)

The end-to-end message latency between a publisher and subscriber that the Master has brokered can be approximated as:

$$L_{total} = L_{transmit} + L_{propagation} + L_{queue} + L_{serialize}$$

Where each term represents a stage in the message journey. The **Master contributes zero to this latency** once the connection is established, since it is not in the data path.

### 2.3.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where ROS Master is used in Industry:** Autonomous vehicles (Waymo, Apollo), warehouse robotics (Amazon Kiva), humanoid robots (Pepper, NAO), drone swarms (PX4 autopilot), and surgical robotics (da Vinci Research Kit). All rely on the Master's decoupling property to scale from a 5-node prototype to 1000+ node production fleets.

## 2.4 Failure Modes and Robustness Considerations

A real-world KTU answer must address the **single point of failure (SPOF)** concern:

- If the Master crashes, **existing publisher-subscriber connections continue to function** (since they are peer-to-peer TCP).
- However, **no new nodes can be discovered**, and **parameter server is lost**.
- In production systems, this is mitigated by running the Master on a dedicated, monitored machine with automatic restart (`systemd`, `supervisord`).
- In ROS 2, the Master has been replaced by the **DDS (Data Distribution Service)** middleware, but ROS 1 (and the KTU 2024 syllabus) still centers on the Master model.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — The Complete Connection Lifecycle

This section provides an **exhaustive, step-by-step analytical breakdown** of the complete ROS Master connection lifecycle. Every transition, every XML-RPC call, and every protocol switch is enumerated in detail to satisfy the KTU 2024 depth requirement.

## 3.1 Sequence of Events: From `roscore` to Active Data Flow

Let $N_p$ represent a publisher node, $N_s$ a subscriber node, and $M$ the Master. The complete lifecycle can be expressed as an ordered sequence of XML-RPC invocations and TCP handshakes.

### Step 1: Master Initialization

The user executes `roscore` (which launches `rosmaster`, `rosout`, and `parameter_server`). The Master binds to TCP port `11311` and begins listening for HTTP/XML-RPC requests.

$$M_{\text{state}} \leftarrow \text{INITIALIZED} \quad \text{at} \quad URI = \text{http://hostname:11311}$$

### Step 2: Publisher Node Bootstraps

The publisher node $N_p$ reads the `ROS_MASTER_URI` environment variable and resolves the Master's address. It performs an HTTP GET to verify reachability.

$$N_p \xrightarrow{\text{XML-RPC}} M : \text{getUri()}$$

The Master responds with its canonical URI string.

### Step 3: Topic Registration by Publisher

The publisher declares the topic name, the message type, and its own XML-RPC callback URI.

$$N_p \xrightarrow{\text{XML-RPC}} M : \text{registerPublisher}(\text{topic} = \text{"/scan"}, \text{type} = \text{"sensor\_msgs/LaserScan"}, \text{node\_uri} = \text{http://N\_p:38291})$$

The Master appends this entry to its **subscriber table**:

$$\text{Table}_{M} \leftarrow \text{Table}_{M} \cup \{(\text{"/scan"}, N_{p,\text{uri}})\}$$

### Step 4: Subscriber Node Bootstraps

Subscriber $N_s$ performs the same `getUri()` check, then registers itself:

$$N_s \xrightarrow{\text{XML-RPC}} M : \text{registerSubscriber}(\text{topic} = \text{"/scan"}, \text{type} = \text{"sensor\_msgs/LaserScan"}, \text{node\_uri} = \text{http://N\_s:40123})$$

### Step 5: Master's Negotiation Callback

The Master compares the new subscription against existing publisher entries. Finding a match on topic name and message type, it pushes the subscriber's URI to the publisher:

$$M \xrightarrow{\text{XML-RPC}} N_p : \text{publisherUpdate}(\text{"/scan"}, [\text{http://N\_s:40123}])$$

### Step 6: Publisher-Subscriber Direct Handshake

The publisher $N_p$ now contacts $N_s$ directly (bypassing the Master for this step) to negotiate the **transport protocol**:

$$N_p \xrightarrow{\text{XML-RPC}} N_s : \text{requestTopic}(\text{"/scan"}, [\{\text{"TCPROS"}, \text{hostname}, \text{port}\}])$$

The subscriber selects `TCPROS` and returns the address where it will listen for the data stream.

### Step 7: TCP Data Channel Establishment

Both parties open a raw TCP socket. From this point onward, the data flow is **peer-to-peer**:

$$N_p \xleftrightarrow{\text{TCP (raw bytes)}} N_s$$

The Master $M$ is no longer in the data path. It only retains the registry entry for the lifetime of the nodes.

## 3.2 Mathematical Model of the Master as a Centralized Directory

We can model the Master's state as a set of tuples:

$$\text{State}_M = \{ (t, \text{type}, \text{URI}_i) \mid t \in \text{Topics}, i \in \text{Nodes} \}$$

The Master's lookup function can be expressed as:

$$L(t) = \{ \text{URI}_i \mid (t, \text{type}, \text{URI}_i) \in \text{State}_M \}$$

When a new subscription for topic $t$ is registered, the Master computes:

$$L(t) \neq \emptyset \implies \text{trigger } \text{publisherUpdate}() \text{ for each } \text{URI}_i \in L(t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** This mathematical formalization helps students articulate the Master's role rigorously in the KTU 14-mark answer. Examiners appreciate seeing this level of precision.

## 3.3 Lifecycle State Diagram (Tabular Form)

| Phase | Master Activity | Publisher Activity | Subscriber Activity | Network Protocol |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0. Cold Start | `roscore` initializes | None | None | N/A |
| 1. Publisher On | Appends to topic table | Calls `registerPublisher` | None | XML-RPC |
| 2. Subscriber On | Detects match, sends `publisherUpdate` | Receives callback | Calls `registerSubscriber` | XML-RPC |
| 3. Handshake | Idle | Calls `requestTopic` on subscriber | Responds with `TCPROS` URI | XML-RPC |
| 4. Data Flow | Idle (out of band) | Streams messages | Receives messages | TCP |
| 5. Shutdown | Removes from table | Unregisters via XML-RPC | Unregisters via XML-RPC | XML-RPC |

## 3.4 Detailed Worked Example: Two Nodes + Master

Consider three processes on a single machine: Master $M$ (port 11311), Publisher $P$ (port 38291), and Subscriber $S$ (port 40123). The topic is `/cmd_vel` of type `geometry_msgs/Twist`.

The complete sequence in **strict chronological order**:

1. $M$ binds to `0.0.0.0:11311`
2. $P$ resolves `ROS_MASTER_URI` and confirms reachability
3. $P$ calls `registerPublisher("/cmd_vel", "geometry_msgs/Twist", "http://P:38291")`
4. $M$ stores the entry and responds `1` (success code) plus its URI
5. $S$ resolves `ROS_MASTER_URI` and confirms reachability
6. $S$ calls `registerSubscriber("/cmd_vel", "geometry_msgs/Twist", "http://S:40123")`
7. $M$ detects the match and calls `publisherUpdate("/cmd_vel", ["http://S:40123"])` on $P$
8. $P$ calls `requestTopic("/cmd_vel", "http://S:40123")` with `TCPROS` proposal on local port `49152`
9. $S$ acknowledges and begins listening on its TCP port
10. $P$ opens TCP socket to $S$:49152 and starts streaming serialized `Twist` messages
11. $M$ takes no further action until a new node registers or a parameter is queried

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — ROS Master Coordination Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** showing the lifecycle of a publisher-subscriber connection mediated by the ROS Master. Node IDs are alphanumeric to comply with Mermaid rendering safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Boot Master: roscore] --> B[Master binds to port 11311]
    B --> C[Master announces ROS_MASTER_URI]
    C --> D[Publisher node starts]
    D --> E[Publisher calls registerPublisher via XMLRPC]
    E --> F[Master appends to topic registry table]
    F --> G[Subscriber node starts]
    G --> H[Subscriber calls registerSubscriber via XMLRPC]
    H --> I[Master detects topic match]
    I --> J[Master sends publisherUpdate callback to Publisher]
    J --> K[Publisher calls requestTopic on Subscriber]
    K --> L[Subscriber responds with TCPROS address]
    L --> M[Publisher opens TCP socket to Subscriber]
    M --> N[Data streams peer to peer bypassing Master]
    N --> O[Master remains idle in data path]
    O --> P[Node shutdown unregisters via XMLRPC]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — XML-RPC vs TCPROS Protocol Separation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ControlPlane[Control Plane: XML-RPC over HTTP]
        M1[Master Registry]
        P1[Publisher RPC Endpoint]
        S1[Subscriber RPC Endpoint]
    end
    subgraph DataPlane[Data Plane: TCPROS over TCP]
        P2[Publisher TCP Socket]
        S2[Subscriber TCP Socket]
    end
    M1 -- registerPublisher --> P1
    M1 -- registerSubscriber --> S1
    M1 -- publisherUpdate callback --> P1
    P1 -- requestTopic handshake --> S1
    P2 -- serialized messages --> S2
    style ControlPlane fill:#fff4cc
    style DataPlane fill:#cce5ff
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Master State Transitions

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ColdStart: roscore invoked
    ColdStart --> Listening: Port 11311 bound
    Listening --> RegistrationActive: Node calls register API
    RegistrationActive --> NegotiationActive: Topic match detected
    NegotiationActive --> DataFlowActive: requestTopic handshake complete
    DataFlowActive --> Listening: Subsequent registrations
    RegistrationActive --> Shutdown: Unregister API
    NegotiationActive --> Shutdown: Unregister API
    DataFlowActive --> Shutdown: Unregister API
    Listening --> Shutdown: Master process killed
    Shutdown --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Three Logical Sub-Components of the Master

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ROSMaster[ROS Master Process]
        A1[XMLRPC Server on Port 11311]
        A2[Parameter Server Dictionary]
        A3[Connection Negotiation Engine]
        A1 --> A3
        A2 --> A3
    end
    B1[Publisher Node] -- registerPublisher XMLRPC --> A1
    B2[Subscriber Node] -- registerSubscriber XMLRPC --> A1
    B3[rosparam client] -- setParam getParam XMLRPC --> A2
    A3 -- publisherUpdate callback --> B1
    B1 -- requestTopic XMLRPC --> B2
    B1 -- TCPROS direct TCP --> B2
    style ROSMaster fill:#d4edda
```

## 4.5 Diagram Interpretation Guide (For Exam Use)

> [!TIP]
> **How to draw this in an exam:** Even if Mermaid cannot be drawn on paper, the **logical grouping** is what examiners look for. Sketch three boxes labeled "Registry", "Parameter Server", and "Negotiator" inside a larger box labeled "ROS Master", with arrows showing XML-RPC going in and TCPROS bypassing it. This visualization is worth at least 3 marks in a 14-mark question.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: ` [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q1] `

> Define ROS Master. List any **two** of its core responsibilities.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The ROS Master is the central name service and registry of a ROS system that enables loose coupling between nodes by maintaining a global registry of topics, services, and parameters. It runs as a single process typically launched via `roscore` and listens on the default XML-RPC port **11311**.

**Responsibilities (1.5 marks — any two):**
1. **Name Registration and Lookup:** Tracks the URI of every active publisher and subscriber in the system, allowing nodes to discover each other without hard-coded addresses.
2. **Parameter Server Hosting:** Maintains a shared, multi-variate, network-accessible dictionary of configuration parameters that any node can read or modify using the `rosparam` tool.
3. **Connection Negotiation:** Facilitates the XML-RPC handshake between publishers and subscribers so they can establish a direct TCPROS data channel.

---

### Question 2: ` [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q2] `

> What is the role of XML-RPC in ROS Master communication? How is it different from TCPROS?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Role of XML-RPC (2 marks):** XML-RPC is a lightweight, XML-based remote procedure call protocol used by the ROS Master for the **control plane** of communication. All registration, lookup, parameter get/set, and connection negotiation traffic uses XML-RPC. It is a request/response protocol running over HTTP, making it ideal for the low-throughput metadata exchange between nodes and the Master. The default port for the Master is **11311**.

**Difference from TCPROS (1 mark):**

| Aspect | XML-RPC | TCPROS |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Control plane (registration, lookup) | Data plane (actual messages) |
| Transport | HTTP | Raw TCP socket |
| Speed | Slow, verbose | Fast, low overhead |
| Involves Master? | Yes (always) | No (Master bypassed) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): ` [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Long Q] `

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **architecture and working of the ROS Master**, including the complete publisher-subscriber connection lifecycle. Illustrate with a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **Parameter Server** component of ROS Master in detail, including supported data types, common command-line tools, and a real-world use case. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part A (a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):** The ROS Master is the central coordination service in a Robot Operating System. It does not transmit data itself; instead, it acts as a **matchmaker** between publisher and subscriber nodes by maintaining a registry of available topics, services, and node URIs.

**Architecture (3 marks):** The Master consists of three logical sub-components:

1. **XML-RPC Server** running on port **11311** that handles all incoming registration and lookup API calls.
2. **Parameter Server** that hosts a shared, multi-variate dictionary accessible network-wide.
3. **Connection Negotiation Engine** that detects when a new subscriber matches an existing publisher and triggers the URI exchange.

**Publisher-Subscriber Connection Lifecycle (3 marks — step by step):**
1. Publisher node $P$ reads `ROS_MASTER_URI` and confirms Master $M$ is reachable.
2. $P$ calls `registerPublisher(topic, type, P_uri)` via XML-RPC.
3. $M$ appends $P$ to its topic registry.
4. Subscriber node $S$ calls `registerSubscriber(topic, type, S_uri)` via XML-RPC.
5. $M$ detects the topic match and calls back $P$ with `publisherUpdate(S_uri)`.
6. $P$ contacts $S$ directly via `requestTopic` XML-RPC and proposes `TCPROS` on a chosen local port.
7. $S$ accepts and opens a TCP socket.
8. $P$ begins streaming serialized messages directly to $S$ using **TCPROS**. The Master $M$ is **not in the data path**.

**Block Diagram (Required for full marks):** Refer to SECTION 4 of these notes for a complete Mermaid block diagram. In a written exam, draw three boxes (Publisher, Master, Subscriber) with two distinct arrow types: dotted for XML-RPC and solid for TCPROS.

---

#### Model Solution for Part A (b) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark):** The Parameter Server is a shared, network-accessible, multi-variate dictionary that runs as part of the ROS Master process. It allows configuration values to be stored centrally and retrieved by any node at runtime.

**Supported Data Types (2 marks):**
- Integers (32-bit and 64-bit)
- Floats (32-bit and 64-bit)
- Booleans
- Strings
- Lists (of any of the above)
- Dictionaries (nested key-value structures)
- Binary blobs (Base64-encoded)

**Command-Line Tools (2 marks):** The `rosparam` tool provides the following operations:
- `rosparam set <key> <value>` — set a parameter
- `rosparam get <key>` — retrieve a parameter
- `rosparam load <yaml_file>` — bulk-load from a YAML file
- `rosparam dump <yaml_file>` — save current parameters to YAML
- `rosparam list` — list all keys
- `rosparam delete <key>` — remove a key

**Real-World Use Case (2 marks):** In an autonomous mobile robot, the Parameter Server can hold the wheel diameter, encoder resolution, and PID gains. At startup, the motion controller node reads these parameters via `getParam`, allowing engineers to tune the robot without recompiling code. The same parameters can be updated in real time for adaptive control.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: ` [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Long Q] `

> **(a)** With a neat sequence diagram, explain how the ROS Master negotiates a connection between a publisher and a subscriber. State the two distinct protocols involved and the role of each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare and contrast the **ROS Master (ROS 1)** architecture with the **DDS-based discovery (ROS 2)** architecture. Highlight three advantages of the ROS 2 approach. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (a) — 7 Marks

**Protocols Involved (1 mark):** The two protocols are **XML-RPC** (control plane) and **TCPROS** (data plane).

**Step-by-Step Sequence (4 marks):**
1. Publisher registers its topic and URI with the Master via XML-RPC.
2. Subscriber registers its interest in the topic via XML-RPC.
3. Master detects a match and pushes the subscriber's URI to the publisher via the `publisherUpdate` XML-RPC callback.
4. Publisher contacts the subscriber directly via XML-RPC's `requestTopic` API.
5. Subscriber responds with the host and port where it will accept a TCPROS connection.
6. Publisher opens a raw TCP socket and begins streaming messages.
7. All subsequent high-throughput data flows over TCPROS, completely bypassing the Master.

**Sequence Diagram (2 marks):** Draw three vertical lifelines (Publisher, Master, Subscriber) with horizontal arrows showing: registerPublisher, registerSubscriber, publisherUpdate, requestTopic, TCPROS-open, data stream.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | ROS 1 (Master-based) | ROS 2 (DDS-based) |
|---|---|---|
| Discovery | Centralized Master | Decentralized peer discovery |
| Single Point of Failure | Yes (Master crash) | No |
| Latency | Higher (extra hop during discovery) | Lower (direct peer discovery) |
| Protocol | XML-RPC + TCPROS | DDS-RTPS (vendor-specific) |
| Configuration Complexity | Simple | More complex |
| Quality of Service | Limited | Rich (reliability, durability, deadlines) |

**Three Advantages of ROS 2 / DDS (4 marks — 1.5 each, with explanation):**

1. **No Single Point of Failure:** DDS uses **distributed discovery** where each node multicasts its presence to others. If one node crashes, the rest of the system continues to operate. The ROS 1 Master is a single point of failure — if it goes down, no new connections can be made.

2. **Built-in Quality of Service (QoS):** DDS provides configurable policies for reliability, durability, deadline, and liveliness. This is critical for real-time and safety-critical applications like autonomous vehicles. ROS 1's Master architecture has very limited QoS support.

3. **Better Real-Time Performance:** DDS supports deterministic transport with bounded latency, making ROS 2 suitable for industrial and embedded real-time systems. ROS 1's XML-RPC-based discovery adds an unpredictable overhead during node startup.

4. *(Bonus point — can replace any of the above)* **Multi-vendor Interoperability:** DDS implementations from different vendors (RTI Connext, eProsima Fast DDS, Cyclone DDS) are interoperable, providing deployment flexibility. ROS 1 was tied to a single roscore implementation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in ROS Master Answers:**
> 1. **Stating the Master passes messages:** The Master does NOT carry actual sensor/control data. It only handles registration and URI exchange. Writing "messages are routed through the Master" loses **2 to 3 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the default port:** The default port is **11311**. Omitting it in a definition question is a typical **0.5 to 1 mark** deduction.
> 3. **Confusing XML-RPC with TCPROS:** XML-RPC is for control; TCPROS is for data. Mixing them up is a fundamental conceptual error.
> 4. **Not drawing a diagram in 7-mark sub-parts:** A 7-mark question on the connection lifecycle **requires** a sequence or block diagram. Skipping it costs **at least 2 marks**.
> 5. **Omitting real-world examples:** When asked about use cases (Parameter Server, Master crash recovery), always cite a concrete engineering example. Generic statements like "used in robots" lose marks.
> 6. **Spelling roscore as "ros-core" or "ROS Core":** The correct lowercase form is `roscore` (one word, no hyphen). Examiners deduct for inconsistencies.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets for ROS Master:**

- The ROS Master is a **name service and registry** — it does NOT transmit data.
- Default port: **11311**, default URI: `http://localhost:11311`, env var: `ROS_MASTER_URI`.
- Started by `roscore` (which also starts `rosout` and `parameter_server`).
- **Two protocols:** XML-RPC (control plane, port 11311) and TCPROS (data plane, raw TCP).
- Master's three components: **XML-RPC Server + Parameter Server + Connection Negotiator**.
- Registration calls: `registerPublisher` and `registerSubscriber` (XML-RPC).
- The negotiation callback is `publisherUpdate`, sent from Master to publisher.
- The final handshake uses `requestTopic` (XML-RPC) to agree on a TCPROS port.
- Once connected, **Master is bypassed**; data flows peer-to-peer.
- **Parameter Server** supports: int, float, bool, string, list, dict, base64 blob.
- `rosparam` tool: `set`, `get`, `load`, `dump`, `list`, `delete`.
- The Master is a **single point of failure** in ROS 1 — addressed by **DDS** in ROS 2.
- Graph resource names use slashes (e.g., `/camera/image_raw`).
- **Master contributes zero latency** to the data path post-connection.
- If Master crashes, **existing connections survive**; new ones cannot form.
- KTU 14-mark answers must include a **diagram** for the connection lifecycle question.
- KTU 3-mark answers must mention the **default port (11311)** for full credit.
- ROS 2's DDS architecture removes the Master but adds **QoS** and **decentralized discovery**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: roscore
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT525 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS)
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** roscore

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**roscore** is the **master process / launch orchestrator** of any running Robot Operating System (ROS) environment. Technically, `roscore` is a collection of three core pre-requisite background services that must be running before any user-defined ROS node can register, discover, or communicate with another node. When you execute the `roscore` command in a terminal, it simultaneously bootstraps and supervises the following three components inside a single parent process tree:

1. **ROS Master** — the central name-lookup and registration server.
2. **Parameter Server** — a shared, distributed multi-variables dictionary.
3. **rosout** — the standard ROS logging and diagnostic streamer node.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 1 — ROS Architecture):**
> *"roscore is the core services that the ROS system depends on. It is composed of a ROS Master, a Parameter Server, and a rosout logging node. It must be running for any ROS node to communicate."*

The **ROS Master** is implemented as an **XML-RPC** server listening on the default TCP port **11311**. It acts as the single source of truth for the computational graph of the entire ROS system.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Post Office of Robots"**
>
> Imagine a giant, busy **post office** in a city full of robot workers. Before any robot (node) can send a message to another robot, it must:
> 1. Walk into the post office and **register** its name and its mailbox address.
> 2. Ask the postmaster, *"Where does 'Robot B' live?"*
> 3. The postmaster replies with the exact address, and **only then** can the two robots talk **directly** to each other, **without** the post office relaying every message.
>
> In this analogy:
> - The **post office** = `roscore` (the entire service)
> - The **postmaster (the person at the counter)** = **ROS Master**
> - The **shared notice board** where everyone can pin and read notes = **Parameter Server**
> - The **security camera log** that records every announcement in the building = **rosout**

### 1.3 Why `roscore` is Mandatory

Every ROS node, when it starts up, sends a bootstrap request to the URI stored in the `ROS_MASTER_URI` environment variable. If no master is alive, the node refuses to start and prints a familiar KTU lab-viva error:

```
Unable to communicate with master!
```

This is why **launching `roscore` is always the *first* command** any ROS developer types in a terminal session.

### 1.4 Key Constants & Defaults

| Default | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Master TCP Port | **11311** | XML-RPC port of ROS Master |
| Master URI | `http://localhost:11311` | Where nodes look for the master |
| Log Directory | `~/.ros/log/` | rosout flushes to this folder |
| API Protocol | **XML-RPC** | Lightweight HTTP-based RPC |
| Discovery Protocol | **XML-RPC + UDP** | Master → node handshake |
| Default Slave URI | `http://<node-host>:<random-port>` | Where the node opens its own XML-RPC server |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of `roscore`

#### Pillar 1 — The ROS Master
- Implemented as a single-threaded XML-RPC server written in C++.
- Maintains the **computational graph** (nodes + topics + services + parameters).
- **Responsibilities** (exactly four):
  1. **Registration** of publishers, subscribers, services.
  2. **Name lookup** — translates logical names to URIs.
  3. **Topic & Service negotiation** — matches publishers to subscribers.
  4. **Parameter CRUD** — exposes the Parameter Server to clients.
- **Crucial point for KTU exams**: The Master is **NOT** in the data path. After two nodes discover each other, they establish a direct **TCPROS** connection and bypass the Master.

#### Pillar 2 — The Parameter Server
- A **multivariable dictionary** accessible by all nodes.
- Stored in memory inside the Master process; can be persisted to YAML via `rosparam`.
- Data types allowed: integers, floats, strings, booleans, lists, dictionaries, ISO timestamps.
- **Lifetime**: Parameters persist only as long as the Master is alive.

#### Pillar 3 — The rosout Node
- A special, always-on subscriber of the `/rosout` topic.
- Aggregates `ROS_INFO`, `ROS_WARN`, `ROS_ERROR`, `ROS_FATAL` messages.
- Mirrors everything to the screen, `/rosout_agg`, and disk files under `~/.ros/log/`.

### 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Memorize this table — it answers roughly 60% of Module-1 short-answer questions on `roscore`.

| Component | Protocol | Default Port | Role | Lifespan |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ROS Master | XML-RPC | **11311** | Central registry & name server | Lives with `roscore` |
| Parameter Server | XML-RPC | **11311** (shared) | Shared configuration dictionary | Lives with Master |
| rosout | TCPROS | random slave port | Aggregated logging | Lives with `roscore` |
| Publisher ↔ Subscriber link | TCPROS | random slave port | Actual data transfer | Direct, no master hop |
| Service call | XML-RPC + TCPROS | random slave port | Synchronous request/reply | Direct, no master hop |

### 2.3 The Four Stages of a Node Talking to `roscore`

1. **Read environment**: The new node reads `ROS_MASTER_URI` to find the Master address.
2. **Register**: The node calls `registerPublisher` / `registerSubscriber` over XML-RPC.
3. **Lookup**: The node calls `lookupNode` to find peer URIs.
4. **Connect directly**: The node opens a TCP socket to the peer (the **TCPROS** handshake). From this point onward, the Master is **out of the loop**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in robotics?**
> In a real autonomous car or drone swarm, the Master may be running on a workstation while sensors run on embedded boards. After the initial handshake, sensor data streams flow peer-to-peer, reducing the Master to a one-time bootstrap cost. This is what makes ROS scalable and **low-latency** for control loops.

### 2.4 How to Launch and Verify `roscore`

The canonical command sequence is:

```
$ roscore                      # starts master + param server + rosout
$ rosnode list                 # should show /rosout
$ rosparam list                # should show /rosversion, /rosdistro
$ rostopic list                # should show /rosout and /rosout_agg
```

To kill it cleanly: press `Ctrl+C` in the `roscore` terminal. Do **not** `kill -9` the master — child nodes will hang in zombie state.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Boot Sequence & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Exhaustive Boot Derivation

The following step-by-step trace shows exactly what happens when you press `Enter` on the `roscore` command. This is the *expected KTU answer* for "Explain the working of roscore" type 7-mark questions.

**Step 1 — Locate the executable**

The shell resolves `roscore` to a Python bootstrap script (`roscore`) and a C++ binary (`rosmaster`).

**Step 2 — Read environment variables**

The script reads, in order of priority:
- `ROS_MASTER_URI` (must end in a valid port; default `11311`)
- `ROS_HOSTNAME` or `ROS_IP` (advertised address)
- `ROS_LOG_DIR` (defaults to `~/.ros/log`)

**Step 3 — Launch the Parameter Server**

The Master process initializes an empty in-memory dictionary. Two well-known keys are auto-populated: `/rosdistro` and `/rosversion`.

**Step 4 — Launch the ROS Master XML-RPC server**

The Master binds a TCP socket to `0.0.0.0:11311` and begins listening for XML-RPC calls. It opens a Slaves dictionary, a Topics dictionary, and a Services dictionary — all in memory.

**Step 5 — Launch the rosout node**

A standard ROS node is spawned that subscribes to `/rosout` and republishes aggregated logs to `/rosout_agg`.

**Step 6 — Print the ready banner**

The terminal displays the famous KTU-lab banner:

```
started core service [/rosout]
started core service [/parameter_server]
started core service [/master]
```

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Failure Modes & Fixes

| Symptom in terminal | Root cause | KTU-correct fix |
|---|---|---|
| `Unable to contact master at http://localhost:11311` | `roscore` not started | Run `roscore` in a separate terminal first |
| `Address already in use` | A previous `roscore` or stray `rosmaster` still holds port 11311 | `killall -9 rosmaster roscore` then retry |
| Nodes register but messages don't flow | `ROS_HOSTNAME` mismatched between two machines | Export same `ROS_IP` on every machine |
| `/rosout` topic missing | Custom `roscore` was launched without rosout | Always use stock `roscore`, do not run Master alone |
| `RLException: [..] is not a launch file` | `roscore` not sourced; setup.bash not run | `source /opt/ros/<distro>/setup.bash` |

### 3.3 Mathematical Model of the Master-Node Handshake

Although `roscore` is a systems-engineering topic, the handshake can be expressed as a state-machine transition that KTU examiners love to ask as a 7-mark "explain with a neat diagram/derivation" question.

$$
S = \{S_0,\ S_1,\ S_2,\ S_3\}
$$

$$
\delta : S \times E \rightarrow S
$$

where each state and event pair is defined as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_0 &\rightarrow \text{NODE\_UNINITIALIZED} \\
S_1 &\rightarrow \text{NODE\_REGISTERED\_WITH\_MASTER} \\
S_2 &\rightarrow \text{PEER\_URI\_RESOLVED} \\
S_3 &\rightarrow \text{TCPROS\_HANDSHAKE\_COMPLETE}
\end{aligned}
$$

The transition function `δ` and the corresponding triggering XML-RPC / TCP events are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(S_0, \text{call\_registerPublisher}) &= S_1 \\
\delta(S_1, \text{call\_lookupNode}) &= S_2 \\
\delta(S_2, \text{open\_TCPROS\_socket}) &= S_3 \\
\delta(S_3, \text{any\_data\_message}) &= S_3 \quad (\text{steady state})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** This 4-state model is the formal foundation of the ROS 2 DDS discovery mechanism. Students aiming for research/MTech should recognize it as a finite-state implementation of the **publisher-subscriber pattern** described in the Gamma et al. *Design Patterns* literature.

### 3.4 Latency Calculation (Worked Example)

Suppose a robot has 10 nodes, each registering 3 topics at startup. The XML-RPC round-trip time on localhost is `1 ms`. Compute the **total bootstrap time `T_boot`**.

$$
T_{boot} = N_{nodes} \times N_{reg} \times T_{rpc}
$$

Substituting the numbers:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{boot} &= 10 \times 3 \times 1\ \text{ms} \\
T_{boot} &= 30\ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is why ROS-based systems can be brought online in well under a second — a key reason ROS is the de-facto standard in academic robotics and is now used by **Boston Dynamics**, **NASA JPL** (Mars Helicopter *Ingenuity*), and **Autonomous Vehicle** stacks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of `roscore`

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    user["USER TERMINAL"]:::userNode
    roscoreProc["ROSCORE PROCESS"]:::coreNode
    master["ROS MASTER XML-RPC SERVER PORT 11311"]:::masterNode
    param["PARAMETER SERVER SHARED DICTIONARY"]:::paramNode
    rosoutNode["ROSOUT LOGGING SUBSCRIBER"]:::logNode
    nodeA["ROBOT NODE A PUBLISHER"]:::leafNode
    nodeB["ROBOT NODE B SUBSCRIBER"]:::leafNode
    nodeC["ROBOT NODE C SERVICE SERVER"]:::leafNode
    logdir["LOG FILES UNDER DOT ROS LOG"]:::diskNode
    topicPipe["DIRECT TCPROS SOCKET BETWEEN A AND B"]:::dataNode

    user -- launches --> roscoreProc
    roscoreProc --> master
    roscoreProc --> param
    roscoreProc --> rosoutNode

    nodeA -- XML-RPC registerPublisher --> master
    nodeB -- XML-RPC registerSubscriber --> master
    nodeC -- XML-RPC registerService --> master
    master -- returns peer URI --> nodeA
    master -- returns peer URI --> nodeB

    nodeA -. discovers .-> nodeB
    nodeA == topicPipe ==> nodeB

    rosoutNode -- flushes --> logdir

    classDef userNode fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000
    classDef coreNode fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
    classDef masterNode fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000
    classDef paramNode fill:#d9f99d,stroke:#3f6212,color:#000
    classDef logNode fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d,color:#000
    classDef leafNode fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#000
    classDef diskNode fill:#e5e7eb,stroke:#374151,color:#000
    classDef dataNode fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412,color:#000,stroke-width:3px
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — 4-State Boot Sequence

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S0
    S0: S0 NODE UNINITIALIZED
    S1: S1 REGISTERED WITH MASTER
    S2: S2 PEER URI RESOLVED
    S3: S3 TCPROS HANDSHAKE COMPLETE
    S0 --> S1: XML-RPC call registerPublisher
    S1 --> S2: XML-RPC call lookupNode
    S2 --> S3: Open direct TCP socket TCPROS
    S3 --> S3: Steady state data flow
    S3 --> [*]: Node shutdown SIGINT
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Failure & Recovery Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start["START NODE"]:::leafNode
    checkCore["IS ROSCORE RUNNING?"]:::coreNode
    err1["EXIT: UNABLE TO CONTACT MASTER"]:::errNode
    setEnv["EXPORT ROS_MASTER_URI"]:::coreNode
    reg["REGISTER WITH MASTER"]:::masterNode
    ok["NODE READY"]:::okNode
    end["SHUTDOWN CLEAN"]:::endNode

    start --> checkCore
    checkCore -- NO --> err1
    checkCore -- YES --> setEnv
    setEnv --> reg
    reg --> ok
    ok --> end

    classDef leafNode fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#000
    classDef coreNode fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
    classDef masterNode fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000
    classDef errNode fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000
    classDef okNode fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    classDef endNode fill:#e5e7eb,stroke:#374151,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The solid arrows represent XML-RPC control messages (the Master is involved). The **bold double-line arrow** in the first diagram represents the direct **TCPROS** data pipe that *bypasses* the Master after handshake. This distinction is the single most important conceptual point of `roscore`.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
*Define `roscore`. List its three main components and state the default port on which the ROS Master listens.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
`roscore` is the core service suite of ROS that must be running before any other ROS node can communicate. It consists of:
1. **ROS Master** — XML-RPC name-lookup server (default TCP port **11311**).
2. **Parameter Server** — shared key-value configuration dictionary.
3. **rosout** — aggregated logging node that subscribes to the `/rosout` topic.

*(1 mark for definition + 1.5 marks for components + 0.5 mark for port = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional Exam]**
*Why does ROS use a Master? What is the role of the environment variable `ROS_MASTER_URI`?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
ROS uses a Master so that nodes running on different processes or machines can **discover each other by name** without hard-coding IP addresses. Once a node starts, it reads the `ROS_MASTER_URI` environment variable to find the master's XML-RPC endpoint (default `http://localhost:11311`) and registers its publishers, subscribers, and services with it. The Master then enables peer-to-peer connection establishment between nodes.

*(1 mark for discovery role + 1 mark for name resolution + 1 mark for `ROS_MASTER_URI` purpose = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Question]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **architecture and working of `roscore`** with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of the **ROS Master** and the **Parameter Server** in detail.

**(b) [7 marks]** With the help of a sequence diagram, explain the **complete handshake process** between a publisher node, the ROS Master, and a subscriber node. Clearly differentiate between **XML-RPC** and **TCPROS** communication.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(a)

The `roscore` process launches three background services:

1. **ROS Master (XML-RPC server on port 11311):** It is the central registry. Each node, on startup, registers its publishers, subscribers, and services. The Master keeps an in-memory graph of all topics, services, and the URIs of the nodes that own them. It is **not** in the data path; its job ends once it has told two nodes where to find each other.

2. **Parameter Server:** A multi-variable dictionary that lives inside the Master process. It allows nodes to share global configuration such as robot dimensions, sensor offsets, controller gains, and calibration matrices. Data is stored in memory but can be dumped/loaded from YAML files using `rosparam dump` / `rosparam load`.

3. **rosout:** A standard subscriber to the `/rosout` topic that aggregates log messages from all nodes, displays them in the terminal, and flushes them to disk under `~/.ros/log/`.

**Block Diagram:** Refer to the Mermaid Functional Architecture diagram in Section 4.1 above.

**Working sequence:**
- Step 1: The user types `roscore`.
- Step 2: The shell script reads `ROS_MASTER_URI` and `ROS_HOSTNAME`.
- Step 3: The Master XML-RPC server binds port 11311.
- Step 4: The Parameter Server initializes with `/rosdistro` and `/rosversion`.
- Step 5: The rosout node is launched and subscribes to `/rosout`.
- Step 6: The terminal prints the "started core service" banner — system is now ready.

*(2 marks for three components + 2 marks for individual roles + 2 marks for block diagram + 1 mark for working sequence = 7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(b)

The handshake between a publisher, the Master, and a subscriber proceeds in **four distinct XML-RPC/TCPROS phases**:

1. **Publisher Registration**: Node A calls `registerPublisher("/cmd_vel", "geometry_msgs/Twist", URI_A)`. The Master stores this in its topics dictionary.
2. **Subscriber Registration**: Node B calls `registerSubscriber("/cmd_vel", "geometry_msgs/Twist", URI_B)`. The Master detects that a publisher already exists and triggers step 3.
3. **Publisher URI lookup**: The Master returns `URI_A` to Node B (and similarly `URI_B` to Node A in the reverse direction).
4. **Direct TCPROS connection**: Node B opens a TCP socket to `URI_A` and sends a ROS connection header containing topic name, message type, and MD5 checksum. Node A validates the header and replies with its own. From this point on, **all data flows directly between A and B** without involving the Master.

**XML-RPC vs TCPROS:**

| Aspect | XML-RPC | TCPROS |
|---|---|---|
| Used for | Control/registration messages | Bulk data streaming |
| Protocol | HTTP-POST with XML body | Raw TCP socket |
| Latency | Higher (HTTP overhead) | Lower (streaming) |
| When used | Startup handshake only | Steady-state data flow |
| Master involvement | Always via Master | Direct peer-to-peer |

*(2 marks for sequence + 2 marks for XML-RPC vs TCPROS + 2 marks for diagram + 1 mark for steady-state explanation = 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main Question Alternate]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **role of the Parameter Server** in ROS. Explain with examples how `rosparam set`, `rosparam get`, and YAML persistence are used in robotics applications.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare the **ROS Master architecture (ROS 1)** with the **DDS-based discovery (ROS 2)**. Why did the ROS community move away from a centralized Master? Mention any two real-world robotics systems that use ROS 2 today.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(a)

The **Parameter Server** is a shared, in-memory key-value dictionary maintained by the `roscore` process. It allows configuration data to be decoupled from the source code, which is critical for robotics applications where the same node must run on different physical robots.

**Use cases with examples:**

- **Wheel radius and track width** of a differential-drive robot:
  ```
  $ rosparam set /wheel_radius 0.05
  $ rosparam set /track_width 0.34
  ```
  The odometry node reads these on startup to compute tick-to-meter conversion.

- **Camera intrinsic matrix `K` and distortion coefficients `D`**:
  ```
  $ rosparam set /camera_matrix "[fx, 0, cx, 0, fy, cy, 0, 0, 1]"
  ```

- **Controller gains** for a PID loop: `/Kp`, `/Ki`, `/Kd`.

**YAML persistence:** A team can dump all parameters to a YAML file:
```
$ rosparam dump my_robot.yaml
$ rosparam load my_robot.yaml
```
This enables **version control** of robot configuration in Git and rapid switching between sim/real setups.

*(2 marks for definition + 2 marks for examples + 2 marks for YAML persistence + 1 mark for engineering utility = 7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(b)

| Feature | ROS 1 (Master-based) | ROS 2 (DDS-based) |
|---|---|---|
| Discovery | Centralized XML-RPC Master | Decentralized DDS peer discovery |
| Single point of failure | Yes (Master) | No (peer-to-peer) |
| Real-time support | Poor | Excellent (Connext DDS, Cyclone DDS) |
| Multicast support | None | Native (UDP multicast) |
| Security | None built-in | SROS2 (DDS-Security) |
| Embedded suitability | Limited | High (micro-ROS on microcontrollers) |

**Why move away from a centralized Master?**
- The Master is a single point of failure — if it crashes, the entire robot stops communicating.
- It cannot satisfy **real-time** guarantees required for hard real-time control loops.
- It does not scale to large multi-robot swarms where hundreds of nodes must discover each other.
- The Master is not **QoS-aware**, whereas DDS provides rich Quality-of-Service policies (reliability, durability, deadline, liveliness).

**Real-world ROS 2 deployments:**
1. **NASA JPL Mars Helicopter *Ingenuity*** — flew on ROS 2 with Foxy + custom DDS.
2. **Autonomous vehicles** such as **Apex.AI** and **Autoware.Auto** — use ROS 2 for safety-critical stacks.
3. **Boston Dynamics Spot** — uses ROS 2 as the official SDK base.

*(2 marks for comparison table + 2 marks for real-time/QoS reasoning + 2 marks for two real-world examples + 1 mark for conclusion = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on `roscore` Questions:**
> 1. **Writing "port 8080" or "port 80"** — the correct default is **11311**. Many students lose 1 full mark here.
> 2. **Stating that the Master relays data** — this is the #1 conceptual error. The Master is **only** a name-lookup server. Data flows peer-to-peer over TCPROS.
> 3. **Forgetting the Parameter Server** — some answers mention only Master + rosout. The Parameter Server is the *third* pillar and carries its own marks.
> 4. **Confusing `roscore` with `roslaunch`** — `roscore` is the *infrastructure*; `roslaunch` is a *launcher* that happens to call `roscore` first.
> 5. **Omitting the `~/.ros/log/` directory** — examiners explicitly check for the log path in the rosout discussion.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **`roscore` = Master + Parameter Server + rosout.** Always start a question answer with this one-line definition.
- Default port of the ROS Master = **TCP 11311 (XML-RPC)**.
- `ROS_MASTER_URI` is the single environment variable every node reads to find the master.
- The Master is **NOT in the data path**; it only performs the startup handshake.
- Steady-state communication is **direct peer-to-peer** over **TCPROS**.
- The Parameter Server is an in-memory dictionary, optionally persisted to YAML via `rosparam`.
- `rosout` aggregates logs to `/rosout_agg` and `~/.ros/log/`.
- A node fails to start with `"Unable to communicate with master"` if `roscore` is not running.
- ROS 2 eliminates the Master by using **DDS** discovery — relevant for real-time, swarm, and embedded applications.
- Always `Ctrl+C` the `roscore` terminal; never `kill -9` the master.
- Real-world ROS 2 adopters include **NASA JPL (Ingenuity)** and **Boston Dynamics (Spot)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision one-liner:**
> *"`roscore` boots Master on 11311, hands out names, then gets out of the way while nodes stream over TCPROS."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Catkin: Workspaces and ROS Packages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT525 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Catkin: Workspaces and ROS Packages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Catkin?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Catkin** is the official **build system** of the Robot Operating System (ROS). It is a collection of **CMake macros** and **Python scripts** that together provide a standardized, unified, and highly parallelizable infrastructure for building, packaging, testing, and installing ROS software modules. The name "Catkin" is a botanical reference (it is the flower cluster of a willow tree), continuing ROS's tradition of turtle-related and nature-related naming (ROS = Robot Operating System, but also a play on words).

Formally, Catkin replaces the older **`rosbuild`** system that was used in ROS Fuerte and earlier distributions. From ROS Groovy onward (2012), Catkin became the standard and is mandatory in all modern ROS 1 distributions (Noetic, Melodic, Kinetic, etc.) and is the foundation underneath **ROS 2 (Foxy, Humble, Iron, Jazzy)** as well, where it has been refactored under **`ament`** with similar concepts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a dedicated build system?** ROS projects are not single executables — they consist of multiple nodes, libraries, message definitions, launch files, and Python scripts that must be cross-compiled and linked together. Catkin solves the orchestration problem of building dozens of interdependent packages coherently.

---

## 1.2 What is a Catkin Workspace?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Catkin Workspace** (often referred to as a `catkin_ws`) is a top-level directory on your filesystem that serves as a **sandbox** where you compile, manage, and develop one or more Catkin packages together. It is the smallest unit under which you invoke the Catkin build tools.

A workspace acts as a **container** that isolates your ROS development environment from the system-wide ROS installation. This isolation prevents you from accidentally modifying the core ROS files (which live under `/opt/ros/<distro>/`) and allows multiple parallel workspaces to coexist.

---

## 1.3 What is a ROS Package?
