> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **ROS Package** is the **atomic unit of software organization and release** in ROS. It is a directory containing your source code, configuration, build manifests, and documentation in a strict, self-describing structure that Catkin can recognize, build, and distribute.

Think of a package as a **single, well-defined library or application** that exposes a specific robotic capability — for example, a package that implements a SLAM algorithm, another that drives a motor controller, another that captures camera frames, etc.

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Real-World Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **The Construction Site Analogy** 🏗️

Imagine you are building a **house** (your robot software):

| ROS Concept | Real-World Analogy | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| **Catkin** | The **construction company** with its standard operating procedures, tools, and protocols | It dictates *how* everything is built, not *what* |
| **Workspace** | The **construction site** itself — a fenced plot of land where all work happens | Isolates your project; multiple sites can exist side-by-side |
| **Package** | A **single prefabricated module** (kitchen unit, bathroom block, electrical panel) | Self-contained, has a manifest (BOM), and can be lifted, tested, and shipped independently |
| **package.xml** | The **Bill of Materials (BOM)** | Lists what is inside and what other modules it depends on |
| **CMakeLists.txt** | The **assembly instructions** for that module | Tells the construction crew how to assemble it |
| **src/** folder | The **raw materials and blueprints** | Where your actual C++/Python source code lives |
| **catkin_make / catkin build** | The act of **operating the cranes and assembling the module** | Transforms source into usable executables |
| **devel/** folder | The **finished, ready-to-install module** sitting at the site gate | Built artifacts waiting to be used |
| **source setup.bash** | The act of **registering the new module** with the city's electrical/water grid | Tells ROS where to find the newly built packages |

This analogy will reappear throughout the notes whenever we need instant intuition.

---

## 1.5 High-Level Architecture — How They Fit Together

The relationship is strictly hierarchical:

```
Catkin (Build System)
        │
        │ operates on
        ▼
   Workspace (catkin_ws)
        │
        │ contains 1..N
        ▼
    Package(s)
        │
        │ built into
        ▼
  Built Artifacts (devel/install)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One key invariant**: A package **cannot** exist outside a workspace, and a workspace **must** contain at least the `src/` subdirectory to be considered valid. Catkin will refuse to build an empty or malformed workspace.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a Catkin Workspace

A Catkin workspace is, by convention, named `catkin_ws` (although any name is valid). Internally, it follows a **strict 4-folder convention** that is enforced by the build tools.

### 2.1.1 The Four Standard Directories

| Directory | Created When | Contents | Visibility |
|---|---|---|---|
| `src/` | **Manually** by you | All your ROS package source code. This is the **only** directory you normally edit. | Editable |
| `build/` | **Automatically** during `catkin_make` | Intermediate build files, object files, CMake/Make cache, testing outputs. Treated as disposable. | Regenerated |
| `devel/` | **Automatically** during `catkin_make` | Built executables, libraries, generated message headers (`*.h`), Python eggs, and the crucial `setup.bash` script that overlays your workspace on top of the system ROS installation. | Read-only (mostly) |
| `install/` | **Automatically** during `catkin_make install` (optional) | A **relocatable, system-install-ready** version of your workspace. Used when packaging for deployment or distribution via `.deb` packages. | Read-only |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Why behind `devel/`**: The `devel/` folder mimics the directory layout of `/opt/ros/<distro>/` (the system ROS install). When you `source devel/setup.bash`, your shell's environment variables (`ROS_PACKAGE_PATH`, `PATH`, `PYTHONPATH`, `LD_LIBRARY_PATH`) are extended so the system *thinks* your freshly-built packages are part of the core ROS install. This is called **workspace overlaying**.

---

## 2.2 The Anatomy of a Catkin Package

Every Catkin package, by ROS REP-0127 and REP-0140 standards, contains **two mandatory files** and any number of optional conventional subdirectories.

### 2.2.1 The Two Mandatory Files

1. **`package.xml`** — the **Manifest**
   - Written in XML, follows the format defined in **REP-0127** (ROS Enhancement Proposal).
   - Declares:
     - `<name>`: package name (must match directory name)
     - `<version>`: semantic version (e.g., `1.2.0`)
     - `<description>`: one-line summary
     - `<maintainer>` and `<license>`
     - **Dependencies**: `<build_depend>`, `<build_export_depend>`, `<exec_depend>`, `<doc_depend>`, `<test_depend>`
     - Optional: `<url>`, `<author>`, `<export>` tag for pluginlib
2. **`CMakeLists.txt`** — the **Build Recipe**
   - CMake script with **exactly four mandatory lines** at the top:
     1. `cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.0.2)`
     2. `project(your_package_name)`
     3. `find_package(catkin REQUIRED COMPONENTS ...)`
     4. `catkin_package()`
   - Then you add your own `add_executable()`, `add_library()`, etc.

### 2.2.2 The Conventional Subdirectories

| Subdirectory | Purpose | Common Use |
|---|---|---|
| `src/` | C++ source files (`.cpp`, `.hpp`) | Node implementations, libraries |
| `include/<pkg>/` | Public C++ headers exposed to other packages | Reusable APIs |
| `scripts/` | Python scripts directly executable (with shebang `#!/usr/bin/env python`) | Quick utility nodes |
| `src/<pkg>/` or `src/<pkg_name>/` (Python) | Python modules following the `<pkg_name>` directory layout | Pure-Python packages |
| `msg/` | Custom `.msg` files | Service/request/response message types |
| `srv/` | Custom `.srv` files | Service definitions |
| `action/` | Custom `.action` files | Action server definitions |
| `launch/` | `.launch` XML files | Multi-node startup orchestration |
| `config/` | YAML/param files | Dynamic reconfigure, controller tuning |
| `urdf/`, `meshes/`, `rviz/` | Robot description, 3D meshes, RViz configs | Visualization |
| `test/` | Unit and integration tests | `rostest` integration |
| `docs/` | Documentation | Optional |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note**: Questions in KTU exams frequently ask you to list the **mandatory components** of a package. The answer is always: **(1) `package.xml` + (2) `CMakeLists.txt`**. Optional subdirectories like `launch/`, `msg/`, `src/` are *not* mandatory for the package to be valid.

---

## 2.3 Catkin Build Process — Theoretical Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why"**: Catkin is essentially CMake + Python glue + parallel jobs. When you run `catkin_make`, the following sequence executes (see SECTION 3 for the explicit command sequence).

**Phase 1 — Discovery**
Catkin recursively scans the `src/` directory, identifies every directory containing a valid `package.xml`, and builds a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** of package dependencies using the `<build_depend>` tags.

**Phase 2 — Topological Sort**
The DAG is topologically sorted so that a package `P` is only built **after all of its dependencies** have been built. This guarantees that headers from dependency packages exist when the consumer package is compiled.

**Phase 3 — Per-Package CMake Invocation**
For each package, in topological order, Catkin invokes CMake inside `build/<pkg>/`. CMake runs `CMakeLists.txt`, generates Makefiles or Ninja files, and produces object files.

**Phase 4 — Parallel Make**
GNU Make (or Ninja) is invoked with `-j$(nproc)` (parallel jobs equal to CPU core count) to compile and link simultaneously.

**Phase 5 — Message Generation**
If a package has `msg/` or `srv/` directories, Catkin auto-invokes the `genmsg` / `gensrv` tools to generate C++ headers and Python modules from the message definitions. These are written back into `devel/include/<pkg>/` and `devel/lib/python.../`.

**Phase 6 — Overlay**
The `devel/` directory's `setup.bash` is regenerated (or appended to), pointing to all built targets so the user can `source` it.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these commands and their meanings** — they appear almost verbatim in KTU examinations.

| # | Command / File | Formula / Structure | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `mkdir -p ~/catkin_ws/src` | Workspace bootstrap | Create the mandatory `src/` folder |
| 2 | `cd ~/catkin_ws/` && `catkin_make` | $\text{build\_state}_{new} = f(\text{src\_state})$ | Build all packages from source |
| 3 | `source devel/setup.bash` | $\text{ROS\_PACKAGE\_PATH}_{new} = \text{ROS\_PACKAGE\_PATH}_{sys} \cup \text{workspace\_path}$ | Overlay built packages onto ROS environment |
| 4 | `catkin_create_pkg <pkg> [deps...]` | $\text{package} = \{\text{package.xml}, \text{CMakeLists.txt}\}$ | Scaffold a new package with dependencies |
| 5 | `cd src && catkin_init_workspace` | Initialize a folder as a workspace root | (Optional for `catkin_make`, required for `catkin build`) |
| 6 | `rospack find <pkg>` | Returns absolute path | Locate any package on the ROS path |
| 7 | `rospack depends1 <pkg>` | Direct dependencies | List immediate build dependencies |
| 8 | `rospack depends <pkg>` | Full transitive closure | List every package required (direct + indirect) |
| 9 | `rosdep install --from-paths src --ignore-src -r -y` | $\bigcup_{p \in \text{pkgs}} \text{system\_deps}(p)$ | Install OS-level (apt) dependencies |
| 10 | `catkin_make -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release` | Build flag | Enable compiler optimizations (`-O3`) |

### 2.4.1 Dependency Semantics in `package.xml`

The five dependency tags are **not** interchangeable. KTU examiners test this distinction:

| Tag | Meaning | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| `<build_depend>` | Needed to **compile this package** (e.g., headers, CMake configs) | Header includes, `find_package` calls |
| `<build_export_depend>` | Needed to compile this package **and** by downstream packages | Public headers exposed in `catkin_package(INCLUDE_DIRS ...)` |
| `<exec_depend>` | Needed at **runtime** (e.g., shared libraries, executables) | Pluginlibs, runtime message packages |
| `<doc_depend>` | Needed to **build documentation** (e.g., rosdoc_lite, sphinx) | Only when building docs |
| `<test_depend>` | Needed only to **run tests** | GTest, rostest, mock libraries |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where this matters in industry:**
> - **Autonomous Vehicle stacks** (e.g., Autoware) ship as dozens of Catkin packages — navigation, perception, control are each a separate package with strict dependency declarations.
> - **Robot arms in manufacturing** use the `ros_control` and `moveit` packages which are deeply nested dependencies. Catkin's topological build ensures the entire chain compiles in one command.
> - **CI/CD pipelines** (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI for ROS) invoke `catkin_make` or `colcon build` in Docker containers — understanding workspace structure is essential for writing correct CI YAML files.
> - **Docker images for robots** (e.g., `ros:humble-ros-base`) overlay user workspaces via `install/` rather than `devel/` for production deployments.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Workflow — Creating Your First Catkin Workspace

This is the **canonical, step-by-step procedure** that KTU expects you to be able to write down from memory in an exam.

### Step 1 — Create the Workspace Root and Mandatory `src/` Directory

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `-p`?** The `-p` flag tells `mkdir` to create parent directories if needed and to suppress errors if the directory already exists. This makes the command **idempotent**.

```bash
mkdir -p ~/catkin_ws/src
cd ~/catkin_ws/src
```

At this point, the state on disk is:

$$
\text{Workspace}_{\text{state}_0} = \{\;\text{catkin\_ws}/\text{src}/\;\}
$$

Only the `src/` directory exists. `build/`, `devel/`, and `install/` do **not** yet exist.

### Step 2 — Initialize the Workspace (Optional but Recommended)

```bash
catkin_init_workspace
```

This creates a hidden marker file `src/.catkin_tools.yaml` (and historically `src/CMakeLists.txt` symlink) that officially marks the directory as a Catkin workspace root. Without it, `catkin_make` will still work, but **`catkin build` (the modern alternative) will refuse to operate**.

### Step 3 — Build the (Initially Empty) Workspace

```bash
cd ~/catkin_ws
catkin_make
```

What `catkin_make` does, internally:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{catkin\_make}(\text{catkin\_ws}) &\rightarrow \text{CMake configure step} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Generate } \text{build/CMakeCache.txt} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Topological sort of 0 packages} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Generate Makefiles} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Invoke make with } -jN \\
&\rightarrow \text{Populate } \text{devel/ with setup.bash, env, lib, include}
\end{aligned}
$$

After this step, the workspace state is:

$$
\text{Workspace}_{\text{state}_1} = \{\;\text{src}/,\;\text{build}/,\;\text{devel}/\;\}
$$

Three directories now exist. The `devel/setup.bash` script is the **most important file** generated.

### Step 4 — Verify the Build Artifacts

```bash
ls ~/catkin_ws/build
ls ~/catkin_ws/devel
```

You should see directories like `build/CMakeFiles/`, `build/Makefile`, `devel/setup.bash`, `devel/setup.zsh`, `devel/env.sh`, and (if any package uses messages) `devel/include/`.

### Step 5 — Source the Workspace Overlay

```bash
source ~/catkin_ws/devel/setup.bash
```

This script appends paths to critical environment variables:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PATH} &\leftarrow \text{PATH} \cup \{\;\text{devel/lib},\;\text{devel/bin}\;\} \\
\text{PYTHONPATH} &\leftarrow \text{PYTHONPATH} \cup \{\;\text{devel/lib/python3/dist-packages}\;\} \\
\text{LD\_LIBRARY\_PATH} &\leftarrow \text{LD\_LIBRARY\_PATH} \cup \{\;\text{devel/lib}\;\} \\
\text{ROS\_PACKAGE\_PATH} &\leftarrow \text{ROS\_PACKAGE\_PATH} \cup \{\;\text{catkin\_ws/src}\;\} \\
\text{CMAKE\_PREFIX\_PATH} &\leftarrow \text{CMAKE\_PREFIX\_PATH} \cup \{\;\text{devel}\;\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight**: To make this overlay permanent for every new terminal, append `source ~/catkin_ws/devel/setup.bash` to your `~/.bashrc`.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Workflow — Creating Your First ROS Package

Now that the workspace exists, the next procedure is package creation.

### Step 1 — Navigate to the Source Directory

```bash
cd ~/catkin_ws/src
```

### Step 2 — Invoke `catkin_create_pkg`

```bash
catkin_create_pkg beginner_tutorials std_msgs rospy roscpp
```

This single command does four things atomically:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{catkin\_create\_pkg} &\rightarrow \text{Create directory } \text{beginner\_tutorials}/ \\
&\rightarrow \text{Generate } \text{package.xml} \text{ with } \text{std\_msgs, rospy, roscpp} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Generate } \text{CMakeLists.txt} \text{ with matching find\_package() lines} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Create stub } \text{include/, src/, scripts/, launch/, config/} \text{ folders}
\end{aligned}
$$

The final on-disk layout:

$$
\text{Package} = \{\;
\begin{array}{l}
\text{beginner\_tutorials/package.xml} \\
\text{beginner\_tutorials/CMakeLists.txt} \\
\text{beginner\_tutorials/include/beginner\_tutorials/} \\
\text{beginner\_tutorials/src/} \\
\text{beginner\_tutorials/scripts/} \\
\text{beginner\_tutorials/launch/} \\
\text{beginner\_tutorials/config/}
\end{array}
\;\}
$$

### Step 3 — Verify Package Metadata

```bash
cat ~/catkin_ws/src/beginner_tutorials/package.xml
```

The generated `package.xml` will look like:

```xml
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<package format="2">
  <name>beginner_tutorials</name>
  <version>0.0.0</version>
  <description>The beginner_tutorials package</description>
  <maintainer email="you@todo.todo">Your Name</maintainer>
  <license>TODO</license>
  <buildtool_depend>catkin</buildtool_depend>
  <build_depend>roscpp</build_depend>
  <build_depend>rospy</build_depend>
  <build_depend>std_msgs</build_depend>
  <build_export_depend>roscpp</build_export_depend>
  <build_export_depend>rospy</build_export_depend>
  <build_export_depend>std_msgs</build_export_depend>
  <exec_depend>roscpp</exec_depend>
  <exec_depend>rospy</exec_depend>
  <exec_depend>std_msgs</exec_depend>
  <export>
  </export>
</package>
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice `format="2"` — this is the **REP-0140 format 2** specification. It is what `catkin_create_pkg` generates by default. Older `format="1"` is deprecated. KTU exam answers should always mention the format version explicitly.

### Step 4 — Build the Package

```bash
cd ~/catkin_ws
catkin_make
```

Build flow summary:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{CMake configure phase: Reads CMakeLists.txt of beginner\_tutorials} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{Calls find\_package(catkin REQUIRED COMPONENTS roscpp rospy std\_msgs)} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{Verifies dependencies exist on ROS\_PACKAGE\_PATH} \\
&\text{Topological order: catkin} \rightarrow \text{roscpp} \rightarrow \text{rospy} \rightarrow \text{std\_msgs} \rightarrow \text{beginner\_tutorials} \\
&\text{Compile phase: No source files yet, so no object files generated} \\
&\text{Link phase: No executables to link} \\
&\text{Overlay phase: devel/setup.bash regenerated to include beginner\_tutorials}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Verify Package Discovery

```bash
rospack find beginner_tutorials
# Expected output: /home/<user>/catkin_ws/src/beginner_tutorials

rospack depends1 beginner_tutorials
# Expected output:
#   roscpp
#   rospy
#   std_msgs

rospack depends beginner_tutorials
# Expected output (transitive closure, abbreviated):
#   roscpp
#   rospy
#   std_msgs
#   genmsg
#   gencpp
#   geneus
#   genlisp
#   gennodejs
#   genpy
#   message_generation
#   cpp_common
#   rostime
#   ...
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation**: `rospack depends1` returns 3 packages (the direct dependencies). `rospack depends` returns 25+ packages because it transitively expands each direct dependency into its full closure.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Build Tools Comparison

| Feature | `catkin_make` | `catkin build` (catkin_tools) | `colcon build` (ROS 2 / ament) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Workspace init required? | No | Yes (`catkin_init_workspace`) | No |
| Builds packages in parallel | No (sequential within workspace, parallel within package) | Yes (per-package parallel, configurable) | Yes (per-package parallel) |
| Builds packages independently | No (whole-workspace CMake project) | Yes (each package is its own CMake project) | Yes |
| Supports `install/` space natively | Partial (`catkin_make install`) | Yes | Yes (default) |
| Standard for ROS 1 | ✅ Yes (default) | Optional | Not used |
| Standard for ROS 2 | N/A | N/A | ✅ Yes (default) |
| Verbose by default | Yes | No (`-v` to enable) | No |
| Profile support (e.g., debug vs release) | No | Yes (`catkin build --profile my_debug`) | Yes (CMake presets) |

---

## 3.4 KTU High-Yield Summary Table — Workspace vs Package

| Aspect | Workspace | Package |
|---|---|---|
| **Identification** | Top-level directory (e.g., `catkin_ws`) | Subdirectory inside `src/` |
| **Mandatory file/folder** | `src/` directory | `package.xml` + `CMakeLists.txt` |
| **Quantity** | One per project (typical) | Many (1 to 100+) per workspace |
| **Edit directly?** | Never edit `build/` or `devel/` | Edit `src/`, `include/`, `msg/`, etc. |
| **Build command target** | Whole workspace (`catkin_make`) | Individual package via `catkin_make --pkg <name>` |
| **Sourced after build?** | Yes, via `devel/setup.bash` | Not directly — via the workspace overlay |
| **Number** | Rarely more than 2–3 on one machine | Can be hundreds across the ROS ecosystem |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Catkin Workspace Internal Architecture

This Mermaid diagram shows the on-disk structure of a fully populated Catkin workspace after a successful build.

```mermaid
graph TD
    root["catkin_ws (Workspace Root)"]
    root --> srcDir["src/ (Editable)"]
    root --> buildDir["build/ (Auto-generated)"]
    root --> develDir["devel/ (Auto-generated)"]
    root --> installDir["install/ (Optional)"]

    srcDir --> pkgA["package_A/"]
    srcDir --> pkgB["package_B/"]
    srcDir --> pkgC["package_C/"]

    pkgA --> pkgAXml["package.xml (Manifest)"]
    pkgA --> pkgACMake["CMakeLists.txt (Build Recipe)"]
    pkgA --> pkgASrc["src/ (C++ Sources)"]
    pkgA --> pkgAInclude["include/"]
    pkgA --> pkgALaunch["launch/"]
    pkgA --> pkgAMsg["msg/"]

    pkgB --> pkgBXml["package.xml"]
    pkgB --> pkgBCMake["CMakeLists.txt"]
    pkgB --> pkgBScripts["scripts/ (Python)"]
    pkgB --> pkgBSrv["srv/"]

    pkgC --> pkgCXml["package.xml"]
    pkgC --> pkgCCMake["CMakeLists.txt"]
    pkgC --> pkgCConfig["config/"]

    buildDir --> buildCMake["CMakeCache.txt"]
    buildDir --> buildMake["Makefile"]
    buildDir --> buildCatkin["catkin_tools cache"]

    develDir --> develSetup["setup.bash (Overlay Script)"]
    develDir --> develLib["lib/ (Libraries + Executables)"]
    develDir --> develInclude["include/ (Generated Headers)"]
    develDir --> develEnv["env.sh"]

    installDir --> instLocal["local_setup.bash"]
    installDir --> instLib["lib/"]
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Catkin Build Process Flow (Function Block Architecture)

This diagram maps the **functional pipeline** that Catkin executes when you type `catkin_make`.

```mermaid
graph TD
    start([User runs: catkin_make]) --> scan[Phase 1: Workspace Scanner]
    scan --> discover[Discover all package.xml in src/]
    discover --> parseXml[Parse XML Manifests]
    parseXml --> buildDAG[Build Dependency DAG]
    buildDAG --> topo[Phase 2: Topological Sort]
    topo --> order[Ordered Package List]
    order --> cmake[Phase 3: CMake Configure per Package]
    cmake --> findpkg[find_package calls resolved]
    findpkg --> genmsg[Phase 4: Message and Service Generation]
    genmsg --> compile[Phase 5: Parallel Compile make -jN]
    compile --> link[Phase 6: Link Libraries and Executables]
    link --> test[Phase 7: Run Tests if requested]
    test --> overlay[Phase 8: Generate devel/setup.bash]
    overlay --> done([Build Complete])
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Workspace Overlay Mechanism (Environment Variable Injection)

This illustrates how `source devel/setup.bash` augments the ROS environment so the user-built packages become discoverable by `rospack`, `rosrun`, and `roslaunch`.

```mermaid
graph LR
    sysInstall["/opt/ros/noetic/ (System ROS)"]
    workspace["catkin_ws/devel/ (User Workspace)"]
    sourceScript["source devel/setup.bash"]
    envVars["Shell Environment Variables"]
    rospackFind["rospack find beginner_tutorials"]
    rosrunCmd["rosrun beginner_tutorials node"]
    resultOK([Returns workspace path])
    resultOK2([Executes workspace binary])

    sysInstall -->|defines default| envVars
    sourceScript -->|appends paths| envVars
    workspace -->|provides artifacts| envVars
    envVars --> rospackFind
    envVars --> rosrunCmd
    rospackFind --> resultOK
    rosrunCmd --> resultOK2
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Package Dependency Graph (DAG Example)

A realistic example showing how three user packages relate to each other and to the ROS core.

```mermaid
graph TD
    sysCore["ros_comm (rospy, roscpp, std_msgs)"]
    sysMsg["message_generation"]
    sysRuntime["message_runtime"]

    pkgA["robot_sensors (Your Package)"]
    pkgB["robot_navigation (Your Package)"]
    pkgC["robot_teleop (Your Package)"]

    sysMsg --> pkgA
    sysCore --> pkgA
    pkgA --> pkgB
    sysCore --> pkgB
    sysCore --> pkgC
    pkgB --> pkgC
    sysRuntime --> pkgC

    topo["Topological Build Order:"]
    sysCore -.-> topo
    sysMsg -.-> topo
    sysRuntime -.-> topo
    pkgA -.-> topo
    pkgB -.-> topo
    pkgC -.-> topo
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the graph**: An arrow from `X` to `Y` means `Y` depends on `X`. Catkin builds in the reverse topological order — sources first, consumers last. In this example, the build order would be: `ros_comm` → `message_generation` → `message_runtime` → `robot_sensors` → `robot_navigation` → `robot_teleop`.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

> **Q1.** Define a **Catkin Workspace**. List its standard directory structure and briefly explain the role of the `src/` and `devel/` directories. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A **Catkin Workspace** is a directory on the file system that serves as the top-level container for compiling, developing, and managing one or more ROS packages using the Catkin build system. Its standard structure consists of four directories:

| Directory | Role |
|---|---|
| `src/` | Source space — contains all package source code and is the **only manually created** directory |
| `build/` | Build space — stores intermediate CMake/Make cache files (auto-generated) |
| `devel/` | Development space — holds built executables, libraries, generated message headers, and the crucial `setup.bash` overlay script |
| `install/` | Optional install space — a relocatable, deployable version of the workspace |

The `src/` directory is where developers place or create packages using `catkin_create_pkg`. The `devel/` directory is auto-generated by `catkin_make` and contains the `setup.bash` file that, when sourced, overlays the workspace onto the system ROS environment by appending to `PATH`, `PYTHONPATH`, `LD_LIBRARY_PATH`, and `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH`.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

> **Q2.** What are the **two mandatory files** that every ROS Catkin package must contain? Briefly state the purpose of each. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Every Catkin package must contain the following two files:

1. **`package.xml`** — the *Manifest*. Written in XML (format="2" per REP-0140), it describes the package's metadata (name, version, maintainer, license, description) and declares its **dependencies** using tags such as `<build_depend>`, `<build_export_depend>`, `<exec_depend>`, `<doc_depend>`, and `<test_depend>`.

2. **`CMakeLists.txt`** — the *Build Recipe*. A CMake script that begins with four mandatory directives (`cmake_minimum_required`, `project()`, `find_package(catkin REQUIRED COMPONENTS ...)`, `catkin_package()`) and contains instructions for compiling C++ sources, linking libraries, building message generators, and declaring install rules.

A directory without these two files is not recognized by Catkin as a valid package, even if it contains source code.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1

> **Q.A)** *(a)* Explain with a neat block diagram the **Catkin build process** from the point a developer invokes `catkin_make` until the workspace is ready to use. Discuss the importance of topological sorting in the process. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* Describe the **anatomy of a ROS Catkin package**. List the conventional subdirectories and explain the role of `package.xml` and `CMakeLists.txt` with respect to the five different dependency tags. *(7 marks)*

### Question B — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)

> **Q.B)** *(a)* With a step-by-step procedure, explain how to **create a Catkin workspace** named `catkin_ws` and a ROS package named `my_robot_pkg` with dependencies `std_msgs`, `roscpp`, and `rospy`. Include all necessary commands and the verification steps. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* Differentiate between `catkin_make` and `catkin build`. When would you prefer one over the other? Also explain the purpose of `rospack find`, `rospack depends1`, and `rosdep install` commands. *(7 marks)*

---

### Detailed Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Catkin Build Process (7 marks)**

The Catkin build process executes in **eight sequential phases**:

1. **Workspace Scan**: Catkin recursively traverses `catkin_ws/src/` and identifies every directory containing a `package.xml` file as a valid package.

2. **Manifest Parsing**: Each `package.xml` is parsed to extract the `<build_depend>` declarations, building an in-memory adjacency list of dependencies.

3. **Dependency DAG Construction**: The dependencies form a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** where each node is a package and each directed edge represents a build-time dependency.

4. **Topological Sort**: Catkin computes a topological ordering of the DAG. This ensures that when package `P` is built, all packages it depends on have **already been built** and their headers/libraries are available. This is why `std_msgs` is built before any package that includes `std_msgs/Header.h`.

5. **Per-Package CMake Configuration**: For each package in topological order, CMake is invoked in `build/<pkg>/`. The `find_package(catkin REQUIRED COMPONENTS ...)` call resolves dependencies.

6. **Message and Service Generation**: If a package contains `msg/`, `srv/`, or `action/` files, the genmsg/gensrv/genaction tools generate language-specific bindings (C++, Python) into `devel/include/` and `devel/lib/python*/`.

7. **Parallel Compilation and Linking**: `make -j$(nproc)` (or Ninja) compiles source files into object files and links them into executables and shared libraries, which are deposited in `devel/lib/`.

8. **Overlay Generation**: A `devel/setup.bash` script is generated (or updated) which, when sourced, augments `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH`, `PATH`, `PYTHONPATH`, `LD_LIBRARY_PATH`, and `CMAKE_PREFIX_PATH` to expose the newly built artifacts.

> **Importance of topological sorting**: Without it, a package `P` might be compiled before its dependency `Q` is built, causing `find_package` errors or "header not found" failures. Topological sorting guarantees the dependency invariant is satisfied at every build step.

**Part (b) — Package Anatomy (7 marks)**

The anatomy of a Catkin package includes the two mandatory files and the conventional subdirectories.

**Mandatory files (2):**
- `package.xml` — XML manifest declaring metadata and dependencies.
- `CMakeLists.txt` — CMake build recipe with four mandatory top-level directives.

**Conventional subdirectories (any of the following):**
| Subdirectory | Typical Content |
|---|---|
| `src/` | C++ `.cpp` and `.hpp` files |
| `include/<pkg>/` | Public C++ headers |
| `scripts/` | Executable Python scripts |
| `msg/` | Custom message definitions (`.msg`) |
| `srv/` | Custom service definitions (`.srv`) |
| `action/` | Custom action definitions (`.action`) |
| `launch/` | `.launch` XML files for starting nodes |
| `config/` | YAML config and parameter files |
| `test/` | Unit and integration tests |
| `urdf/`, `meshes/`, `rviz/` | Robot description and visualization |

**The five dependency tags in `package.xml` (format="2"):**

| Tag | When required | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `<build_depend>` | Compile-time headers and CMake configs | `<build_depend>std_msgs</build_depend>` |
| `<build_export_depend>` | Headers exposed to downstream consumers | `<build_export_depend>roscpp</build_export_depend>` |
| `<exec_depend>` | Runtime libraries and executables | `<exec_depend>roscpp</exec_depend>` |
| `<doc_depend>` | Documentation building only | `<doc_depend>rosdoc_lite</doc_depend>` |
| `<test_depend>` | Test execution only | `<test_depend>rostest</test_depend>` |

---

### Detailed Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Workspace and Package Creation Procedure (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Open a terminal and create the workspace root with its mandatory `src/` directory:

```bash
mkdir -p ~/catkin_ws/src
cd ~/catkin_ws/src
```

**Step 2:** Initialize the workspace (creates the `.catkin_tools` marker):

```bash
catkin_init_workspace
```

**Step 3:** Build the empty workspace to generate `devel/` and `build/`:

```bash
cd ~/catkin_ws
catkin_make
```

**Step 4:** Create the package `my_robot_pkg` with the three specified dependencies:

```bash
cd ~/catkin_ws/src
catkin_create_pkg my_robot_pkg std_msgs roscpp ropy
```

**Step 5:** Verify the package structure exists:

```bash
ls ~/catkin_ws/src/my_robot_pkg
# Expected output: CMakeLists.txt  config  include  launch  package.xml  scripts  src
```

**Step 6:** Build the workspace again to compile the new package:

```bash
cd ~/catkin_ws
catkin_make
```

**Step 7:** Source the workspace and verify discoverability:

```bash
source ~/catkin_ws/devel/setup.bash
rospack find my_robot_pkg
# Expected: /home/<user>/catkin_ws/src/my_robot_pkg

rospack depends1 my_robot_pkg
# Expected: roscpp, rospy, std_msgs
```

**Part (b) — `catkin_make` vs `catkin build` and rospack commands (7 marks)**

| Aspect | `catkin_make` | `catkin build` (catkin_tools) |
|---|---|---|
| Build model | **Single CMake project** for the whole workspace | **Independent CMake project per package** |
| Parallelism | Parallel within one package, sequential across packages | Parallel across packages simultaneously |
| Configuration | One `devel/` space | Multiple profile-based build spaces |
| Required setup | None | `catkin_init_workspace` mandatory |
| Use when | Small/simple workspaces, tutorials, CI scripts | Large multi-package projects, mixed C++/Python, advanced workflows |

**Prefer `catkin_make`** when: you are following beginner tutorials, your workspace has only a handful of packages, or you need a minimal toolchain for CI/CD.

**Prefer `catkin build`** when: you have a large workspace (10+ packages), need per-package isolated builds, want profile-based switching between Debug/Release, or are working on ROS 2 projects via `colcon`.

**`rospack` commands:**
- `rospack find <pkg>` — Returns the absolute filesystem path of a package (e.g., `/opt/ros/noetic/share/std_msgs`). Used to confirm ROS can locate a package.
- `rospack depends1 <pkg>` — Lists the **direct** (one-hop) dependencies of a package, as declared in its `package.xml`.
- `rospack depends <pkg>` — Lists the **full transitive closure** of dependencies, recursively expanding every dependency.

**`rosdep install` command:**
- `rosdep install --from-paths src --ignore-src -r -y` — Installs all **system-level (apt)** dependencies declared in the `package.xml` files under `src/`. The `--ignore-src` flag skips packages found in the workspace itself (so it doesn't try to apt-install your local code), and `-y` auto-confirms the apt prompts.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing workspace with package** — Writing "a workspace is a package" loses 1–2 marks. Always state clearly that a *workspace* contains *packages*, hierarchically.
>
> 2. **Forgetting `format="2"`** in `package.xml` answers — Modern Catkin uses REP-0140 format 2 with five separate dependency tags. Writing the old single `<depend>` tag loses marks.
>
> 3. **Skipping the `source devel/setup.bash` step** in procedures — Without sourcing, the workspace packages are invisible to `rosrun` and `roslaunch`. Examiners explicitly check for this line.
>
> 4. **Calling `catkin_make` a "compiler"** — It is a **build system orchestrator**, not a compiler. The actual compiler is `gcc`/`g++`/`clang`, invoked by CMake-generated Makefiles.
>
> 5. **Ignoring `rospack depends` vs `rospack depends1` distinction** — `depends1` is direct (first-hop); `depends` is transitive (full closure). Examiners test this with diagram-based questions.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning parallel build (`-jN`)** — A high-quality answer must mention that Catkin exploits multi-core CPUs via parallel make jobs, which is a key engineering advantage over `rosbuild`.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam**

- ☐ **Catkin** = the ROS build system (collection of CMake macros + Python tools)
- ☐ **Workspace** = top-level directory containing `src/`, `build/`, `devel/`, optional `install/`
- ☐ **Package** = atomic ROS unit with **2 mandatory files**: `package.xml` + `CMakeLists.txt`
- ☐ `mkdir -p ~/catkin_ws/src` → creates the **only** manually-made directory
- ☐ `catkin_init_workspace` → marks the folder (required for `catkin build`, optional for `catkin_make`)
- ☐ `catkin_make` → builds the entire workspace into `build/` and `devel/`
- ☐ `source devel/setup.bash` → **mandatory final step** to overlay workspace onto ROS environment
- ☐ `catkin_create_pkg <name> [dep1 dep2 ...]` → scaffolds a new package with dependencies
- ☐ `package.xml` format="2" uses **5 dependency tags**: `build_depend`, `build_export_depend`, `exec_depend`, `doc_depend`, `test_depend`
- ☐ `CMakeLists.txt` must begin with 4 lines: `cmake_minimum_required`, `project()`, `find_package(catkin ...)`, `catkin_package()`
- ☐ Optional conventional subdirs: `src/`, `include/`, `scripts/`, `msg/`, `srv/`, `action/`, `launch/`, `config/`, `test/`
- ☐ `rospack find <pkg>` → absolute path; `rospack depends1` → direct deps; `rospack depends` → transitive closure
- ☐ `rosdep install --from-paths src --ignore-src -r -y` → installs apt-level system dependencies
- ☐ Catkin replaces legacy `rosbuild` (deprecated since ROS Groovy, 2012)
- ☐ Modern build tools: `catkin_make` (simple), `catkin build` (parallel profiles), `colcon build` (ROS 2)
- ☐ Topological sort guarantees dependencies are built **before** consumers (DAG ordering)
- ☐ `devel/` mimics `/opt/ros/<distro>/` layout; sourcing it fools ROS into thinking your packages are part of the core install

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: rosrun
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT525 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** rosrun

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Robot Operating System (ROS)** architecture, `rosrun` is a **command-line tool** that belongs to the `rosbash` family of ROS command-line utilities. Its primary function is to **locate and execute a specific node executable (binary or script) from a named ROS package** without requiring the user to manually navigate to the package's installation directory or specify the absolute path of the executable.

Formally stated, given a valid ROS workspace that has been sourced into the current shell environment, the tool resolves the syntax:

$$
\text{rosrun} \; [\text{package\_name}] \; [\text{executable\_name}] \; [\text{args}]
$$

The tool internally queries the **ROS package path** (managed by the `rospack` backend), resolves the executable, and then forks a new process to execute it as a **ROS node** that can communicate over the ROS Computation Graph.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – ROS Basics):**
> Under the 2024 scheme, Module 1 introduces the foundational command-line tools of ROS. `rosrun` is explicitly listed as a core file-system and execution utility alongside `roscd`, `rosls`, `rospack`, and `roscore`. Students are expected to know its **syntax, options, and practical use with starter packages such as `turtlesim`**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **large office building** with hundreds of rooms. Each room contains a different tool (a hammer, a screwdriver, a wrench). Now, instead of walking down the corridors to find a specific tool, you have a **smart receptionist at the front desk**. You simply tell the receptionist:

> *"Please give me the wrench from the 3rd floor toolbox."*

The receptionist knows exactly where every tool is stored and hands it to you immediately. Here:

- **The building** → Your ROS workspace (a collection of `catkin` packages).
- **The receptionist** → The `rosrun` command.
- **The tool** → The executable (node) inside a package.
- **You** → The developer running a robot program.

`rosrun` is the receptionist. You never need to know *where* the executable physically lives on disk — you just ask for it by **package name + node name**, and `rosrun` does the lookup and launch for you.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in real robotics:** When you have 20+ nodes spread across 8 different packages (sensors, control, planning, perception, UI), manually `cd`-ing into each one to launch them becomes impossible. `rosrun` is the lightweight, single-node launcher you use during **development and debugging** before promoting a setup to a `roslaunch` file.

## 1.3 Physical / System Constants & Defaults

- **Default ROS Master URI:** `http://localhost:11311`
- **Default executable resolution timeout:** Standard shell process inheritance (no built-in timeout; relies on the underlying OS).
- **Logging directory (default):** `~/.ros/log/`
- **Standard naming override flag length:** `__name:=<new_name>` (note the **double underscore** — a strict ROS convention).

> [!TIP]
> The double-underscore prefix (`__`) for command-line argument overrides is **not a typo**. ROS treats any argument beginning with `__` as a **special remapping/injection** handled by the client library, NOT passed to your program's `main()`.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and "How"

The execution of `rosrun` follows a **five-stage internal pipeline**:

1. **Environment Validation** — The tool verifies that the shell has been initialized with the ROS environment via `source /opt/ros/<distro>/setup.bash` (and, optionally, `source ~/catkin_ws/devel/setup.bash`). If the `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` is missing, the command fails immediately with a `command not found` or path-resolution error.

2. **Package Resolution** — `rosrun` calls `rospack find <package_name>` under the hood. `rospack` traverses every directory listed in `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` and `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` derived from the workspace, looking for a package manifest (`package.xml`) matching the name.

3. **Executable Lookup** — Once the package directory is located, `rosrun` inspects the `CMakeLists.txt` (for C++ nodes) or the `package.xml` `<exec_depend>` and `setup.py`/`scripts/` folder (for Python nodes) to find an executable matching the supplied name.

4. **Argument Forwarding & Remap Parsing** — All tokens after the executable name are scanned. Tokens beginning with `__` are intercepted as **private namespace remaps** or **parameter injections**. The remaining tokens are passed unmodified as `argv` to the executable.

5. **Process Fork & Hand-off** — A new process is spawned that inherits the **ROS Master URI**, **namespace**, and **parameter server** handles, allowing the newly launched node to **register with the ROS Master** and start publishing/subscribing to topics.

## 2.2 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Aspect | Syntax / Value | Engineering Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Basic Syntax** | `rosrun <pkg> <node>` | Launches a single executable |
| **Rename a node** | `rosrun <pkg> <node> __name:=myBot` | Reassigns the node's identity on the graph |
| **Remap a topic** | `rosrun <pkg> <node> <old>:=<new>` | Standard ROS topic remap (e.g., `cmd_vel:=/robot1/cmd_vel`) |
| **Set a private param** | `rosrun <pkg> <node> _param:=value` | Single underscore → private parameter in `~param` namespace |
| **Set a global param** | `rosrun <pkg> <node> __param:=value` | Double underscore → global `/param` namespace |
| **Log to a file** | `rosrun <pkg> <node> __log:=~/myLogs/run.log` | Redirects `rosout` to a custom file |
| **Find a package** | `rospack find <pkg>` | Manual equivalent of step 2 above |
| **List package contents** | `rosls <pkg>` | Quick directory inspection |
| **Run with logging enabled** | `rosrun <pkg> <node> --screen` (Python) or build with `rospy` defaults | Forces stdout to the terminal |
| **Typical chain** | `roscore` → `rosrun turtlesim turtlesim_node` | Master MUST be running first |
| **Standard env var** | `ROS_MASTER_URI`, `ROS_HOSTNAME`, `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` | Inherited by every spawned node |
| **Default log dir** | `~/.ros/log/` | Created automatically per session |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners frequently test the difference between a **single underscore** (`_param`) — which sets a *private* parameter in the node's own namespace — and a **double underscore** (`__param`) — which sets a *global* parameter at the root `/` namespace. Memorize this distinction; it is a classic 2-mark question.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industrial robotics and autonomous-vehicle stacks, `rosrun` is the **first-line debugging tool**. Engineers typically use it:

- During **unit testing of individual nodes** (e.g., running just the camera driver to verify it publishes `/image_raw`).
- To **isolate faults** in a multi-node system by launching only the suspect node and watching its terminal output.
- As a **pedagogical entry point** in university labs (especially KTU's PERUT525 practicals, where students run `turtlesim_node`, `turtle_teleop_key`, and custom `talker`/`listener` nodes).
- To **inject test parameters** on the fly (e.g., overriding a robot's max velocity before a real-world trial).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Workflow: From Zero to a Running `rosrun` Node

Below is the **complete, step-by-step derivation** of how a student would set up and execute a node using `rosrun`. Every command and its rationale is shown explicitly — no shortcuts.

**Step 1 — Initialize a Catkin Workspace**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{\$} \;\; \text{mkdir -p \textasciitilde{}/catkin\_ws/src} \\
\text{\$} \;\; \text{cd \textasciitilde{}/catkin\_ws/src} \\
\text{\$} \;\; \text{catkin\_init\_workspace}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** The `catkin` build system requires a strict `src/` subfolder to look for packages. `catkin_init_workspace` creates a `CMakeLists.txt` symlink so the top-level directory is recognized as a valid workspace.

**Step 2 — Create a Package**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{\$} \;\; \text{cd \textasciitilde{}/catkin\_ws/src} \\
\text{\$} \;\; \text{catkin\_create\_pkg beginner\_tutorials std\_rospy roscpp}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** `catkin_create\_pkg` generates the package skeleton including `package.xml`, `CMakeLists.txt`, and the `src/` and `include/` folders. The dependencies (`std_msgs`, `rospy`, `roscpp`) are declared up front.

**Step 3 — Write a Source File (Python example for clarity)**

File: `~/catkin_ws/src/beginner_tutorials/scripts/talker.py`

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
import rospy
from std_msgs.msg import String

def talker():
    pub = rospy.Publisher('chatter', String, queue_size=10)
    rospy.init_node('talker', anonymous=True)
    rate = rospy.Rate(10)  # 10 Hz
    while not rospy.is_shutdown():
        msg = "Hello ROS @ %s" % rospy.get_time()
        rospy.loginfo(msg)
        pub.publish(msg)
        rate.sleep()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    try:
        talker()
    except rospy.ROSInterruptException:
        pass
```

**Step 4 — Make the Script Executable**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{\$} \;\; \text{chmod +x \textasciitilde{}/catkin\_ws/src/beginner\_tutorials/scripts/talker.py}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** ROS Python nodes are launched as executable scripts. Without execute permission, `rosrun` will fail with a permission error.

**Step 5 — Modify `CMakeLists.txt` to Install Scripts**

Add the following lines near the bottom of `CMakeLists.txt`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{catkin\_install\_python(PROGRAMS scripts/talker.py} \\
\text{    DESTINATION \${CATKIN\_PACKAGE\_BIN\_DESTINATION})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** This declaration tells `catkin` where to copy the script during the build phase so `rosrun` can later discover it.

**Step 6 — Build the Workspace**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{\$} \;\; \text{cd \textasciitilde{}/catkin\_ws} \\
\text{\$} \;\; \text{catkin\_make}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** `catkin_make` compiles C++ nodes, copies Python scripts to the `devel/` space, and generates the environment setup script.

**Step 7 — Source the Workspace**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{\$} \;\; \text{source \textasciitilde{}/catkin\_ws/devel/setup.bash}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** This appends your workspace's `devel/lib/pythonX.Y/dist-packages` and `devel/lib/` paths to `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH`, making your new package discoverable.

**Step 8 — Launch the ROS Master**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{\$} \;\; \text{roscore}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** No node can register on the graph without a running `roscore` (the ROS Master).

**Step 9 — Execute the Node with `rosrun`**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{\$} \;\; \text{rosrun beginner\_tutorials talker.py}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** `rosrun` resolves `beginner_tutorials` via `rospack`, finds `talker.py` in the installed scripts directory, and executes it. The node now publishes to `/chatter`.

**Step 10 — Verify with a Second `rosrun` Instance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{\$} \;\; \text{rosrun beginner\_tutorials listener.py}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Assuming `listener.py` is also created.) The two nodes handshake via the Master, and the listener prints the messages.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — `rosrun` vs Other ROS Launchers

| Dimension | `rosrun` | `roslaunch` | `ros2 run` | Direct execution (`./binary`) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Single node | Multiple nodes, with config | Single node (ROS 2) | Single node |
| **Parameter server setup** | Manual via `_param:=` | YAML file, automatic | YAML or CLI | None |
| **Topic remapping** | Inline `:=` syntax | `<remap>` tags | Inline `:=` | None |
| **Respawn on crash** | No | Yes (`respawn="true"`) | No | No |
| **Environment handling** | Inherits current shell | Sets env per `<env>` tag | Inherits | Inherits |
| **Best for** | Debugging / development | Production / multi-node | ROS 2 debugging | Unit tests / gdb |
| **File output** | None | Generates `~/.ros/log/` | N/A | None |
| **KTU exam weight** | **High** (Module 1 focus) | Medium (Module 2) | Not in 2024 scheme | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The choice between `rosrun` and `roslaunch` is not arbitrary. Use `rosrun` for **surgical, single-node debugging**; use `roslaunch` for **deterministic, reproducible, multi-node mission launches** (e.g., bringing up an entire autonomous car stack).

## 3.3 Derivation: How a Remap is Resolved Internally

When you execute:

$$
\text{rosrun my\_pkg my\_node cmd\_vel:=/robot1/cmd\_vel
$$

The resolution proceeds as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{argv} &= [\,\text{``my\_node''},\;\text{``cmd\_vel''},\;\text{``:=''},\;\text{``/robot1/cmd\_vel''}\,] \\
\text{ROS client library scan} &\rightarrow \text{detects token containing ``:=''} \\
\text{Parse LHS} &= \text{``cmd\_vel''} \rightarrow \text{relative name} \\
\text{Parse RHS} &= \text{``/robot1/cmd\_vel''} \rightarrow \text{absolute name} \\
\text{Result} &= \text{Any topic the node declares as ``cmd\_vel''} \\
&\quad \text{is remapped to ``/robot1/cmd\_vel''} \\
&\quad \text{on the ROS graph at registration time.}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is why a remap **must occur before the node calls `rospy.init_node()` or `ros::init()`** — the substitution happens during graph registration, not at runtime.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Execution Flow of `rosrun`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User types rosrun pkg node] --> B{ROS Env Sourced?}
    B -- No --> X1[ERROR: command not found / package not found]
    B -- Yes --> C[rospack find pkg]
    C --> D{Package Exists?}
    D -- No --> X2[ERROR: ResourceNotFound]
    D -- Yes --> E[Locate executable in pkg/lib or pkg/scripts]
    E --> F[Parse arguments for __ remaps and _ params]
    F --> G[roscore running?]
    G -- No --> X3[ERROR: Unable to register with master]
    G -- Yes --> H[Fork process and execute node binary]
    H --> I[Node registers with ROS Master]
    I --> J[Node begins publishing / subscribing on Graph]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture: How a `rosrun` Node Integrates with the ROS Graph

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USER[User Shell]
        CMD[rosrun my_pkg my_node __name:=bot1 _rate:=50]
    end

    subgraph BACKEND[rosrun Backend]
        RP[rospack find]
        EXEC[Executable Resolver]
        ARGP[Argument Parser]
    end

    subgraph MASTER[ROS Master roscore]
        REG[Node Registry]
        PS[Parameter Server]
    end

    subgraph NODE[My Node Process]
        INIT[init_node]
        PUB[Publishers]
        SUB[Subscribers]
    end

    CMD --> RP
    RP --> EXEC
    EXEC --> ARGP
    ARGP --> INIT
    INIT --> REG
    ARGP --> PS
    INIT --> PUB
    INIT --> SUB
    PUB -. topics .-> MASTER
    SUB -. topics .-> MASTER
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — When to Use `rosrun`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Need to launch ROS code?] --> Q2{How many nodes?}
    Q2 -- One node --> Q3{Debugging or quick test?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> R1[Use rosrun]
    Q3 -- No, production --> R2[Use roslaunch with single node tag]
    Q2 -- Multiple nodes --> R3[Use roslaunch with launch file]
    Q2 -- ROS 2 --> R4[Use ros2 run or ros2 launch]
    R1 --> S1[rosrun pkg node args]
    R3 --> S2[roslaunch pkg file.launch]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the `rosrun` command. Write its general syntax and explain the role of the `__name` argument with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

`rosrun` is a ROS command-line utility that locates and executes a single node executable from a specified package without requiring the user to navigate to the package directory.

**Syntax:**

$$
\text{rosrun [package\_name] [executable\_name] [arguments]}
$$

**Role of `__name`:** The double-underscore `__name` argument allows the user to **override the default node name** assigned during `init_node()`. This is essential when running multiple instances of the same node simultaneously, each needing a unique identity on the ROS graph.

**Example:**

$$
\text{rosrun turtlesim turtlesim\_node \_\_name:=turtleA}
$$

This launches the `turtlesim_node` from the `turtlesim` package but registers it on the graph as `turtleA` instead of the default `turtlesim`.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between `_param` and `__param` when used with `rosrun`. Provide one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Suffix | Scope | Namespace | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `_param` | Private | `~param` (inside the node's own namespace) | `rosrun my_pkg my_node _max_speed:=2.0` |
| `__param` | Global | `/param` (at the root of the parameter tree) | `rosrun my_pkg my_node __robot_id:=7` |

A **single underscore** sets a parameter that is namespaced under the node itself, making it accessible only to that node. A **double underscore** sets a parameter in the **global root namespace**, making it accessible to all nodes that query `/robot_id`.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the complete procedure to create a ROS package named `my_controller` with dependencies `roscpp`, `std_msgs`, and `geometry_msgs`, and to launch a custom C++ node `velocity_controller` from this package using `rosrun`. List every terminal command in the correct sequence. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss four practical scenarios where `rosrun` is preferred over `roslaunch` in robotics development. Justify each with a real-world context. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1:** Navigate to the source folder of the workspace.

$$
\text{\$} \;\; \text{cd \textasciitilde{}/catkin\_ws/src}
$$

**Step 2:** Create the package with the required dependencies.

$$
\text{\$} \;\; \text{catkin\_create\_pkg my\_controller roscpp std\_msgs geometry\_msgs}
$$

**Step 3:** Place the C++ source file (e.g., `velocity_controller.cpp`) inside the `src/` directory of the new package. Edit `CMakeLists.txt` to declare the executable:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{add\_executable(velocity\_controller src/velocity\_controller.cpp)} \\
\text{target\_link\_libraries(velocity\_controller \$\{catkin\_LIBRARIES\})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Build the workspace.

$$
\text{\$} \;\; \text{cd \textasciitilde{}/catkin\_ws && catkin\_make}
$$

**Step 5:** Source the new environment.

$$
\text{\$} \;\; \text{source devel/setup.bash}
$$

**Step 6:** Start the master.

$$
\text{\$} \;\; \text{roscore}
$$

**Step 7:** Launch the node.

$$
\text{\$} \;\; \text{rosrun my\_controller velocity\_controller}
$$

**Mark allocation:** 1 mark per critical step (initial dir, package create, build, source, roscore, rosrun, plus 1 mark for the CMakeLists configuration).

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

1. **Single-node unit testing (2 marks):** When verifying a newly written perception node in isolation, engineers launch *only* that node via `rosrun` and observe its terminal logs directly. A `roslaunch` file would add noise by starting unnecessary nodes.

2. **Parameter override experimentation (2 marks):** During controller tuning, an engineer may run `rosrun my_pkg controller _kp:=0.5 _ki:=0.01` repeatedly with different gains. This rapid iteration is more cumbersome with a launch file that requires editing YAML.

3. **Crash-and-restart debugging (1.5 marks):** When a node is suspected of memory leaks, running it under `rosrun` allows attaching `gdb` or `valgrind` directly to the forked PID — something that requires extra `<node>` attributes in `roslaunch`.

4. **Multi-instance same-node testing (1.5 marks):** Spawning two instances of `turtlesim_node` simultaneously is trivial with `rosrun ... __name:=turtleA` and `rosrun ... __name:=turtleB`, enabling multi-robot simulation studies.

---

### **Question B — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of `rosrun` and its interaction with `rospack`, `roscore`, and the spawned node process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A student runs the following command:

$$
\text{rosrun beginner\_tutorials talker.py \_\_name:=publisherA cmd\_vel:=/robot1/cmd\_vel \_rate:=20
$$

Identify and explain every argument being passed, distinguishing between remaps, name overrides, and parameter settings. What is the final node name and the effective topic it would publish to? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

The internal architecture consists of four interacting subsystems:

1. **Shell Environment Layer** — The `setup.bash` script populates `ROS_PACKAGE_PATH` and `PATH`. (1.5 marks)
2. **Package Resolver (`rospack`)** — Invoked by `rosrun` to translate the package name into an absolute directory. (1.5 marks)
3. **Executable Resolver** — Searches for the named binary/script in standard install locations (`lib/`, `bin/`, `scripts/`). (2 marks)
4. **Process Spawner + Master Registration** — Forks the executable, which then calls `ros::init` or `rospy.init_node` to register with the running `roscore`. (2 marks)

**Suggested diagram (Mermaid):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[rosrun CLI] --> B[rospack find]
    B --> C[Find executable in pkg path]
    C --> D[Parse __ and _ args]
    D --> E[Fork and exec node]
    E --> F[roscore Master]
    F --> G[Graph registration]
```

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

| Argument Token | Type | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| `__name:=publisherA` | **Node name override (double underscore)** | The node is registered on the graph as `publisherA` instead of its default internal name. (2 marks) |
| `cmd_vel:=/robot1/cmd_vel` | **Topic remap** | Any topic the node declares as `cmd_vel` is remapped to the absolute path `/robot1/cmd_vel`. (2 marks) |
| `_rate:=20` | **Private parameter (single underscore)** | Sets a parameter named `rate` with value `20` in the node's private namespace (`~rate` = `/publisherA/rate`). (2 marks) |
| The leading `talker.py` | Executable identifier | Locates the script in `beginner_tutorials`. (1 mark) |

**Final Answers:**
- **Node name on the graph:** `/publisherA`
- **Effective publish topic:** `/robot1/cmd_vel`
- **Effective private parameter:** `/publisherA/rate` = 20

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing `_` vs `__`:** The single-underscore vs double-underscore distinction is the #1 reason students lose 1–2 marks in Part A. Always state the resulting namespace explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting to source the workspace:** Many students write the correct `rosrun` command but never `source devel/setup.bash`, leading to a `ResourceNotFound` error. Examiners will deduct marks if the source step is missing in a procedure-based question.
> 3. **Assuming `rosrun` handles multi-node launches:** It does NOT. Writing "use `rosrun` to start three nodes together" will cost you 2+ marks in any Part B comparison question.
> 4. **Wrong `chmod` omission:** Forgetting `chmod +x` on a Python script in a procedure question is a classic 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **`rosrun` is a single-node launcher**, not a multi-node orchestrator. For multi-node setups, use `roslaunch`.
- **Mandatory prerequisites before `rosrun`:** (1) `roscore` running, (2) workspace sourced via `setup.bash`, (3) package built via `catkin_make`.
- **Syntax:** `rosrun <package_name> <executable_name> [args]`
- **Underscore rules:**
  - `_param:=value` → **private** parameter (`~param`)
  - `__param:=value` → **global** parameter (`/param`)
  - `__name:=new_id` → **rename the node** on the graph
  - `<old_topic>:=<new_topic>` → **remap a topic** (no underscore required, just `:=`)
- **`rosrun` internally calls `rospack find`**, so package-name typos surface as `ResourceNotFound` errors.
- **Python nodes** need executable permission (`chmod +x`) AND must be declared in `catkin_install_python()` in `CMakeLists.txt`.
- **C++ nodes** must be declared with `add_executable()` and `target_link_libraries()` in `CMakeLists.txt`.
- **`rosrun` vs `roslaunch`:** `rosrun` = surgical, terminal-friendly, ideal for debugging. `roslaunch` = production-grade, supports respawn, YAML params, and multi-node orchestration.
- **Logging:** All `rosrun`-launched nodes write to `~/.ros/log/` automatically.
- **ROS 2 equivalent:** `ros2 run <pkg> <executable>` — *not* in the KTU 2024 PERUT525 syllabus, but worth knowing academically.
- **The double-underscore convention** is unique to ROS 1 client libraries (`roscpp`, `rospy`); never confuse it with Python's own `__name__` dunder, which is a different language feature.
- **Most common exam question type:** "Differentiate `_param` vs `__param`" — practice writing the resulting namespace explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
