## 4.1 End-to-End Publish Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** showing the entire publish pipeline, including the ROS Master handshake.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Publisher Node: talker_node] --> A1[Initialize: ros::init / rospy.init_node]
    A1 --> A2[Create NodeHandle]
    A2 --> A3[Call advertise<MsgType> on /chatter]
    A3 --> B[ROS Master: roscore]
    B --> B1[XMLRPC Registration on Port 11311]
    B1 --> B2[Topic Registry / Parameter Server]
    B2 --> C{Subscriber Exists?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Master Returns Subscriber URIs]
    C -- No --> E[Master Stores Topic, Waits for Subscriber]
    D --> F[Establish Direct TCPROS Connection]
    E --> G[No Transport Needed Yet]
    F --> H[Main Publish Loop]
    G --> H
    H --> H1[Construct std_msgs::String msg]
    H1 --> H2[Serialize msg to CDR Binary Blob]
    H2 --> H3[chatter_pub.publish msg]
    H3 --> I[TCPROS Socket Stream]
    I --> J[Subscriber Node: listener_node]
    J --> J1[Deserialize msg]
    J1 --> J2[Invoke User Callback]
    J2 --> J3[Process data, e.g., print to terminal]
    H3 --> K[ros::spinOnce / rospy.Rate.sleep]
    K --> H
```

## 4.2 Publisher-Subscriber State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Uninitialized
    Uninitialized --> Registered: advertise call to Master
    Registered --> Connected: Subscriber discovered via Master
    Registered --> WaitingForSubscriber: No subscriber yet
    WaitingForSubscriber --> Connected: New subscriber registers
    Connected --> Publishing: Loop calls publish
    Publishing --> Publishing: Continuous broadcast
    Publishing --> Shutdown: ros::shutdown / Ctrl-C
    Shutdown --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Many-to-Many Decoupling Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Publisher Node 1] -->|pub| T[/chatter Topic/]
    P2[Publisher Node 2] -->|pub| T
    T -->|sub| S1[Subscriber Node A]
    T -->|sub| S2[Subscriber Node B]
    T -->|sub| S3[Subscriber Node C]
    style T fill:#f9c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Exams:** Notice that publishers and subscribers are **completely decoupled**. A publisher does not need to know how many subscribers exist. This is the core design property that makes ROS horizontally scalable.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a ROS Topic. What is the role of a Publisher?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **ROS Topic** is a named, strongly-typed bus used for asynchronous, many-to-many message passing between nodes. A **Publisher** is a client-side endpoint, created by calling `advertise()` on a NodeHandle, that **serializes** a message of a fixed `.msg` type and pushes it onto the topic. The Master (`roscore`) is queried once for subscriber URIs, after which a direct **TCPROS** connection carries the data. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between TCPROS and UDPROS. When would you choose UDPROS for publishing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Property | TCPROS | UDPROS |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Reliability | Guaranteed, ordered | Best-effort, lossy |
| Latency | Higher (ack overhead) | Lower (no handshake) |
| Use Case | Commands, logs, low-rate | High-bandwidth sensor streams (LiDAR, camera) |

Choose **UDPROS** for high-frequency sensor data where losing a few packets is acceptable and low latency is critical. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When answering publisher questions, students frequently **forget to call `ros::spinOnce()`** in a `while(ros::ok())` loop, which prevents subscriber-side callbacks from firing even though data has been published. This costs **2 marks** in practicals. Additionally, do **not** confuse the *topic name* with the *message type* — they are different entities!

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the publish/subscribe communication model in ROS. Discuss the role of the ROS Master. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Block Diagram** *(3 marks)*:
   Draw the diagram from §4.1: Publisher → Master → Subscriber, with the XMLRPC registration and TCPROS data path labeled.
2. **Publish/Subscribe Model** *(2 marks)*:
   It is an **asynchronous, many-to-many** transport where publishers and subscribers are temporally and spatially decoupled. Publishers do not block waiting for subscribers.
3. **Role of ROS Master** *(2 marks)*:
   The Master (started by `roscore`) provides **name service registration** (URI ↔ topic/node name mapping) and the **parameter server**. It does **not** relay data — all data flows peer-to-peer via TCPROS/UDPROS.

**(b)** Write a C++ ROS node that publishes a `geometry_msgs/Twist` message on the topic `/turtle1/cmd_vel` at 1 Hz, commanding a turtle to move in a circle. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```cpp
#include "ros/ros.h"
#include "geometry_msgs/Twist.h"

int main(int argc, char **argv)
{
    ros::init(argc, argv, "circle_driver");
    ros::NodeHandle nh;
    ros::Publisher pub = nh.advertise<geometry_msgs::Twist>("/turtle1/cmd_vel", 10);
    ros::Rate rate(1);                          // 1 Hz
    while (ros::ok()) {
        geometry_msgs::Twist msg;
        msg.linear.x  = 2.0;                    // forward velocity
        msg.angular.z = 1.0;                    // turn rate -> circular path
        pub.publish(msg);
        ros::spinOnce();
        rate.sleep();
    }
    return 0;
}
```

**Step-by-step evaluation** *(7 marks)*:
- Correct `ros::init` and NodeHandle creation: 1 mark
- Correct `advertise<geometry_msgs::Twist>` syntax: 2 marks
- Proper message construction with `linear.x` and `angular.z`: 2 marks
- Inclusion of `ros::spinOnce()` and `Rate::sleep()`: 1 mark
- Correct inclusion of `geometry_msgs` dependency: 1 mark

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** What is message serialization in ROS? Why is it necessary? Name the wire protocols used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Definition** *(2 marks)*: Message serialization is the process of converting a structured message object (e.g., a C++ `std_msgs::String`) into a flat, contiguous **binary byte stream** suitable for socket transmission. Deserialization is the inverse on the subscriber side.
2. **Why necessary?** *(3 marks)*:
   - Enables **cross-language** interop (C++ ↔ Python ↔ Java).
   - Avoids human-readable formats (JSON, XML) which are bandwidth-wasteful.
   - Ensures deterministic, version-stable wire layout.
3. **Wire Protocols** *(2 marks)*: **TCPROS** (reliable, default) and **UDPROS** (lossy, low-latency).

**(b)** Explain the concept of a **latched topic**. Write a Python publisher that publishes a static map on `/static_map` exactly once, and a Python subscriber that logs the map on first connection. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Latched Topic Concept** *(3 marks)*: A latched publisher stores the *most recent* message in the Master and pushes it **immediately** to any *new* subscriber on connection. This eliminates the "first-message race condition" common in robot startup.
2. **Python Publisher** *(2 marks)*:
   ```python
   import rospy
   from nav_msgs.msg import OccupancyGrid
   rospy.init_node('map_server')
   pub = rospy.Publisher('/static_map', OccupancyGrid, queue_size=1, latch=True)
   rate = rospy.Rate(1)
   while not rospy.is_shutdown():
       pub.publish(my_map)   # published once is enough; latch retains it
       rate.sleep()
   ```
3. **Python Subscriber** *(2 marks)*:
   ```python
   def cb(msg):
       rospy.loginfo("Map received: %d x %d", msg.info.width, msg.info.height)
   rospy.Subscriber('/static_map', OccupancyGrid, cb, queue_size=1)
   rospy.spin()
   ```

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **Topic** is a named bus; a **Publisher** is the broadcaster endpoint.
- ✅ Communication is **asynchronous**, **many-to-many**, and **anonymous**.
- ✅ Two wire protocols: **TCPROS** (reliable, TCP) and **UDPROS** (lossy, UDP).
- ✅ The **ROS Master** is contacted *only for registration* — data flows **peer-to-peer**.
- ✅ Always use `ros::spinOnce()` inside custom `while(ros::ok())` loops.
- ✅ Use `latch=True` when late-joining subscribers must receive the latest state.
- ✅ Set `queue_size` to avoid memory blow-up on slow subscribers.
- ✅ `anonymous=True` in `rospy.init_node` prevents duplicate name conflicts in launch files.
- ✅ Command-line debugging: `rostopic list`, `rostopic echo`, `rostopic hz`, `rostopic info`.
- ✅ The C++ publisher API is `nh.advertise<MsgType>(topic, queue_size)`.
- ✅ The Python publisher API is `rospy.Publisher(topic, MsgType, queue_size=10)`.
- ✅ A typical KTU 14-mark question tests: API syntax + lifecycle + Master role.
- ✅ Common pitfall: **topic name ≠ message type** (e.g., topic `/cmd_vel`, type `Twist`).
- ✅ Maximum publishing rate: $f_{pub} \le \min(B/S,\; 1/\tau)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain publishing to a topic", always cover **four points**: (1) the `advertise()` API call, (2) Master registration, (3) serialization, (4) peer-to-peer TCPROS transport. Missing any one of these typically costs 2–3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Subscribing to a Topic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT525 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS)
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Subscribing to a Topic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 1 — Subscribing to a Topic

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

In the **Robot Operating System (ROS)** architecture, **Subscribing to a Topic** is the formal mechanism by which a ROS node declares its interest in receiving data streams published by one or more other nodes on a named communication channel called a **Topic**.

A node that wishes to receive messages **must register itself as a Subscriber** with the **ROS Master**, bind to a specific topic name, declare the expected **message type**, and provide a **callback function** that ROS will automatically invoke every time a new message arrives on that topic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *A Subscriber is a node that pulls messages of a pre-defined Message Type from a Topic by registering with the ROS Master. Each incoming message triggers a user-defined Callback Function, enabling asynchronous, decoupled, many-to-many inter-process communication.*

**Key Terminology Snapshot:**

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Node** | An executable process (a single ROS program) |
| **Topic** | A named bus over which messages flow |
| **Message** | A strictly typed data packet (e.g., `String`, `Int32`, `Twist`) |
| **Publisher** | Node that pushes messages onto a Topic |
| **Subscriber** | Node that pulls / receives those messages |
| **Master** | Central name-service that matches Pubs ↔ Subs |
| **Callback** | Function invoked automatically per incoming message |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The YouTube Channel Model 🎥

Imagine a **TV broadcasting channel** (Publisher) and a **viewer with a set-top box** (Subscriber).

- The **TV channel** keeps broadcasting on a fixed frequency, called `"/weather_news"`.
- You, the viewer, do **not** own the broadcast — you simply **tune in** (subscribe).
- The cable provider (the **ROS Master**) tells your set-top box *which frequency to tune to* and *what kind of content* to expect.
- Every time new content arrives on that frequency, your TV **automatically plays it** (the callback is triggered) — you don't have to call the TV station to ask, "Hey, anything new?"

### Why does this matter in robotics?
A robot typically has dozens of sensors (cameras, LIDAR, IMUs) all streaming data simultaneously. A navigation module doesn't *ask* the camera every millisecond, "Got a new frame?" — instead, it **subscribes once**, and ROS pushes every new frame into its callback automatically. This is **event-driven**, **asynchronous**, and **decoupled**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> The publisher and subscriber are **completely anonymous** to each other. They do not know each other's names, locations, or languages beyond the agreed-upon **topic name** and **message type**. This is what makes ROS a *loosely-coupled* distributed system.

---

## 1.3 When Do You Subscribe? (Real-World Triggers)

| Robot Scenario | Subscribed Topic | Typical Message Type |
|---|---|---|
| Autonomous navigation | `/scan` | `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` |
| Vision-based object tracking | `/camera/rgb/image_raw` | `sensor_msgs/Image` |
| Robot arm teleoperation | `/cmd_vel` | `geometry_msgs/Twist` |
| Battery monitoring dashboard | `/battery_state` | `sensor_msgs/BatteryState` |
| Human–Robot Interaction | `/voice_command` | `std_msgs/String` |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Publish / Subscribe Pattern

## 2.1 Architectural Flow (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Node Initialisation:** The subscriber node calls `rospy.init_node()`, registering itself with the ROS Master under a **unique node name**.
2. **Topic Registration:** The node calls `rospy.Subscriber("topic_name", MessageType, callback)`. Internally, ROS sends an **XMLRPC registration request** to the Master.
3. **Publisher Discovery:** The Master checks its registry for any Publishers on the requested topic. If found, it returns the **URI (IP + port)** of each publisher.
4. **TCP Connection Establishment:** The subscriber opens a **TCP socket** with each discovered publisher using the **TCPROS protocol** on port `11434`-range.
5. **Message Reception Loop:** ROS spins an internal listener thread. When bytes arrive on the socket, they are **deserialised** from ROS wire-format back into a Python object.
6. **Callback Dispatch:** The deserialised message is handed to the user-defined callback function. The callback runs in the **listener thread** (not the main thread).
7. **Loop Maintenance:** `rospy.spin()` keeps the node alive, preventing Python from exiting, so callbacks can fire indefinitely.

> [!TIP]
> **Why `rospy.spin()` is mandatory:** Without `spin()` (or `rate.sleep()` in a manual loop), the main thread will exit immediately after `init_node`, killing all subscriber listeners.

---

## 2.2 Mathematical / Behavioural Model

While ROS is event-driven, we can model the **inter-arrival time** of messages as a stochastic process. Let:

$$T_i = \text{Time of arrival of the } i^{th} \text{ message}$$

The **Mean Inter-Arrival Time (MIT)** is given by:

$$\text{MIT} = \mathbb{E}[T_{i+1} - T_i] = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

where $\lambda$ is the average message **publication rate** (Hz, i.e., **messages per second**).

The **callback processing budget** must obey the **real-time inequality**:

$$\tau_{cb} \leq \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

where $\tau_{cb}$ is the worst-case execution time of the callback. Violating this means messages arrive faster than they are processed → **queue growth → memory bloat → dropped frames**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 🔑 Core `rospy` Functions for Subscribing

| Function | Purpose | Mandatory? |
|---|---|---|
| `rospy.init_node(name, anonymous=False)` | Registers node with Master | ✅ Yes |
| `rospy.Subscriber(topic, type, cb)` | Creates a subscriber | ✅ Yes |
| `rospy.spin()` | Blocks main thread, keeps callbacks alive | ✅ Yes |
| `rospy.get_param(key)` | Reads a runtime parameter | Optional |
| `rospy.loginfo(msg)` | Logs to `rosout` topic | Optional |
| `rospy.is_shutdown()` | Checks for Ctrl-C / shutdown signal | Useful |
| `rospy.wait_for_message(topic, type, timeout)` | Blocks until one message arrives | Optional |
| `rospy.Time.now()` | Current ROS time | Optional |

### 📐 Formula / Parameter Table

| Parameter | Symbol / Value | Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Topic name | $\mathcal{T}$ | string | Unique bus identifier |
| Message type | $\mathcal{M}$ | class | e.g., `std_msgs/String` |
| Queue size | $Q$ | integer (≥ 1) | Back-pressure buffer size |
| Publication rate | $\lambda$ | Hz | Messages per second |
| Callback latency | $L_{cb}$ | seconds | Time from receive to handler entry |
| Wire protocol | TCPROS | — | Reliable, ordered byte stream |

> [!WARNING]
> **`queue_size` rules for KTU 2024:** In ROS Noetic, `queue_size=None` is **forbidden** (deprecated). Always set an explicit integer ≥ 1. Setting $Q = 1$ discards old messages when the listener is slow; setting $Q = 10$ retains recent history.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The subscribe pattern underpins nearly every autonomous robotic system:

- **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping):** Subscribes to `/scan` (LIDAR) and `/odom` (wheel encoders) at $\lambda \approx 40$ Hz.
- **Pick-and-Place Manipulation:** Subscribes to `/joint_states` at $\lambda \approx 100$ Hz for closed-loop control.
- **Fleet Management:** Multiple robots subscribe to `/coordination/goal` from a central planner.

> [!NOTE]
> Industry deployments (e.g., **Amazon Robotics Kiva systems**, **Autonomous Mobile Robots by MiR**) rely on this exact Pub-Sub pattern over ROS 2 with DDS middleware for fault-tolerant, real-time message delivery.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — Building a Subscriber

## 3.1 Canonical Subscriber Template (Python / rospy)

Below is the **complete, line-by-line dissected** code for a subscriber that listens to a `String` message on topic `/chatter` and prints its content.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import rospy
from std_msgs.msg import String

def callback(data):
    rospy.loginfo("I heard: %s", data.data)

def listener():
    rospy.init_node('listener_node', anonymous=True)
    rospy.Subscriber('chatter', String, callback)
    rospy.spin()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    listener()
```

### 3.2 Exhaustive Line-by-Line Analysis

| Line | Code | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `#!/usr/bin/env python3` | **Shebang** — tells the OS to execute with Python 3. Required for `rosrun`. |
| 2 | `import rospy` | Imports the **ROS Python client library**. Gives access to `init_node`, `Subscriber`, `spin`, etc. |
| 3 | `from std_msgs.msg import String` | Imports the `String` message class from `std_msgs` package. This binds the **message type contract**. |
| 4 | (blank) | Readability. |
| 5 | `def callback(data):` | Defines the **callback function**. ROS will call this *automatically* every time a new message arrives. Parameter `data` is the deserialised Python object. |
| 6 | `rospy.loginfo("I heard: %s", data.data)` | `loginfo` writes to `/rosout`. `%s` is replaced by `data.data` — note: the **outer** `data` is the message, the **inner** `.data` is the string field inside the message. |
| 7 | (blank) | Readability. |
| 8 | `def listener():` | Defines the **main node function** (a wrapper for cleanliness). |
| 9 | `rospy.init_node('listener_node', anonymous=True)` | Registers the node. `anonymous=True` appends a random suffix (e.g., `listener_node_3847`) so multiple instances don't collide on the Master. |
| 10 | `rospy.Subscriber('chatter', String, callback)` | **The core line.** Creates a subscriber on topic `chatter`, expecting `String` messages, dispatching to `callback`. Returns a `Subscriber` object (often discarded). |
| 11 | `rospy.spin()` | Blocks until `rospy.shutdown()` is called (e.g., Ctrl-C). During blocking, the **listener thread** continuously pumps incoming messages into `callback`. |
| 12 | (blank) | Readability. |
| 13 | `if __name__ == '__main__':` | Standard Python idiom — only run `listener()` if this script is executed directly (not imported). |
| 14 | `listener()` | Entry point. |

### 3.3 Mathematical Justification of the Subscriber Call

The `rospy.Subscriber` invocation internally executes the following logical sequence:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \mathcal{S} = \text{Subscriber}(\mathcal{T}, \mathcal{M}, f_{cb}) \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Register}(\mathcal{S}, \text{Master}, URI_{\mathcal{S}}) \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Master} \rightarrow \{ URI_{\mathcal{P}_1}, URI_{\mathcal{P}_2}, \dots \} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \forall \mathcal{P}_i: \text{OpenSocket}(URI_{\mathcal{P}_i}, \text{TCPROS}) \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{onRecv}(bytes) \rightarrow \text{deserialize} \rightarrow m \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & f_{cb}(m) \quad \text{(invoked in listener thread)}
\end{aligned}$$

where $\mathcal{S}$ is the subscriber object, $\mathcal{T}$ is the topic name, $\mathcal{M}$ is the message class, $f_{cb}$ is the callback function, and $\{ URI_{\mathcal{P}_i} \}$ is the set of publisher URIs returned by the Master.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the callback is called in a separate thread:**
> This prevents a slow callback from blocking the TCP socket read loop. However, it also means that if your callback is **not thread-safe** (e.g., uses shared mutable globals without locks), you can get race conditions — a favourite KTU viva question!

### 3.4 Enhanced Subscriber (with `queue_size` and shutdown hook)

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import rospy
from std_msgs.msg import String

def callback(msg):
    rospy.loginfo("Received at %s: %s", rospy.get_time(), msg.data)

def listener():
    rospy.init_node('enhanced_listener', anonymous=True)
    sub = rospy.Subscriber(
        'chatter',
        String,
        callback,
        queue_size=10,                 # Buffer up to 10 messages
        buff_size=65536,               # TCP recv buffer size (bytes)
        tcp_nodelay=True               # Disable Nagle's algorithm → lower latency
    )
    rospy.spin()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    listener()
```

#### Line-by-Line Additions:

| Argument | Value | Why |
|---|---|---|
| `queue_size` | `10` | Allows transient bursts without dropping. |
| `buff_size` | `65536` | Large enough for full Image messages (~250 KB). |
| `tcp_nodelay` | `True` | Sends small packets immediately — critical for control loops. |

---

## 3.5 Manual Loop Alternative (No `spin()`)

Sometimes you need to do **other work** in the main thread while still receiving messages. In that case, you replace `spin()` with a `while` loop that calls `rospy.sleep`:

```python
rate = rospy.Rate(10)  # 10 Hz main loop
while not rospy.is_shutdown():
    # Do periodic work here
    rate.sleep()
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake:** Writing `time.sleep(0.1)` instead of `rate.sleep()` will block ROS callbacks from being serviced. Always use `rospy.Rate.sleep()`.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 4. Structural Diagrams — Publisher / Subscriber Architecture

## 4.1 Master-Mediated Communication Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Publisher Node\ntalker_node"] -->|register_publish| nodeM[("ROS Master\nroscore")]
    nodeM -->|publisher URI| nodeB["Subscriber Node\nlistener_node"]
    nodeB -->|register_subscribe| nodeM
    nodeM -->|subscriber URI| nodeA
    nodeA -->|TCPROS direct\nconnection| nodeB
    nodeP["Topic /chatter\nmsg std_msgs/String"] -.-> nodeA
    nodeQ["callback function\nauto_invoked"] -.-> nodeB
```

**Read this as:** Both nodes talk to the Master only for **discovery** (XMLRPC). All actual message data flows **directly** between the Publisher and Subscriber over a **peer-to-peer TCP connection** — the Master never sees the payload.

---

## 4.2 Subscriber Internal Threading Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeMain["Main Thread\nrospy.spin block"] --> nodeSub["Subscriber Object\nqueue_size 10"]
    nodeSub --> nodeList["Listener Thread\nTCP socket recv"]
    nodeList --> nodeDeser["Deserializer\nbytes to Python obj"]
    nodeDeser --> nodeQ["Internal Queue\nFIFO buffer"]
    nodeQ --> nodeCb["Callback Dispatcher\ninvokes f_cb m"]
    nodeCb --> nodeWork["User Logic\nloginfo, control, etc"]
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Subscriber Node

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "listener_node process"
        direction TB
        blockInit["Block 1: Init\nrospy init_node anonymous True"]
        blockSub["Block 2: Subscribe\nrospy Subscriber topic type callback"]
        blockSpin["Block 3: Spin\nrospy spin blocks main thread"]
        blockCb["Block 4: Callback\ndef callback data\nrospy loginfo"]
        blockShut["Block 5: Shutdown\non Ctrl-C clean exit"]
    end

    blockInit --> blockSub --> blockSpin
    blockSpin -.->|async dispatch| blockCb
    blockCb -.->|returns| blockSpin
    blockSpin --> blockShut
```

---

## 4.4 Topic Naming Convention Tree (Namespace Visualisation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeRoot["Global Namespace /"] --> nodeA["robot1 /"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeB["robot2 /"]
    nodeA --> nodeA1["sensors /"]
    nodeA --> nodeA2["actuators /"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeA1a["lidar /scan"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeA1b["imu /data"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA2a["cmd_vel"]
    nodeB --> nodeB1["sensors /scan"]
```

**For KTU Exams:** When asked about topic remapping, the answer is that ROS uses **relative** names by default. A subscriber listening to `sensors/lidar/scan` launched inside `robot1` namespace will actually subscribe to `/robot1/sensors/lidar/scan`.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📘 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
*With a neat diagram, explain the Publisher-Subscriber communication model in ROS. Why is the ROS Master not involved in actual data transfer?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Publisher-Subscriber model in ROS is a **many-to-many, asynchronous, loosely-coupled** communication pattern.

- A **Publisher node** pushes messages onto a named **Topic**.
- A **Subscriber node** registers interest in that topic and provides a **callback function**.
- Both nodes first contact the **ROS Master** via XMLRPC for **name registration and discovery** (1 mark).
- After discovery, the Master returns the publisher's **URI** to the subscriber, which establishes a **direct peer-to-peer TCP connection** (TCPROS) (1 mark).
- The Master is **not** in the data path; this decouples nodes and allows the system to scale without bottlenecking at a central server. If the Master crashes, existing connections continue to function, but no new nodes can join (1 mark).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*What is the role of the `callback` function in a ROS subscriber? What happens if the callback takes longer than the message inter-arrival time?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **callback function** is a user-defined Python function that ROS invokes **automatically** every time a new message is deserialised from the topic. It receives the message as its argument and contains the application logic (logging, control, computation) (1 mark).

If the callback's worst-case execution time $\tau_{cb}$ exceeds the **mean inter-arrival time** $1/\lambda$, then:

$$\tau_{cb} > \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

The internal queue begins to **grow unboundedly** until `queue_size` is filled, after which **incoming messages are silently dropped** (1 mark). In real-time control systems, this leads to **stale state estimates**, **control instability**, and **potential hardware damage** in physical robots (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the `rospy.Subscriber()` function with its syntax, parameters, and return value. Discuss the significance of `queue_size`, `buff_size`, and `tcp_nodelay` parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design and explain a complete ROS subscriber node (Python) that listens to a `std_msgs/String` topic named `/robot_status` and logs each message along with the current ROS timestamp and a custom message counter. Use a callback function, and demonstrate proper shutdown handling. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📐 Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Syntax:**

```python
rospy.Subscriber(topic_name, msg_class, callback_function, queue_size=N)
```

**Parameter Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Type | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `topic_name` | string | Logical channel name (e.g., `/chatter`) |
| `msg_class` | Python class | Message type imported from a `*.msg` package |
| `callback` | function | Called on each incoming message |
| `queue_size` | int ≥ 1 | Back-pressure limit (1 mark) |
| `buff_size` | int (bytes) | TCP socket receive buffer (1 mark) |
| `tcp_nodelay` | bool | Disables Nagle's algorithm for low latency (1 mark) |

**Return Value:** A `rospy.Subscriber` object that holds a reference to the connection. Usually discarded but can be used with `sub.unregister()` to stop listening.

**Significance Discussion (1 mark):** `queue_size` prevents memory bloat under slow-callback conditions; `buff_size` ensures large messages like images fit; `tcp_nodelay` is critical for control loops where every millisecond matters.

---

#### 📐 Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Complete Code:**

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import rospy
from std_msgs.msg import String

counter = 0  # Global counter

def status_callback(msg):
    global counter
    counter += 1
    timestamp = rospy.get_time()
    rospy.loginfo("[#%d | t=%.3f] %s", counter, timestamp, msg.data)

def robot_status_listener():
    global counter
    counter = 0
    rospy.init_node('robot_status_subscriber', anonymous=True)
    sub = rospy.Subscriber(
        '/robot_status',
        String,
        status_callback,
        queue_size=10
    )
    rospy.loginfo("Subscriber started on /robot_status. Waiting for messages...")
    rospy.spin()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    try:
        robot_status_listener()
    except rospy.ROSInterruptException:
        rospy.loginfo("Subscriber terminated cleanly.")
```

**Explanation Breakdown (1 mark per key element):**

1. **Shebang & Imports:** Uses `#!/usr/bin/env python3` and imports `rospy` plus `String`.
2. **Global Counter:** `counter` tracks total messages received; declared `global` inside the callback.
3. **Callback Logic:** Increments counter, fetches timestamp, logs structured output.
4. **Init Node:** `anonymous=True` prevents name collisions.
5. **Subscriber Declaration:** Explicit `queue_size=10`.
6. **`rospy.spin()`:** Keeps main thread alive.
7. **Exception Handling:** Catches `ROSInterruptException` (Ctrl-C) for clean exit.

---

### **Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Topics** vs **Services** vs **Actions** in ROS. In which scenario is a Subscriber over a Topic the *correct* choice over a Service call? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Topic Remapping** with an example. Write the command-line syntax to remap a subscriber from `/input_topic` to `/sensors/lidar/scan` when running with `rosrun`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📐 Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Feature | Topic | Service | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pattern | Pub-Sub (async) | Req-Res (sync) | Goal-Feedback-Result |
| Direction | Many-to-Many | One-to-One | One-to-One |
| Use Case | Continuous streams | One-shot queries | Long-running tasks |
| Blocking? | No | Yes (client blocks) | No (preemptable) |
| Examples | `/scan`, `/cmd_vel` | `/get_map`, `/spawn` | `/move_base`, `/follow_path` |

**When Subscriber is correct (2 marks):**
Use a Topic subscriber when **data is continuous**, **lossy / stale data is acceptable**, and **multiple consumers** may want the same stream (e.g., LIDAR scan → simultaneously used by SLAM, obstacle avoidance, and visualisation). A Service would force the camera module to "stop and ask" every frame, which is wasteful and synchronous — completely wrong for streaming data.

---

#### 📐 Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Concept (3 marks):**
**Topic Remapping** allows a node to be launched with its hard-coded topic names *overridden* at runtime without modifying the source code. This is essential for **code reuse** and **namespacing** in multi-robot systems.

**Example Use Case (2 marks):**
A generic `image_processor` node subscribes to `/camera/image_raw`. On Robot A, you want it to subscribe to `/robot1/front_camera/image_raw` instead. Remapping lets you achieve this without touching code.

**Command-Line Syntax (2 marks):**

```bash
rosrun my_package image_processor _image_topic:=/robot1/front_camera/image_raw
```

Here, `_image_topic` is the **private parameter syntax** (`~`) which tells ROS to remap the internally-declared topic name `image_topic` to the external path `/robot1/front_camera/image_raw`.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `rospy.spin()`** → callbacks never fire → examiner marks 0 for "node behaviour" (lose ~3 marks).
> 2. **Confusing `data` vs `data.data`** — the message object and the inner field have the same name. Wrong access gives `AttributeError` and code fails (lose ~2 marks).
> 3. **Using `time.sleep()` instead of `rospy.Rate.sleep()`** — blocks the callback dispatcher, message reception stops (lose ~2 marks).
> 4. **Not handling `ROSInterruptException`** — Ctrl-C leaves the node in undefined state; examiner deducts for missing exception handling (lose ~1 mark).
> 5. **Claiming Master handles data transfer** — it does NOT; only discovery. Wrong answer = lose the diagram mark (lose ~2 marks).

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ⭐ **Subscriber** = node that **receives** messages on a named **Topic**.
- ⭐ Requires `rospy.init_node()` → `rospy.Subscriber()` → `rospy.spin()`.
- ⭐ The **callback function** is auto-invoked on every incoming message.
- ⭐ **Master does NOT carry data** — only does **discovery**; data flows via **direct TCP** between nodes.
- ⭐ `queue_size` is **mandatory** (≥ 1) in ROS Noetic; controls back-pressure.
- ⭐ `tcp_nodelay=True` is essential for **real-time control loops**.
- ⭐ Use `rospy.Rate.sleep()` instead of `time.sleep()` to keep callbacks alive.
- ⭐ **Topic remapping** enables code reuse without code modification.
- ⭐ Callback latency budget: $\tau_{cb} \leq 1/\lambda$ to prevent queue overflow.
- ⭐ Subscriber runs callback in a **separate listener thread** → beware race conditions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Latched Topic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT525 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Latched Topic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the Robot Operating System (ROS) communication middleware, a **Latched Topic** (also referred to as a *latched publisher* mechanism) is a specialized publisher–subscriber communication pattern in which the publisher node retains the **most recently published message** in its in-memory cache and **automatically retransmits** this cached message to **any new subscriber** that establishes a connection to that topic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PERUT525 – ROS, Module 1), latched topics form a critical subsection of the **ROS Master-Slave Communication** architecture and the **Topic-based Publish/Subscribe** model. Mastery of latched topics is essential for understanding state propagation in distributed robotic systems.

The formal ROS specification defines latching as a boolean attribute of a publisher. When `latch = True`:

$$
\text{PublisherState} = \begin{cases} \text{Buffered (last message retained)} & \text{if } latch = True \\ \text{Transient (no buffer)} & \text{if } latch = False \end{cases}
$$

The retained message is delivered exactly **once** to a newly connected subscriber, independent of the publisher's current publishing rate.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **digital notice board** mounted in a university hallway:

| **Scenario** | **Real-World Analogue** | **ROS Equivalent** |
|---|---|---|
| A notice is pinned to the board. Students who walk past **after** the notice was pinned see it only if they look. | Old notice — gone forever | **Regular (Non-Latched) Topic** |
| A notice is pinned, but the board **remembers** it permanently. Any student who walks past at any later time **immediately sees the most recent notice** upon looking. | Latched notice board | **Latched Topic** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** A latched topic is like a "remember-the-last-message" memory. The publisher behaves like a considerate speaker who, whenever a new listener joins the room, replays the last announcement so the newcomer is brought up to speed without waiting for the next periodic update.

## 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics (KTU Board Standard)

The following canonical ROS parameters govern latched topic behavior:

- **Default Latch Value:** `False` (regular topic)
- **Cache Size:** Exactly **1 message** (the most recent)
- **Delivery Guarantee:** **At-Most-Once** per new subscriber connection
- **QoS Reliability Override:** In ROS 2, the `TRANSIENT_LOCAL` Durability QoS replaces the legacy `latch` flag

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Why and How

A latched topic fundamentally alters the temporal semantics of ROS pub/sub communication. The following step-by-step logic explains its operation:

1. **Publisher Initialization** — The publisher node declares the topic as latched by passing `latch=True` (in `rospy`) or setting `latch=true` (in `roscpp`). This signals to the ROS Master that the topic requires retention.

2. **Message Buffering** — Each time the publisher calls `publish(msg)`, the new message **overwrites** the previous cached message in the publisher's internal one-slot buffer.

   > *Why?* The publisher's job, when latched, is not merely to broadcast but to **preserve state** for future subscribers.

3. **Subscriber Connection Event** — When a new subscriber registers with the ROS Master, the Master notifies the publisher of this new connection.

4. **Automatic Retransmission** — The publisher, upon receiving the connection handshake, **immediately pushes** the currently cached message to the new subscriber over the established XML-RPC / TCP connection.

5. **Subsequent Updates** — All future `publish(msg)` calls are delivered to **all connected subscribers** (both legacy and newly joined) as per standard ROS semantics.

6. **Subscriber Disconnection** — If the subscriber disconnects and reconnects, the cycle repeats: the **cached message** is replayed upon reconnection.

## 2.2 Difference Matrix — Latched vs. Non-Latched Topics

| Feature | Non-Latched Topic (Default) | Latched Topic |
|---|---|---|
| Buffer of last message | None | **1 message** |
| New subscriber receives past messages | No | **Yes (most recent only)** |
| Use case | Sensor streams, high-frequency data | Static maps, robot descriptions, parameters |
| Network overhead at subscribe time | Zero | **One extra message burst** |
| Publisher parameter | `latch=False` | **`latch=True`** |
| ROS 2 equivalent | Volatile durability | **`DurabilityPolicy.TRANSIENT_LOCAL`** |
| Suitable data velocity | High ( $> 1 \text{ Hz}$ ) | Low ( $< 1 \text{ Hz}$ or static ) |
| Delivery semantics | Real-time stream | **State snapshot** |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Equation Sheet — Latched Topic Communication**

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Standard Value / Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| $L$ | Latch flag (boolean) | $L \in \{ \text{True}, \text{False} \}$ |
| $M_{\text{cache}}$ | Cached message slot | $\vert M_{\text{cache}} \vert \leq 1$ |
| $f_p$ | Publisher frequency (Hz) | $f_p \geq 0$ |
| $D_{\text{new}}$ | Delivery to new subscriber | $D_{\text{new}} = \begin{cases} M_{\text{cache}} & \text{if } L = \text{True} \\ \emptyset & \text{if } L = \text{False} \end{cases}$ |
| $N_s$ | Number of currently connected subscribers | $N_s \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ |
| $T_{\text{conn}}$ | Time-to-first-message for new subscriber | $T_{\text{conn}} \approx \frac{1}{f_p}$ (regular), $\approx 0$ (latched) |
| $\tau_{\text{latency}}$ | ROS message transport latency | Typically $\tau \approx 1 \text{ ms}$ to $10 \text{ ms}$ on localhost |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Latched topics are indispensable in production robotic deployments for the following scenarios:

- **Robot Description (URDF):** The `/robot_description` topic typically uses a latched publisher so any tool (RViz, MoveIt, tf2) started *after* the robot_state_publisher automatically receives the kinematic tree.
- **Static Transforms:** Fixed frame relationships (e.g., `world → map`) are published once with latching.
- **Map Server:** The `/map` topic uses latching so that late-starting navigation stacks receive the occupancy grid without waiting for a re-publish.
- **Configuration Parameters:** Initial joint states, calibration offsets, and mode flags.
- **Emergency Stop State:** A latched `/e_stop` topic ensures that any newly-spawned safety monitor sees the current emergency status immediately.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Derivations

## 3.1 Analytical Model of a Latched Publisher

We now derive the expected message count and timing behavior of a latched topic publisher over a finite observation window.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

Let:

- $T$ = total observation window (in seconds)
- $f_p$ = publisher message frequency (Hz)
- $N_s(t)$ = number of active subscribers at time $t$
- $L$ = latch flag

**Assumption (Standard):** Publisher publishes at constant rate $f_p$, i.e., inter-message interval $\Delta t = \dfrac{1}{f_p}$.

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Total Messages Delivered

**Step 1:** Total number of messages published by the publisher in window $T$:

$$
M_{\text{pub}} = f_p \cdot T
$$

*Explanation:* A publisher emitting at $f_p$ Hz for $T$ seconds produces $f_p \cdot T$ discrete messages.

**Step 2:** Total number of subscriber-connections occurring in window $T$:

$$
C_{\text{new}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_s} \mathbb{1}_{\text{subscriber } i \text{ connects during } T}
$$

where $\mathbb{1}$ is the indicator function.

**Step 3:** Additional messages delivered due to latching:

$$
M_{\text{latch}} = \begin{cases} C_{\text{new}} & \text{if } L = \text{True} \\ 0 & \text{if } L = \text{False} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4:** Total downstream message volume:

$$
M_{\text{total}} = (M_{\text{pub}} \cdot N_s) + M_{\text{latch}}
$$

Expanding:

$$
M_{\text{total}} = (f_p \cdot T \cdot N_s) + C_{\text{new}} \cdot L
$$

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A latched publisher emits at $f_p = 1$ Hz over a $T = 10$ second window. There is one persistent subscriber $N_s = 1$ that connects at $t = 0$. Calculate $M_{\text{total}}$.

**Step 1:** Compute published messages.

$$
M_{\text{pub}} = f_p \cdot T = 1 \cdot 10 = 10 \text{ messages}
$$

**Step 2:** Since the subscriber connected at $t=0$, no "new connection" event occurs mid-window. $C_{\text{new}} = 0$.

**Step 3:** Total delivery to subscriber:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{total}} &= (10 \cdot 1) + (0 \cdot 1) \\
&= 10 \text{ messages}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compare with non-latched: identical here because no late subscribers joined. ✅

**Counter-example:** Now suppose a second subscriber connects at $t = 5$ s (mid-window).

- $C_{\text{new}} = 1$
- Latched delivery bonus: $M_{\text{latch}} = 1$
- Regular delivery to subscriber 1: $10$ messages
- Latched delivery to subscriber 2 (joins at $t=5$): receives the cached message at $t=5$ plus future messages from $t=5$ to $t=10$, totaling $5$ messages
- $M_{\text{total}} = (10 \cdot 2) + 1 = 21$ messages ✅

### 3.1.4 Time-to-First-Message Derivation

For a new subscriber arriving at time $t_a$, the expected wait time for the **first message** is:

$$
E[T_{\text{first}}] = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2 f_p} & \text{if } L = \text{False (uniform random arrival)} \\ \dfrac{1}{\tau_{\text{RTT}}} \approx 0 & \text{if } L = \text{True} \end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A latched subscriber experiences near-zero time-to-first-message because the cached payload is pushed immediately upon connection establishment. A non-latched subscriber must wait, on average, half a publishing cycle.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Publisher–Subscriber Behavior Over Time

The following Mermaid sequence diagram contrasts the message flow of a **Regular Topic** versus a **Latched Topic**. It is a board-exam favorite diagram type for Module 1.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant Pub as Publisher Node
    participant Cache as Latch Buffer (size = 1)
    participant SubA as Subscriber A
    participant SubB as Subscriber B (Late Joiner)

    Note over Pub,Cache: REGULAR TOPIC (latch = false)
    Pub->>SubA: publish(msg1) at t = 0s
    Note right of SubA: msg1 received
    Note over SubB: SubB not yet connected
    Pub->>SubA: publish(msg2) at t = 1s
    Note right of SubA: msg2 received
    SubB-->>Pub: SUBSCRIBE at t = 2s
    Note over SubB: SubB receives NOTHING until msg3
    Pub->>SubA: publish(msg3) at t = 3s
    Pub->>SubA: publish(msg3) at t = 3s
    Pub->>SubB: publish(msg3) at t = 3s
    Note right of SubB: msg3 received (waited 1 second)

    Note over Pub,Cache: LATCHED TOPIC (latch = true)
    Pub->>SubA: publish(msg1) at t = 0s
    Pub->>Cache: store(msg1)
    Note right of Cache: slot = msg1
    Pub->>SubA: publish(msg2) at t = 1s
    Pub->>Cache: overwrite with msg2
    Note right of Cache: slot = msg2
    SubB-->>Pub: SUBSCRIBE at t = 2s
    Pub->>Cache: read current slot
    Pub->>SubB: AUTO-PUSH msg2 at t = 2s
    Note right of SubB: msg2 received INSTANTLY (state snapshot)
```

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — When to Use Latched Topics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New ROS Topic to Publish] --> B{Is data static or slowly changing?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Did late subscribers need immediate state?}
    B -- No --> D[Use REGULAR topic latch=false]
    C -- Yes --> E[Use LATCHED topic latch=true]
    C -- No --> D
    E --> F[Set publisher parameter latch equals True]
    F --> G[Verify in rostopic info output]
    D --> H[Default publisher behavior]
    G --> I[Monitor with rostopic echo]
    H --> I
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (ROS Pub/Sub with Latching)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ROS_Master
        M[ROS Master Registry]
    end

    subgraph Publisher_Node
        P[Publisher Core]
        LB[Latch Buffer 1-slot]
        P --> LB
    end

    subgraph Subscriber_Nodes
        S1[Subscriber A persistent]
        S2[Subscriber B late-joiner]
    end

    M -.registers.-> P
    M -.registers.-> S1
    M -.notifies new subscriber.-> P
    P -->|stream to all| S1
    LB -->|cached snapshot on connect| S2
    P -->|future messages| S2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a latched topic in ROS. Mention any two use cases where latched topics are preferred over regular topics.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A latched topic is a publisher–subscriber communication channel in ROS where the publisher retains the **last published message** in its internal one-slot buffer and **automatically retransmits** this cached message to any subscriber that connects to the topic at a later time. **(1.5 marks)**

Two use cases: **(2 × 0.75 = 1.5 marks)**

1. **Robot Description (URDF):** Static kinematic data published once and replayed for late-starting tools like RViz or MoveIt.
2. **Map Server (`/map` topic):** The occupancy grid is published with latching so navigation stacks that launch after map generation receive the map immediately.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What is the default value of the `latch` parameter when creating a ROS publisher? How is latching realized in ROS 2?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Default value:** `latch = False` (regular, non-latched topic). **(1 mark)**
- **Mechanism in ROS 1:** Set during publisher creation, e.g., `rospy.Publisher('topic_name', MsgType, queue_size=10, latch=True)`. **(1 mark)**
- **ROS 2 equivalent:** The boolean `latch` flag is deprecated; latching is achieved by setting the **Durability QoS policy** to `DurabilityPolicy.TRANSIENT_LOCAL` for the publisher. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **publisher–subscriber communication model** in ROS. Differentiate between a regular topic and a latched topic with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A latched publisher publishes robot configuration data at a frequency of $0.5 \text{ Hz}$ on the topic `/robot_config`. Two subscribers, A and B, connect at $t = 0$ and $t = 6 \text{ s}$ respectively. Calculate the total number of messages each subscriber receives over a window of $T = 20 \text{ s}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The ROS pub/sub model consists of three entities: **(1 mark)**

- **ROS Master:** Central registry that matches publishers with subscribers.
- **Publisher Node:** Declares a topic, message type, and creates a publisher object.
- **Subscriber Node:** Registers interest in a topic and receives messages via TCP/XML-RPC.

**Communication flow:** **(2 marks)**

1. Publisher registers with Master.
2. Subscriber registers with Master.
3. Master returns publisher's URI to subscriber.
4. Subscriber establishes direct P2P connection with publisher.
5. Messages flow publisher → subscriber(s).

**Regular vs Latched Topic — Comparison Table:** **(3 marks)**

| Parameter | Regular | Latched |
|---|---|---|
| Last-message buffer | No | **Yes** |
| Late subscriber wait | Up to $1/f_p$ | **Near-zero** |
| Use case | Sensor streams | **Static state** |
| Network cost at join | Zero | **One message** |
| ROS 2 equivalent | Volatile | **TRANSIENT_LOCAL** |

**Diagram:** *(Draw the sequence diagram from Section 4.1 here — 1 mark)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1:** Identify publisher parameters.

$$
f_p = 0.5 \text{ Hz}, \quad T = 20 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute total published messages.

$$
M_{\text{pub}} = f_p \cdot T = 0.5 \cdot 20 = 10 \text{ messages}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute messages received by Subscriber A (joined at $t = 0$, persistent).

Since A is connected for the entire window, it receives every published message:

$$
M_A = M_{\text{pub}} = 10 \text{ messages}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute messages received by Subscriber B (joins at $t = 6 \text{ s}$).

B misses the first 3 messages (published at $t = 0, 2, 4$). However, because the topic is **latched**, B automatically receives the **cached message** (the message at $t = 4 \text{ s}$) upon connecting at $t = 6 \text{ s}$. **(2 marks)**

From $t = 6$ to $t = 20$ (i.e., $14$ seconds), B receives:

$$
N_{\text{stream}} = \lfloor 14 \cdot 0.5 \rfloor = 7 \text{ messages}
$$

Plus the auto-pushed latched snapshot:

$$
M_B = 1 \text{ (latched)} + 7 \text{ (stream)} = 8 \text{ messages}
$$

**Final Answer:** Subscriber A receives **10 messages**, Subscriber B receives **8 messages**. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat sequence diagram, describe the **message flow** when a new subscriber joins an active latched topic. Compare this with the ROS 2 Durability QoS policy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **expected time-to-first-message** for a new subscriber on (i) a regular topic and (ii) a latched topic. Discuss the practical implications for a navigation stack that starts **after** the map server has finished publishing the occupancy grid. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution Outline

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

- Sequence diagram (reproduce from Section 4.1): 4 marks
- ROS 2 QoS mapping table: 3 marks

| ROS 1 Concept | ROS 2 Equivalent |
|---|---|
| `latch=True` | `DurabilityPolicy.TRANSIENT_LOCAL` |
| `latch=False` | `DurabilityPolicy.VOLATILE` |
| Reliable TCP | `ReliabilityPolicy.RELIABLE` / `BEST_EFFORT` |

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

- Derivation as in Section 3.1.4
- For navigation stack: latched map ensures `move_base` receives `/map` instantly on launch; non-latched would require `map_server` to keep republishing or the navigation to wait up to $\frac{1}{f_p}$ seconds.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `queue_size`:** In ROS 1 `rospy`, omitting `queue_size` raises a `rospy.exceptions.ROSException`. Students often write `latch=True` without `queue_size`, losing **1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing latched with reliable:** Latching controls **retention for late subscribers**, not delivery guarantees. Students frequently mix it up with QoS reliability, losing up to **2 marks**.
> 3. **Believing the buffer holds multiple messages:** The cache size is **exactly 1**, not a queue. Mentioning "queue" costs **0.5–1 mark**.
> 4. **ROS 1 vs ROS 2 confusion:** ROS 2 has **no `latch` parameter**; use `TRANSIENT_LOCAL` durability. Writing `latch=True` in a ROS 2 answer is a **deduction of 2 marks**.
> 5. **Not drawing the diagram:** Part (a) of 14-mark questions typically mandates a diagram. Skipping it costs **2–3 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **latched topic** retains the **last published message** in a **single-slot buffer** at the publisher.
- ✅ New subscribers automatically receive the cached message **once** upon connection.
- ✅ Default value is `latch=False`; enable with `latch=True` (ROS 1).
- ✅ Use latching for **static / slowly-changing / state-bearing** data (maps, URDF, transforms, calibration).
- ✅ **Do NOT** use latching for high-frequency sensor streams (cameras, LIDAR, IMU) — it wastes bandwidth at subscribe time.
- ✅ In **ROS 2**, the equivalent is `DurabilityPolicy.TRANSIENT_LOCAL` QoS, not the legacy `latch` flag.
- ✅ Cache size is **exactly 1 message** — not a multi-message queue.
- ✅ Latching affects **delivery to late joiners only**; it does **not** change the real-time streaming semantics for already-connected subscribers.
- ✅ Time-to-first-message ≈ **0** for latched; **$\frac{1}{2 f_p}$** (average) for non-latched.
- ✅ Latched topics minimize the synchronization problem in modular robotic systems where nodes launch in arbitrary order.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Defining Message Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT525 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Defining Message Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Robot Operating System (ROS)**, a **Message** is the elementary data-structure unit transmitted between computational processes called **Nodes**. The **Message Type** is a strongly-typed, language-agnostic contract that describes the exact binary layout, field names, data types, and serialization rules used when two or more ROS nodes communicate over the **ROS Communication Infrastructure** (Topics, Services, or Actions).

A **Message Type Definition** is written in a simplified **Interface Description Language (IDL)** stored inside plain-text files with the `.msg` extension. ROS supports three categories of interface definitions:

| Interface File | Extension | Communication Pattern | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Message | `.msg` | Asynchronous (Topic Publish/Subscribe) | Continuous sensor streams, robot state |
| Service | `.srv` | Synchronous Request/Reply | One-shot computations, triggers |
| Action | `.action` | Asynchronous with Feedback + Goal | Long-running tasks with cancel support |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 explicitly demands understanding of `.msg` file structure, standard primitive types, complex message composition (nested messages), header inclusion, and the build-time code-generation pipeline that converts `.msg` definitions into language-specific classes (C++/Python).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Postal Letter System

Imagine ROS nodes as **people living in different houses across a city** who need to exchange information constantly.

| Real-World Element | ROS Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The house address | Node name (`/camera_node`) |
| A standardized letter envelope with labeled fields | **Message Type** (`.msg` definition) |
| The postal service | ROS Master + TCPROS transport |
| The mailbox category (Priority, Regular) | **Topic** (`/camera/image_raw`) |
| The letter content (data inside fields) | **Message Instance** (the actual data sent) |

Just as every postal envelope has **fixed labeled fields** ("To:", "From:", "Date:"), every ROS message has **declared fields** (e.g., `int32 x`, `float64 y`). The publisher **cannot** stuff extra undefined fields into the envelope, and the subscriber **knows exactly what to expect** before opening it. This guarantees that two nodes — one written in C++ and the other in Python — interpret the same bytes identically.

## 1.3 Why Message Types Matter — The Interoperability Guarantee

ROS is a **distributed, multi-language, multi-process framework**. To maintain interoperability:

- **Strong Typing:** Every field has a fixed primitive type → no ambiguity.
- **Language Neutrality:** `.msg` is plain text → any language can parse it.
- **Self-Describing:** The type name (e.g., `geometry_msgs/Twist`) acts as a runtime contract.
- **Build-Time Code Generation:** `genmsg` tooling converts `.msg` → C++ headers / Python modules at compile time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (Exam-Ready):** A ROS *Message Type* is a declarative specification (stored in `.msg` files) that defines the field names, order, primitive data types, and structural composition of data exchanged between ROS nodes. It is published under a fully-qualified name of the form `<package_name>/<MessageName>` (e.g., `std_msgs/String`).

## 1.4 Standard Message Type Hierarchy

ROS ships with several pre-built packages of commonly-used message types:

```
std_msgs/          → Primitive types (String, Int32, Float64, Bool, Header, ...)
geometry_msgs/     → Spatial primitives (Point, Vector3, Pose, Twist, Quaternion)
sensor_msgs/       → Sensor data (Image, LaserScan, Imu, JointState, PointCloud2)
nav_msgs/          → Navigation (OccupancyGrid, Odometry, Path)
actionlib_msgs/    → Action server goals/results/feedback
diagnostic_msgs/   → System health & diagnostics
```

> [!TIP]
> A complete list is always available via terminal: `ros2 interface list` (ROS 2) or `rosmsg list` (ROS 1).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a `.msg` File — Field Structure Rules

A `.msg` file is composed of **one field declaration per line**, with the following syntactic rules:

```
<type> <field_name>
```

Where:
- `<type>` is either a **primitive type** or a **complex type** (another message).
- `<field_name>` follows standard identifier rules (letters, digits, underscores; must start with a letter).
- Lines beginning with `#` are treated as **comments** and ignored by the code generator.
- A **constant** field can be declared with `=` assignment (visible across languages).

### 2.1.1 Example Canonical `.msg` File

```
# PersonInfo.msg
# Carries identifying data about a detected human

std_msgs/Header header      # Timestamp + coordinate frame
string name                 # Person's identifier
uint8 age                   # Age in years
float64 height              # Height in meters
bool is_tracked             # Tracking status flag
geometry_msgs/Point position # 3D position (x, y, z)
sensor_msgs/Image face      # Optional nested face crop
float64[] confidence_scores # Variable-length confidence array
uint8[3] rgb_color          # Fixed-length RGB triplet
int32 MAX_AGE = 150         # Compile-time constant
```

## 2.2 Primitive Field Types — The Complete Cheat Sheet

The table below is the **high-yield reference** KTU examiners expect students to memorize.

| Primitive Type | C++ Equivalent | Python Equivalent | Size (bytes) | Range / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `bool` | `bool` | `bool` | 1 | `True` / `False` |
| `int8` | `int8_t` | `int` | 1 | $-128$ to $+127$ |
| `uint8` | `uint8_t` | `int` | 1 | $0$ to $255$ (also used for raw bytes/image data) |
| `int16` | `int16_t` | `int` | 2 | $-32\,768$ to $32\,767$ |
| `uint16` | `uint16_t` | `int` | 2 | $0$ to $65\,535$ |
| `int32` | `int32_t` | `int` | 4 | $-2.147 \times 10^{9}$ to $2.147 \times 10^{9}$ |
| `uint32` | `uint32_t` | `int` | 4 | $0$ to $4.29 \times 10^{9}$ |
| `int64` | `int64_t` | `int` | 8 | $-9.22 \times 10^{18}$ to $9.22 \times 10^{18}$ |
| `uint64` | `uint64_t` | `int` | 8 | $0$ to $1.84 \times 10^{19}$ |
| `float32` | `float` | `float` | 4 | IEEE-754 single precision |
| `float64` | `double` | `float` | 8 | IEEE-754 double precision (default for ROS) |
| `string` | `std::string` | `str` | variable | UTF-8 encoded |
| `time` | `builtin_interfaces/Time` (ROS 2) | builtin | 8+4 | seconds (int32) + nanoseconds (uint32) |
| `duration` | `builtin_interfaces/Duration` | builtin | 8+4 | seconds + nanoseconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The default floating-point type in ROS is **`float64`** (double-precision), *not* `float32`. Examiners frequently ask students why `float64` is preferred — answer: **precision** for odometry, IMU integration, and SLAM where accumulated errors matter.

## 2.3 Complex / Composite Field Types

### 2.3.1 Arrays (Variable-Length)

Syntax: `<type>[] <name>`

```
float64[] joint_positions    # List of joint angles
string[] tags               # List of semantic tags
```

Internally serialized as a **4-byte length prefix** (uint32) followed by N elements.

### 2.3.2 Bounded Arrays (Fixed-Length)

Syntax: `<type>[N] <name>`

```
uint8[3] rgb_color           # Always exactly 3 elements
float64[6] homogeneous_matrix_row
```

Bounded arrays are serialized **without** the length prefix, yielding compact, deterministic byte layouts.

### 2.3.3 Nested Messages (Composition)

A field can be any other message type, enabling hierarchical structures:

```
# RobotState.msg
std_msgs/Header header
geometry_msgs/Pose pose           # Composed of Point + Quaternion
geometry_msgs/Twist velocity      # Composed of linear (Vector3) + angular (Vector3)
sensor_msgs/JointState[] joints   # Array of a complex message type
```

## 2.4 The Standard Header — `std_msgs/Header`

Nearly every high-level message contains a Header for temporal/spatial synchronization:

```
# std_msgs/msg/Header.msg (ROS 2)
builtin_interfaces/Time stamp     # When the data was produced
string frame_id                   # Coordinate frame reference (e.g., "base_link")
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Header serves three engineering purposes: (1) **latency measurement** via `stamp`, (2) **TF-tree anchoring** via `frame_id`, and (3) **multi-sensor fusion** by aligning timestamps across heterogeneous sensors.

## 2.5 Message Serialization — The Mathematical Model

ROS messages are serialized into a **byte-stream** for TCP/IP transmission. The serialization cost is given by:

$$
S_{msg} = \underbrace{4 \cdot N_{var\text{-}arr}}_{\text{array length prefixes}} + \sum_{i=1}^{N_{fields}} \big[ \, B_i + \underbrace{A_i \cdot |arr_i|}_{\text{array payload}} \, \big]
$$

Where:

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $S_{msg}$ | Total serialized size in bytes |
| $N_{var\text{-}arr}$ | Number of variable-length arrays |
| $N_{fields}$ | Total number of declared fields |
| $B_i$ | Fixed size of field $i$ in bytes |
| $A_i$ | Per-element size for array fields |
| $\vert arr_i \vert$ | Number of elements in array $i$ |

### Worked Example — Serialization Size of `geometry_msgs/Twist`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Twist fields} &= \{ \text{Vector3 linear},\ \text{Vector3 angular} \} \\
\text{Vector3} &= \{ \text{float64 x},\ \text{float64 y},\ \text{float64 z} \} \\
S_{\text{Vector3}} &= 8 + 8 + 8 = 24\ \text{bytes} \\
S_{\text{Twist}} &= S_{\text{Vector3}} + S_{\text{Vector3}} = 24 + 24 = 48\ \text{bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the well-known ROS statement: `geometry_msgs/Twist` is **48 bytes** wire-format.

## 2.6 Build-Time Code-Generation Pipeline

The transition from `.msg` → executable library follows a deterministic pipeline:

```
┌─────────────┐    genmsg     ┌─────────────┐   IDL parser   ┌──────────────┐
│ .msg files  │ ────────────▶ │ .idl / .msg │ ─────────────▶ │ C++ Headers  │
│ (source)    │  (genmsg)     │  (parsed)   │  (gencpp)      │ Python mods  │
└─────────────┘               └─────────────┘                └──────────────┘
        ▲                            ▲                              │
        │                            │                              ▼
   package.xml                CMakeLists.txt                  <node binary>
   (declares                  (add_message_files,          
    msg deps)                 generate_messages)            
```

### Step-by-Step Build Logic

1. **Package Declaration:** `package.xml` lists `<build_depend>std_msgs</build_depend>` etc.
2. **Source Listing:** `CMakeLists.txt` invokes `add_message_files(FILES PersonInfo.msg)`.
3. **Dependency Declaration:** `generate_messages(DEPENDENCIES std_msgs geometry_msgs sensor_msgs)`.
4. **Code Generation:** During `catkin_make` / `colcon build`, `genmsg` + `gencpp` / `genpy` emit language bindings.
5. **Artifact Availability:** C++ headers appear in `devel/include/<pkg>/`; Python modules under `devel/lib/python3/...`.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Message Type Application |
|---|---|
| Autonomous Vehicles | `sensor_msgs/PointCloud2` from LiDAR, `nav_msgs/Odometry` from INS fusion |
| Industrial Robotics | `trajectory_msgs/JointTrajectory` for arm motion |
| Aerial Drones (UAV) | `mavros_msgs/State`, `geometry_msgs/PoseStamped` for setpoints |
| Healthcare Robotics | Custom `PatientVitals.msg` carrying HR, SpO₂, posture |
| Agricultural Robotics | `sensor_msgs/NavSatFix` (GPS), `sensor_msgs/Imu` for terrain compensation |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Message Serialized Size Formula (General Case)

We begin from first principles. ROS uses the **CDR (Common Data Representation)** serialization in ROS 2 and a similar length-delimited format in ROS 1.

### Step 1 — Define the per-field payload

Each primitive field contributes a fixed number of bytes $B_i$ equal to its declared type's width:

$$
B_i = \begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } type \in \{ \text{bool},\ \text{int8},\ \text{uint8} \} \\
2 & \text{if } type \in \{ \text{int16},\ \text{uint16} \} \\
4 & \text{if } type \in \{ \text{int32},\ \text{uint32},\ \text{float32} \} \\
8 & \text{if } type \in \{ \text{int64},\ \text{uint64},\ \text{float64} \} \\
\text{variable} & \text{if } type = \text{string}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply nesting recursively

For a nested complex field of type $T_{child}$, we treat its serialized size $S_{T_{child}}$ as the contribution:

$$
B_{\text{nested},i} = S_{T_{child},i}
$$

### Step 3 — Account for variable-length array headers

Each variable-length array prepends a **uint32 length prefix** = **4 bytes**:

$$
H_{\text{array},i} = 4 \quad \text{(only if the array is unbounded)}
$$

### Step 4 — Combine all contributions

$$
\boxed{\,S_{msg} \;=\; \sum_{i \in \text{fields}} \Big[ \, H_{\text{array},i} \;+\; B_i \;+\; A_i \cdot \vert \text{arr}_i \vert \, \Big]\,}
$$

For a message with **no variable-length arrays and no strings**, this reduces to:

$$
S_{msg} = \sum_{i \in \text{fields}} B_i
$$

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Verification

Compute the wire-size of `geometry_msgs/PoseStamped`:

```
PoseStamped.msg:
  std_msgs/Header header     ← nested (Header = 8 + 4 + variable string)
  geometry_msgs/Pose pose    ← nested (Pose = Point + Quaternion)
```

We compute each component size:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{Point}} &= \text{float64}_x + \text{float64}_y + \text{float64}_z = 8 + 8 + 8 = 24\ \text{bytes} \\
S_{\text{Quaternion}} &= 8 + 8 + 8 + 8 = 32\ \text{bytes} \\
S_{\text{Pose}} &= S_{\text{Point}} + S_{\text{Quaternion}} = 24 + 32 = 56\ \text{bytes} \\
S_{\text{Header}} &= \underbrace{4 + 4}_{time} + \underbrace{4 + L_{frame\_id}}_{\text{string prefix+data}} \\
\text{Assuming } L_{frame\_id} &= 8 \quad (\text{e.g., "base_link"}) \\
S_{\text{Header}} &= 8 + 12 = 20\ \text{bytes} \\
S_{\text{PoseStamped}} &= S_{\text{Header}} + S_{\text{Pose}} = 20 + 56 = 76\ \text{bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the verified ROS standard value (excluding padding).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Guide for Choosing Field Types

| Use Case | Recommended Field Type | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| RGB pixel intensity | `uint8` | Ranges only 0–255 |
| Pixel coordinates (image) | `uint32` | Avoid overflow for large images |
| LiDAR range in meters | `float32` | Range rarely exceeds sensor precision |
| Odometry translation | `float64` | Cumulative error demands precision |
| Quaternion components | `float64` | Unit-norm constraint demands precision |
| Timestamps | `builtin_interfaces/Time` (ROS 2) | Built-in clock synchronization |
| Joint torques (Nm) | `float64` | Wide dynamic range |
| Boolean flags (enabled/disabled) | `bool` | Single byte, semantic clarity |
| Identifying string (tag, name) | `string` | Human-readable metadata |
| Sensor matrix rows (fixed) | `<type>[N]` bounded | Predictable byte layout |

## 3.3 Derivation: Type Promotion and Compatibility Rules

When a **subscriber** requests a message, ROS uses **MD5 checksum-based equivalence** to confirm type compatibility. The MD5 is computed deterministically over the **canonical text form** of the `.msg` file.

The compatibility test is:

$$
\text{Compatible}(T_p, T_s) = \begin{cases}
\text{True} & \text{if } \text{MD5}(T_p) = \text{MD5}(T_s) \\
\text{False} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

Where:
- $T_p$ = published message type
- $T_s$ = subscribed message type

> [!NOTE]
> This explains why renaming a field in your `.msg` file (even without changing the C++ code) **breaks all existing subscribers** — the MD5 changes, and the runtime connection is rejected.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Message Type Lifecycle Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Developer writes PersonInfo.msg] --> B[Place in package msg/ directory]
    B --> C[Declare deps in package.xml]
    C --> D[add_message_files in CMakeLists.txt]
    D --> E[generate_messages DEPENDENCIES]
    E --> F[genmsg parses IDL]
    F --> G1[C++ Header PersonInfo.h generated]
    F --> G2[Python _PersonInfo.py module generated]
    G1 --> H1[include person_info/PersonInfo.h]
    G2 --> H2[from person_info.msg import PersonInfo]
    H1 --> I1[Publisher Node Cpp]
    H2 --> I2[Subscriber Node Py]
    I1 --> J[ROS Topic /person_info]
    I2 --> J
    J --> K[TCPROS Serialization 48 bytes payload]
    K --> L[Receiver deserializes using same MD5 contract]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Message Type Field Composition Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Hdr[std_msgs Header] --> RobotStateMsg
    Pose[geometry_msgs Pose] --> RobotStateMsg
    Twist[geometry_msgs Twist] --> RobotStateMsg
    Point[geometry_msgs Point] --> Pose
    Quat[geometry_msgs Quaternion] --> Pose
    Vec3[geometry_msgs Vector3] --> Twist
    Point2[geometry_msgs Point] --> Vec3
    JointArr[sensor_msgs JointState array] --> RobotStateMsg
    RobotStateMsg[Custom RobotState msg]
    classDef custom fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A06000,color:#000
    classDef std fill:#C8E6FF,stroke:#003F7F,color:#000
    class RobotStateMsg custom
    class Hdr,Pose,Twist,Point,Quat,Vec3,Point2,JointArr std
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Publisher Subscriber Type Compatibility Check

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P as Publisher Node
    participant M as ROS Master
    participant S as Subscriber Node
    P->>M: advertise(/topic, std_msgs/String, MD5=abc123)
    S->>M: subscribe(/topic, std_msgs/String, MD5=abc123)
    M->>P: connection approved MD5 match
    M->>S: connection approved MD5 match
    P->>S: serialize msg bytes and transmit
    S->>S: deserialize using known layout
    Note over P,S: If MD5 mismatched connection refused
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Build-Time Code Generation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[package.xml deps] --> B[catkin colcon build trigger]
    C[CMakeLists.txt msg list] --> B
    B --> D[genmsg parser]
    D --> E[Canonical text form]
    E --> F1[MD5 hash generator]
    E --> F2[gencpp C++ template]
    E --> F3[genpy Python template]
    F2 --> G1[devel include headers]
    F3 --> G2[devel lib python3 modules]
    G1 --> H[Link into node binary]
    G2 --> H
    F1 --> I[rosmsg md5 lookup table]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a ROS Message Type. Explain the role of the `.msg` file with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A ROS Message Type is a structured data contract that defines the field names, field types, and serialization format for data exchanged between ROS nodes. It is specified in plain-text `.msg` files located inside the `msg/` directory of a ROS package. For example:

```
# SensorReading.msg
std_msgs/Header header
string sensor_name
float64 value
bool valid
```

This declaration tells the ROS build system to generate C++ and Python bindings so that nodes can publish and subscribe to the `SensorReading` type on a topic. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for role, 1 for example)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any six primitive field types supported in a ROS `.msg` file. State why `float64` is preferred over `float32` for odometry messages.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Six primitive types: `bool`, `int8`, `int32`, `uint8`, `float32`, `float64`, `string`, `time`, `duration`. `float64` is preferred for odometry because it provides **double-precision (≈15 decimal digits)** accuracy which prevents accumulated rounding errors during position integration over long robot trajectories. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of the ROS message build-time code generation pipeline. List the steps involved in defining a custom message type named `RobotState.msg` containing a header, pose, velocity, and an array of joint states. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between variable-length arrays and bounded arrays in ROS message definitions. Compute the serialized byte size of the following `SensorPacket.msg`:

```
# SensorPacket.msg
std_msgs/Header header          # 20 bytes (with 8-char frame_id)
float64[] recent_samples        # 10 elements
uint8 status_code
bool critical_alert
```

**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Architecture Diagram (refer Section 4.4):**

The pipeline consists of:
1. **Authoring Phase:** Developer writes `RobotState.msg` in `~/catkin_ws/src/my_robot/msg/`.
2. **Manifest Update:** Add `<build_depend>` and `<exec_depend>` for `std_msgs`, `geometry_msgs`, `sensor_msgs` in `package.xml`.
3. **Build Script Update:** Add the following lines to `CMakeLists.txt`:
   - `add_message_files(FILES RobotState.msg)`
   - `generate_messages(DEPENDENCIES std_msgs geometry_msgs sensor_msgs)`
4. **Compilation:** Running `catkin_make` invokes `genmsg` which parses the IDL, computes the MD5, and emits:
   - C++ header `RobotState.h` in `devel/include/my_robot/`
   - Python module `_RobotState.py` in `devel/lib/python3/dist-packages/`
5. **Consumption:** Publisher includes `<my_robot/RobotState.h>` and constructs an instance; subscriber does the same on the receiving end.

**Steps to define `RobotState.msg`:**

```
# RobotState.msg
std_msgs/Header header
geometry_msgs/Pose pose
geometry_msgs/Twist velocity
sensor_msgs/JointState[] joints
```

Then update `package.xml`, `CMakeLists.txt`, and rebuild. **(7 marks — 3 for diagram, 4 for steps)**

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Differentiation Table:**

| Feature | Variable-Length Array `<type>[]` | Bounded Array `<type>[N]` |
|---|---|---|
| Length | Determined at runtime | Fixed at compile time |
| Wire prefix | 4-byte uint32 length prefix | None |
| Memory | Dynamic allocation | Static allocation |
| Use case | Variable sensor data, list of detections | Fixed RGB triplets, matrix rows |

**Computation of `SensorPacket` size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{Header}} &= 4_{\text{sec}} + 4_{\text{nsec}} + 4_{\text{str prefix}} + 8_{\text{frame\_id data}} = 20\ \text{bytes} \\
S_{\text{recent\_samples}} &= \underbrace{4}_{\text{length prefix}} + \underbrace{10 \times 8}_{10 \times \text{float64}} = 4 + 80 = 84\ \text{bytes} \\
S_{\text{status\_code}} &= 1\ \text{byte} \\
S_{\text{critical\_alert}} &= 1\ \text{byte} \\
S_{\text{total}} &= 20 + 84 + 1 + 1 = \mathbf{106\ \text{bytes}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(7 marks — 3 for differentiation, 4 for computation)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the significance of `std_msgs/Header` in ROS messages. Describe its three constituent fields and their engineering purposes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a custom message `ObstacleArray.msg`:

```
# ObstacleArray.msg
std_msgs/Header header          # 20 bytes
geometry_msgs/Point[] points    # 5 points
float32[] radii                 # 5 elements
uint8 NUM_OBSTACLES = 5
```

Calculate the total serialized wire size and identify which fields contribute most to the payload. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

The `std_msgs/Header` is a near-universal convention that provides **temporal and spatial metadata** for nearly all high-level ROS messages. Its three constituent fields are:

| Field | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `stamp` | `time` | Records the moment the data was sampled — critical for sensor fusion and latency analysis |
| `frame_id` | `string` | Identifies the coordinate frame (e.g., `"base_link"`, `"odom"`) in which the data is expressed — anchors the message to the TF tree |
| `seq` (ROS 1 only) | `uint32` | Monotonic sequence number for ordering |

Engineering purposes:
1. **Time synchronization** across heterogeneous sensors using the `stamp`.
2. **TF-tree anchoring** so other nodes can transform the data into any desired reference frame.
3. **Latency monitoring** by comparing `stamp` against reception time. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Wire-size Computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{Header}} &= 20\ \text{bytes (given)} \\
S_{\text{points}} &= \underbrace{4}_{\text{length prefix}} + \underbrace{5 \times 24}_{5 \times \text{Point}(x,y,z)} = 4 + 120 = 124\ \text{bytes} \\
S_{\text{radii}} &= \underbrace{4}_{\text{length prefix}} + \underbrace{5 \times 4}_{5 \times \text{float32}} = 4 + 20 = 24\ \text{bytes} \\
S_{\text{constants}} &= 0\ \text{bytes (constants are not transmitted)} \\
