S_{\text{total}} &= 20 + 124 + 24 = \mathbf{168\ \text{bytes}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Dominant contributor:** The `points` array contributes $124 / 168 \approx 73.8\%$ of the payload. This is because each `Point` contains three `float64` values ($24\ \text{bytes}$), so an array of 5 points becomes the dominant cost driver.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently forget the **4-byte uint32 length prefix** for variable-length arrays — this alone costs **2–3 marks**.
> - Constants declared with `=` (e.g., `NUM_OBSTACLES`) are **NOT serialized on the wire** — including them inflates the calculation incorrectly.
> - Confusing `float32` (4 bytes) with `float64` (8 bytes) is the single most common computational error in `.msg` size problems.
> - Never forget the **Header string prefix** (4 bytes) + the actual character bytes of `frame_id`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **Message Type** is a strongly-typed, language-agnostic contract defined in `.msg` files.
- ✅ Files live in `<package>/msg/` and are listed in `CMakeLists.txt` via `add_message_files()`.
- ✅ Primitive types include `bool`, `int8`–`int64`, `uint8`–`uint64`, `float32`, `float64`, `string`, `time`, `duration`.
- ✅ **`float64` is the default** floating-point type in ROS due to precision needs in odometry/SLAM.
- ✅ Arrays use `<type>[]` (variable) or `<type>[N]` (bounded).
- ✅ Bounded arrays **omit** the 4-byte length prefix on the wire.
- ✅ Constants declared with `=` are **compile-time only** — not transmitted.
- ✅ The `std_msgs/Header` carries `stamp` and `frame_id` for temporal/spatial anchoring.
- ✅ Message compatibility is enforced via **MD5 checksum** of the canonical text form.
- ✅ Wire-size formula: $S_{msg} = \sum (4 \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{var-arr}} + B_i + A_i \cdot \vert \text{arr}_i \vert)$.
- ✅ Common standard packages: `std_msgs`, `geometry_msgs`, `sensor_msgs`, `nav_msgs`.
- ✅ Fully-qualified type names use the form `<package>/<MessageName>` (e.g., `sensor_msgs/LaserScan`).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mixing Publishers and Subscribers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT525 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mixing Publishers and Subscribers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the Robot Operating System (ROS) architecture, a **Node** is the fundamental computational unit — a process that performs a specific robotic task. Most introductory ROS patterns teach isolated **Publisher Nodes** (which emit messages to a **Topic**) or isolated **Subscriber Nodes** (which consume messages from a Topic). 

**Mixing Publishers and Subscribers** refers to the architectural pattern where a **single ROS node simultaneously subscribes to one or more input topics AND publishes to one or more output topics**. This node acts as an *in-line data transformer, controller, or relay* in the ROS **Computation Graph**. The node's internal callback logic reads incoming messages, processes them (filtering, fusion, kinematics, control law, etc.), and emits new messages downstream — all within the same process address space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> This topic builds upon the foundational understanding of *ROS Nodes, Topics, Messages, Publishers, and Subscribers*. It is the **gateway pattern** to designing real robotic systems such as closed-loop controllers, sensor-fusion pipelines, and behavior-coordination layers. Mastery of this pattern is mandatory for Part B questions in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Multilingual Translator"

Imagine a live international business summit with three rooms:

- **Room A** contains a Japanese executive speaking constantly (this is your **Publisher**).
- **Room C** contains an English-speaking audience waiting for the translation (this is your **Subscriber**).
- **The Translator** stands in **Room B**. They have one earphone listening to Japanese (the **Subscriber callback**) and one microphone broadcasting English to Room C (the **Publisher callback**).

The Translator doesn't *create* the original speech nor *store* the English translation permanently. They **listen → translate → speak → repeat** in a continuous loop. This single human (one node) is *simultaneously* a listener and a speaker — exactly what a "mixed pub-sub node" does in ROS.

## 1.3 Why This Pattern Matters

In real robotics, almost no node is purely a sensor emitter or purely a motor consumer. A robot arm controller must:
1. **Subscribe** to joint state feedback (`/joint_states`),
2. Compute inverse kinematics,
3. **Publish** joint velocity commands (`/joint_velocity_command`).

An autonomous vehicle must:
1. **Subscribe** to LiDAR (`/scan`), Camera (`/camera/image`), and GPS (`/fix`),
2. Fuse these in a Kalman filter,
3. **Publish** a unified obstacle map (`/obstacle_map`) or steering command (`/cmd_vel`).

Without the mixed pub-sub pattern, every transformation would require *two separate nodes* communicating via an intermediate topic — wasteful in CPU, latency, and design complexity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metric:** A mixed pub-sub node introduces **only one network hop** internally (callback to publish). Splitting the same logic into two nodes would introduce **two network hops** plus serialisation/deserialisation overhead. For high-frequency control loops (≥ 100 Hz), this distinction is **critical**.

## 1.4 Foundational Vocabulary Required

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Node** | An executable process using ROS client libraries (`roscpp` / `rospy`) |
| **Master (`roscore`)** | The naming and registration service (deprecated in ROS 2 but still valid in ROS 1 KTU syllabus) |
| **Topic** | A named bus over which nodes exchange messages |
| **Message** | A strictly typed data structure (`.msg` files) |
| **Publisher** | Object that sends messages *to* a topic |
| **Subscriber** | Object that receives messages *from* a topic via a callback |
| **Callback** | Function executed automatically upon message arrival |
| **Queue Size** | Buffer length for asynchronous message delivery |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The lifecycle of a mixed Publisher-Subscriber node follows a **strict sequence**:

**Step 1 — Master Discovery & Node Initialization**
The node calls `ros::init()` (C++) or `rospy.init_node()` (Python). It registers with the ROS Master, receives the URI of other nodes, and establishes XML-RPC connections.

**Step 2 — Topic Subscription Registration**
The node creates a `Subscriber` object, binds it to a specific topic name and message type, and registers a callback function. The Master matches this request with any existing Publishers of that topic.

**Step 3 — Publisher Object Creation**
The node creates a `Publisher` object, binds it to an *output* topic name and message type, and registers it with the Master so future Subscribers can find it.

**Step 4 — Asynchronous Event Loop Entry**
The node calls `ros::spin()` (C++) or `rospy.spin()` (Python). Control is transferred to ROS's internal scheduler, which listens for incoming messages and invokes the appropriate callback.

**Step 5 — Callback Execution (The "Mixing" Moment)**
Upon receipt of an input message, the scheduler invokes the subscribed callback. Inside this callback, the node:
- Reads incoming data fields.
- Applies a transformation (mathematical, logical, or kinematic).
- Constructs an output message.
- Calls `publish()` on the Publisher object.

**Step 6 — Loop Continuation**
After callback completion, control returns to the event loop. ROS remains responsive to further incoming messages.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Engineering Insight:** The callback is the *only* place where the "mix" occurs. The Publisher object **cannot** fire until the callback is triggered. Thus, the output rate of the Publisher is **bounded above** by the input rate of the Subscriber. If you need the node to publish at a fixed frequency (e.g., a 50 Hz controller) *regardless* of input, you must combine this pattern with a `Timer` object (covered in advanced modules).

## 2.2 The Data Flow Contract

The contract of a mixed pub-sub node is:

$$f : \mathcal{M}_{in} \longrightarrow \mathcal{M}_{out}$$

where $\mathcal{M}_{in}$ is the set of all possible input messages and $\mathcal{M}_{out}$ is the set of all possible output messages. The function $f$ is the *callback logic*. For a deterministic system, $f$ must be **pure** with respect to the message content (no hidden state mutations that affect future outputs unpredictably), or it must carefully manage internal state using mutexes (`threading.Lock` in Python, `std::mutex` in C++).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### Table 2.3.A — Standard Message Types Encountered in Mixed Pub-Sub Nodes

| Message Type | Package | Key Fields | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| `geometry_msgs/Twist` | `geometry_msgs` | `linear.x, linear.y, linear.z, angular.x, angular.y, angular.z` | Mobile-base velocity commands (m/s, rad/s) |
| `geometry_msgs/Pose` | `geometry_msgs` | `position.{x,y,z}`, `orientation.{x,y,z,w}` | Robot end-effector / waypoint pose |
| `turtlesim/Pose` | `turtlesim` | `x, y, theta, linear_velocity, angular_velocity` | Turtle position feedback |
| `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` | `sensor_msgs` | `ranges[]`, `angle_min`, `angle_max`, `range_min`, `range_max` | LiDAR distance array |
| `sensor_msgs/Image` | `sensor_msgs` | `width, height, data[]`, `encoding` | Camera frames |
| `std_msgs/Float64` | `std_msgs` | `data` (float64) | Generic scalar telemetry |
| `nav_msgs/Odometry` | `nav_msgs` | `pose.pose`, `twist.twist`, header with frame_id | Wheel encoder fusion |

### Table 2.3.B — Core API Mapping (C++ vs Python)

| Operation | C++ (`roscpp`) | Python (`rospy`) |
|---|---|---|
| Initialize node | `ros::init(argc, argv, "node_name")` | `rospy.init_node("node_name", anonymous=True)` |
| Create Publisher | `nh.advertise<MsgType>("topic", queue_size)` | `pub = rospy.Publisher("topic", MsgType, queue_size)` |
| Create Subscriber | `nh.subscribe("topic", queue_size, callback)` | `sub = rospy.Subscriber("topic", MsgType, callback)` |
| Publish message | `pub.publish(msg)` | `pub.publish(msg)` |
| Enter event loop | `ros::spin()` | `rospy.spin()` |
| Get parameter | `nh.param<T>("name", default)` | `rospy.get_param("name", default)` |

### Table 2.3.C — Boundary Conditions & Constraints

| Constraint | Value / Rule |
|---|---|
| Default topic name resolution | Resolved against **namespace** of the node |
| Callback execution thread | Default: **single-threaded** in Python (GIL); multi-threaded `AsyncSpinner` in C++ |
| Message delivery guarantee | **Best-effort UDP-like** in ROS 1; reliable with `tcp_keepalive` |
| Queue overflow policy | **FIFO discard of oldest** when queue is full |
| Spin frequency bound | $\le$ input message rate (unless `ros::Timer` is added) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Mixed Pub-Sub |
|---|---|
| **Mobile Robotics** | Subscribe to `/odom`, compute velocity to reach `/goal`, publish `/cmd_vel` |
| **Manipulation** | Subscribe to `/joint_states`, run IK solver, publish `/joint_trajectory` |
| **Sensor Fusion** | Subscribe to `/imu`, `/gps`, publish fused `/odometry` |
| **Computer Vision** | Subscribe to `/camera/image_raw`, publish `/camera/edges` (Canny output) |
| **Swarm Robotics** | Subscribe to `/neighbor_pose`, publish `/broadcast_pose` |
| **Industrial Automation** | Subscribe to PLC sensor topic, publish robot command topic |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Code Derivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The canonical KTU Module 1 example for "Mixing Publishers and Subscribers" is the **Turtle-Following Controller** from the official `turtlesim` tutorial suite. The derivation below walks through every line of code with engineering rationale.

## 3.1 Problem Specification

**Goal:** Build a ROS node that makes Turtle 2 chase Turtle 1 across the `turtlesim` canvas.

**I/O Contract:**

- **Input Topic:** `/turtle1/pose` (type `turtlesim/Pose`)
- **Output Topic:** `/turtle2/cmd_vel` (type `geometry_msgs/Twist`)

**Control Law (Proportional Controller):** The follower turtle should drive toward the leader such that its linear velocity is proportional to the distance to the leader, and its angular velocity is proportional to the heading error.

## 3.2 Mathematical Derivation of the Control Law

Let the leader pose be $(x_1, y_1, \theta_1)$ and the follower pose be $(x_2, y_2, \theta_2)$.

**Distance Error:**

$$
e_d = \sqrt{(x_1 - x_2)^2 + (y_1 - y_2)^2}
$$

**Heading Error (angle from follower to leader):**

$$
\theta_{desired} = \arctan2(y_1 - y_2, \; x_1 - x_2)
$$

**Angular Error:**

$$
e_\theta = \theta_{desired} - \theta_2
$$

For wrap-around correction (since angles are modulo $2\pi$), we use:

$$
e_\theta = \mathrm{atan2}(\sin e_\theta, \cos e_\theta)
$$

**Control Output:**

$$
v_{linear} = K_p \cdot e_d
$$

$$
\omega_{angular} = K_\alpha \cdot e_\theta
$$

where $K_p$ and $K_\alpha$ are tunable gain constants. For simulation stability:

$$
K_p \le 1.0, \quad K_\alpha \le 4.0
$$

## 3.3 Code Derivation — Python (`rospy`)

### Line-by-Line Walkthrough

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import rospy
from turtlesim.msg import Pose
from geometry_msgs.msg import Twist
```

*Row 1:* Shebang line declaring the Python 3 interpreter for the ROS build system.
*Row 2:* Imports `rospy` — the Python client library.
*Row 3:* Imports the `Pose` message class from the `turtlesim` package.
*Row 4:* Imports the `Twist` message class — our output message type.

```python
# Global state variables to hold the latest leader pose
leader_x = 0.0
leader_y = 0.0
leader_theta = 0.0
```

*Row 5–8:* Global variables cache the most recent leader pose. Because the callback is invoked from a different execution context in C++ (and conceptually from an event scheduler in Python), shared state must be declared at module scope.

```python
def pose_callback(msg):
    global leader_x, leader_y, leader_theta
    leader_x = msg.x
    leader_y = msg.y
    leader_theta = msg.theta
```

*Row 9–13:* The **Subscriber callback**. The argument `msg` is an auto-instantiated `Pose` object populated by the ROS transport layer. We extract three scalar fields and store them globally.

```python
def control_loop(event):
    # Compute errors
    dx = leader_x - 2.0  # turtle2 is initialised near (2,2) but we use 0,0 frame approximation
    dy = leader_y - 2.0
    distance = (dx**2 + dy**2) ** 0.5
    desired_theta = __import__('math').atan2(dy, dx)
    angular_error = desired_theta  # assuming follower theta ~ 0 for simplicity

    cmd = Twist()
    cmd.linear.x = 1.5 * distance
    cmd.angular.z = 4.0 * angular_error

    pub.publish(cmd)
```

*Row 14–25:* The **Publisher trigger**, fired by a `Timer` at fixed rate (e.g., 10 Hz). The control law computes distance and heading errors, packages them into a `Twist` message, and publishes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** This is the *hybrid* variant — the callback updates *shared state* while a *timer* triggers the publish. Pure pub-sub mixing would publish *inside* the callback itself.

```python
rospy.init_node('turtle_follower', anonymous=True)
pub = rospy.Publisher('/turtle2/cmd_vel', Twist, queue_size=10)
rospy.Subscriber('/turtle1/pose', Pose, pose_callback)
rospy.Timer(rospy.Duration(0.1), control_loop)
rospy.spin()
```

*Row 26–30:* Node bootstrap. The order is critical: `init_node` must precede any `Publisher`, `Subscriber`, or `Timer` creation. `spin()` transfers control to ROS's scheduler indefinitely.

## 3.4 Code Derivation — C++ (`roscpp`)

```cpp
#include "ros/ros.h"
#include "turtlesim/Pose.h"
#include "geometry_msgs/Twist.h"

void poseCallback(const turtlesim::Pose::ConstPtr& msg) {
    ROS_INFO("Leader pose: x=%f, y=%f, theta=%f", msg->x, msg->y, msg->theta);
}

int main(int argc, char** argv) {
    ros::init(argc, argv, "turtle_subscriber");
    ros::NodeHandle nh;
    ros::Subscriber sub = nh.subscribe("/turtle1/pose", 10, poseCallback);
    ros::spin();
    return 0;
}
```

*Step 1–3:* Include headers — `ros/ros.h` is mandatory; the other two provide message types.
*Step 5–7:* Callback receives a `ConstPtr` (constant smart pointer) to the message — efficient zero-copy transfer.
*Step 10:* `init()` parses command-line remapping arguments (e.g., `__name:=`, `__log:=`).
*Step 11:* `NodeHandle` is the gateway to all ROS communication primitives.
*Step 12:* `subscribe()` registers the callback. The `10` is the queue size.
*Step 13:* `spin()` enters the event loop.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Mixed Pub-Sub

| Scenario | Use Mixed Pub-Sub? | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Pure data relay (no transformation) | ❌ No | Use `topic_tools/relay` or `roscore` proxy |
| Pure data logger | ❌ No | Use `rosbag` or dedicated logger node |
| Sensor data transformation | ✅ Yes | Latency-critical, single-process efficient |
| Closed-loop control (≤ 100 Hz) | ✅ Yes | Avoid network round-trip overhead |
| Asynchronous multi-sensor fusion | ✅ Yes | Synchronise inside one callback |
| Cross-machine communication | ⚠️ Careful | Mixed node couples tightly to single host |
| When output rate must differ from input | ✅ Yes + Timer | Hybrid callback + timer pattern |

## 3.6 Common Pitfall Trace — "Why Isn't My Publisher Working?"

**Symptom:** Subscriber callbacks fire, but the Publisher never sends messages.

**Root Cause Analysis:**

1. **Publisher not registered before spin?** → Master has no record → no peer connection.
2. **Output topic name mismatch?** → Subscribers cannot discover it.
3. **Message type mismatch?** → Type-based discovery fails silently.
4. **Callback never invoked?** → Input topic has no Publisher or wrong name.
5. **Latched topic required but `latch=true` not set?** → Late-joining subscribers miss one-shot data.
6. **Queue full and ROS_DROP?** → Check `ROSCONSOLE_FORMAT` for warnings.

**Diagnostic Command:**

```bash
rostopic echo /turtle2/cmd_vel
rostopic info /turtle2/cmd_vel
rosnode list
rqt_graph
```

    N->>M: Register Subscriber /turtle1/pose

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PERUT525]`

**Q: What is meant by a "mixed publisher and subscriber" node in ROS? Give one practical scenario where this pattern is mandatory.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *mixed publisher-subscriber node* is a single ROS node that **simultaneously subscribes to one or more input topics and publishes to one or more output topics**, performing an in-line transformation of data within the same process. **(1 mark)**

It is created by instantiating both a `rospy.Subscriber` / `ros::Subscriber` object and a `rospy.Publisher` / `ros::Publisher` object within the same node handle. The data flow is: **Input Topic → Subscriber Callback → Processing → Publisher → Output Topic**. **(1 mark)**

**Practical Scenario:** A *mobile robot follow-the-leader controller* where the node must subscribe to the leader's pose topic (`/leader/pose`) and publish velocity commands (`/follower/cmd_vel`) to drive a follower robot. This pattern is mandatory when the **output rate is causally tied to the input rate** and when network round-trip latency between two separate nodes is unacceptable for the control loop. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PERUT525]`

**Q: Compare the `queue_size` parameter of a Subscriber and a Publisher. What happens when the queue overflows?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `queue_size` parameter defines the **FIFO buffer length** for messages arriving at a Subscriber or messages awaiting transmission at a Publisher. **(1 mark)**

For a **Subscriber**, incoming messages are placed in the queue; the scheduler pops them one-by-one and dispatches them to the registered callback. For a **Publisher**, outgoing messages are queued before being serialised and sent over the TCP/UDP socket. **(1 mark)**

When the queue overflows (i.e., messages arrive faster than the callback can process them, or the network is slow), ROS applies the **FIFO drop-oldest policy** — the oldest unprocessed message is discarded to make room for the new one. This ensures real-time freshness but at the cost of **message loss**. Therefore, choosing `queue_size` is a **latency-vs-completeness trade-off**. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Weight Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PERUT525]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a ROS node that **mixes publishers and subscribers**. Describe how the ROS Master is involved in setting up the connections. Use the turtle follower example to illustrate.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write the complete **Python (`rospy`) program** for a mixed pub-sub node that subscribes to `/turtle1/pose` and publishes a `geometry_msgs/Twist` to `/turtle2/cmd_vel`. Use a **proportional control law** with $K_p = 1.5$ and $K_\alpha = 4.0$. Include the subscriber callback and publisher trigger inside a single callback.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper PERUT525]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **internal execution model** of a mixed pub-sub node. What are the possible threading issues that arise when multiple subscribers share state in the callback, and how can they be resolved in Python and C++?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design a ROS node (architecture + pseudo-code) that subscribes to a LiDAR topic (`sensor_msgs/LaserScan`) and publishes a filtered scan where ranges below **0.5 m** are clipped to `inf`. Show the topic names, message types, and the callback logic.

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Architecture & Master Role [7 Marks]

The architecture of a mixed publisher-subscriber node comprises three logical layers: **Input Reception Layer** (Subscribers), **Processing Layer** (callback logic), and **Output Emission Layer** (Publishers). **(2 marks)**

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Topic /turtle1/pose] --> B[Subscriber]
    B --> C[Callback Function]
    C --> D[Processing Logic]
    D --> E[Publisher]
    E --> F[Output Topic /turtle2/cmd_vel]
    M[ROS Master] -. registers .-> B
    M -. registers .-> E
    M -. connects .-> A
    M -. connects .-> F
```

**Master Role (3 marks):**

1. Upon `ros::init()` / `rospy.init_node()`, the node registers its **URI** with the Master. **(0.5 mark)**
2. When the node creates the `Subscriber` object, the Master records the subscription request for `/turtle1/pose`. **(0.5 mark)**
3. When the node creates the `Publisher` object, the Master records the advertisement for `/turtle2/cmd_vel`. **(0.5 mark)**
4. The Master **matches** the subscriber with any existing publisher of the same topic and vice-versa, returning URIs. **(0.5 mark)**
5. Subsequent communication is **direct node-to-node TCP/UDP** — the Master is *not* in the data path. **(0.5 mark)**
6. The Master also tracks **parameter server** values (e.g., `$K_p$, $K_\alpha`). **(0.5 mark)**

**Turtle Follower Illustration (2 marks):** Turtle1 publishes its pose continuously. The mixed node receives the pose, computes the heading error $\theta_e = \arctan2(y_1 - y_2, x_1 - x_2) - \theta_2$, and publishes a `Twist` with $v = K_p \cdot \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$ and $\omega = K_\alpha \cdot \theta_e$. Turtle2's velocity controller consumes this `Twist`.

---

#### Part (b) — Python Program [7 Marks]

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import rospy
import math
from turtlesim.msg import Pose
from geometry_msgs.msg import Twist

def pose_callback(msg):
    # ---- Sub -> Process -> Pub inside one callback ----
    cmd = Twist()

    # Follower is Turtle2; in this simplified version we treat its pose as
    # the origin of the local frame (0, 0, 0). Real systems read /turtle2/pose too.
    dx = msg.x - 0.0
    dy = msg.y - 0.0
    distance = math.sqrt(dx**2 + dy**2)
    desired_theta = math.atan2(dy, dx)
    # Assumed follower_theta = 0 for illustrative simplicity
    angular_error = desired_theta

    Kp = 1.5
    Ka = 4.0

    cmd.linear.x = Kp * distance
    cmd.angular.z = Ka * angular_error

    pub.publish(cmd)
    rospy.loginfo("Publishing Twist: v=%.3f, w=%.3f", cmd.linear.x, cmd.angular.z)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    rospy.init_node('turtle_mixed_node', anonymous=True)
    pub = rospy.Publisher('/turtle2/cmd_vel', Twist, queue_size=10)
    rospy.Subscriber('/turtle1/pose', Pose, pose_callback)
    rospy.spin()
```

**Mark Allocation Breakdown:**

| Component | Marks |
|---|---|
| Correct `#!/usr/bin/env python3` + imports | 1 |
| `rospy.init_node` + `Publisher` + `Subscriber` setup | 2 |
| Callback function definition with msg extraction | 1 |
| Control law math (`atan2`, distance, gains) | 2 |
| `pub.publish(cmd)` + `rospy.spin()` | 1 |
| **Total** | **7** |

---

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Internal Execution & Threading [7 Marks]

**Internal Execution Model (4 marks):**

When a mixed pub-sub node calls `spin()`, control transfers to the **ROS callback queue scheduler**. This scheduler blocks on the network socket, receives serialised messages, deserialises them, and dispatches them to the registered callback in **FIFO order** on a **single thread by default** (Python's Global Interpreter Lock enforces this naturally; in C++, the default `ros::spin()` is also single-threaded).

**Threading Issues (2 marks):**

1. **Shared Mutable State Race Condition:** If two subscribers share a global variable (e.g., `latest_pose`) and one callback writes while another reads, data corruption or inconsistent reads can occur in multi-threaded spinners.
2. **Publisher Re-entrancy:** Calling `publish()` from within a callback that itself was triggered by a message on the same topic can cause **feedback loops**.

**Resolution (1 mark):**

- **Python:** Use `threading.Lock()` around shared state; consider `rospy.rospy.Duration` and `rospy.wait_for_message` for synchronous needs.
- **C++:** Use `std::mutex` or `boost::mutex`; switch to `ros::AsyncSpinner(N)` for true multi-threaded dispatch; for periodic publishes independent of input, use `ros::Timer`.

---

#### Part (b) — LiDAR Filter Node [7 Marks]

**Architecture (2 marks):**

- **Input Topic:** `/scan` of type `sensor_msgs/LaserScan`
- **Output Topic:** `/scan_filtered` of type `sensor_msgs/LaserScan`
- **Callback Logic:** For each `ranges[i]`, if `ranges[i] < 0.5`, set `ranges[i] = float('inf')`.

**Pseudo-code / Python Implementation (5 marks):**

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import rospy
import math
from sensor_msgs.msg import LaserScan

MIN_RANGE = 0.5

def scan_callback(msg):
    filtered = LaserScan()
    filtered.header = msg.header
    filtered.angle_min = msg.angle_min
    filtered.angle_max = msg.angle_max
    filtered.angle_increment = msg.angle_increment
    filtered.time_increment = msg.time_increment
    filtered.scan_time = msg.scan_time
    filtered.range_min = msg.range_min
    filtered.range_max = msg.range_max

    filtered.ranges = [
        r if r >= MIN_RANGE else float('inf')
        for r in msg.ranges
    ]
    filtered.intensities = msg.intensities

    pub.publish(filtered)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    rospy.init_node('lidar_filter_node', anonymous=True)
    pub = rospy.Publisher('/scan_filtered', LaserScan, queue_size=10)
    rospy.Subscriber('/scan', LaserScan, scan_callback)
    rospy.spin()
```

**Mark Allocation:**

| Component | Marks |
|---|---|
| Correct topic names and message types | 1 |
| Publisher + Subscriber setup | 1 |
| Copying header and metadata fields | 1 |
| List comprehension filtering logic | 2 |
| Publishing and spinning | 1 |
| **Total** | **7** |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `queue_size` argument** → Compilation/runtime error. Examiners deduct **1 mark** if unspecified.
> 2. **Publishing *before* `spin()`** → Message is lost because no subscriber has connected yet. Use **latched topics** for one-shot initial data.
> 3. **Mixing `ros::Rate` loop with `ros::spin()`** → Conflict in control flow; only one event loop can own the main thread.
> 4. **Using global variables without locks** in C++ multi-threaded spinner → Race condition → **intermittent failure**, which examiners explicitly test.
> 5. **Confusing `ros::NodeHandle` and `ros::NodeHandle("~")`** → The latter adds a private namespace `~`. Examiners look for this in 7-mark code questions.
> 6. **Writing `import geometry_msgs.msg` instead of `from geometry_msgs.msg import Twist`** → Import error. **0 marks** for code if it doesn't run.
> 7. **Not specifying message type explicitly in C++ template** → `nh.advertise<Twist>(...)` — missing `<Twist>` is a syntax error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Mixed pub-sub = one node, both roles.** It is the *default* pattern in real robots, not an exception.
- **Three mandatory creations** before `spin()`: `init_node`, `Publisher`, `Subscriber`. Order matters.
- **Callback = mixing point.** Input arrives → callback fires → output published. No callback, no publish.
- **`queue_size` is mandatory** in modern ROS (since `indigo`/`kinetic`). Omitting it triggers a deprecation warning and lost marks in exams.
- **Threading default is single-threaded.** Plan ahead if you need concurrency — use `AsyncSpinner` (C++) or `MultiThreadedSpinner`.
- **The Master is only for discovery, not data relay.** Data flows peer-to-peer after handshake.
- **Output rate ≤ input rate** unless you use a `Timer` for decoupled periodic publishing.
- **Publish by reference, subscribe by `ConstPtr`** in C++ for zero-copy efficiency.
- **Global state is acceptable in single-threaded spin**, but wrap with locks the moment you go multi-threaded.
- **For turtle follower:** $v = K_p \cdot d$, $\omega = K_\alpha \cdot \theta_e$, with `atan2` for quadrant-safe heading.
- **Filter pattern (LiDAR clip):** iterate `msg.ranges`, replace below-threshold values with `inf`, republish as a new `LaserScan` message preserving all metadata fields.
- **Always verify with `rqt_graph` and `rostopic info`** before debugging deeper — 80% of pub-sub issues are misnamed topics.
- **For KTU 14-mark questions**, examiners expect: block diagram + control law math + complete runnable code + explanation of Master role + threading caveats.
- **For KTU 3-mark questions**, focus on **definition + scenario + one technical detail** (queue_size, threading, or Master role).
- **Module 1 closing note:** Mastering mixed pub-sub sets you up for Module 2's **Services**, Module 3's **TF (Transforms)**, and Module 4's **ActionLib** — all of which are extensions of this fundamental pub-sub + callback paradigm.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Defining a Service
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT525 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS) > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Defining a Service ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the Robot Operating System (ROS), a **Service** is a synchronous, request-response communication paradigm used for direct, one-to-one interaction between nodes. Unlike the asynchronous, broadcast nature of Topics, a service follows a strict **Client-Server architecture** where a *Service Client* node sends a request and blocks until the *Service Server* node returns a response. A Service in ROS is formally **defined** by a pair of messages bundled into a Service Description file with the `.srv` extension. This file declares: - The **Request Message**: Data sent from the client to the server. - The **Response Message**: Data returned from the server to the client. These two are visually separated by a delimiter line of exactly three hyphens (`---`). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** "Defining a Service" refers not merely to its conceptual use, but to the procedural creation of the `.srv` file and the required modifications to `CMakeLists.txt` and `package.xml` to make ROS compile the new service definition into Python/C++ source code. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **restaurant**: - The **Menu** is your `.srv` file — it formally declares what you can ask for (request) and what you will receive (response). - The **Kitchen** is the *Service Server* — it waits for orders, processes them, and sends the dish back. - The **Customer (Waiter)** is the *Service Client* — it places a specific request, waits (blocks), and only continues once the dish is delivered. Unlike a radio broadcast (Topics), the customer does not just shout into the air; they wait at the counter until the order is fulfilled. This blocking, deterministic behavior makes services ideal for tasks like triggering a robot's motion, querying a sensor once, or performing a calculation. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** Topics use a **many-to-many, anonymous** publish-subscribe model. Services use a **one-to-one, blocking, identified** request-reply model. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 The Service Description File (`.srv`) — Structure Breakdown A service description is a plain text file placed inside the `srv/` subdirectory of a ROS package. It has a rigid, two-part structure separated by a delimiter. **Step-by-step breakdown of a `.srv` file:** - **Line Group 1 (Above `---`) — The Request:** Each line specifies one field of the request message using the format `type field_name`. The order and types here dictate what the client must send. - **The Separator Line:** A single line containing exactly `---`. This is the syntactic boundary between request and response. - **Line Group 2 (Below `---`) — The Response:** Each line specifies one field of the response message using the same `type field_name` format. If the service returns nothing of consequence, it is conventional to return a single `string` (often a success message) or an empty response. ### 2.2 Allowed Field Types in `.srv` Files ROS enforces a strict, fixed set of primitive and standard types for fields within a `.srv` file. They are divided into two categories based on the underlying MD5 checksum and serialization rules: - **Primitive Types:** `bool`, `int8`, `int16`, `int32`, `int64`, `uint8`, `uint16`, `uint32`, `uint64`, `float32`, `float64`, `string`, `time`, `duration`, `byte`, `char` - **Standard Message Types (from `std_msgs` package):** `Empty`, `Header`, `ColorRGBA`, `Int8MultiArray`, etc. - **User-defined Types:** Any `.msg` file defined in the same package or a dependency. > [!IMPORTANT] > Constants in a `.srv` file are not allowed — unlike C++. The message structure is purely a data contract. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet | Aspect | Requirement / Standard | KTU Exam Weightage | |---|---|---| | File Extension | `.srv` | High | | File Location | `/srv/MyService.srv` | High | | Request/Response Separator | A line containing exactly `---` | Very High | | Type Field Encoding | `type fieldname` (space separated) | High | | CMakeLists.txt Addition | `add_service_files(FILES MyService.srv)` | Very High | | Generate Messages Macro | `generate_messages(DEPENDENCIES std_msgs ...)` | Very High | | `package.xml` (Build Dep) | `message_generation` | High | | `package.xml` (Exec Dep) | `message_runtime` | High | | Build Command | `catkin_make` or `catkin build` | Medium | | Communication Pattern | Synchronous Request-Reply | High | | Generated Language Headers | `MyService.h`, `MyServiceRequest.h`, `MyServiceResponse.h` (C++); Python module `srv` | Medium | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In industrial robotics, services are the workhorse for **deterministic, transactional** operations: - **Querying robot state**: "Where is the gripper right now?" → `GetJointState.srv` - **Triggering actions**: "Begin the welding sequence." → `TriggerWeld.srv` - **Motion primitives**: `MoveToPose.srv` (request: target pose; response: success bool). - **Sensor calibration**: "Compute the kinematic offset for this camera." → `CalibrateCamera.srv` Services guarantee that the requester knows exactly when the task finished, which is critical in safety-critical robotic pipelines where the next move depends on the success of the previous one. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Procedure for Defining a Service (Analytical Matrix) This section details the exhaustive, step-by-step procedure a KTU student must follow to define a custom ROS service from scratch. We will model the service on the canonical "Add Two Ints" example from the official ROS tutorials. ### Step 3.1 — Create the Package First, navigate to the source space of your catkin workspace and create a new package. We assume the student is using the `catkin_make` build system (standard in older KTU lab setups). **Step 3.1.1:** Open a terminal and source your ROS environment. **Step 3.1.2:** Create the package with `roscpp`, `rospy`, and `std_msgs` as dependencies. ```bash cd ~/catkin_ws/src catkin_create_pkg beginner_tutorials rospy roscpp std_msgs ``` ### Step 3.2 — Create the `.srv` File **Step 3.2.1:** Create the `srv` subdirectory inside the newly created package. **Step 3.2.2:** Create the actual `.srv` file. For our canonical example, name it `AddTwoInts.srv`. The complete content of the file `AddTwoInts.srv` is: ``` int64 a int64 b --- int64 sum ``` **Explanation of the file:** - `int64 a` → Request field 1: a 64-bit signed integer. - `int64 b` → Request field 2: a 64-bit signed integer. - `---` → Mandatory delimiter line. - `int64 sum` → Response field: the arithmetic sum returned by the server. ### Step 3.3 — Modify `package.xml` Two `` tags (or one `` for newer formats) must be added so the build system knows to invoke the message generator. Find the existing `` block in `package.xml` and ensure these two lines are present: ```xml message_generation message_runtime ``` ### Step 3.4 — Modify `CMakeLists.txt` This is the most exam-relevant step. **Three** changes are required in `CMakeLists.txt`: **Step 3.4.1:** Find the `find_package` call and ensure `message_generation` is added. The line should now read: ```cmake find_package(catkin REQUIRED COMPONENTS roscpp rospy std_msgs message_generation ) ``` **Step 3.4.2:** Declare any dependencies your `.srv` file uses. Since our `AddTwoInts.srv` only uses standard `int64`, this step is technically optional here, but for services using custom messages, you would add: ```cmake add_message_files(FILES MyOtherMessage.msg) add_service_files(FILES AddTwoInts.srv) generate_messages(DEPENDENCIES std_msgs) ``` **Step 3.4.3:** Ensure the `CATKIN_DEPENDS` macro includes `message_runtime`: ```cmake catkin_package( CATKIN_DEPENDS rospy roscpp std_msgs message_runtime ) ``` ### Step 3.5 — Build the Package Navigate to the workspace root and compile. The `message_generation` macros will scan the `srv/` folder and generate the language-specific headers. ```bash cd ~/catkin_ws catkin_make ``` ### Step 3.6 — Mathematical Representation of the Data Contract The service can be formally represented as a contract tuple: $$\mathcal{S} = (R, P, \Phi)$$ where: - $\mathcal{S}$ is the defined Service. - $R = \{ a, b \vert a, b \in \mathbb{Z}, \vert a \vert < 2^{63}, \vert b \vert < 2^{63} \}$ is the Request domain. - $P = \{ s \vert s \in \mathbb{Z}, \vert s \vert < 2^{63} \}$ is the Response domain. - $\Phi : R \rightarrow P$ is the server-implemented function such that $\Phi(a,b) = a + b$. The **boundary conditions** are dictated by the type system — here, the `int64` constraint imposes a hard limit: $$-2^{63} \leq a + b \leq 2^{63} - 1$$ Violations result in overflow, which the client should handle via input validation before invocation. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Service Definition and Compilation Architecture The following Mermaid flowchart maps the entire lifecycle of "Defining a Service" — from authoring the `.srv` file to the build-time generation of language-specific classes. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Developer Authors MyService.srv"] --> B["Place File in /srv Subdirectory"] B --> C{"Edit package.xml"} C --> C1["Add message_generation Build Dep"] C --> C2["Add message_runtime Exec Dep"] B --> D{"Edit CMakeLists.txt"} D --> D1["Add message_generation to find_package"] D --> D2["Call add_service_files"] D --> D3["Call generate_messages"] C1 --> E["Run catkin_make in Workspace Root"] C2 --> E D1 --> E D2 --> E D3 --> E E --> F["Message Generator Scans /srv"] F --> G["Generates C++ Headers"] F --> H["Generates Python srv Module"] G --> I["Ready for C++ Server Client Coding"] H --> J["Ready for Python Server Client Coding"] ``` ### 4.2 Runtime Service Interaction Diagram This diagram illustrates the synchronous, blocking runtime interaction between a Service Client and a Service Server once the service has been defined and compiled. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client as Service Client Node participant Master as ROS Master participant Server as Service Server Node Client->>Master: advertiseService lookup Master-->>Client: returns Server URI Client->>Server: Send Request Message Note over Client: Client BLOCKS here Server->>Server: Execute Callback Function Server-->>Client: Return Response Message Note over Client: Client resumes execution ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the `.srv` File ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FileBlock[MyService.srv File] R["REQUEST BLOCK
Type FieldName
Type FieldName"] S["--- SEPARATOR ---"] Resp["RESPONSE BLOCK
Type FieldName
Type FieldName"] end R --> S S --> Resp ``` > [!NOTE] > The Mermaid diagrams above strictly avoid reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`) as standalone node names and use only alphanumeric identifiers with quoted, clean text labels. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** What is a ROS service? List the three main steps involved in defining a custom service in ROS. **Model Answer:** A ROS service is a synchronous, request-response communication mechanism used between nodes. It is defined by a `.srv` file that specifies the request and response message structures, separated by a line containing `---`. The three main steps in defining a custom service are: 1. Creating the `.srv` file inside the `srv/` directory of the ROS package. 2. Modifying `package.xml` to add `message_generation` and `message_runtime` dependencies. 3. Modifying `CMakeLists.txt` to invoke `add_service_files()` and `generate_messages()`, followed by building the package with `catkin_make`. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Explain the role of the `---` line in a `.srv` file. What would happen if it were omitted? **Model Answer:** The `---` line acts as the syntactic delimiter separating the **request** message fields (above) from the **response** message fields (below) in a `.srv` file. It tells the ROS message generator how to partition the data contract into the request and response message classes. If the `---` line were omitted, the message generator would be unable to parse the file correctly. The build would fail with a syntax or schema error, and no language-specific headers (C++ or Python) would be generated, rendering the service unusable. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly lose 3–4 marks in Part B questions by (1) forgetting the exact macro names in `CMakeLists.txt` (`add_service_files` vs `add_message_files`), (2) omitting `message_runtime` from `package.xml`, and (3) failing to mention the need to rebuild (`catkin_make`) after defining the service. Always spell out the macro names and quote the exact CMake invocations. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model]** **(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a ROS service. Differentiate between a service and a topic. (7 Marks) **(b)** Write a step-by-step procedure to define a custom ROS service called `TriggerGripper.srv` that takes a `bool close_gripper` as request and returns a `string status_message` as response. Include all necessary modifications to `CMakeLists.txt` and `package.xml`. (7 Marks) **Model Solution for (a):** The ROS service follows a strict **Client-Server architecture**: 1. **Service Definition (`.srv` file)**: Acts as the contract — declares the request and response message types. 2. **Service Server Node**: A node that advertises the service using `advertiseService()`. It contains a callback that processes the request and returns the response. 3. **Service Client Node**: A node that calls the service using `call()` or `call_async()`. It blocks (in the synchronous case) until the response arrives. 4. **ROS Master**: Facilitates the initial lookup so the client discovers the server's URI. **Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart LR Master["ROS Master"] Server["Service Server
(advertiseService)"] Client["Service Client
(call / call_async)"] Master -.Lookup.-> Client Client -- Request --> Server Server -- Response --> Client ``` **Differentiation Table:** | Aspect | Topic | Service | |---|---|---| | Communication Pattern | Publish-Subscribe (many-to-many) | Request-Response (one-to-one) | | Synchrony | Asynchronous | Synchronous (blocking) | | Direction | One-way | Bi-directional (single transaction) | | Use Case | Continuous data streams (e.g., LIDAR) | Discrete, transactional tasks (e.g., trigger, query) | | Discovery | Anonymous, by topic name | Identified by service name + URI | | Continuation | Publisher never waits | Client blocks until response | **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1 — Create the `.srv` file:** Create a file at `~/catkin_ws/src/my_robot_pkg/srv/TriggerGripper.srv` with the following content: ``` bool close_gripper --- string status_message ``` **Step 2 — Modify `package.xml`:** Add the following lines within the `` block: ```xml message_generation message_runtime ``` **Step 3 — Modify `CMakeLists.txt`:** Ensure `find_package` includes `message_generation`: ```cmake find_package(catkin REQUIRED COMPONENTS roscpp rospy std_msgs message_generation ) ``` Add the service file and generate the messages: ```cmake add_service_files(FILES TriggerGripper.srv) generate_messages(DEPENDENCIES std_msgs) ``` Update `catkin_package` macro: ```cmake catkin_package(CATKIN_DEPENDS roscpp rospy std_msgs message_runtime) ``` **Step 4 — Build the package:** ```bash cd ~/catkin_ws && catkin_make ``` Upon successful build, the C++ headers `TriggerGripper.h`, `TriggerGripperRequest.h`, and `TriggerGripperResponse.h` will be available in the `devel/include` directory, and the Python module `TriggerGripper` will be importable from `my_robot_pkg.srv`. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Alternative]** **(a)** Explain the structure of a `.srv` file with a suitable example. What are the primitive types allowed in a service description? (7 Marks) **(b)** Compare the synchronous (`call`) and asynchronous (`call_async`) service invocation patterns in ROS. When would you choose one over the other? (7 Marks) **Model Solution for (a):** **Structure of a `.srv` file:** A `.srv` file is a plain text file with two sections separated by a line containing exactly `---`. The section above `---` defines the request, and the section below defines the response. **Example — `ComputeArea.srv`:** ``` float64 radius --- float64 area ``` Here, the client sends a single `float64 radius` and receives back the computed `float64 area` from the server. **Primitive types allowed in service descriptions include:** - Integer variants: `int8`, `int16`, `int32`, `int64` (signed) and `uint8`, `uint16`, `uint32`, `uint64` (unsigned) - Floating-point variants: `float32`, `float64` - Boolean: `bool` - Text: `string` - Temporal: `time`, `duration` - Raw byte: `byte`, `char` - Special standard types from `std_msgs`: `Header`, `Empty`, `ColorRGBA`, etc. In addition, any user-defined `.msg` type from a dependent ROS package may be used. **Model Solution for (b):** **Synchronous Invocation (`call`):** In synchronous mode, the client calls the service and **blocks** the calling thread until the response is received or a timeout expires. The return value of `call()` is the response object itself. - **Pros:** Simple, intuitive control flow; guarantees response before next line executes. - **Cons:** Halts all other processing in that node; inefficient if response is delayed. **Asynchronous Invocation (`call_async`):** In asynchronous mode, the client sends the request and immediately returns a `Future` object. The node continues executing other tasks (e.g., processing sensor data). The response is later retrieved by polling or attaching a callback. - **Pros:** Non-blocking; ideal for concurrent, real-time nodes. - **Cons:** More complex logic; requires careful handling of unfulfilled futures. **When to choose:** - Choose `call` when the next action strictly depends on the service result (e.g., "compute path, then move"). - Choose `call_async` when the node must remain responsive while waiting (e.g., a robot controller that must keep reading joint states while a calibration service runs in the background). --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - A **Service** in ROS is defined by a `.srv` file containing **two message blocks** separated by a `---` line. - Always place `.srv` files inside the `srv/` subdirectory of a ROS package. - Three mandatory build modifications: `package.xml` (`message_generation`, `message_runtime`), `CMakeLists.txt` (`add_service_files`, `generate_messages`), and the `catkin_make` invocation. - The data contract follows the format `type fieldname` — never use constants or default values inside a `.srv` file. - Services are **synchronous by default**; the client blocks until the response is returned or a timeout occurs. - Use the **service** for discrete, transactional, request-reply tasks; use **topics** for continuous, streaming, anonymous data flow. - After defining the service, you must rebuild the workspace — otherwise the new headers/modules will not be available to your server/client code. - Common error: typo in macro name (e.g., `add_message_files` instead of `add_service_files`) — this will fail silently or produce confusing build errors. - Always source the workspace (`source devel/setup.bash`) before running nodes that depend on your newly defined service. ---

topics.title: Implementing a Service
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT525 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS)
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Implementing a Service

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the Robot Operating System (ROS), a **Service** is a strict, synchronous, one-to-one communication paradigm used for **Remote Procedure Calls (RPC)** between nodes. Unlike the asynchronous, many-to-many Topic/Publish-Subscribe model, a service interaction follows a strict **Request-Response** handshake: a *Service Client* node sends a structured *Request* message, blocks its execution thread, and waits until a *Service Server* node processes the request and returns a single structured *Response* message.

The service interface is defined by a service description file (`.srv`), which declares the request field(s) and the response field(s) separated by a line of three dashes (`---`). When compiled using `catkin_make` (ROS 1) or `colcon build` (ROS 2), this file generates native language message classes for the underlying **Simple Object Access Protocol (SOAP)** over TCP/IP transport.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**: A service in ROS is a synchronous, bidirectional, one-time communication mechanism used when a node requires an immediate computational result from another node (e.g., "compute inverse kinematics", "trigger a hardware reset", "spawn a robot in Gazebo").

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

To build intuition, think of a restaurant:

- **Topic (Publisher/Subscriber)** is like the radio in the restaurant playing background music. Anyone can listen, the music plays continuously, and a chef doesn't stop cooking just because no one is listening. It is *fire-and-forget*.
- **Service (Client/Server)** is like a **waiter taking a specific customer order**. The customer (Client) makes a specific request ("Paneer Butter Masala, less spicy"). The waiter walks to the kitchen (Server), waits for the dish to be prepared (computation), and returns with the dish (Response) only when it is ready. The customer cannot do anything else until the waiter returns. This is a **blocking, request-response transaction**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology and Metrics

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **`.srv` file** | Service description file specifying request and response structure |
| **Service Server** | Node that provides the service; it must register a callback function |
| **Service Client** | Node that calls the service and waits for the response |
| **Synchronous Call** | Client blocks until response arrives (default in `rospy`) |
| **Asynchronous Call** | Client fires request without blocking (supported in `roscpp` and `rospy` via `call_async()`) |
| **Service Namespace** | The naming path used to register and look up the service (e.g., `/turtle1/teleport_absolute`) |
| **`advertiseService()`** | Function used to register a service server in a node |
| **`waitForService()`** | Client-side blocking call to ensure the server is alive before sending a request |

> [!NOTE]
> Standard ROS services communicate over **TCP/IP** by default and use the **XML-RPC / ROSRPC** protocol. Standard latency targets in ROS 1 local networks range from **0.1 ms to 5 ms** for simple services, while complex services (e.g., SLAM localization) can take up to **30 seconds**.

## 1.4 Why Implement a Service?

In a robotics stack, services are preferred over topics when:

1. The robot requires an **immediate decision** (e.g., "Should I turn left or right?").
2. The operation must complete **exactly once** per trigger (e.g., calibration).
3. The result must be **acknowledged** before proceeding (e.g., mission abort confirmation).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Lifecycle of a ROS Service

The implementation of a ROS service follows a strict 5-stage pipeline:

### Stage 1 — Service Definition (`.srv`)
The contract is declared in a plain text file inside the `srv/` folder of a package.
**Example**: `AddTwoInts.srv`

```
int64 a
int64 b
---
int64 sum
```

The compiler (`srv_to_generate`) splits the file at the `---` delimiter. Fields above are the **Request**, fields below are the **Response**.

### Stage 2 — Build System Configuration
The package's `package.xml` must declare:

```xml
<build_depend>message_generation</build_depend>
<exec_depend>message_runtime</exec_depend>
```

The `CMakeLists.txt` must include:

```cmake
find_package(catkin REQUIRED COMPONENTS rospy std_msgs message_generation)
add_service_files(FILES AddTwoInts.srv)
generate_messages(DEPENDENCIES std_msgs)
```

### Stage 3 — Code Generation
Running `catkin_make` invokes the Python `genmsg` / `gencpp` toolchains. This produces:
- For Python: `AddTwoInts.py` containing `AddTwoIntsRequest`, `AddTwoIntsResponse` classes.
- For C++: `AddTwoInts.h` / `AddTwoInts.cpp` headers.

### Stage 4 — Server Implementation
The Server node advertises the service using a callback function. The callback signature MUST match the response structure.

### Stage 5 — Client Implementation
The Client node first waits for the service to exist, then sends the request and parses the response.

## 2.2 Mathematical Model of Service Latency

The total round-trip latency $T_{total}$ of a ROS service is governed by:

$$
T_{total} = T_{lookup} + T_{marshal} + T_{network} + T_{compute} + T_{demarshal}
$$

Where:

- $T_{lookup}$ = Time taken by the ROS Master to resolve the service name to a URI ($\approx 0.1$ ms on local Master).
- $T_{marshal}$ = Time to serialize Python objects into ROS message wire format.
- $T_{network}$ = TCP/IP transmission delay ($RTT \approx 0.05$ ms on LAN).
- $T_{compute}$ = Time the server callback takes to execute the business logic.
- $T_{demarshal}$ = Time to deserialize the response in the client.

> [!TIP]
> In ROS 2 (DDS-based), $T_{lookup}$ is effectively **0 ms** because there is no central Master; discovery is peer-to-peer via the Real-Time Publish-Subscribe (RTPS) protocol.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Service APIs

### Python (`rospy`) API Reference

| API Function | Purpose | Module |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `rospy.Service(name, type, handler)` | Registers a service server | `rospy` |
| `rospy.ServiceProxy(name, type)` | Creates a service client | `rospy` |
| `proxy.wait_for_service(timeout)` | Blocks until server is alive | `rospy` |
| `proxy(request_obj)` | Synchronous call | `rospy` |
| `ServiceException` | Raised on transport failure | `rospy.service` |

### C++ (`roscpp`) API Reference

| API Function | Purpose | Header |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `advertiseService(name, handler)` | Registers a service server | `ros/service_server.h` |
| `serviceClient<ServiceType>(name)` | Creates a service client | `ros/service_client.h` |
| `client.waitForExistence()` | Blocks until server is alive | `ros/service_client.h` |
| `client.call(request, response)` | Synchronous call | `ros/service_client.h` |
| `asyncSendRequest(req, cb)` | Asynchronous call | `ros/service_client.h` |

### Service Resolution Semantics

| Concept | ROS 1 Behavior | ROS 2 Behavior |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Master / Discovery** | Central ROS Master (`roscore`) | Decentralized DDS Discovery |
| **Lookup Time** | $> 0$ ms (Master dependent) | $\approx 0$ ms (peer-to-peer) |
| **Persistent Connections** | Yes (XML-RPC) | Yes (DDS RTPS over UDP) |
| **Service Persistence** | Persists for the server's lifetime | Persists for the server's lifetime |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**: Services are foundational in industrial robotics for *setup* and *safety-critical* operations. Example: A vision system publishes images on a **topic** at 30 Hz (continuous), but when the robot arm needs an exact grasp pose, it makes a **service call** to a neural network inference server.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Implementation Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Implementation of an `AddTwoInts` Service

This walkthrough implements the canonical "Add Two Ints" service, a standard reference for any ROS service implementation tutorial.

### Step A — Create the Package Skeleton

Open a terminal and source your ROS environment:

```bash
source /opt/ros/<distro>/setup.bash
```

Replace `<distro>` with your installed ROS version (`noetic`, `melodic`, or `foxy`). Next, navigate to the `src` directory of your workspace and create a package:

```bash
cd ~/catkin_ws/src
catkin_create_pkg beginner_tutorials rospy roscpp std_msgs
```

This command produces a folder named `beginner_tutorials` containing `package.xml` and `CMakeLists.txt`.

### Step B — Define the `.srv` File

Create a `srv` subdirectory:

```bash
mkdir srv
cd srv
touch AddTwoInts.srv
```

Edit `AddTwoInts.srv` and type the following content:

```
int64 a
int64 b
---
int64 sum
```

This declares that the **Request** carries two 64-bit integers `a` and `b`, and the **Response** returns one 64-bit integer `sum`.

### Step C — Modify `package.xml`

Open `package.xml` and ensure these two lines exist:

```xml
<build_depend>message_generation</build_depend>
<exec_depend>message_runtime</exec_depend>
```

These dependencies ensure the message generator runs at build time and the generated Python/C++ message modules are present at runtime.

### Step D — Modify `CMakeLists.txt`

Append the following block:

```cmake
find_package(catkin REQUIRED COMPONENTS rospy roscpp std_msgs message_generation)

add_service_files(FILES AddTwoInts.srv)

generate_messages(DEPENDENCIES std_msgs)
```

The `add_service_files` macro registers the `.srv` files; `generate_messages` invokes the code generator.

### Step E — Build the Workspace

Return to the workspace root and compile:

```bash
cd ~/catkin_ws
catkin_make
source devel/setup.bash
```

You can verify the generated Python module exists:

```bash
rosmsg show beginner_tutorials/AddTwoInts
```

Expected output:

```
int64 a
int64 b
---
int64 sum
```

### Step F — Write the Server Node (Python)

Create `scripts/add_two_ints_server.py`:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3

from __future__ import print_function
import rospy
from beginner_tutorials.srv import AddTwoInts, AddTwoIntsResponse

def handle_add_two_ints(req):
    """Callback executed when a request arrives."""
    rospy.loginfo("Returning [%s + %s = %s]" % (req.a, req.b, req.a + req.b))
    return AddTwoIntsResponse(req.a + req.b)

def add_two_ints_server():
    rospy.init_node('add_two_ints_server')
    s = rospy.Service('add_two_ints', AddTwoInts, handle_add_two_ints)
    rospy.loginfo("Ready to add two ints.")
    rospy.spin()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    add_two_ints_server()
```

Make it executable:

```bash
chmod +x scripts/add_two_ints_server.py
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight**: The `handle_add_two_ints` callback returns a `Response` object. In Python, ROS automatically constructs the response from the returned tuple or dict if the keys match the `.srv` field names. Returning an `AddTwoIntsResponse(sum=...)` object is the most explicit and recommended pattern.

### Step G — Write the Client Node (Python)

Create `scripts/add_two_ints_client.py`:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3

from __future__ import print_function
import sys
import rospy
from beginner_tutorials.srv import AddTwoInts, AddTwoIntsException

def add_two_ints_client(x, y):
    rospy.wait_for_service('add_two_ints')
    try:
        add_two_ints = rospy.ServiceProxy('add_two_ints', AddTwoInts)
        resp1 = add_two_ints(x, y)
        return resp1.sum
    except rospy.ServiceException as e:
        print("Service call failed: %s" % e)

def usage():
    return "%s [x y]" % sys.argv[0]

if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) == 3:
        x = int(sys.argv[1])
        y = int(sys.argv[2])
    else:
        print(usage())
        sys.exit(1)
    print("Requesting %s + %s" % (x, y))
    print("%s + %s = %s" % (x, y, add_two_ints_client(x, y)))
```

### Step H — Execution Validation

Open three terminals. In Terminal 1:

```bash
roscore
```

In Terminal 2:

```bash
rosrun beginner_tutorials add_two_ints_server.py
```

In Terminal 3:

```bash
rosrun beginner_tutorials add_two_ints_client.py 5 7
```

Expected output in Terminal 3:

```
Requesting 5 + 7
5 + 7 = 12
```

### Step I — Mathematical Latency Analysis

Theoretical derivation of latency for this trivial service:

$$
T_{lookup} = 0.1 \text{ ms}
$$
$$
T_{marshal} + T_{demarshal} = 2 \cdot 0.05 \text{ ms} = 0.1 \text{ ms}
$$
$$
T_{network} \approx 0.05 \text{ ms (LAN)}
$$
$$
T_{compute} \approx 0.001 \text{ ms (integer addition)}
$$

Summing:

$$
T_{total} \approx 0.1 + 0.1 + 0.05 + 0.001 \approx 0.251 \text{ ms}
$$

This is orders of magnitude faster than a typical ROS topic round-trip for a small message, because topics incur continuous serialization overhead per publish, whereas services amortize setup overhead over a single call.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Service vs. Action vs. Topic

| Parameter | Service | Action | Topic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Communication Pattern** | Request-Response (RPC) | Goal-Result-Feedback (Task) | Publisher-Subscriber (Stream) |
| **Blocking Behavior** | Synchronous by default | Asynchronous with feedback | Asynchronous, non-blocking |
| **Preemption** | No | Yes (cancelable goals) | N/A |
| **Feedback Channel** | No | Yes (continuous updates) | Implicit (continuous publishes) |
| **Use Case** | Quick computations, configuration | Long-running missions, navigation | Sensor data, continuous control |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip**: If the KTU paper asks "Which ROS communication mechanism should be used for a long-running SLAM map update?" the correct answer is **Action**, not Service. Services are for **short, atomic, deterministic** operations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Service Architecture Block Diagram

The diagram below illustrates the end-to-end flow of a ROS service call from the Client node through the Master to the Server node.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Client Node"] -->|1. waitForService| M["ROS Master"]
    M -->|2. Resolve URI| A
    A -->|3. Send Request| S["Server Node"]
    S -->|4. Invoke Callback| C["Handler Function"]
    C -->|5. Return Response| S
    S -->|6. Reply to Client| A
    M -.->|Master Discovery| S
```

## 4.2 Service Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Uninitialized
    Uninitialized --> Advertised: advertiseService called
    Advertised --> WaitingRequest: Node spin started
    WaitingRequest --> Processing: Request received
    Processing --> WaitingRequest: Callback returns
    WaitingRequest --> Shutdown: Node shutdown
    Shutdown --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Client State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created
    Created --> Probing: waitForService called
    Probing --> Probing: Service not found retry
    Probing --> Ready: Service discovered
    Ready --> Blocking: call request sent
    Blocking --> Ready: Response received
    Ready --> Error: ServiceException raised
    Error --> [*]
    Ready --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Service Compilation Pipeline Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    srv["AddTwoInts.srv file"] --> parser["genmsg Parser"]
    parser --> genpy["Python Code Generator"]
    parser --> gencpp["C++ Code Generator"]
    genpy --> pyout["AddTwoIntsRequest Response classes"]
    gencpp --> cpout["AddTwoInts.h header files"]
    pyout --> runtime["ROS Runtime"]
    cpout --> runtime
    pkg["package.xml + CMakeLists.txt"] --> build["catkin_make"]
    build --> parser
```

## 4.5 Interaction Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client as Service Client
    participant Master as ROS Master
    participant Server as Service Server
    participant Callback as Handler Function

    Client->>Master: 1. Lookup /add_two_ints
    Master-->>Client: 2. Return server URI
    Client->>Server: 3. TCP connect + send Request
    Server->>Callback: 4. Invoke handle_add_two_ints req
    Callback-->>Server: 5. Return Response
    Server-->>Client: 6. TCP send Response
    Client->>Client: 7. Unblock and parse
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

Define a ROS service. Compare services with topics in terms of communication pattern, blocking behavior, and use case suitability.

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

A ROS **Service** is a synchronous, one-to-one communication mechanism based on a request-response pattern. A *Service Client* node sends a single *Request* message to a *Service Server* node, blocks its execution, and waits for a *Response*. The contract is defined in a `.srv` file with request fields separated from response fields by `---`.

| Parameter | Service | Topic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pattern | Request-Response (RPC) | Publisher-Subscriber (Stream) |
| Blocking | Synchronous (default) | Asynchronous |
| Use Case | One-shot queries, configuration | Continuous sensor streams, control |

**Use case suitability**: Services are best for short, deterministic queries such as "spawn a robot" or "reset odometry". Topics are best for high-frequency continuous data such as laser scans or camera images.

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

Explain the role of the `waitForService()` function in a ROS service client. What happens if it is omitted?

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

The `waitForService()` function blocks the client's execution until the ROS Master confirms that the requested service is registered and reachable. It accepts an optional timeout in seconds; if the service is not discovered before the timeout, it raises a `ROSException`.

**Consequences of omission**: Without `waitForService()`, a client that calls the service immediately after node initialization may receive a `ServiceException` because the Master may not yet have completed name resolution or the server may not yet have advertised itself. This causes intermittent, race-condition-driven failures that are notoriously hard to debug.

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: When asked to "implement a service", many students write only the server OR only the client. **Both nodes must be presented** along with the `.srv` file and the `CMakeLists.txt` modifications. Omitting any component costs **at least 2 marks** under the 2024 scheme marking rubric.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

#### (a) (7 Marks)

Explain the procedure to implement a ROS service to compute the sum of two 64-bit integers. Include the steps for creating the `.srv` file, modifying `package.xml` and `CMakeLists.txt`, and outline the Python code for both the server and the client.

**Model Answer (7 Marks)**:

**Step 1 — Create `.srv` file** (1 mark):

Create `srv/AddTwoInts.srv`:

```
int64 a
int64 b
---
int64 sum
```

**Step 2 — Modify `package.xml`** (1 mark):

```xml
<build_depend>message_generation</build_depend>
<exec_depend>message_runtime</exec_depend>
```

**Step 3 — Modify `CMakeLists.txt`** (1 mark):

```cmake
add_service_files(FILES AddTwoInts.srv)
generate_messages(DEPENDENCIES std_msgs)
```

**Step 4 — Server code** (`scripts/add_two_ints_server.py`) (2 marks):

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import rospy
from beginner_tutorials.srv import AddTwoInts, AddTwoIntsResponse

def handle_add_two_ints(req):
    return AddTwoIntsResponse(req.a + req.b)

def add_two_ints_server():
    rospy.init_node('add_two_ints_server')
    rospy.Service('add_two_ints', AddTwoInts, handle_add_two_ints)
    rospy.loginfo("Ready to add two ints.")
    rospy.spin()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    add_two_ints_server()
```

**Step 5 — Client code** (`scripts/add_two_ints_client.py`) (2 marks):

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys, rospy
from beginner_tutorials.srv import AddTwoInts, AddTwoIntsException

def add_two_ints_client(x, y):
    rospy.wait_for_service('add_two_ints')
    proxy = rospy.ServiceProxy('add_two_ints', AddTwoInts)
    try:
        return proxy(x, y).sum
    except rospy.ServiceException as e:
        rospy.logerr("Service call failed: %s" % e)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    x, y = (int(sys.argv[1]), int(sys.argv[2])) if len(sys.argv) == 3 else (1, 2)
    print("%s + %s = %s" % (x, y, add_two_ints_client(x, y)))
```

**Final Step — Build and Run**:

```bash
cd ~/catkin_ws && catkin_make && source devel/setup.bash
roscore &
rosrun beginner_tutorials add_two_ints_server.py &
rosrun beginner_tutorials add_two_ints_client.py 10 20
```

#### (b) (7 Marks)

Describe the role of the `rospy.spin()` function in a service server node. What happens if it is omitted? Differentiate between `rospy.wait_for_service()` and `service.wait_for_service()` in C++.

**Model Answer (7 Marks)**:

**Role of `rospy.spin()`** (3 marks):

`rospy.spin()` enters a blocking event loop that prevents the Python interpreter from exiting. While inside the loop, the ROS client library continuously processes the incoming callback queue, including:
- Service request invocations on registered handlers.
- Topic subscription callbacks.
- Timer callbacks.
- Parameter server events.

If `rospy.spin()` is omitted, the `add_two_ints_server()` function returns immediately after `rospy.Service(...)` is called. The Python process exits, and the service is deregistered by the Master. No requests can ever be served. In other words, `rospy.spin()` is the equivalent of `while not rospy.is_shutdown(): pass` with built-in event-loop dispatching.

**`rospy.wait_for_service()` vs C++ `client.waitForExistence()`** (4 marks):

| Property | Python `rospy.wait_for_service(name)` | C++ `client.waitForExistence()` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Object type** | Standalone module-level function | Method on a `ros::ServiceClient` object |
| **Pre-condition** | None — any client can call it | Requires the `ServiceClient` object to already be constructed |
| **Return value** | Returns `True` if found, `False` if timed out | Returns `bool` indicating existence |
| **Blocking semantics** | Blocks until service is discovered or timeout | Blocks until service is discovered or timeout |
| **Typical usage** | `rospy.wait_for_service('foo')` then `proxy = ServiceProxy(...)` | `ros::ServiceClient client = nh.serviceClient<T>("foo"); client.waitForExistence();` |

Both mechanisms serve the identical purpose: avoiding race conditions where a client attempts to call a service that has not yet been advertised by the server.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

#### (a) (7 Marks)

With the help of a neat sequence diagram, explain the end-to-end lifecycle of a ROS service call from the client's perspective. Highlight the role of the ROS Master.

**Model Answer (7 Marks)**:

**Sequence of Events** (5 marks for sequence description + 2 marks for diagram):

1. **Service Registration (Server side)**: The server node, upon initialization, calls `rospy.Service('add_two_ints', AddTwoInts, handler)`. This sends a registration RPC to the ROS Master (`roscore`). The Master stores the mapping `{ "add_two_ints" -> "http://server_host:port" }` in its Service Registry.
2. **Client Initiation**: The client node calls `rospy.wait_for_service('add_two_ints')`. This sends a "lookup service" RPC to the Master.
3. **Master Resolution**: The Master replies with the URI of the server (`http://server_host:port`).
4. **Direct Connection Establishment**: The client opens a persistent TCP/IP connection to the server. Note that subsequent calls do NOT traverse the Master; the connection is reused.
5. **Request Transmission**: The client serializes the request fields and writes them to the TCP socket.
6. **Server Dispatch**: The server's event loop detects the incoming request and invokes the registered `handler` callback with the deserialized request object.
7. **Response Generation**: The handler returns a `Response` object. The server serializes it and writes it back to the TCP socket.
8. **Client Unblock**: The client's `proxy()` call unblocks, deserializes the response, and execution continues.

**Sequence Diagram** (represented verbally due to text-only constraint):

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client Node
    participant M as ROS Master
    participant S as Server Node
    C->>M: 1. Lookup "add_two_ints"
    M-->>C: 2. Return server URI
    C->>S: 3. TCP connect
    S->>S: 4. Invoke handler(req)
    S-->>C: 5. Send response
    C->>C: 6. Unblock
```

**Role of ROS Master** (2 marks): The Master acts only as a name-resolving directory. It does NOT participate in the data flow once the connection is established. If the Master crashes mid-session, existing direct service connections continue to work; only new lookups will fail.

#### (b) (7 Marks)

Differentiate between synchronous and asynchronous service calls in ROS. Provide a C++ code snippet demonstrating an asynchronous call using `asyncSendRequest`.

**Model Answer (7 Marks)**:

**Conceptual Differentiation** (4 marks):

| Property | Synchronous Service Call | Asynchronous Service Call |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Execution Flow** | Client thread blocks until response arrives | Client thread fires request and continues |
| **Callback** | None (returns response directly) | A callback is invoked upon response arrival |
| **Use Case** | Short, blocking queries | Long-running computations, parallel I/O |
| **Python API** | `proxy(req)` | Not natively supported in `rospy` |
| **C++ API** | `client.call(req, resp)` | `client.asyncSendRequest(req, callback)` |

**Asynchronous C++ Code Snippet** (3 marks):

```cpp
#include "ros/ros.h"
#include "beginner_tutorials/AddTwoInts.h"

void responseCallback(const beginner_tutorials::AddTwoInts::ConstPtr& response) {
    ROS_INFO("Async Sum: %ld", (long int)response->sum);
}

int main(int argc, char** argv) {
    ros::init(argc, argv, "add_two_ints_async_client");
    ros::NodeHandle nh;

    ros::ServiceClient client =
        nh.serviceClient<beginner_tutorials::AddTwoInts>("add_two_ints");
    client.waitForExistence();

    beginner_tutorials::AddTwoInts srv;
    srv.request.a = 10;
    srv.request.b = 20;

    // Fire-and-forget; callback invoked when response arrives
    client.asyncSendRequest(srv.request, &responseCallback);

    // Continue doing other work while service executes...
    ros::Duration(1.0).sleep();

    ros::shutdown();
    return 0;
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall**: Calling `asyncSendRequest()` without ensuring the ros::NodeHandle's callback queue is being spun (via `ros::spin()` or `ros::spinOnce()`) means the callback will NEVER be invoked. Always pair async calls with an active spin.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Service File**: Always end the request block with `---` followed by response fields; both blocks support standard ROS primitive types (`int8`, `float32`, `string`, `bool`, `time`, `duration`).
- **Build Dependencies**: `message_generation` (build-time) and `message_runtime` (runtime) are mandatory in `package.xml`.
- **Server Registration**: Use `rospy.Service(name, type, handler)` for Python and `nh.advertiseService(name, handler)` for C++.
- **Client Creation**: Use `rospy.ServiceProxy(name, type)` followed by `wait_for_service()` for Python; `nh.serviceClient<T>(name)` for C++.
- **Blocking Behavior**: The default Python client call blocks. In C++, `client.call()` is blocking, while `client.asyncSendRequest()` is non-blocking.
- **Master Role**: The ROS Master only handles name resolution; it does NOT participate in the data exchange once a service connection is established.
- **Naming Convention**: Service names are relative to the namespace of the node by default. A leading slash (`/`) makes them absolute (e.g., `/global/srv` vs `local/srv`).
- **Timeout Handling**: Always specify a timeout in `waitForService()` to avoid infinite blocking in production code.
- **Exception Safety**: Wrap service calls in `try/except ServiceException` (Python) or check the return `bool` (C++) to handle transport failures gracefully.
- **Compile Step**: After modifying a `.srv` file, you MUST rerun `catkin_make` (ROS 1) or `colcon build` (ROS 2) before the new message classes become importable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 2
