<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 2: Using a Service in ROS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **ROS Service** is a synchronous, request/reply communication mechanism defined by a pair of messages: one for the request and one for the reply. Unlike topics (which are asynchronous and one-to-many), services provide a one-to-one, blocking call where a **Service Client** sends a request and waits for a **Service Server** to return a single response.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 💡

Imagine you walk into a hotel reception and ask the receptionist: *"What is the Wi-Fi password?"* The receptionist pauses, looks it up, and gives you a **single, specific answer**. You then walk away. This is exactly how a ROS service works:

| Real-World Concept | ROS Equivalent |
|---|---|
| You (the questioner) | **Service Client Node** |
| Receptionist (the answerer) | **Service Server Node** |
| Your question | **Service Request Message** |
| Receptionist's answer | **Service Response Message** |
| Wi-Fi inquiry (one-shot, blocking) | **Synchronous, one-to-one call** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Characteristic:** A service is **synchronous** — the client node *blocks* (pauses execution) until the server responds. This makes services ideal for **discrete, request-driven operations** such as triggering a behavior, querying a sensor value once, or performing a calculation on demand.

### When to Use Services (vs Topics)

- **Use a Service** when: the robot needs an immediate answer to a specific question (e.g., *"Is the gripper open?"*, *"What is the current joint angle?"*, *"Spawn a new turtle at (x, y)"*).
- **Use a Topic** when: continuous, streaming data is needed (e.g., sensor streams, velocity commands).

### Standard Service Metrics in ROS

- **Service Definition File:** `.srv` extension (e.g., `AddTwoInts.srv`)
- **Request/Response Separator:** Three dashes `---` inside the `.srv` file
- **Service Name Convention:** CamelCase or snake_case, namespaced (e.g., `/turtle1/set_pen`)
- **Standard Service Library:** `std_srvs` package (e.g., `std_srvs/Empty`, `std_srvs/Trigger`)
- **Default Service Transport:** TCP/IP, with **MD5 sum verification** for type compatibility
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Service Communication Model

The ROS service model follows a strict **Client–Server** (also called **Request–Reply** or **Remote Procedure Call – RPC**) pattern. The architectural flow consists of five canonical steps:

1. **Service Definition Compilation** — A `.srv` file is processed by `rossrv` to generate language-specific request/response classes (Python, C++).
2. **Service Server Node Launch** — A node advertises a service under a specific name (e.g., `/spawn`). ROS Master registers the URI.
3. **Service Client Initialization** — A client node creates a proxy object (Python `rospy.ServiceProxy` or C++ `ros::ServiceClient`) bound to the service name and type.
4. **Request Transmission** — The client calls the proxy with request data; ROS serializes the message (CDR over TCP) and sends it.
5. **Response Handling** — The server's callback executes, fills the response, and returns it. The client unblocks and reads the response.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a `.srv` file?** The `.srv` file is a *contract*. Both the client and server must compile the same `.srv`, ensuring that the **MD5 sum** of the message definition matches. This is ROS's compile-time type-safety mechanism.

## 2.2 Anatomy of a `.srv` File

A service description file contains **two message blocks** separated by exactly three hyphens:

```text
# Request fields (sent FROM client TO server)
int64 a
int64 b
---
# Response fields (sent FROM server TO client)
int64 sum
```

| Field Type | Direction | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Above the `---` | Client → Server (Request) | `int64 a`, `int64 b` |
| Below the `---` | Server → Client (Response) | `int64 sum` |
| Comment lines | Start with `#` | `# Sum of two ints` |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Service Architecture Table

| Component | Symbol / Notation | Definition | ROS Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Service Name | $S_n$ | Unique string identifier (e.g., `/turtle1/teleport_absolute`) | Globally resolved via ROS Master |
| Service Type | $S_t$ | Strongly-typed contract from `.srv` | e.g., `turtlesim/Spawn` |
| MD5 Sum | $H_{srv}$ | 128-bit hash of the service definition | Used for type-safety checks |
| Request Message | $M_{req}$ | Data structure sent client → server | Generated by `rossrv` |
| Response Message | $M_{res}$ | Data structure sent server → client | Generated by `rossrv` |
| Transport | $T$ | `TCPROS` (default) or `UDPROS` (low-latency) | Configurable per service |
| Persistent Connection | $P$ | Boolean — reuse TCP socket? | Default: **false** |

## 2.4 Service vs. Topic: Critical Distinctions

| Feature | Service 📞 | Topic 📡 |
|---|---|---|
| Communication Pattern | Request/Reply (RPC) | Publish/Subscribe (Pub/Sub) |
| Synchronous? | Yes (blocking) | No (non-blocking, asynchronous) |
| Cardinality | One-to-One | One-to-Many |
| Data Flow | Discrete (on-demand) | Continuous (streaming) |
| Use Case | Triggering actions, queries | Sensor data, control commands |
| Example | `/spawn` turtle | `/turtle1/cmd_vel` velocity |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

ROS services underpin mission-critical robotics operations such as:

- **Industrial Manipulation:** Requesting the robot to "pick object A from bin B".
- **Autonomous Navigation:** Calling `/global_planning/make_plan` to obtain a path.
- **Fleet Management:** Querying the state of a specific drone in a swarm.
- **Simulation Control:** Spawning/despawning objects in `Gazebo`.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked to "differentiate", always mention **blocking vs. non-blocking** AND **one-to-one vs. one-to-many** — these two pairs alone can earn full marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Writing & Using a Custom Service

The following exhaustive walkthrough demonstrates **creating, building, and using a service** in ROS Noetic. We use the canonical `AddTwoInts` example from the official ROS tutorials.

---

### **Step 1: Create the Service Definition File**

Navigate to the `srv` folder of your package and create `AddTwoInts.srv`:

```bash
cd ~/catkin_ws/src/beginner_tutorials
mkdir srv
cd srv
nano AddTwoInts.srv
```

**File Contents (`AddTwoInts.srv`):**

```text
int64 a
int64 b
---
int64 sum
```

> **Logic:** The first block (above `---`) defines the **request**: two 64-bit integers named `a` and `b`. The second block (below `---`) defines the **response**: one 64-bit integer named `sum` representing $a + b$.

---

### **Step 2: Modify `package.xml` and `CMakeLists.txt`**

**In `package.xml`, add the required dependencies:**

```xml
<build_depend>message_generation</build_depend>
<exec_depend>message_runtime</exec_depend>
```

> **Logic:** `message_generation` is needed at **compile-time** to generate language bindings from `.srv` files. `message_runtime` is needed at **runtime** to deserialize messages.

**In `CMakeLists.txt`, add the following lines:**

```cmake
find_package(catkin REQUIRED COMPONENTS
  roscpp
  rospy
  std_msgs
  message_generation
)

add_service_files(FILES
  AddTwoInts.srv
)

generate_messages(DEPENDENCIES
  std_msgs
)
```

> **Logic:** `add_service_files` tells `catkin` which `.srv` files to compile. `generate_messages` triggers the code generation pipeline, producing Python modules in `devel/lib/pythonX.Y/dist-packages/beginner_tutorials/srv/` and C++ headers in `devel/include/beginner_tutorials/`.

---

### **Step 3: Build the Package**

```bash
cd ~/catkin_ws
catkin_make
source devel/setup.bash
```

> **Logic:** `catkin_make` invokes CMake, which detects `add_service_files` and runs the `genmsg` toolchain. `source` registers the new generated messages with the current shell.

---

### **Step 4: Write the Service Server (Python)**

Create `scripts/add_two_ints_server.py`:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
from __future__ import print_function
from beginner_tutorials.srv import AddTwoInts, AddTwoIntsResponse
import rospy

def handle_add_two_ints(req):
    # Mathematical operation: sum = a + b
    sum_value = req.a + req.b
    print("Returning [%s + %s = %s]" % (req.a, req.b, sum_value))
    return AddTwoIntsResponse(sum_value)

def add_two_ints_server():
    rospy.init_node('add_two_ints_server')
    s = rospy.Service('add_two_ints', AddTwoInts, handle_add_two_ints)
    print("Ready to add two ints.")
    rospy.spin()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    add_two_ints_server()
```

> **Step-by-step logic:**
> 1. `rospy.init_node('add_two_ints_server')` registers the node with the ROS Master.
> 2. `rospy.Service('add_two_ints', AddTwoInts, handle_add_two_ints)` advertises a service named `/add_two_ints` of type `AddTwoInts`, with callback `handle_add_two_ints`.
> 3. When a request arrives, ROS automatically deserializes the bytes into a `AddTwoIntsRequest` object (`req`) and calls `handle_add_two_ints(req)`.
> 4. The function returns an `AddTwoIntsResponse` object. ROS serializes this and sends it back to the client.
> 5. `rospy.spin()` keeps the node alive, listening for incoming requests.

---

### **Step 5: Write the Service Client (Python)**

Create `scripts/add_two_ints_client.py`:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
import sys
import rospy
from beginner_tutorials.srv import AddTwoInts, AddTwoIntsException

def add_two_ints_client(x, y):
    rospy.wait_for_service('add_two_ints')
    try:
        add_two_ints = rospy.ServiceProxy('add_two_ints', AddTwoInts)
        resp = add_two_ints(x, y)
        return resp.sum
    except rospy.ServiceException as e:
        print("Service call failed: %s" % e)

def usage():
    return "%s [x y]" % sys.argv[0]

if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) == 3:
        x = int(sys.argv[1])
        y = int(sys.argv[2])
    else:
        print(usage())
        sys.exit(1)
    print("Requesting %s + %s" % (x, y))
    print("%s + %s = %s" % (x, y, add_two_ints_client(x, y)))
```

> **Step-by-step logic:**
> 1. `rospy.wait_for_service('add_two_ints')` blocks until the server is online. This is **mandatory** in Python to avoid race conditions.
> 2. `rospy.ServiceProxy('add_two_ints', AddTwoInts)` creates a callable proxy. This is a local stub that, when invoked, performs the network call.
> 3. `add_two_ints(x, y)` sends the request (synchronous, blocks) and returns the response object `resp` once the server replies.
> 4. The `try/except` block catches `rospy.ServiceException`, which is raised on transport failure or timeout.

---

### **Step 6: Run the Service**

**Terminal 1 — Start ROS Master:**

```bash
roscore
```

**Terminal 2 — Start the Server:**

```bash
rosrun beginner_tutorials add_two_ints_server.py
```

**Terminal 3 — Call the Service from the Command Line:**

```bash
rosservice call /add_two_ints 5 3
```

**Expected Output:**

```text
sum: 8
```

> **Logic:** `rosservice call` is the CLI tool that performs the same action as a client node: it constructs a request, sends it to the service URI registered with the Master, prints the YAML-formatted response, and exits. This is invaluable for debugging.

---

### **Step 7: Mathematical Validation of the Response**

Given the request $a = 5$, $b = 3$, the server's callback computes:

$$
\text{sum} = a + b
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\text{sum} = 5 + 3
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{sum} = 8}
$$

The client receives `resp.sum = 8` and prints it. The system is **mathematically consistent**.

---

## 3.2 KTU High-Yield Command-Line Tool Matrix (rosservice family)

| Command | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `rosservice list` | List all active services | `rosservice list` |
| `rosservice type <srv>` | Print service type | `rosservice type /add_two_ints` |
| `rosservice info <srv>` | Show service URI, type, transport | `rosservice info /clear` |
| `rosservice call <srv> <args>` | Call a service from CLI | `rosservice call /spawn 2 3 0.5 ""` |
| `rosservice find <type>` | Find services of a given type | `rosservice find turtlesim/Spawn` |
| `rosservice uri <srv>` | Print service XMLRPC URI | `rosservice uri /clear` |
| `rosservice args <srv>` | Show service argument structure | `rosservice args /spawn` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Service Communication Flow (Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Master as ROS Master
    participant Client as Service Client Node
    participant Server as Service Server Node
    participant Topic as TCPROS Transport

    Note over Master: Step 0: Registration Phase
    Server->>Master: advertiseService("/add_two_ints", AddTwoInts, URI)
    Master-->>Server: ACK (Registered)

    Note over Client: Step 1: Discovery Phase
    Client->>Master: lookupService("/add_two_ints")
    Master-->>Client: Returns server URI + type

    Note over Client,Server: Step 2: Request Phase (Blocking)
    Client->>Topic: Serialize request (a, b) via XMLRPC/TCP
    Topic->>Server: Deserialized AddTwoIntsRequest
    Server->>Server: Execute callback handle_add_two_ints(req)
    Server->>Server: Compute sum = a + b

    Note over Server,Client: Step 3: Response Phase
    Server->>Topic: Serialize response (sum) via TCP
    Topic->>Client: Deserialized AddTwoIntsResponse
    Client->>Client: resp.sum returned; client unblocks
```

---

## 4.2 Service Type Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Node] --> B{ServiceProxy Object}
    B --> C[Request Message a, b]
    C --> D[Serialization CDR Format]
    D --> E[TCPROS Transport Layer]
    E --> F[Service Server Node]
    F --> G{Service Callback Function}
    G --> H[Business Logic sum = a + b]
    H --> I[Response Message sum]
    I --> J[Serialization CDR Format]
    J --> K[TCPROS Transport Layer]
    K --> L[Client Node Receives Response]
    L --> M[Client Unblocks and Proceeds]
```

---

## 4.3 Service Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Uninitialized
    Uninitialized --> Registered: advertiseService
    Registered --> WaitingForRequest: spin starts
    WaitingForRequest --> Processing: request received
    Processing --> SendingResponse: callback returns
    SendingResponse --> WaitingForRequest: response sent
    WaitingForRequest --> [*]: shutdown called
    Processing --> [*]: error or timeout
```

---

## 4.4 Service vs Topic Interaction Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PUB_SUB["Topic Model (Async, 1-to-Many)"]
        T1[Publisher Node] -->|continuous stream| T2[Subscriber A]
        T1 -->|continuous stream| T3[Subscriber B]
        T1 -->|continuous stream| T4[Subscriber C]
    end

    subgraph REQ_REP["Service Model (Sync, 1-to-1)"]
        S1[Client Node] -->|request blocks| S2[Server Node]
        S2 -->|single response| S1
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define a ROS Service and state its key characteristics.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **ROS Service** is a synchronous, request/reply communication mechanism in ROS defined by a `.srv` file. Its key characteristics are:

1. **Synchronous (Blocking):** The client pauses execution until the server responds.
2. **One-to-One:** A single client interacts with a single server (no multicast).
3. **Strongly Typed:** Defined by a `.srv` file with an MD5 sum contract.
4. **Service Definition:** Uses a request block and a response block separated by `---`.
5. **Standard Transport:** TCPRO (TCP) by default; UDPRO optional for low-latency.

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between ROS Topics and ROS Services.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Topic | Service |
|---|---|---|
| Pattern | Publish/Subscribe | Request/Reply |
| Synchronous? | No (async) | Yes (blocking) |
| Cardinality | One-to-Many | One-to-One |
| Use Case | Streaming data | Discrete queries/actions |
| Example | `/scan` (LiDAR) | `/spawn` (turtle) |

---

## 📝 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module ESE question has internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**, each carrying **14 marks** split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Service Implementation** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the ROS service communication model. Describe the role of the ROS Master in service discovery. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Service Communication Model:**

A ROS service uses a **Client–Server architecture** with the ROS Master acting as a name resolver. The process is:

1. **Server Registration:** When the server node starts, it registers the service name and type with the ROS Master via XMLRPC.
2. **Client Lookup:** When a client node starts, it queries the Master with the service name. The Master returns the server's URI and service type.
3. **Direct Connection:** The client then establishes a **direct TCPROS connection** to the server (bypassing the Master for the actual data transfer).
4. **Request/Response:** The client sends a serialized request, blocks, and receives a serialized response.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C[Client Node] -->|lookupService| M[ROS Master]
    M -->|returns URI + Type| C
    C -->|TCPROS Request| S[Server Node]
    S -->|TCPROS Response| C
```

**Role of ROS Master:**

- Maintains a registry mapping **service names → service URIs** and **service types**.
- Enables **loose coupling** — clients do not need to know the server's network address at compile time.
- Verifies **MD5 sum compatibility** to ensure type-safety between client and server.
- Acts as a **centralized registry** but does not relay data (data flows directly between client and server).

---

**(b) Write a complete ROS Python service server and client for an `AddTwoInts` service. Show the `.srv` file, the server code, and the client code. Explain the `rosservice call` command with an example. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**`AddTwoInts.srv` file:**

```text
int64 a
int64 b
---
int64 sum
```

**Service Server (`add_two_ints_server.py`):**

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
from beginner_tutorials.srv import AddTwoInts, AddTwoIntsResponse
import rospy

def handle_add_two_ints(req):
    return AddTwoIntsResponse(req.a + req.b)

def add_two_ints_server():
    rospy.init_node('add_two_ints_server')
    rospy.Service('add_two_ints', AddTwoInts, handle_add_two_ints)
    rospy.spin()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    add_two_ints_server()
```

**Service Client (`add_two_ints_client.py`):**

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
import rospy
from beginner_tutorials.srv import AddTwoInts

def add_two_ints_client(x, y):
    rospy.wait_for_service('add_two_ints')
    proxy = rospy.ServiceProxy('add_two_ints', AddTwoInts)
    resp = proxy(x, y)
    return resp.sum
```

**`rosservice call` command:**

```bash
rosservice call /add_two_ints 10 25
```

**Output:**

```text
sum: 35
```

**Explanation:** `rosservice call` constructs a request message from CLI arguments, sends it to the service, and prints the YAML-formatted response. It is a **quick debugging tool** that bypasses the need to write a client node.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Custom Service Design & Lifecycle** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Design a custom `.srv` file for a `WordCount` service that takes a string and returns the word count and character count. Show the server and client implementations. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**`WordCount.srv` file:**

```text
string sentence
---
int32 word_count
int32 char_count
```

**Service Server (`word_count_server.py`):**

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
from beginner_tutorials.srv import WordCount, WordCountResponse
import rospy

def handle_word_count(req):
    words = req.sentence.split()
    return WordCountResponse(
        word_count=len(words),
        char_count=len(req.sentence)
    )

def word_count_server():
    rospy.init_node('word_count_server')
    rospy.Service('word_count', WordCount, handle_word_count)
    rospy.spin()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    word_count_server()
```

**Service Client (`word_count_client.py`):**

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
import rospy
from beginner_tutorials.srv import WordCount

def word_count_client(sentence):
    rospy.wait_for_service('word_count')
    proxy = rospy.ServiceProxy('word_count', WordCount)
    resp = proxy(sentence)
    return resp.word_count, resp.char_count
```

**CLI Test:**

```bash
rosservice call /word_count "Hello ROS World"
```

**Output:**

```text
word_count: 3
char_count: 15
```

**Mathematical validation:** For the string `"Hello ROS World"`:
- Words = `["Hello", "ROS", "World"]` → $n_w = 3$
- Characters (including spaces) = 15 → $n_c = 15$ ✓

---

**(b) Explain the service lifecycle with a state diagram. Discuss the role of `rospy.wait_for_service()` in a client and what happens if the service is unavailable. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Service Lifecycle States:**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Uninitialized
    Uninitialized --> Registered: advertiseService call
    Registered --> Active: spin/rospy.spin starts
    Active --> Processing: incoming request
    Processing --> Active: response sent
    Active --> Shutdown: rospy.signal_shutdown
    Shutdown --> [*]
```

**Detailed Lifecycle:**

1. **Uninitialized:** The node is created but the service is not yet advertised.
2. **Registered:** `rospy.Service()` is called; the Master records the service name, type, and URI.
3. **Active:** The node enters the event loop (`spin`) and waits for incoming requests.
4. **Processing:** A request arrives; the callback is invoked, executing the business logic.
5. **Response Sent:** The callback's return value is serialized and sent back to the client.
6. **Shutdown:** On `Ctrl+C` or `rospy.signal_shutdown()`, the service is unregistered from the Master and the node terminates.

**Role of `rospy.wait_for_service()`:**

- **Purpose:** Blocks the client until the named service becomes available on the Master. This prevents a **race condition** where the client starts before the server is ready.
- **Timeout:** Optional argument `timeout=5` raises `ROSException` if the service does not appear within 5 seconds.
- **Failure Behavior:** If the service never appears, the client will block indefinitely (or until timeout) — it does **not** automatically retry.

**Example:**

```python
try:
    rospy.wait_for_service('add_two_ints', timeout=5)
except rospy.ROSException:
    print("Service not available — check if server is running")
```

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting `rospy.wait_for_service()`** in Python clients — leads to `ROSException` on first call. **[-2 marks]**
> 2. **Wrong separator in `.srv` file** — using `===` instead of `---` causes compilation failure. **[-1 mark]**
> 3. **Confusing Topics with Services** — stating that services are "asynchronous" or "one-to-many" is factually wrong. **[-2 marks]**
> 4. **Not sourcing `devel/setup.bash`** after `catkin_make` — generated message modules won't be importable. **[-1 mark]**
> 5. **Mixing up Request/Response blocks** — placing response fields above `---` and request below is a common error. **[-1 mark]**

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **ROS Service** is a **synchronous, request/reply** mechanism defined by a `.srv` file.
- ✅ The `.srv` file has **two blocks** separated by `---` (request above, response below).
- ✅ Services use **Client–Server** architecture with the **ROS Master** as the name registry.
- ✅ The **MD5 sum** of the `.srv` ensures type-safety between client and server.
- ✅ Default transport is **TCPROS** (TCP); UDPROS is optional.
- ✅ Python clients **must** call `rospy.wait_for_service()` to avoid race conditions.
- ✅ `rosservice call` is the CLI tool for quick service invocation and debugging.
- ✅ Services are **one-to-one and blocking**; topics are **one-to-many and non-blocking**.
- ✅ Standard service library: `std_srvs` (provides `Empty`, `Trigger`, `SetBool`, etc.).
- ✅ Service types are resolved at **runtime** by querying the ROS Master, not at compile time.
- ✅ C++ uses `ros::ServiceClient`; Python uses `rospy.ServiceProxy`.
- ✅ On **service failure**, the client raises `rospy.ServiceException` (Python) or returns `false` (C++).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Defining an Action
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT525 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Defining an Action

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **Robot Operating System (ROS)**, an **Action** is a peer-to-peer, asynchronous communication paradigm designed for **long-running, goal-directed tasks** that require continuous feedback during execution and a definitive result upon completion. An action bundles together the request-reply semantics of a **service** with the streaming semantics of a **topic**, producing a four-stage interaction: **Goal → Feedback (streamed) → Result**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Essence):** An *action* is defined by an `.action` interface description file that explicitly separates three message types — a **Goal** (request), a **Result** (terminal response), and **Feedback** (intermediate updates) — using standardized delimiter lines. The action server and action client exchange these messages over the ROS 2 middleware (typically built on DDS).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Ordering a Pizza via a Food Delivery App**

Imagine you order a pizza through a delivery app:

| App Interaction | ROS Equivalent |
|---|---|
| You tap "Order Pizza" (sending the request) | **Client sends a `Goal` message** |
| App shows "Restaurant accepted", "Preparing dough", "Baking", "Out for delivery" | **Server streams `Feedback` messages** |
| App shows "Delivered" with the final bill | **Server sends a single `Result` message** |
| You can tap "Cancel order" mid-way | **Client sends a `Cancel` request** |

This is fundamentally **different from a service** (where you wait silently until the pizza arrives with no tracking) and **different from a topic** (where you only see status broadcasts but never send your own request). The action is the **only** ROS primitive that natively supports all four: goal, feedback, result, and preemption/cancellation.

## 1.3 Why Actions Exist — The Three ROS Communication Primitives

ROS 2 provides **three core communication paradigms**, each suited to a specific interaction pattern:

- **Topics** — Anonymous, one-way, streaming publish/subscribe (e.g., sensor data).
- **Services** — Identified, two-way, *blocking* request/reply (e.g., "trigger a calibration snapshot").
- **Actions** — Identified, two-way, *non-blocking*, preemptible long tasks with feedback (e.g., "navigate to a waypoint," "rotate the robot 180°," "pick an object").

The key metric is **task duration**: services are appropriate for sub-second tasks; **actions are mandatory for any task that may take longer than a few hundred milliseconds or that requires progress monitoring**.

## 1.4 Physical / Standard Metrics Highlighted

- **Goal timeout** is typically configured in the range of **0 seconds (infinite) to a few seconds**, measured in **seconds (s)**.
- **Feedback publishing rate** is application-defined; common values range from **1 Hz to 100 Hz** depending on the hardware and bandwidth.
- **Action server lifecycle** is measured by the ROS 2 wall clock (`rclcpp::Clock` / `rclpy::Clock`).
- **Result codes** are conventionally integers, with `0` representing **SUCCESS** and non-zero values representing domain-specific failure modes (e.g., `1` = `ABORTED`, `2` = `CANCELED`).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Action — The `.action` File

An action is formally defined in a **plain-text file with the `.action` extension**. This file is a **declarative interface description**, analogous to `.msg` (for topics) and `.srv` (for services). It is **not** executable code; it is compiled by the ROS 2 build system (`colcon`) into language-specific bindings (C++ headers, Python modules).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The `.action` file is split into three sections by **two unique delimiter lines**:
> - The first delimiter is `---` (three hyphens) — separates the **Goal** from the **Result**.
> - The second delimiter is `---` (three hyphens) — separates the **Result** from the **Feedback**.

### 2.1.1 Canonical Structure of an `.action` File

```
# Comment: Goal section (the request)
<field_type> <field_name>
<field_type> <field_name>
---
# Comment: Result section (the terminal response)
<field_type> <field_name>
---
# Comment: Feedback section (intermediate updates)
<field_type> <field_name>
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Field types** mirror standard ROS 2 message types: `int32`, `float64`, `string`, `bool`, `geometry_msgs/msg/Pose`, `sensor_msgs/msg/Image`, arrays (`type[]`), bounded strings (`string<=64`), and constants.

### 2.1.2 Worked Example — `Fibonacci.action`

The classic textbook example (used in `rclcpp`/`rclpy` tutorials) is the Fibonacci sequence generator:

```
# Goal: ask the server to compute the Fibonacci sequence of a given order
int32 order
---
# Result: the complete sequence, returned once when the goal terminates
int32[] sequence
---
# Feedback: the partial sequence published as it is being built
int32[] partial_sequence
```

**Interpretation:**
- The client sends a `Goal` containing an `int32 order` (e.g., `order = 5`).
- The server computes the sequence and, **periodically**, publishes `Feedback` messages containing the growing `partial_sequence` array.
- When computation completes (or is canceled), the server returns a single `Result` containing the full `sequence`.

## 2.2 The Action Client / Action Server Architecture

An action involves **exactly two endpoints**:

1. **Action Server** — A node that *accepts goals*, *executes* the underlying long-running task, *publishes feedback*, and *returns a result*.
2. **Action Client** — A node that *sends a goal*, *monitors feedback*, and *receives the final result*.

The ROS 2 middleware transparently creates **seven underlying topics and one service** for each action pair, but the developer interacts only with the high-level client/server API.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Students often confuse **action** (the message interface) with **action client/server** (the runtime nodes). The `.action` file defines the *data contract*; the client and server are the *software components* that exchange that data.

## 2.3 Goal State Machine — The Eight Legal States

Every action goal traverses a deterministic finite state machine. The states are part of the standard `action_msgs/msg/GoalStatus` message.

| State Code | State Name | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `1` | `ACCEPTED` | Server has received the goal and accepted it. |
| `2` | `EXECUTING` | Server is actively working on the goal; feedback may be published. |
| `3` | `CANCELING` | Client requested cancellation; server is winding down. |
| `4` | `SUCCEEDED` | Goal completed successfully; a Result is available. |
| `5` | `CANCELED` | Goal was canceled before completion. |
| `6` | `ABORTED` | Server terminated the goal due to an unrecoverable error. |
| `7` | `REJECTED` | Server refused the goal (e.g., invalid parameters or server busy). |
| `8` | `CANCELING` (re-entry) | Rare internal re-entry state during cancellation. |

> [!NOTE]
> States `0` (`UNKNOWN`) and the missing state `4`/`5` semantics are evaluated by the **action client** via a built-in *goal handle* object, not by the user.

## 2.4 Comparison Matrix — Topic vs Service vs Action

This is one of the **highest-yield tables for KTU 2024 examination questions**.

| Property | Topic | Service | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Communication pattern | Publish / Subscribe | Request / Reply | Goal / Feedback / Result |
| Direction | One-way (anonymous) | Two-way (identified) | Two-way (identified) |
| Blocking? | No | **Yes** (synchronous by default) | **No** (asynchronous) |
| Feedback during execution? | Continuous stream, but no request | **No** | **Yes** (structured) |
| Cancellation supported? | No | No (cannot interrupt a running service) | **Yes** (preemption) |
| Suitable task duration | Any (typically streaming) | **< 1 s recommended** | **> 1 s, up to hours** |
| Returns a result? | No | Yes (one reply) | Yes (one terminal Result) |
| Example use case | LIDAR scan, camera feed | Toggle a gripper, query a TF | Navigate to pose, execute a trajectory |
| Underlying mechanism | DDS topic | DDS service (request/reply) | 7 DDS topics + 1 service |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Rule of Thumb:** If the task **takes more than 1 second**, **needs progress updates**, or **may need to be canceled**, it **must** be modeled as an **action**, never as a service.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Total time for a streaming feedback | $T_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{feedback}} \cdot \Delta t_{\text{feedback}}$ | seconds (s) |
| Number of feedback messages | $N_{\text{feedback}} = \left\lvert \dfrac{T_{\text{exec}}}{\Delta t_{\text{feedback}}} \right\rvert$ | dimensionless |
| Mean feedback rate | $f_{\text{fb}} = \dfrac{1}{\Delta t_{\text{feedback}}}$ | hertz (Hz) |
| Goal success rate (over $K$ attempts) | $P_{\text{success}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{succeeded}}}{K}$ | dimensionless ratio in $[0,1]$ |
| Total action latency (client → first feedback) | $T_{\text{latency}} = T_{\text{dispatch}} + T_{\text{accept}} + T_{\text{start}}$ | seconds (s) |
| Action termination guarantee | Goal state $\in \{\text{SUCCEEDED, CANCELED, ABORTED, REJECTED}\}$ | boolean terminal |
| Termination latency | $T_{\text{term}} = T_{\text{goal\_sent}} - T_{\text{result\_received}}$ | seconds (s) |

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical pipe notation `$\vert x \vert$` above denotes **absolute value / cardinality**, used here to keep the markdown table syntax safe from collisions with the cell-separator pipe character.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Action-based communication is the **standard pattern** in industrial and research robotics for:

- **Mobile robot navigation** — `nav2_msgs/action/NavigateToPose.action` (used by Nav2 stack).
- **Manipulator motion** — `moveit_msgs/action/MoveGroup.action` (used by MoveIt 2).
- **Aerial drones** — PX4-ROS 2 bridge uses actions for offboard mode, arming, takeoff, landing.
- **Warehouse automation** — Pick-and-place pipelines in Amazon Robotics / Fetch use action interfaces for arm trajectories.

In every case, the engineer **first defines the action** (writes the `.action` file), then generates bindings via `colcon build`, and finally implements the server logic that performs the long-running work while publishing feedback.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Deriving the Action Lifecycle from First Principles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section is **exhaustive**. Every transition is justified. No step is skipped. The derivation below shows *why* the eight legal goal states emerge naturally from the interaction requirements of an asynchronous, preemptible, long-running task.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Identify the Functional Requirements

A long-running robot task (e.g., "drive to the kitchen") must support four requirements:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 &: \text{Initiate the task with parameters (a Goal).} \\
R_2 &: \text{Observe progress while the task runs (Feedback).} \\
R_3 &: \text{Receive a final outcome (Result).} \\
R_4 &: \text{Allow the user to stop the task mid-flight (Cancel).}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Map Requirements to Message Types

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mapping:} \quad R_1 \mapsto \text{Goal message} \quad R_2 \mapsto \text{Feedback message} \quad R_3 \mapsto \text{Result message} \quad R_4 \mapsto \text{Cancel request}
\end{aligned}
$$

The `.action` file therefore **must** contain three sections. The Cancel request is a built-in ROS 2 message (`action_msgs/srv/CancelGoal.srv`), not user-defined.

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Define the Goal State Machine

A goal transitions through a finite set of states. The transitions are driven by four events: *acceptance*, *execution start*, *feedback emission*, *termination*.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial} \rightarrow \text{ACCEPTED} \rightarrow \text{EXECUTING} \rightarrow \{\text{SUCCEEDED, ABORTED, CANCELED}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

We also need a *rejection* branch (server refuses the goal) and a *canceling-in-progress* state to handle the race between cancellation and normal termination.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{REJECTED} &: \text{terminal, server refused the goal} \\
\text{CANCELING} &: \text{transient, server is honoring a cancel request}
\end{aligned}
$$

The set of legal states is therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S = \{\text{ACCEPTED, EXECUTING, CANCELING, SUCCEEDED, CANCELED, ABORTED, REJECTED}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Encode State Transitions in the `GoalStatus` Message

Each transition writes a new `GoalStatus` message into the action's status topic. The terminal states are exactly those with **no outgoing transitions**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{terminal}} = \{\text{SUCCEEDED, CANCELED, ABORTED, REJECTED}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **mathematical guarantee** that every action goal eventually terminates — a critical property for real-time robotic control loops that must never hang.

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Aggregate into the Full Lifecycle Equation

The total time a goal occupies a server is the sum of four intervals:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{goal}} = T_{\text{accept}} + T_{\text{execute}} + T_{\text{terminate}} + T_{\text{cleanup}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where each $T_i \geq 0$ and is measured in seconds. The expected number of feedback messages is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{fb}} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{T_{\text{execute}}}{\Delta t_{\text{fb}}} \right\rfloor
\end{aligned}
$$

with $\Delta t_{\text{fb}}$ being the user-configured feedback period.

## 3.2 Derivation — When to Choose an Action Over a Service

A service is appropriate when the **synchronous blocking time is acceptable**. The decision boundary is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Choose Action iff} \quad \Big( T_{\text{exec}} > T_{\text{client\_deadline}} \Big) \lor \Big( \text{feedback required} \Big) \lor \Big( \text{preemption required} \Big)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $T_{\text{client\_deadline}}$ is the maximum time the client can block without losing real-time responsiveness. The disjunction ($\lor$) is inclusive: any one of the three conditions is sufficient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule (KTU 2024):** $T_{\text{client\_deadline}}$ is empirically **$T_{\text{deadline}} \approx 0.1 \text{ s}$** for teleoperation and **$T_{\text{deadline}} \approx 1.0 \text{ s}$** for autonomous mission control. Tasks that exceed these bounds must use actions.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Defining an Action (Step-by-Step Procedure)

The following matrix enumerates the **exact, ordered steps** a KTU student must follow when defining a new action in a ROS 2 package.

| Step | Action | Tool / Command | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Create or locate a ROS 2 package (e.g., `my_robot_interfaces`). | `ros2 pkg create` | Package directory tree. |
| 2 | Create the `action` subdirectory inside the package. | `mkdir action` | `my_robot_interfaces/action/` |
| 3 | Write the `.action` file (e.g., `DriveToPose.action`). | Text editor | `DriveToPose.action` |
| 4 | Declare the action in `CMakeLists.txt` (C++) and `package.xml`. | `rosidl_generate_interfaces` | Build dependency on `rosidl_default_generators`. |
| 5 | Build the package with `colcon`. | `colcon build --packages-select my_robot_interfaces` | Generated C++ headers and Python modules. |
| 6 | Verify with `ros2 interface show`. | `ros2 interface show my_robot_interfaces/action/DriveToPose` | Prints the three sections. |
| 7 | Source the workspace and import in client/server code. | `source install/setup.bash` | Action type available as `DriveToPose` in the respective language. |

### 3.3.1 Worked Specification — `DriveToPose.action`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Goal: } & \quad \texttt{geometry\_msgs/msg/PoseStamped target\_pose} \\
\text{Result: } & \quad \texttt{builtin\_interfaces/msg/Duration total\_elapsed\_time} \\
\text{Feedback: } & \quad \texttt{geometry\_msgs/msg/PoseStamped current\_pose} \\
\text{Feedback: } & \quad \texttt{float32 distance\_remaining}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Plain-text representation of the file:**

```
# Goal: destination pose for the mobile base
geometry_msgs/msg/PoseStamped target_pose
---
# Result: total elapsed time upon completion
builtin_interfaces/msg/Duration total_elapsed_time
---
# Feedback: periodic updates on the robot's progress
geometry_msgs/msg/PoseStamped current_pose
float32 distance_remaining
```

### 3.3.2 Build-System Wiring (Conceptual)

In `CMakeLists.txt`, the action must be registered with the ROS interface generator:

```
rosidl_generate_interfaces(${PROJECT_NAME}
  "action/DriveToPose.action"
  DEPENDENCIES geometry_msgs builtin_interfaces
)
```

In `package.xml`, the build-time and exec-time dependencies are declared:

```
<depend>geometry_msgs</depend>
<depend>builtin_interfaces</depend>
<build_depend>rosidl_default_generators</build_depend>
<exec_depend>rosidl_default_runtime</exec_depend>
<member_of_group>rosidl_interface_packages</member_of_group>
```

> [!NOTE]
> These fragments are **declarative build configuration**, not application logic. They are part of the ROS 2 interface-generation pipeline and are the standard pattern across all ROS 2 packages.

## 3.4 Verification — Counting the Three Sections

A common KTU 2024 exam question asks: *"How many sections does an `.action` file contain, and how are they separated?"*

**Derivation of the answer:**

1. An action must convey three logically distinct payloads: a request, intermediate updates, and a final response.
2. Each payload is a separate message type and must be independently generated by the IDL compiler.
3. Therefore the file requires **three sections**, separated by **two delimiter lines**.
4. The standard delimiter line is `---` (three ASCII hyphens, U+002D), consistent with the `.srv` file format.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of sections} &= 3 \\
\text{Number of delimiters} &= 2 \\
\text{Delimiter string} &= \texttt{---} \quad (\text{three hyphens})
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the canonical, exam-ready formulation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Action Client / Action Server Communication Flow

The following diagram maps the **full message exchange** between an action client and an action server, including the goal, feedback stream, result, and cancellation paths.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ActionClient node] -- "1. send_goal: Goal message" --> B[ActionServer node]
    B -- "2. goal_response: ACCEPTED / REJECTED" --> A
    B -- "3. feedback stream: partial_sequence (repeated)" --> A
    A -- "4. cancel_request (optional)" --> B
    B -- "5. cancel_response: CANCELING" --> A
    B -- "6. result: final sequence" --> A
    A -- "7. status updates: GoalStatus msgs" --> A
    B -- "7. status updates: GoalStatus msgs" --> B

    subgraph MiddlewareLayer [DDS Middleware under the hood]
        T1[Topic: _action/goal]
        T2[Topic: _action/feedback]
        T3[Topic: _action/result]
        T4[Topic: _action/status]
        S1[Service: _action/cancel_goal]
    end
```

**Reading guide:**
- Steps **1–2** are the goal handshake.
- Step **3** is the streaming feedback channel.
- Steps **4–5** are the optional preemption path.
- Step **6** is the terminal result delivery.
- The `MiddlewareLayer` subgraph shows the **seven DDS topics and one service** that ROS 2 automatically creates — this is an *implementation detail* and is normally hidden from the developer.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Goal State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ACCEPTED : client sends goal, server accepts
    ACCEPTED --> EXECUTING : server begins work
    ACCEPTED --> REJECTED : server refuses goal
    EXECUTING --> SUCCEEDED : task completes successfully
    EXECUTING --> ABORTED : unrecoverable error
    EXECUTING --> CANCELING : client requests cancel
    CANCELING --> CANCELED : server stops gracefully
    CANCELING --> SUCCEEDED : race: task finishes during cancel
    SUCCEEDED --> [*]
    ABORTED --> [*]
    CANCELED --> [*]
    REJECTED --> [*]
```

**Reading guide:** Only four states are **terminal** (no outgoing arrows): `SUCCEEDED`, `ABORTED`, `CANCELED`, `REJECTED`. This matches the state set derived in Section 3.1.4.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Action Definition

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph StepA [Step 1: Author the .action file]
        A1[Write DriveToPose.action] --> A2[Three sections: Goal, Result, Feedback]
    end
    subgraph StepB [Step 2: Register in build system]
        B1[Edit CMakeLists.txt] --> B2[Edit package.xml]
    end
    subgraph StepC [Step 3: Generate language bindings]
        C1[colcon build] --> C2[C++ headers in include/]
        C1 --> C3[Python modules in module_name/]
    end
    subgraph StepD [Step 4: Implement runtime nodes]
        D1[Action Server: DriveToPoseServer] --> D2[Action Client: DriveToPoseClient]
    end
    A2 --> B1
    B2 --> C1
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D2
    D1 -- "Goal / Feedback / Result" --> D2
    D2 -- "Goal / Cancel" --> D1
```

**Reading guide:** This is a **four-stage build pipeline**. Stages 1–3 occur at **compile time**; stage 4 occurs at **runtime**. The arrows between `D1` and `D2` represent the actual ROS 2 message flow at runtime.

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Topic vs Service vs Action Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the data a continuous stream?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> P1[Use a Topic]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is the task short and atomic?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> P2[Use a Service]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Does the task need feedback or cancellation?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> P3[Use an Action]
    Q3 -- No --> P2
```

**Reading guide:** This decision tree is the **KTU 2024 recommended flowchart** for choosing the correct communication primitive during exam answers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question carries **3 marks**. Model answers below are calibrated to the expected length (~80–120 words).

### Question 1: Define an Action in ROS 2. List its three constituent message types.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **action** in ROS 2 is an asynchronous communication paradigm designed for **long-running, goal-directed robotic tasks** that need continuous progress feedback and the ability to be canceled. It is defined in an `.action` interface file that contains three distinct message sections:

1. **Goal** — the request sent by the client (e.g., target pose, desired angle).
2. **Result** — the terminal response returned when the task ends (success, abort, or cancel).
3. **Feedback** — periodic intermediate updates streamed by the server during execution.

These three sections are separated by `---` delimiter lines. The action is consumed by an **action client** and an **action server** node, exchanging messages over the DDS middleware. Actions are the preferred primitive for tasks longer than one second or for any task requiring **preemption**.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between a ROS 2 Service and an Action.
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Service | Action |
|---|---|---|
| Blocking | Yes (synchronous) | No (asynchronous) |
| Feedback | None | Streamed |
| Cancellation | Not supported | Supported |
| Suitable for | Quick atomic operations (< 1 s) | Long-running tasks with progress reporting |

A **service** is a synchronous request-reply exchange best suited to short, atomic operations such as toggling a gripper or querying a sensor reading. An **action**, in contrast, is asynchronous, allows the client to continue other work while the server executes the task, streams feedback, and supports **preemption (cancellation)**. For example, "navigate to the kitchen" is an action, but "save the current map" is a service.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Part B questions provide an **internal choice** — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both questions are provided below with complete model solutions. Each carries 14 marks, typically split into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an action client and an action server. Describe the message flow between them.

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture Overview (3 marks):** An action involves two endpoints: an **Action Client** node and an **Action Server** node. The client sends a **Goal**, the server streams **Feedback** messages, and finally returns a **Result**. ROS 2 implements this on top of **DDS** by transparently creating **seven topics and one service** for each action pair, but the developer uses the high-level client/server API.

**Message Flow (4 marks):**

1. **Goal Dispatch** — The client invokes `async_send_goal(goal_msg)`. The client library publishes the goal on the `.../goal` topic.
2. **Goal Acceptance** — The server receives the goal, validates it, and publishes a `GoalStatus` of `ACCEPTED` (or `REJECTED` if invalid).
3. **Execution & Feedback** — The server executes the underlying task in a loop (e.g., publishing velocity commands), calling `publish_feedback(feedback_msg)` at a fixed rate.
4. **Cancellation (optional)** — At any point during `EXECUTING`, the client may call `async_cancel_goal()`. The server transitions to `CANCELING`, halts the task, and transitions to `CANCELED`.
5. **Result Delivery** — On successful completion, the server calls `succeed()` and publishes the final `Result` message on the `.../result` topic. The client's future resolves, and the result is available via the goal handle.

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid flow).** The diagram shows the client on the left, the server on the right, the seven DDS topics in the middle, and the directional flow of Goal, Feedback, Result, and Cancel messages.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Define a ROS 2 action interface file for the following scenario: a robotic arm must move its end-effector to a target Cartesian pose. The action must report the current end-effector pose periodically and return the total time taken upon completion.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Create the `.action` file named `MoveEndEffector.action`** (3 marks for content, 1 mark for correct delimiter usage, 1 mark for type choice, 2 marks for completeness).

```
# Goal: target Cartesian pose for the end-effector
geometry_msgs/msg/Pose target_pose
---
# Result: total elapsed time upon completion
builtin_interfaces/msg/Duration total_elapsed_time
---
# Feedback: current end-effector pose and time elapsed so far
geometry_msgs/msg/Pose current_pose
builtin_interfaces/msg/Duration time_elapsed
```

**Step 2: Register in `CMakeLists.txt`** (1 mark):

```
rosidl_generate_interfaces(${PROJECT_NAME}
  "action/MoveEndEffector.action"
  DEPENDENCIES geometry_msgs builtin_interfaces
)
```

**Step 3: Add dependencies in `package.xml`** (1 mark):

```
<depend>geometry_msgs</depend>
<depend>builtin_interfaces</depend>
<build_depend>rosidl_default_generators</build_depend>
<exec_depend>rosidl_default_runtime</exec_depend>
<member_of_group>rosidl_interface_packages</member_of_group>
```

**Step 4: Build and verify** (1 mark):

```bash
colcon build --packages-select my_robot_interfaces
ros2 interface show my_robot_interfaces/action/MoveEndEffector
```

The action is now usable. The server will populate `current_pose` in the feedback loop (e.g., at 10 Hz) and finally set `total_elapsed_time` when `succeed()` is called.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) [7 Marks] Compare and contrast the three ROS 2 communication primitives: Topic, Service, and Action. Provide one example use case for each.

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Property | Topic | Service | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pattern | Pub/Sub | Request/Reply | Goal/Feedback/Result |
| Blocking | No | Yes (synchronous) | No (asynchronous) |
| Feedback | Continuous stream | None | Structured periodic |
| Cancellation | Not supported | Not supported | **Supported** |
| Task duration | Any (streaming) | < 1 s recommended | > 1 s up to hours |
| Result returned | No | One reply | One terminal Result |
| Identifier | Topic name | Service name | Action name (namespace) |

**Example Use Cases (2 marks):**

- **Topic** — `sensor_msgs/msg/LaserScan` published by a 2D LIDAR at 40 Hz. The publisher is anonymous; any number of subscribers (mapping, obstacle detection, visualization) can listen.
- **Service** — `std_srvs/srv/Trigger` named `/save_map` that serializes the current occupancy grid to disk. The call blocks until the file write completes (typically < 500 ms).
- **Action** — `nav2_msgs/action/NavigateToPose` used to send a mobile robot to a goal in a map. The server streams the robot's current pose as feedback and the client may cancel the goal if the robot is rerouted.

**Conclusion:** The choice of primitive is dictated by **task duration**, **need for feedback**, and **need for preemption**. The decision tree in Section 4.4 formalizes this choice.

#### (b) [7 Marks] List and explain the eight legal states of a ROS 2 action goal. Draw the goal state machine.

**Model Answer:**

**State List (4 marks):**

1. **`ACCEPTED` (code 1)** — The server has received the goal and has agreed to attempt it. No work has begun yet.
2. **`EXECUTING` (code 2)** — The server is actively working on the goal; feedback messages are being published.
3. **`CANCELING` (code 3)** — The client has requested cancellation; the server is winding down the task gracefully.
4. **`SUCCEEDED` (code 4)** — Terminal state: the goal completed successfully and a `Result` is available.
5. **`CANCELED` (code 5)** — Terminal state: the goal was canceled before natural completion.
6. **`ABORTED` (code 6)** — Terminal state: the server terminated the goal due to an unrecoverable internal error (e.g., hardware fault).
7. **`REJECTED` (code 7)** — Terminal state: the server refused the goal at submission time (e.g., invalid parameters, resource conflict).

(The eighth state, code `0` `UNKNOWN`, is reserved and not used in normal operation.)

**State Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to the Mermaid `stateDiagram-v2` block in Section 4.2. The diagram shows the **seven legal non-terminal transitions** and the **four terminal states** with no outgoing arrows.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating "action" with "action server."** The `.action` file is the *interface*; the server is the *runtime component*. A 2-mark deduction is typical for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the Cancel path.** A state diagram that omits the `CANCELING → CANCELED` transition loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Writing "actions use services" — wrong.** Actions are *implemented* using a combination of **seven topics and one service** under the hood, not a single service.
> 4. **Forgetting the `---` delimiter syntax.** The `.action` file uses the *same* delimiter as `.srv`, but with **two** delimiters (not one). Missing this loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing feedback period with feedback count.** A common derivation error: students write $N_{\text{fb}} = T_{\text{exec}} \cdot \Delta t_{\text{fb}}$ instead of $N_{\text{fb}} = T_{\text{exec}} / \Delta t_{\text{fb}}$. Remember the **inverse** relationship.
> 6. **Saying "Action = Service + Topic" — partially correct but imprecise.** A better phrasing is "Action combines the request-reply structure of a service with the streaming structure of a topic, **plus** preemption."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this checklist for last-minute revision before the KTU 2024 University Exam.**

- [ ] An **action** is defined by an `.action` file containing **three sections**: Goal, Result, Feedback.
- [ ] The delimiter line is `---` (three hyphens). The action file uses **two** such delimiters.
- [ ] An action involves an **action client** and an **action server** — these are **two separate nodes**.
- [ ] The seven legal goal states are: `ACCEPTED, EXECUTING, CANCELING, SUCCEEDED, CANCELED, ABORTED, REJECTED`.
- [ ] Of these, **four are terminal** (no outgoing transitions): `SUCCEEDED, CANCELED, ABORTED, REJECTED`.
- [ ] **Feedback** is a streamed, optional, intermediate message published by the server.
- [ ] **Cancellation** is the unique capability of an action — neither topics nor services support it.
- [ ] Use an action when: $T_{\text{exec}} > 1\text{ s}$ **OR** feedback is required **OR** preemption is required.
- [ ] Use a service for: short, atomic, blocking operations (< 1 s).
- [ ] Use a topic for: continuous, anonymous, one-way streaming (sensors, status broadcasts).
- [ ] ROS 2 implements actions via **7 DDS topics + 1 service** under the hood, transparently to the user.
- [ ] Build-system wiring: edit `CMakeLists.txt` with `rosidl_generate_interfaces(...)` and `package.xml` with `<depend>...</depend>` tags.
- [ ] Verify an action with: `ros2 interface show <pkg>/action/<ActionName>.action`.
- [ ] The feedback rate is user-defined: $f_{\text{fb}} = 1 / \Delta t_{\text{fb}}$; number of feedback messages: $N_{\text{fb}} = T_{\text{exec}} / \Delta t_{\text{fb}}$.
- [ ] Common ROS 2 actions you will see in Nav2 and MoveIt 2: `NavigateToPose`, `MoveGroup`, `FollowPath`, `RotateAbsolute`.
- [ ] **Mnemonic:** **G**oal → **F**eedback → **R**esult → **C**ancel — the **"GFRC"** order of an action interaction.
- [ ] **Exam trap:** Never say "an action is a service." Say "an action is a **first-class communication primitive** in ROS 2 that *internally leverages* DDS topics and services."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementing a Basic Action Server
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT525 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Implementing a Basic Action Server

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **ROS Action Server (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> An **Action Server** in the Robot Operating System (ROS) is a long-running, goal-oriented communication node that executes a specific task over an extended period of time. It is built on top of the ROS **Actionlib** package and follows a strict **Client–Server communication paradigm** using a custom `.action` interface definition.

Unlike standard ROS Topics (which are anonymous, fire-and-forget) or Services (which are synchronous and blocking), an Action Server is designed for tasks that are:
- **Long-running** (seconds to hours)
- **Cancellable** by the client
- **Preemptable** (can be replaced by a newer goal)
- **Provide continuous feedback** during execution
- **Return a final result** upon completion

The communication is governed by a **quintuple tuple** of messages:

$$\text{Action Interface} = \{Goal,\;Feedback,\;Result,\;ActionGoal,\;ActionResult,\;ActionFeedback\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Action Server uses **three distinct message types** internally — `ActionGoal`, `ActionResult`, and `ActionFeedback` — which wrap the user-defined Goal, Result, and Feedback messages. This wrapping allows ROS to add standard metadata (status, timestamps, header).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen" Model

Imagine you walk into a busy restaurant:

| Restaurant Element | ROS Action Equivalent |
|---|---|
| **You (Customer)** | **Action Client** |
| **The Waiter** | **ROS Communication Layer (Action Protocol)** |
| **The Kitchen** | **Action Server** |
| **Your Order** | **Goal Message** |
| **"Your food is being prepared" updates** | **Feedback Messages** |
| **The final dish served** | **Result Message** |
| **"I changed my mind, cancel!"** | **Preemption / Cancellation Request** |
| **Order Ticket Status (Pending/Active/Done)** | **Action Status State Machine** |

**How the flow works:**
1. You (Client) give the waiter a detailed order (**Goal**).
2. The kitchen (Server) acknowledges it and starts cooking.
3. Every few minutes, the waiter updates you — *"Soups are ready, main course in 5 mins"* (**Feedback**).
4. Finally, the dish arrives (**Result**).
5. If you leave mid-way, the order is **cancelled** (Preemption).

This is precisely how a robot arm picking up an object, a drone flying to a waypoint, or a navigation stack moving to a coordinate works in ROS.

---

## 1.3 State Machine of an Action Server

An Action Server internally transitions through **7 well-defined states**:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Seven Canonical States (as per actionlib specification):**
> 1. **PENDING** — Goal received but not yet picked up for processing.
> 2. **ACTIVE** — Goal is currently being executed.
> 3. **PREEMPTED** — Goal was cancelled by client before completion.
> 4. **SUCCEEDED** — Goal completed successfully.
> 5. **ABORTED** — Goal failed during execution (e.g., collision).
> 6. **REJECTED** — Server refused to accept the goal.
> 7. **RECALLED** — Goal was cancelled before it became active (in PENDING state).
> 8. **LOST** — Communication with server was lost mid-execution.

The terminal states are: `SUCCEEDED`, `PREEMPTED`, `ABORTED`, `REJECTED`, `RECALLED`, `LOST`.

---

## 1.4 Why Use Action Servers? (vs Topics and Services)

| Feature | Topic | Service | **Action Server** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Communication Pattern | Many-to-Many | 1-to-1 (Req/Reply) | **1-to-1 (Long-running)** |
| Feedback | ❌ No | ❌ No | **✅ Continuous Feedback** |
| Preemption | ❌ No | ❌ No | **✅ Yes** |
| Blocking | Non-blocking | Blocking | **Non-blocking with result polling** |
| Use Case | Sensor streams | Quick calculations | **Navigation, Manipulation, Trajectories** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 The `.action` File — The Heart of Action Interface

In ROS, the action interface is declared in a custom `.action` file (typically placed inside the `action/` folder of a package).

### 2.1.1 Anatomy of an `.action` File

```text
# Define the goal
int32 target_position
---
# Define the result
int32 final_position
float32 elapsed_time
---
# Define the feedback
int32 current_position
float32 percent_complete
```

The three sections separated by `---` are:
- **Goal** — Input the client sends.
- **Result** — Final output the server returns.
- **Feedback** — Periodic updates during execution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked *"Define the .action file structure"*, you MUST mention the three sections and the `---` delimiter. This is a recurring 3-mark question.

### 2.1.2 Auto-Generated Messages

When you build the package, ROS automatically generates **6 message types** in the `actionlib_msgs` namespace:

$$\text{Generated Types} = \{ActionGoal,\;ActionResult,\;ActionFeedback,\;GoalID,\;GoalStatus,\;GoalStatusArray\}$$

These contain the user-defined fields plus a standard **`Header`**, **`goal_id`**, and **`status`** enum.

---

## 2.2 Communication Topology

The Action Server and Client communicate over **three implicit topics** under the hood:

| Internal Topic | Direction | Message Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| `namespace/goal` | Client → Server | `<ActionName>ActionGoal` | Submit new goals |
| `namespace/cancel` | Client → Server | `actionlib_msgs/GoalID` | Cancel a specific goal |
| `namespace/feedback` | Server → Client | `<ActionName>ActionFeedback` | Periodic progress updates |
| `namespace/result` | Server → Client | `<ActionName>ActionResult` | Final outcome |
| `namespace/status` | Server → Client | `actionlib_msgs/GoalStatusArray` | Status updates for all goals |

> [!NOTE]
> The `namespace` is the action name (e.g., `/fibonacci`, `/move_base`, `/turtle_move`).

---

## 2.3 The Two Communication Models: GoalHandle & SimpleActionServer

ROS provides **two API styles** to write an Action Server:

### Model A: `SimpleActionServer` (Callback-based — Easy)
- The server calls a **goal callback** when a goal arrives.
- The server calls a **preempt callback** when a cancel request arrives.
- You must launch a **separate execution thread** (or use the auto-start mode).

### Model B: `ActionServer` (GoalHandle-based — Advanced)
- Manual control using a `ServerGoalHandle` object.
- You explicitly call `acceptNewGoal()`, `setSucceeded()`, `setAborted()`.
- Suitable for **multi-threaded, complex, real-world robotic systems**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus:** The syllabus emphasizes the **`SimpleActionServer`** model for the basic implementation. Always default to this in exam answers unless specifically asked for advanced control.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

| **Concept** | **Definition / Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Action Server | Node that executes long-duration tasks | `actionlib` package |
| Action Client | Node that submits goals to the server | Same `actionlib` package |
| Goal | User-defined input request | Custom fields |
| Feedback | Periodic update during execution | Sent at **≥ 1 Hz** typically |
| Result | Terminal output | Sent **once** on completion |
| Preemption | Cancellation of an active goal | True/False boolean |
| State Machine States | 8 states (7 in KTU books) | Enum: PENDING, ACTIVE, etc. |
| `.action` file location | `<package>/action/<name>.action` | Compiled by `actionlib_msgs` |
| Time-to-Complete | $T_{\text{end}} - T_{\text{start}}$ | Seconds (s) |
| Feedback Frequency | $f_{\text{feedback}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{updates}}}{T_{\text{total}}}$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| Goal Success Condition | `setSucceeded()` called + Result sent | Terminal state |
| Goal Failure Condition | `setAborted()` called | Terminal state |
| Preempt Condition | `isPreemptRequested()` returns `true` | Terminal state |
| Action Namespace | Matches server's advertised action name | `~action_name` ROS param |
| Server Advertise Call | `as.start()` | Required for callback model |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry:**
> - **Autonomous Vehicles (Self-Driving Cars):** Action server is used by the `move_base` package to navigate from point A to point B, sending feedback like *"distance to goal: 45m, obstacle detected ahead"*.
> - **Industrial Robotic Arms:** Welding, painting, pick-and-place tasks where duration is in seconds and intermediate progress must be monitored.
> - **Aerial Drones (MAVROS):** Waypoint navigation where the drone may need to be re-routed mid-flight.
> - **Warehouse Robots (AMRs):** Material handling where orders can be dynamically cancelled.

The Action Server pattern is the **backbone of any time-extended, feedback-critical robotic operation**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Implementation Walkthrough

This section provides a **complete, compilable, line-by-line explanation** of building a basic Action Server in ROS (Noetic) using the `SimpleActionServer` model in **C++**. A Python version is also provided for clarity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Rule:** You may write the answer in **either C++ or Python**. The C++ version requires you to show the `CMakeLists.txt` modifications. The Python version does not need a build step — making it **shorter to write in an exam**.

---

## 3.1 Step 1 — Create the Package with Required Dependencies

Open a terminal in your ROS workspace (`~/catkin_ws/src`) and execute:

```bash
cd ~/catkin_ws/src
catkin_create_pkg my_action_server roscpp rospy actionlib actionlib_msgs std_msgs
```

**Explanation of flags:**
- `roscpp` — C++ client library for ROS.
- `rospy` — Python client library (needed even for C++ servers if Python clients must connect).
- `actionlib` — The core action server/client library.
- `actionlib_msgs` — Standard message types (GoalID, GoalStatus, etc.).
- `std_msgs` — Standard primitive types (int, float, string).

---

## 3.2 Step 2 — Define the `.action` File

Create the directory and the action file:

```bash
mkdir -p ~/catkin_ws/src/my_action_server/action
touch ~/catkin_ws/src/my_action_server/action/CountUntil.action
```

Edit `CountUntil.action`:

```text
# Goal: Count from 0 to a target value
int32 target_count
float32 rate_hz
---
# Result: Final outcome
int32 final_count
float32 total_elapsed_time
bool success
---
# Feedback: Periodic updates
int32 current_count
float32 percent_complete
float32 time_remaining
```

**Field-by-field meaning:**
- `target_count` — How high the server should count.
- `rate_hz` — How fast the server should count (publishing frequency).
- `final_count` — The actual count reached.
- `total_elapsed_time` — How long the task took in seconds.
- `success` — True if reached `target_count`, false if cancelled.
- `current_count` — Live counter value.
- `percent_complete` — $\dfrac{\text{current\_count}}{\text{target\_count}} \times 100$.
- `time_remaining` — Estimated seconds left.

---

## 3.3 Step 3 — Modify `package.xml` to Declare Action Generation

Add the following to `package.xml` so `catkin` knows to generate the action messages:

```xml
<build_depend>actionlib</build_depend>
<build_depend>actionlib_msgs</build_depend>
<exec_depend>actionlib</exec_depend>
<exec_depend>actionlib_msgs</exec_depend>
```

---

## 3.4 Step 4 — Modify `CMakeLists.txt`

Append the following inside `CMakeLists.txt` (above the `catkin_package(...)` call):

```cmake
add_action_files(
   FILES
   CountUntil.action
)

generate_messages(
   DEPENDENCIES
   actionlib_msgs
   std_msgs
)
```

Also ensure your `catkin_package()` line has `CATKIN_DEPENDS actionlib_msgs`.

---

## 3.5 Step 5 — Build the Package

```bash
cd ~/catkin_ws
catkin_make
source devel/setup.bash
```

After this, ROS will auto-generate the following headers in `devel/include/my_action_server/`:
- `CountUntilAction.h`
- `CountUntilGoal.h`
- `CountUntilResult.h`
- `CountUntilFeedback.h`
- `CountUntilActionGoal.h` (wrapped version)

---

## 3.6 Step 6 — Write the C++ Action Server (`count_server.cpp`)

Create a `src/` folder and the source file:

```cpp
#include <ros/ros.h>
#include <actionlib/server/simple_action_server.h>
#include <my_action_server/CountUntilAction.h>

class CountUntilAction {
protected:
    ros::NodeHandle nh_;
    actionlib::SimpleActionServer<my_action_server::CountUntilAction> as_;
    std::string action_name_;

    // Create messages that are used to publish feedback and result
    my_action_server::CountUntilFeedback feedback_;
    my_action_server::CountUntilResult   result_;

    int counter_;
    bool stop_requested_;

public:
    // Constructor
    CountUntilAction(std::string name) :
        as_(nh_, name, boost::bind(&CountUntilAction::executeCB, this, _1), false),
        action_name_(name)
    {
        as_.registerPreemptCallback(boost::bind(&CountUntilAction::preemptCB, this));
        as_.start();
        ROS_INFO("Action Server %s started. Waiting for goals...", action_name_.c_str());
    }

    ~CountUntilAction(void) {}

    // Preemption Callback (called when client cancels)
    void preemptCB() {
        ROS_WARN("[%s] Preempt requested by client. Halting execution.", action_name_.c_str());
        stop_requested_ = true;
    }

    // Goal Execution Callback
    void executeCB(const my_action_server::CountUntilGoalConstPtr &goal) {
        ROS_INFO("[%s] Received goal: count to %d at %.2f Hz",
                 action_name_.c_str(), goal->target_count, goal->rate_hz);

        // Validation: reject impossible goals
        if (goal->target_count <= 0 || goal->rate_hz <= 0.0) {
            ROS_ERROR("[%s] Invalid goal. Rejecting.", action_name_.c_str());
            result_.success = false;
            as_.setAborted(result_);
            return;
        }

        ros::Rate loop_rate(goal->rate_hz);
        counter_           = 0;
        stop_requested_    = false;
        float start_time   = ros::Time::now().toSec();

        while (ros::ok()) {
            // Check for client preemption
            if (as_.isPreemptRequested() || stop_requested_) {
                result_.final_count         = counter_;
                result_.total_elapsed_time  = ros::Time::now().toSec() - start_time;
                result_.success             = false;
                as_.setPreempted(result_);
                ROS_INFO("[%s] Goal preempted at count %d.", action_name_.c_str(), counter_);
                return;
            }

            // Increment and publish feedback
            counter_++;
            feedback_.current_count    = counter_;
            feedback_.percent_complete = (float(counter_) / float(goal->target_count)) * 100.0f;
            feedback_.time_remaining   = (goal->target_count - counter_) / goal->rate_hz;
            as_.publishFeedback(feedback_);

            ROS_INFO("[%s] Count: %d (%.1f%% complete)", action_name_.c_str(),
                     counter_, feedback_.percent_complete);

            // Completion check
            if (counter_ >= goal->target_count) {
                result_.final_count         = counter_;
                result_.total_elapsed_time  = ros::Time::now().toSec() - start_time;
                result_.success             = true;
                ROS_INFO("[%s] Goal SUCCEEDED. Count: %d, Time: %.2f s",
                         action_name_.c_str(), result_.final_count, result_.total_elapsed_time);
                as_.setSucceeded(result_);
                return;
            }

            loop_rate.sleep();
        }
    }
};

int main(int argc, char** argv) {
    ros::init(argc, argv, "count_until_server");
    CountUntilAction count_server("count_until");
    ros::spin();
    return 0;
}
```

### 3.6.1 Exhaustive Code Walkthrough

| Line Block | Purpose / Logic |
|---|---|
| `#include <actionlib/server/simple_action_server.h>` | Pulls in the `SimpleActionServer` class template. |
| `actionlib::SimpleActionServer<...> as_` | Templated server class specialized for our `CountUntilAction` type. |
| `boost::bind(&CountUntilAction::executeCB, this, _1)` | Binds the goal callback. The `_1` placeholder holds the incoming goal. |
| `registerPreemptCallback(...)` | Hooks a separate function to handle cancel requests. |
| `as_.start()` | Activates the server — must be called or it won't accept goals. |
| `as_.isPreemptRequested()` | Checks every loop iteration if a cancel came in. |
| `as_.publishFeedback(feedback_)` | Pushes the current feedback to the client. |
| `as_.setSucceeded(result_)` | Marks goal as **SUCCEEDED** and publishes the result. |
| `as_.setAborted(result_)` | Marks goal as **ABORTED** (used for validation failures). |
| `as_.setPreempted(result_)` | Marks goal as **PREEMPTED** (cancelled mid-execution). |
| `ros::Rate loop_rate(goal->rate_hz)` | Maintains a fixed loop frequency from the goal parameter. |
| `loop_rate.sleep()` | Blocks to maintain the desired rate. |

### 3.6.2 Add Executable in `CMakeLists.txt`

Append at the bottom of `CMakeLists.txt`:

```cmake

add_dependencies(count_server ${${PROJECT_NAME}_EXPORTED_TARGETS} ${catkin_EXPORTED_TARGETS})
target_link_libraries(count_server ${catkin_LIBRARIES})
```

```bash

catkin_make
source devel/setup.bash
```

## 3.7 Step 7 — Write a Simple C++ Action Client (`count_client.cpp`)

```cpp
#include <ros/ros.h>
#include <actionlib/client/simple_action_client.h>
#include <my_action_server/CountUntilAction.h>

typedef actionlib::SimpleActionClient<my_action_server::CountUntilAction> Client;

void doneCb(const actionlib::SimpleClientGoalState &state,
            const my_action_server::CountUntilResultConstPtr &result) {
    ROS_INFO("Finished in state: %s", state.toString().c_str());
    ROS_INFO("Final count: %d, Elapsed: %.2f s, Success: %d",
             result->final_count, result->total_elapsed_time, result->success);
}

void activeCb() {
    ROS_INFO("Goal just went active.");
}

void feedbackCb(const my_action_server::CountUntilFeedbackConstPtr &feedback) {
    ROS_INFO("Feedback: count=%d, %.1f%% complete, %.2f s remaining",
             feedback->current_count, feedback->percent_complete, feedback->time_remaining);
}

int main(int argc, char** argv) {
    ros::init(argc, argv, "count_until_client");
    Client ac("count_until", true);
    ROS_INFO("Waiting for action server to start...");
    ac.waitForServer();

    my_action_server::CountUntilGoal goal;
    goal.target_count = 10;
    goal.rate_hz      = 2.0;

    ROS_INFO("Sending goal: count to %d at %.1f Hz", goal.target_count, goal.rate_hz);
    ac.sendGoal(goal, &doneCb, &activeCb, &feedbackCb);

    // Wait 3 seconds, then optionally cancel
    bool finished_before_timeout = ac.waitForResult(ros::Duration(15.0));
    if (finished_before_timeout) {
        actionlib::SimpleClientGoalState state = ac.getState();
        ROS_INFO("Action finished: %s", state.toString().c_str());
    } else {
        ROS_WARN("Action did not finish before timeout. Cancelling...");
        ac.cancelGoal();
    }
    return 0;
}
```

### 3.7.1 Exhaustive Code Walkthrough

| Line Block | Purpose / Logic |
|---|---|
| `Client ac("count_until", true)` | Creates the client. The `true` flag means **auto-spin a separate thread**. |
| `ac.waitForServer()` | Blocks until the server advertises itself. |
| `ac.sendGoal(goal, doneCb, activeCb, feedbackCb)` | Sends the goal and registers 3 callbacks: on completion, on activation, and on each feedback. |
| `ac.waitForResult(Duration(15.0))` | Waits up to 15 seconds for the result. |
| `ac.cancelGoal()` | Sends a cancellation request to the server. |

Add to `CMakeLists.txt`:

```cmake
add_executable(count_client src/count_client.cpp)
add_dependencies(count_client ${${PROJECT_NAME}_EXPORTED_TARGETS} ${catkin_EXPORTED_TARGETS})
target_link_libraries(count_client ${catkin_LIBRARIES})
```

---
