# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PERUT525]**
Define the term **"Forward Kinematics"** for a robotic manipulator arm. State the standard Denavit-Hartenberg parameters used to model a joint.

**Model Answer:**
Forward Kinematics is the process of computing the **Cartesian pose (position and orientation)** of the end-effector of a manipulator arm, given the **joint variables** $q = [q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n]^T$ as input. Mathematically, it is represented as a homogeneous transformation matrix $T_0^n(q) \in SE(3)$.

The four standard DH parameters used to model a joint $i$ are:
- $\theta_i$: rotation about the $z_{i-1}$ axis.
- $d_i$: translation along the $z_{i-1}$ axis.
- $a_i$: translation along the $x_i$ axis (link length).
- $\alpha_i$: rotation about the $x_i$ axis (link twist).

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PERUT525]**
List any **three differences** between **Revolute** and **Prismatic** joints in a manipulator arm.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Revolute (R) Joint | Prismatic (P) Joint |
|---|---|---|
| Motion Type | Rotational (angular) | Linear (translational) |
| Joint Variable | Angle $\theta_i$ (in radians) | Displacement $d_i$ (in meters) |
| Typical Actuator | Servo / BLDC motor | Linear actuator / lead screw |
| DH Representation | Variable in $\theta_i$ column | Variable in $d_i$ column |
| Example | Elbow, shoulder of a robot arm | Slider in a Cartesian gantry |

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Format)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PERUT525, Module 3 Internal Choice]**
**(a)** For a **2-link planar manipulator** with link lengths $L_1 = 1 \text{ m}$ and $L_2 = 0.8 \text{ m}$, derive the **forward kinematics equations** using the standard DH convention. Clearly state the DH parameter table.

**(b)** With reference to the ROS framework, explain the **role of the `controller_manager`** and the **`joint_trajectory_controller`** in executing a planned trajectory for a 6-DOF manipulator arm. Mention at least two key configuration parameters that must be set in the YAML launch file.

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Define the DH Parameter Table**

| Joint $i$ | $\theta_i$ | $d_i$ | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q_1$ | 0 | $L_1$ | 0 |
| 2 | $q_2$ | 0 | $L_2$ | 0 |

**Step 2: Write Individual Transformations**

$$
A_0^1 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos q_1 & -\sin q_1 & 0 & L_1 \cos q_1 \\ \sin q_1 & \cos q_1 & 0 & L_1 \sin q_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
A_1^2 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos q_2 & -\sin q_2 & 0 & L_2 \cos q_2 \\ \sin q_2 & \cos q_2 & 0 & L_2 \sin q_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: Multiply the Matrices**

Multiplying $A_0^1 \cdot A_1^2$ and extracting the position components, with the substitution $\cos(q_1+q_2) = \cos q_1 \cos q_2 - \sin q_1 \sin q_2$:

$$
x = L_1 \cos(q_1) + L_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2)
$$

$$
y = L_1 \sin(q_1) + L_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2)
$$

$$
\phi = q_1 + q_2
$$

**Step 4: Substitute Numerical Values**

With $L_1 = 1 \text{ m}, L_2 = 0.8 \text{ m}$:

$$
x = 1.0 \cos q_1 + 0.8 \cos(q_1 + q_2) \text{ m}
$$

$$
y = 1.0 \sin q_1 + 0.8 \sin(q_1 + q_2) \text{ m}
$$

These are the final forward kinematics equations.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Role of `controller_manager`**

The `controller_manager` is a **ROS package** (`controller_manager_msgs`) that acts as the **centralized server** for all hardware controllers. It provides services to:
- **Load** a controller (e.g., `joint_trajectory_controller`).
- **Unload** a controller when not needed.
- **Switch** between controllers (e.g., from `position_controllers/JointPositionController` to `effort_controllers/JointPositionController`).
- **List** all active controllers and their states.

It manages **resource conflicts** when multiple controllers attempt to write to the same joint command interface, ensuring **mutual exclusion**.

**Step 2: Role of `joint_trajectory_controller`**

The `joint_trajectory_controller` is a **mid-level controller** that accepts **time-parameterized trajectory waypoints** (position, velocity, acceleration at specified timestamps) and uses an **internal PID loop** to track them. It subscribes to the `FollowJointTrajectory` action and publishes commands at a fixed rate to the hardware interface.

**Step 3: Key YAML Configuration Parameters**

1. **`type: position_controllers/JointTrajectoryController`** — Specifies the controller class.
2. **`joints: [joint1, joint2, joint3, joint4, joint5, joint6]`** — Lists the controlled joints.
3. **`state_publish_rate: 100`** — Sets the joint state publication rate in Hz.
4. **`action_monitor_rate: 20`** — Rate at which the action server checks goal status.
5. **`constraints.goal_time: 0.6`** — Allowed time tolerance for reaching the final waypoint.
6. **`gains.{joint_name}.p: 100.0`** — PID proportional gain (must be tuned per joint).

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PERUT525, Module 3 Internal Choice]**
**(a)** Explain the **concept of singularities** in a robotic manipulator arm. Derive the **Jacobian matrix** for the 2-link planar manipulator from Part A and determine the condition under which a singularity occurs.

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **complete ROS software architecture** for actuating a 6-DOF manipulator arm, starting from the URDF model up to the real motor driver.

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Definition of Singularity**

A **singularity** of a manipulator is a configuration in which the **Jacobian matrix $J(q)$ loses full rank**, i.e., $\det(J(q)) = 0$. At a singularity, the manipulator loses one or more degrees of freedom in Cartesian space, and the inverse velocity mapping becomes unbounded.

**Step 2: Derive the Jacobian**

The Jacobian relates joint velocities to end-effector velocities: $\dot{x} = J(q)\dot{q}$. For the 2-link arm with $x = L_1 \cos q_1 + L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2)$ and $y = L_1 \sin q_1 + L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2)$, we compute the partial derivatives:

$$
J(q) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial q_1} & \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial q_2} \\ \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial q_1} & \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial q_2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 \sin q_1 - L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) & -L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) \\ L_1 \cos q_1 + L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) & L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Determinant**

$$
\det(J) = [-L_1 \sin q_1 - L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2)] \cdot [L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2)] - [-L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2)] \cdot [L_1 \cos q_1 + L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2)]
$$

Expanding and using the identity $\sin(A+B)\cos A - \cos(A+B)\sin A = \sin B$:

$$
\det(J) = L_1 L_2 \sin(q_2)
$$

**Step 4: Singularity Condition**

$$
\det(J) = 0 \iff L_1 L_2 \sin(q_2) = 0 \iff q_2 = 0 \text{ or } q_2 = \pi
$$

This corresponds to the **"arm fully stretched out"** or **"arm fully folded back"** configurations — the two boundary singularities of the planar 2-link arm.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

The complete ROS software architecture for a 6-DOF manipulator is layered as follows:

1. **URDF / xacro Model** — Describes the kinematic and visual structure of the robot, including joint limits, inertial properties, and mesh files. Loaded into the **ROS Parameter Server**.

2. **robot_state_publisher** — Subscribes to `/joint_states` and publishes the **TF (Transform) tree**, which MoveIt! uses to know the current pose of every link.

3. **MoveIt! Node** — The high-level motion planner. It contains:
   - **Planning Scene** (with collision objects).
   - **OMPL / Pilz / STOMP** planners.
   - **KDL / TRAC-IK** solver for inverse kinematics.
   - Outputs a `FollowJointTrajectory` action goal.

4. **controller_manager** — Receives the action goal and dispatches it to the appropriate controller.

5. **joint_trajectory_controller** — Uses internal PID to track the trajectory; publishes commands at $100\text{–}1000 \text{ Hz}$.

6. **hardware_interface** — The plugin that talks to the physical bus (EtherCAT / CAN / USB).

7. **Motor Drivers** — Real hardware that drives the BLDC / servo motors.

8. **Encoders** — Feedback sensors publishing back to `/joint_states`, closing the loop.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Jacobian:** Most students forget the negative sign in the partial derivative of $\cos$, leading to an incorrect Jacobian. **Always re-derive** carefully.
> 2. **Missing the $\sin(q_2) = 0$ condition:** Students often only state $q_2 = 0$ and forget $q_2 = \pi$ (folded-back configuration). Both must be mentioned for full marks.
> 3. **Confusing Modified DH with Standard DH:** In MoveIt! KDL solver, the **Modified DH** convention is used, NOT the standard. State this explicitly if asked about MoveIt! internals.
> 4. **YAML Parameter Confusion:** Students often forget to specify `type`, `joints`, and `gains` in the controller YAML. These are **mandatory** for the controller_manager to load the controller.
> 5. **Singularity ≠ Joint Limit:** Singularity is a **kinematic** property, NOT a joint-limit violation. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this confusion.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU PERUT525 Module 3:**

- **Actuation** in ROS = the layered software + hardware chain from MoveIt! → ros_control → hardware_interface → motor.
- A **manipulator arm** is a serial chain of **rigid links** connected by **joints** (R, P, or F types).
- **DOF** for full spatial tasking is **6**; 3 for position + 3 for orientation.
- **DH parameters** are **4 per joint**: $\theta, d, a, \alpha$. Standard DH uses pre-indexed frame, Modified DH uses post-indexed.
- The **FK** transformation is $T_0^n = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_{i-1}^{i}$.
- The **Jacobian** maps joint velocity to Cartesian velocity: $\dot{x} = J(q)\dot{q}$.
- **Singularities** occur when $\det(J) = 0$; they represent lost DOF in task space.
- The **2-link planar arm** has a closed-form FK and a unique geometric IK using law of cosines + atan2.
- **Cubic polynomial** trajectories ensure position continuity; **quintic** ensures both position AND acceleration continuity.
- In ROS, **`controller_manager`** loads, unloads, and switches controllers; **`joint_trajectory_controller`** tracks planned trajectories via PID.
- The **URDF** is the foundational model — it defines joints, links, visuals, and inertia. MoveIt! consumes this model via the **robot state publisher** and **TF tree**.
- **Repeatability** of industrial arms is typically $\pm 0.02 \text{ mm}$; standard gravity $g = 9.80665 \text{ m/s}^2$.
- **MoveIt!** is the high-level planner; **OMPL** is its default sampling-based planner; **KDL / TRAC-IK** are the default IK solvers.
- The **`FollowJointTrajectory` action** is the standard interface between MoveIt! and the controller manager.
- Standard control loop rates: **$100\text{–}1000 \text{ Hz}$**; standard joint state publication rate: **$50\text{–}100 \text{ Hz}$**.
- **PID gains** must be tuned per joint; a poorly tuned `Kp` leads to oscillation, while a low `Kd` causes overshoot.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT525 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the **Robot Operating System (ROS)** context, **Sensors** are physical or virtual devices that measure a physical quantity (such as distance, light, acceleration, angular velocity, or magnetic field) and convert it into a signal that can be interpreted by a ROS node, typically published as a standardized message on a ROS topic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A *sensor in ROS* is defined as a hardware abstraction layer that exposes a physical measurement to the ROS computation graph through a `sensor_msgs` message type, a defined `frame_id` in the TF tree, and an associated coordinate convention compliant with **REP-103 (Standard Units of Measure and Coordinate Conventions)** and **REP-105 (Coordinate Frames for Mobile Platforms)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PERUT525**, sensors are studied under the following layered understanding:

1. **Hardware Layer** – the physical transducer (e.g., LiDAR photodiode, MEMS accelerometer, CCD/CMOS pixel array).
2. **Driver Layer** – a ROS package (or `rosserial`/`ros2_control` plugin) that talks to the hardware over USB, Ethernet, I²C, SPI, or CAN.
3. **Message Layer** – the standardized `sensor_msgs/*` data structures.
4. **Processing Layer** – downstream nodes performing filtering, fusion, perception.
5. **Visualization Layer** – tools like `rviz2`, `rqt_plot`, `rqt_image_view` displaying the data.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Five Senses of a Robot"

Imagine a **human baby** learning to navigate a room with closed eyes, then opening them one sense at a time.

| Human Sense | Robotic Equivalent | ROS Message |
|-------------|-------------------|-------------|
| Eyes (vision) | RGB / Stereo / Depth Camera | `sensor_msgs/Image`, `sensor_msgs/PointCloud2` |
| Ears (sound) | Microphone array / Ultrasonic | `sensor_msgs/Range`, `audio_common_msgs/AudioData` |
| Touch (proximity) | LiDAR / IR / Time-of-Flight | `sensor_msgs/LaserScan`, `sensor_msgs/PointCloud2` |
| Inner-ear (balance) | IMU (Accel + Gyro + Mag) | `sensor_msgs/Imu` |
| Skin (force) | Force/Torque sensor | `geometry_msgs/WrenchStamped` |

Just as a baby builds an *internal model* of the world by fusing all five senses, a robot builds its world model by **fusing** multiple sensor streams inside a single ROS computation graph.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** ROS does **not** "own" the sensor. ROS is the *nervous system* that carries the electrical impulse (the message) from the sense organ (the driver) to the brain (the perception/fusion node). Without the standardized `sensor_msgs` package, every sensor would speak a different language, and the brain could not generalize.

## 1.3 Classification of Sensors

Sensors in robotics are classified along three orthogonal axes — a topic that is a **favourite KTU Part-A question** and an ESE favourite for short notes.

### 1.3.1 By Information Source
- **Proprioceptive Sensors** – measure the robot's *internal* state (e.g., wheel encoders, joint encoders, IMU, battery voltage).
- **Exteroceptive Sensors** – measure the robot's *external* environment (e.g., camera, LiDAR, GPS, ultrasonic).

### 1.3.2 By Energy Emission
- **Active Sensors** – emit energy and measure the reflection (LiDAR, Radar, Sonar, Structured-Light depth cameras).
- **Passive Sensors** – only receive ambient energy (cameras, microphones, magnetometers, GPS receivers).

### 1.3.3 By Measurement Type
- **Distance / Range** (1-D, 2-D, 3-D)
- **Visual / Imaging** (intensity, colour, depth)
- **Inertial** (linear acceleration, angular velocity, orientation)
- **Position / Localization** (GPS, wheel odometry)
- **Force / Torque** (joint torque, end-effector wrench)

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic (KTU Quick-Recall):**
> **P**roprio vs **E**xtero (internal vs external)
> **A**ctive vs **P**assive (emits vs receives)
> These two axes cross-multiply to give **4 canonical sensor categories** that examiners love to test.

## 1.4 Standard Sensor Performance Metrics

The following quantitative metrics are **bold-defined** in the KTU syllabus and must be memorized for numerical problems:

- **Range** – minimum and maximum measurable distance (e.g., Hokuyo URG-04LX: **60 mm to 4000 mm**).
- **Resolution** – smallest detectable change in the measured quantity.
- **Accuracy** – closeness of measurement to the true value.
- **Precision / Repeatability** – consistency of repeated measurements.
- **Field of View (FoV)** – angular extent of the sensing cone (e.g., RealSense D435 RGB FoV: **87° ± 3°** horizontal).
- **Update Rate / Frequency** – Hz at which messages are published.
- **Latency** – time delay between physical event and message publication.
- **Noise Density** – statistical noise per √Hz (critical for IMU modelling).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Standard ROS Sensor Pipeline

Every ROS sensor follows the same five-stage pipeline. Understanding this pipeline is the **single most important concept** for ESE Module-3 questions.

### Stage 1 — Hardware Transduction
The physical phenomenon (light, sound, acceleration) is converted into an analog electrical signal by the transducer.

### Stage 2 — Driver Node
A ROS node (often in C++ for performance) runs a loop:
1. Read raw bytes from the device (USB, serial, I²C, SPI, Ethernet).
2. Parse the manufacturer-specific protocol.
3. Convert raw counts to SI units (meters, radians, m/s²).
4. Stamp the `header.stamp` with `node->now()`.
5. Publish to a ROS topic.

### Stage 3 — Message Standardization
The driver publishes a standardized message from the `sensor_msgs` package. This is what makes ROS sensors **interoperable** — a `LaserScan` from a Hokuyo is *structurally identical* to a `LaserScan` from a SICK or a simulated RPLIDAR in Gazebo.

### Stage 4 — Coordinate Frame Assignment
Every sensor message carries a `header.frame_id`. REP-105 mandates the following canonical frames:
- `base_link` – robot's centre of body.
- `odom` – local frame, continuous, drifts over time.
- `map` – global frame, discrete jumps allowed (loop closure).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently forget that the **TF tree** must be populated with a `static_transform_publisher` from `base_link` → `laser_frame` (or `camera_frame`, `imu_frame`). Without this, RViz cannot visualize the sensor data, and the navigation stack will crash. Always include a TF broadcaster in the launch file.

### Stage 5 — Downstream Consumption
A perception, mapping, or control node subscribes to the topic, applies filtering (e.g., `pcl_ros` for point clouds, `image_transport` for images), and either:
- Publishes a higher-level topic (e.g., `/scan` → `/map` via `gmapping` or `slam_toolbox`).
- Provides an action/service to the motion planner.

## 2.2 The `sensor_msgs` Package — Message Inventory

This is the **core cheat-sheet** for KTU Part-A and Part-B questions.

| Message Type | Physical Quantity | Key Fields (Type) | Typical Sensor |
|--------------|-------------------|-------------------|----------------|
| `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` | 2-D planar range scan | `angle_min`, `angle_max`, `angle_increment`, `time_increment`, `range_min`, `range_max`, `ranges[]`, `intensities[]` | 2-D LiDAR (Hokuyo, SICK, RPLIDAR) |
| `sensor_msgs/PointCloud2` | 3-D point cloud | `height`, `width`, `is_bigendian`, `point_step`, `data[]`, `fields[]` | 3-D LiDAR (Velodyne, Ouster), Depth Cam |
| `sensor_msgs/Image` | Raw camera frame | `height`, `width`, `encoding` (`rgb8`, `bgr8`, `mono8`, `16UC1`), `data[]`, `step` | USB webcam, CSI camera |
| `sensor_msgs/CompressedImage` | JPEG/PNG compressed image | `format` (`jpeg`/`png`), `data[]` | Bandwidth-limited networks |
| `sensor_msgs/CameraInfo` | Intrinsic calibration | `K` (3×3), `D` (distortion), `R`, `P` (projection) | Any camera driver |
| `sensor_msgs/Imu` | Inertial Measurement Unit | `orientation` (quat), `angular_velocity`, `linear_acceleration` (all with covariance) | MPU6050, BMI088, Bosch, Xsens |
| `sensor_msgs/NavSatFix` | GPS / GNSS fix | `latitude`, `longitude`, `altitude`, `status`, `position_covariance` | u-blox, NovAtel |
| `sensor_msgs/Range` | Single-beam range | `radiation_type` (`ULTRASOUND`/`INFRARED`), `field_of_view`, `min_range`, `max_range`, `range` | HC-SR04, Sharp IR |
| `sensor_msgs/MagneticField` | Magnetometer | `magnetic_field.x/y/z`, covariance | HMC5883L, RM3100 |
| `sensor_msgs/FluidPressure` | Barometer | `fluid_pressure`, variance | BMP280, MS5611 |
| `sensor_msgs/Temperature` | Temperature | `temperature`, variance | Onboard thermistor |
| `sensor_msgs/JointState` | Joint encoder | `name[]`, `position[]`, `velocity[]`, `effort[]` | Wheel/joint encoders |
| `sensor_msgs/BatteryState` | Battery health | `voltage`, `current`, `charge`, `capacity`, `percentage` | BMS (Battery Management System) |
| `geometry_msgs/WrenchStamped` | Force/Torque | `force.x/y/z`, `torque.x/y/z` | ATI Mini45, Robotiq FT-300 |
| `nav_msgs/Odometry` | Wheel/visual odometry | `pose.pose`, `twist.twist` (with covariance) | Encoders + EKF |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **canonical mathematical models** for the sensors covered in Module-3. Memorize the formula, the symbol, and the SI unit.

### 2.3.1 LiDAR Range-to-Cartesian Conversion

Given a `LaserScan` message with `angle_increment` $\alpha$ and range array $r_i$:

$$
\theta_i = \theta_{\min} + i \cdot \alpha
$$

$$
x_i = r_i \cos(\theta_i), \quad y_i = r_i \sin(\theta_i)
$$

where $i \in [0, N-1]$ and $N$ is the number of beams.

### 2.3.2 LiDAR Beam-Count Equation

$$
N = \left\lfloor \frac{\theta_{\max} - \theta_{\min}}{\alpha} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

### 2.3.3 Camera Pinhole Projection Model (Intrinsic Matrix)

A 3-D point $\mathbf{P} = (X, Y, Z)^\top$ in the camera frame projects to a 2-D pixel $(u, v)^\top$ via:

$$
\begin{pmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{Z} \begin{pmatrix} f_x & s & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{pmatrix}
$$

where $f_x, f_y$ are the focal lengths in pixels, $(c_x, c_y)$ is the principal point, and $s$ is the skew (usually 0).

### 2.3.4 IMU Measurement Model (with Bias and Noise)

$$
\tilde{\omega}(t) = \omega(t) + b_g(t) + \eta_g(t)
$$

$$
\tilde{a}(t) = R^{\top}(t)\,(a(t) - g) + b_a(t) + \eta_a(t)
$$

where $\omega$ is true angular velocity, $a$ is true linear acceleration, $b_g, b_a$ are biases, and $\eta_g, \eta_a$ are zero-mean Gaussian noise terms with $\sigma_g, \sigma_a$ noise densities.

### 2.3.5 Range Sensor Model (1-D Beam)

For a beam reflected off a surface at true distance $d^*$ with additive Gaussian noise:

$$
z = d^* + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_r^2), \quad \text{clipped to } [r_{\min}, r_{\max}]
$$

### 2.3.6 Odometry Update from Wheel Encoders

For a differential-drive robot with wheel radius $r$, wheel base $b$, and encoder ticks $\Delta N_L, \Delta N_R$ per time step $\Delta t$:

$$
\Delta s = \frac{r}{2}\,(\Delta \phi_R + \Delta \phi_L) = \frac{\pi r}{T}\,(\Delta N_R + \Delta N_L)
$$

$$
\Delta \theta = \frac{r}{b}\,(\Delta \phi_R - \Delta \phi_L) = \frac{\pi r}{b T}\,(\Delta N_R - \Delta N_L)
$$

where $T$ is encoder ticks per revolution.

### 2.3.7 Sensor Fusion — Kalman Filter Update Step

$$
K_k = P_{k\mid k-1} H^\top (H P_{k\mid k-1} H^\top + R)^{-1}
$$

$$
\hat{x}_{k\mid k} = \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} + K_k (z_k - H\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1})
$$

$$
P_{k\mid k} = (I - K_k H) P_{k\mid k-1}
$$

where $H$ is the observation matrix, $R$ is measurement noise covariance, and $K_k$ is the Kalman gain.

### 2.3.8 Resolution of an Encoder

$$
\text{Resolution (rad/tick)} = \frac{2\pi}{T}
$$

### 2.3.9 LiDAR Angular Resolution vs Update Rate Trade-off

$$
\text{Points per second} = N \cdot f_{\text{update}} = \frac{(\theta_{\max} - \theta_{\min})}{\alpha} \cdot f_{\text{update}}
$$

This is the **bandwidth budget** for the USB/Ethernet link.

### 2.3.10 Depth from Stereo Baseline

For a stereo camera with baseline $B$ and focal length $f$, depth $Z$ from disparity $d = u_L - u_R$:

$$
Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Quick-Scan:** The four equations 2.3.1, 2.3.3, 2.3.4, and 2.3.7 appear in **almost every KTU university exam** for Module 3. Memorize them verbatim with units.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sensor Stack | Industry Application |
|--------------|----------------------|
| 2-D LiDAR + Wheel Odometry + IMU (EKF fused via `robot_localization`) | Warehouse AGVs (Amazon Kiva, Fetch Robotics) |
| 3-D LiDAR (Velodyne) + Camera (for colour) | Autonomous cars (Waymo, Cruise, Apollo) |
| RGB-D Camera (RealSense, Azure Kinect) | Indoor SLAM, pick-and-place, gesture recognition |
| Stereo Camera (ZED 2, OAK-D) | Outdoor autonomous navigation, drone vision |
| IMU + GPS + Barometer | UAVs, drones (PX4, ArduPilot flight stacks) |
| Force/Torque + Joint Encoders | Collaborative robot arms (UR5, Franka Panda) compliance control |
| Tactile Sensor Arrays (GelSight, SynTouch) | Dexterous manipulation, slip detection |
| Event Camera (DAVIS346, Prophesee) | High-speed tracking (10,000 Hz), low-latency odometry |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — LiDAR Scan-to-Point-Cloud Conversion

**Problem:** A Hokuyo URG-04LX publishes a `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` with:
- `angle_min = -2.356` rad  (≈ -135°)
- `angle_max = +2.356` rad  (≈ +135°)
- `angle_increment = 0.00654` rad
- `range_min = 0.06` m, `range_max = 4.0` m
- `ranges[180]` (the 180th beam) = 1.75 m

**Task:** Compute the Cartesian coordinates of the 180th beam in the `laser_frame`.

### Step 1 — Number of Beams

$$
N = \left\lfloor \frac{\theta_{\max} - \theta_{\min}}{\alpha} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

$$
N = \left\lfloor \frac{2.356 - (-2.356)}{0.00654} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

$$
N = \left\lfloor \frac{4.712}{0.00654} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

$$
N = \left\lfloor 720.48 \right\rfloor + 1 = 720 + 1 = 721
$$

### Step 2 — Angle of the 180th Beam

Using $\theta_i = \theta_{\min} + i \cdot \alpha$ with $i = 180$:

$$
\theta_{180} = -2.356 + 180 \cdot 0.00654
$$

$$
\theta_{180} = -2.356 + 1.1772
$$

$$
\theta_{180} = -1.1788 \text{ rad} \quad (\approx -67.54°)
$$

### Step 3 — Cartesian Conversion

Given the measured range $r_{180} = 1.75$ m:

$$
x_{180} = r_{180} \cos(\theta_{180}) = 1.75 \cdot \cos(-1.1788)
$$

$$
\cos(-1.1788) = 0.3827
$$

$$
x_{180} = 1.75 \cdot 0.3827 = 0.6697 \text{ m}
$$

$$
y_{180} = r_{180} \sin(\theta_{180}) = 1.75 \cdot \sin(-1.1788)
$$

$$
\sin(-1.1788) = -0.9239
$$

$$
y_{180} = 1.75 \cdot (-0.9239) = -1.6168 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 4 — Validity Check

We verify that $r_{180} = 1.75$ m lies within $[r_{\min}, r_{\max}] = [0.06, 4.0]$: **PASS**.

### Step 5 — Result

$$
\boxed{(x_{180},\; y_{180}) = (0.670 \text{ m},\; -1.617 \text{ m})}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The negative $y$ value indicates the beam points to the *left* of the `laser_frame` x-axis (the typical ROS convention is x-forward, y-left, z-up — REP-103).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Camera Pinhole Projection (Forward and Inverse)

**Problem:** A camera has intrinsic matrix:

$$
K = \begin{pmatrix} 525 & 0 & 320 \\ 0 & 525 & 240 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

A 3-D point in the camera frame is $\mathbf{P}_c = (1.2,\; 0.3,\; 3.0)^\top$ m.

**Task:** Find the pixel coordinates $(u, v)$.

### Step 1 — Apply the Projection Equation

$$
\begin{pmatrix} u \\ v \\ w \end{pmatrix} = K \cdot \mathbf{P}_c = \begin{pmatrix} 525 & 0 & 320 \\ 0 & 525 & 240 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1.2 \\ 0.3 \\ 3.0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

### Step 2 — Matrix Multiplication

$$
u = 525 \cdot 1.2 + 0 \cdot 0.3 + 320 \cdot 3.0 = 630 + 0 + 960 = 1590
$$

$$
v = 0 \cdot 1.2 + 525 \cdot 0.3 + 240 \cdot 3.0 = 0 + 157.5 + 720 = 877.5
$$

$$
w = 0 \cdot 1.2 + 0 \cdot 0.3 + 1 \cdot 3.0 = 3.0
$$

### Step 3 — Perspective Division (Homogeneous Normalization)

$$
(u, v) = \left( \frac{1590}{3.0},\; \frac{877.5}{3.0} \right) = (530.0,\; 292.5)
$$

### Step 4 — Result

$$
\boxed{(u,\; v) = (530,\; 292.5) \text{ pixels}}
$$

### Step 5 — Inverse Projection (Pixel to Ray)

Given a pixel $(u, v)$ and depth $Z$, recover the 3-D ray:

$$
X = \frac{(u - c_x) \cdot Z}{f_x}, \quad Y = \frac{(v - c_y) \cdot Z}{f_y}
$$

With $Z = 3.0$ m, $f_x = f_y = 525$, $c_x = 320$, $c_y = 240$:

$$
X = \frac{(530 - 320) \cdot 3.0}{525} = \frac{630}{525} = 1.20 \text{ m} \;\;\checkmark
$$

$$
Y = \frac{(292.5 - 240) \cdot 3.0}{525} = \frac{157.5}{525} = 0.30 \text{ m} \;\;\checkmark
$$

The inverse operation recovers the original 3-D point, confirming the forward model is consistent.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Differential-Drive Odometry Update

**Problem:** A TurtleBot3 (wheel radius $r = 0.033$ m, wheel base $b = 0.160$ m, encoder resolution $T = 4096$ ticks/rev) reports encoder increments $\Delta N_L = 120$ and $\Delta N_R = 130$ over $\Delta t = 0.05$ s.

**Task:** Compute the linear displacement $\Delta s$ and angular displacement $\Delta \theta$.

### Step 1 — Convert Encoder Ticks to Wheel Angles

$$
\Delta \phi_L = 2\pi \cdot \frac{\Delta N_L}{T} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{120}{4096} = 0.1841 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
\Delta \phi_R = 2\pi \cdot \frac{\Delta N_R}{T} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{130}{4096} = 0.1995 \text{ rad}
$$

### Step 2 — Linear Displacement of Each Wheel

$$
\Delta s_L = r \cdot \Delta \phi_L = 0.033 \cdot 0.1841 = 0.006075 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\Delta s_R = r \cdot \Delta \phi_R = 0.033 \cdot 0.1995 = 0.006584 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 3 — Robot Linear Displacement

$$
\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_L + \Delta s_R}{2} = \frac{0.006075 + 0.006584}{2} = 0.006330 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 4 — Robot Angular Displacement

$$
\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b} = \frac{0.006584 - 0.006075}{0.160} = \frac{0.000509}{0.160} = 0.00318 \text{ rad}
$$

### Step 5 — Result

$$
\boxed{\Delta s = 6.33 \text{ mm}, \quad \Delta \theta = 3.18 \text{ mrad}}
$$

### Step 6 — Pose Update in the `odom` Frame

$$
x_{k+1} = x_k + \Delta s \cos(\theta_k + \Delta\theta/2)
$$

$$
y_{k+1} = y_k + \Delta s \sin(\theta_k + \Delta\theta/2)
$$

$$
\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \Delta \theta
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This mid-point integration is **2nd-order accurate**. The naive form $x + \Delta s \cos\theta_k$ is only 1st-order accurate and drifts faster — a common ESE trap.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — IMU Pre-integration Factor (Brief)

**Problem:** An IMU reports angular velocity $\omega = (0, 0, 0.05)$ rad/s over $\Delta t = 0.01$ s starting at rest. Compute the resulting orientation quaternion.

### Step 1 — Angular Displacement

$$
\Delta\phi = \omega \cdot \Delta t = 0.05 \cdot 0.01 = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}
$$

### Step 2 — Quaternion Increment (Small-Angle Approximation)

$$
\Delta q \approx \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ \Delta\phi_x/2 \\ \Delta\phi_y/2 \\ \Delta\phi_z/2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \end{pmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — Orientation Update

$$
q_{k+1} = q_k \otimes \Delta q
$$

Starting from $q_0 = (1, 0, 0, 0)^\top$:

$$
q_1 = (1, 0, 0, 2.5\times 10^{-4})^\top
$$

### Step 4 — Result

$$
\boxed{q_1 \approx (1,\; 0,\; 0,\; 0.00025)^\top}
$$

After re-normalization, this represents a rotation of $0.0573°$ about the z-axis.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Comparison Table

| Property | 2-D LiDAR | 3-D LiDAR | Stereo Camera | RGB-D Camera | IMU | GPS |
|----------|-----------|-----------|---------------|--------------|-----|-----|
| Output Msg | `LaserScan` | `PointCloud2` | `Image`+`CameraInfo` | `Image`+`PointCloud2` | `Imu` | `NavSatFix` |
| Range | 0.06 – 30 m | 0.5 – 200 m | ∞ (passive) | 0.1 – 10 m | n/a | global |
| FoV | 270°–360° | 360° H, 30° V | 60°–110° | 60°–90° | n/a | n/a |
| Update Rate | 10 – 75 Hz | 10 – 20 Hz | 30 – 60 Hz | 30 – 90 Hz | 100 – 1000 Hz | 1 – 10 Hz |
| Indoors? | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ |
| Outdoors? | ✗ (sun-blinded) | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ (IR flooded) | ✓ | ✓ |
| Cost | Low–Med | High | Med | Med | Very Low | Med |
| Data Size | ~10 KB/s | ~10 MB/s | ~30 MB/s | ~30 MB/s | ~3 KB/s | ~200 B/s |
| Failure Mode | Black/reflective surfaces | Rain/fog | Darkness/rain | Sunlight IR | Drift | Multipath |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The ROS Sensor Data Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HW1[LiDAR Hardware] -->|USB/Ethernet| DR1[LiDAR Driver Node]
    HW2[Camera Hardware] -->|USB/MIPI| DR2[Camera Driver Node]
    HW3[IMU Hardware] -->|I2C/SPI| DR3[IMU Driver Node]
    HW4[Wheel Encoders] -->|GPIO/CAN| DR4[Robot Controller Node]

    DR1 -->|sensor_msgs/LaserScan| TOPIC1[/scan Topic/]
    DR2 -->|sensor_msgs/Image| TOPIC2[/camera/image_raw Topic/]
    DR2 -->|sensor_msgs/CameraInfo| TOPIC3[/camera/camera_info Topic/]
    DR3 -->|sensor_msgs/Imu| TOPIC4[/imu/data Topic/]
    DR4 -->|sensor_msgs/JointState| TOPIC5[/joint_states Topic/]

    TOPIC1 --> PROC1[Scan Matcher Node]
    TOPIC2 --> PROC2[Image Pipeline Node]
    TOPIC3 --> PROC2
    TOPIC4 --> FUSION[Robot Localization EKF]
    TOPIC5 --> ODOM[Odometry Node]
    ODOM --> FUSION

    PROC1 --> MAP[SLAM Node]
    PROC2 --> PERC[Perception Node]
    FUSION --> NAV[Navigation Stack]
    MAP --> NAV
    PERC --> NAV

    NAV --> RVIZ[RViz Visualization]
    NAV --> CTRL[Motion Controller]

    style HW1 fill:#ffcc66
    style HW2 fill:#ffcc66
    style HW3 fill:#ffcc66
    style HW4 fill:#ffcc66
    style DR1 fill:#88ddff
    style DR2 fill:#88ddff
    style DR3 fill:#88ddff
    style DR4 fill:#88ddff
    style FUSION fill:#ff9999
    style NAV fill:#99ff99
    style RVIZ fill:#cc99ff
```

## 4.2 TF Tree for a Standard Mobile Robot with Three Sensors

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MAP[map]
    ODOM[odom]
    BASE[base_link]
    WHEEL[base_footprint]
    LASER[laser_frame]
    CAM_OPT[camera_optical_frame]
    CAM_LINK[camera_link]
    IMU[imu_frame]
    GPS[gps_frame]

    MAP -->|SLAM update| ODOM
    ODOM -->|EKF odom base| BASE
    BASE -->|static TF| WHEEL
    BASE -->|static TF x=0.1 y=0 z=0.2| LASER
    BASE -->|static TF x=0.2 y=0 z=0.3| CAM_LINK
    CAM_LINK -->|optical rotation| CAM_OPT
    BASE -->|static TF x=0 y=0 z=0.1| IMU
    BASE -->|static TF x=-0.1 y=0 z=0.5| GPS

    style MAP fill:#99ff99
    style ODOM fill:#99ff99
    style BASE fill:#ffcc66
    style LASER fill:#88ddff
    style CAM_OPT fill:#88ddff
    style IMU fill:#ff9999
    style GPS fill:#cc99ff
```

## 4.3 Sensor Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT[Robot Sensors]

    ROOT --> PROP[Proprioceptive]
    ROOT --> EXT[Exteroceptive]

    PROP --> P1[Wheel Encoders]
    PROP --> P2[Joint Encoders]
    PROP --> P3[IMU]
    PROP --> P4[Battery Monitor]

    EXT --> E1[Active Sensors]
    EXT --> E2[Passive Sensors]

    E1 --> A1[2D LiDAR]
    E1 --> A2[3D LiDAR]
    E1 --> A3[Ultrasonic Sonar]
    E1 --> A4[Radar]
    E1 --> A5[Structured Light Depth Cam]

    E2 --> PA1[RGB Camera]
    E2 --> PA2[Stereo Camera]
    E2 --> PA3[Thermal Camera]
    E2 --> PA4[GPS Receiver]
    E2 --> PA5[Magnetometer]
    E2 --> PA6[Microphone]

    style ROOT fill:#ffcc66
    style PROP fill:#88ddff
    style EXT fill:#88ddff
    style E1 fill:#ff9999
    style E2 fill:#99ff99
```

## 4.4 URDF Sensor Mounting Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    URDF[URDF File] --> LINK[base_link]
    LINK --> JOINT1[joint_laser]
    JOINT1 --> LASER_LINK[laser_frame]
    LINK --> JOINT2[joint_camera]
    JOINT2 --> CAM_LINK[camera_link]
    CAM_LINK --> OPT_LINK[optical_frame]

    LASER_LINK --> GAZEBO1[GAZEBO SENSOR BLOCK]
    GAZEBO1 -->|ray| GAZEBO2[LiDAR Plugin]
    GAZEBO1 -->|camera| GAZEBO3[Depth Plugin]
    GAZEBO1 -->|imu| GAZEBO4[IMU Plugin]

    style URDF fill:#ffcc66
    style GAZEBO1 fill:#cc99ff
    style GAZEBO2 fill:#88ddff
    style GAZEBO3 fill:#88ddff
    style GAZEBO4 fill:#ff9999
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The URDF (Unified Robot Description Format) defines both the kinematic chain (`base_link → laser_frame`) and the simulation properties (`<gazebo>` blocks). In Gazebo, the same URDF is loaded and the plugins (e.g., `libgazebo_ros_ray_sensor.so`) publish the simulated sensor data on the exact same topics a real driver would use. This is the foundation of **sim-to-real transfer** in ROS.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q: List and briefly explain the standardized message types from the `sensor_msgs` package used to publish data from a 2-D LiDAR, an IMU, and a stereo camera in ROS. Mention the key fields of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **`sensor_msgs/LaserScan`** — Used by 2-D LiDAR (e.g., Hokuyo, RPLIDAR).
   - **Key fields:** `header` (with `frame_id` like `laser_frame`), `angle_min`, `angle_max`, `angle_increment`, `time_increment`, `scan_time`, `range_min`, `range_max`, `ranges[]` (float32 array of measured distances in metres), `intensities[]` (optional signal strengths).

2. **`sensor_msgs/Imu`** — Used by Inertial Measurement Units.
   - **Key fields:** `header`, `orientation` (quaternion $x, y, z, w$), `orientation_covariance` (9-element row-major 3×3 matrix), `angular_velocity` (vector $x, y, z$ in rad/s), `linear_acceleration` (vector $x, y, z$ in m/s²), each with its own 3×3 covariance matrix.

3. **`sensor_msgs/Image`** (one per camera) + **`sensor_msgs/CameraInfo`** — Used by stereo cameras.
   - **`Image` fields:** `header`, `height`, `width`, `encoding` (e.g., `bgr8`, `mono8`), `is_bigendian`, `step` (row length in bytes), `data[]` (raw pixel buffer).
   - **`CameraInfo` fields:** `header`, `height`, `width`, `distortion_model` (e.g., `plumb_bob`), `D` (distortion coefficients), `K` (3×3 camera intrinsic matrix), `R` (rectification), `P` (projection matrix).

> [!NOTE]
> A stereo camera publishes *two* `Image` topics (one per eye) and a single `CameraInfo` per eye. Depth is computed downstream by a `stereo_image_proc` node that produces a `stereo_msgs/DisparityImage` or a `sensor_msgs/PointCloud2`.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors. Give two examples of each and explain why both categories are essential for autonomous navigation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Proprioceptive Sensors | Exteroceptive Sensors |
|----------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Definition | Measure the robot's *internal* state | Measure the *external* environment |
| Frame of reference | Robot's own body frame | World frame or map frame |
| Independence from surroundings | Yes — works in complete darkness, fog, or empty rooms | No — requires physical interaction or signal reflection |
| Drift | Yes — accumulates error over time | Generally no drift (relative to the environment) |
| Absolute or Relative? | Relative (except IMU-aided yaw) | Can be absolute (e.g., GPS) or relative |
| Examples | Wheel encoders, joint encoders, IMU, battery voltage sensor | Camera, LiDAR, GPS, ultrasonic, radar |
| Role in Navigation | Provide short-term, high-rate motion estimate (`/odom`) | Provide global localization and map correction (`/map`) |

**Why both are essential:**
- *Proprioceptive* sensors give smooth, high-frequency (100–1000 Hz) state estimates but **drift** because encoder integration accumulates error.
- *Exteroceptive* sensors anchor the pose to the world (e.g., GPS) or detect loop closures (SLAM) but are noisy and lower frequency.
- The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly mandates an **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** fusion (e.g., the `robot_localization` package) to combine both — proprioceptive for prediction, exteroceptive for correction. Without this fusion, the robot cannot achieve centimetre-level accuracy over long trajectories.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question B1 (Choice A) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the standard pipeline of a ROS sensor, from the physical transducer to the downstream consumer node. Use the example of a 2-D LiDAR (e.g., RPLIDAR A1) publishing on the `/scan` topic. Include the role of the `header.frame_id`, the TF tree, and the `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` message structure.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A RPLIDAR A1 has `angle_min = 0°`, `angle_max = 360°`, `angle_increment = 1°`, and the `ranges[90]` element (the 90th beam) reports 2.50 m. Compute the Cartesian coordinates of this beam in the `laser_frame` and the total number of beams $N$ in one full scan.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The standard ROS sensor pipeline has **five stages**:

**Stage 1 — Hardware Transduction (1 mark):**
The RPLIDAR A1 uses a laser diode (typically 785 nm) and a photodiode receiver. The laser is swept by a rotating motor at 5.5 Hz. The time-of-flight $\Delta t$ between emission and detection of the reflected pulse is measured, and the range is computed as $r = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$, where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s is the speed of light.

**Stage 2 — Driver Node (1.5 marks):**
A ROS node `rplidar_node` (from the `rplidar_ros` package) runs a loop:
1. Opens the USB serial port (e.g., `/dev/ttyUSB0`) at 115200 baud.
2. Sends the scan-start command (manufacturer-specific protocol).
3. Reads the data packets (each packet contains multiple range samples).
4. Converts raw counts to metres and applies the calibration correction.
5. Stamps `header.stamp = node->now()` (current ROS time).
6. Sets `header.frame_id = "laser_frame"`.
7. Publishes a `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` message on `/scan`.

**Stage 3 — Message Standardization (1.5 marks):**
The `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` message has the following structure:

```text
std_msgs/Header header
float32 angle_min
float32 angle_max
float32 angle_increment
float32 time_increment
float32 scan_time
float32 range_min
float32 range_max
float32[] ranges
float32[] intensities
```

This message is **identical** for a real RPLIDAR, a simulated one in Gazebo, and a SICK LiDAR. Downstream nodes do not know — and do not need to know — which physical device produced the scan.

**Stage 4 — TF Tree Integration (1.5 marks):**
The launch file includes:

```xml
<node pkg="tf2_ros" type="static_transform_publisher" name="base_to_laser"
      args="0 0 0.2 0 0 0 base_link laser_frame" />
```

This publishes a static transform from `base_link` to `laser_frame` with translation (0, 0, 0.2) — the LiDAR is mounted 20 cm above the robot's base link. Without this TF, RViz cannot transform the scan points into the world frame, and the `amcl` localization node will fail.

**Stage 5 — Downstream Consumption (1.5 marks):**
Subscribers include:
- `rviz2` — visualizes the scan as red dots in the 3-D viewer.
- `slam_toolbox` or `gmapping` — builds a 2-D occupancy grid map.
- `amcl` — localizes the robot in a pre-built map.
- `laser_filters` — applies range filters (e.g., shadow filtering, angle filters).
- `pointcloud_to_laserscan` — converts to a `PointCloud2` for 3-D processing.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Convert angles to radians:**

$$
\theta_{\min} = 0° = 0 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
\theta_{\max} = 360° = 2\pi \approx 6.2832 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
\alpha = 1° = \frac{\pi}{180} \approx 0.01745 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of beams:**

$$
N = \left\lfloor \frac{2\pi}{0.01745} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

$$
N = \left\lfloor 360 \right\rfloor + 1 = 361
$$

**Step 3 — Angle of the 90th beam (using zero-based index $i = 90$):**

$$
\theta_{90} = 0 + 90 \cdot 0.01745 = 1.5708 \text{ rad} \;(\approx 90°)
$$

**Step 4 — Cartesian coordinates:**

$$
x = r \cos\theta = 2.50 \cdot \cos(1.5708) = 2.50 \cdot 0 = 0.000 \text{ m}
$$

$$
y = r \sin\theta = 2.50 \cdot \sin(1.5708) = 2.50 \cdot 1 = 2.500 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Result:**

$$
\boxed{N = 361 \text{ beams}, \quad (x_{90}, y_{90}) = (0.000 \text{ m},\; 2.500 \text{ m})}
$$

This beam points **directly to the left** of the robot (assuming x-forward convention) and hits a wall 2.5 m away.

---

### Question B1 (Choice B) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the role of the `robot_localization` package in ROS. Explain the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) prediction and update equations used to fuse wheel odometry, IMU, and GPS, and derive the Kalman gain expression.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A differential-drive robot uses wheel encoders with wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m, wheel base $b = 0.30$ m, and $T = 2000$ ticks/rev. Over a 0.1 s interval, the left and right encoders report $\Delta N_L = 80$ and $\Delta N_R = 100$. Compute (i) the linear displacement, (ii) the angular displacement, and (iii) the new pose $(x, y, \theta)$ given the previous pose $(0, 0, 0)$.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The **`robot_localization`** package is the canonical ROS EKF/UKF node. It accepts an arbitrary number of sensor inputs (each with its own `sensor_msgs` topic and covariance) and produces a smoothed, fused odometry output on `/odometry/filtered`.

**Prediction Step (using the motion model from wheel odometry):**

$$
\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} = f(\hat{x}_{k-1\mid k-1}, u_k)
$$

$$
P_{k\mid k-1} = F_k P_{k-1\mid k-1} F_k^\top + Q_k
$$

where $F_k$ is the Jacobian of the motion model and $Q_k$ is the process noise covariance.

For a differential-drive robot, $f$ is the standard kinematic update:

$$
f : \begin{cases} x_{k} = x_{k-1} + \Delta s \cos(\theta_{k-1} + \Delta\theta/2) \\ y_{k} = y_{k-1} + \Delta s \sin(\theta_{k-1} + \Delta\theta/2) \\ \theta_{k} = \theta_{k-1} + \Delta\theta \end{cases}
$$

**Update Step (using a measurement from IMU or GPS):**

$$
K_k = P_{k\mid k-1} H^\top (H P_{k\mid k-1} H^\top + R_k)^{-1}
$$

$$
\hat{x}_{k\mid k} = \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} + K_k (z_k - H \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1})
$$

$$
P_{k\mid k} = (I - K_k H) P_{k\mid k-1}
$$

**Derivation of the Kalman Gain (2 marks):**
The Kalman gain is the value of $K$ that minimizes the posterior covariance $\text{tr}(P_{k\mid k})$. Setting $\frac{\partial}{\partial K} \text{tr}(P_{k\mid k}) = 0$:

$$
P_{k\mid k} = (I - KH)P_{k\mid k-1}(I - KH)^\top + K R K^\top
$$

Expanding the trace derivative and solving for $K$:

$$
\boxed{K_k = P_{k\mid k-1} H^\top (H P_{k\mid k-1} H^\top + R_k)^{-1}}
$$

**Role in multi-sensor fusion:**
- The wheel odometry provides the **control input** $u_k = (\Delta s, \Delta\theta)$.
- The IMU provides a high-rate $(100\text{ Hz})$ **angular velocity** and **linear acceleration** update — the EKF uses these to correct the orientation between wheel-encoder updates.
- The GPS provides a low-rate $(1\text{ Hz})$ **absolute position** update — the EKF uses this to bound the drift of the wheel-encoder integration.

The `robot_localization` configuration file specifies for each sensor:
- The topic name.
- The frame_id.
- Which dimensions of the state vector to fuse (e.g., GPS may update $x, y$ but not $z$; IMU may update orientation but not position).

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Wheel angular increments:**

$$
\Delta\phi_L = 2\pi \cdot \frac{80}{2000} = 2\pi \cdot 0.04 = 0.2513 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
\Delta\phi_R = 2\pi \cdot \frac{100}{2000} = 2\pi \cdot 0.05 = 0.3142 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 2 — Wheel linear displacements:**

$$
\Delta s_L = r \cdot \Delta\phi_L = 0.05 \cdot 0.2513 = 0.01257 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\Delta s_R = r \cdot \Delta\phi_R = 0.05 \cdot 0.3142 = 0.01571 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — (i) Linear displacement of robot centre:**

$$
\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_L + \Delta s_R}{2} = \frac{0.01257 + 0.01571}{2} = 0.01414 \text{ m} \approx 14.14 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — (ii) Angular displacement:**

$$
\Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b} = \frac{0.01571 - 0.01257}{0.30} = \frac{0.00314}{0.30} = 0.01047 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
\Delta\theta \approx 0.5994° \approx 0.6°
$$

**Step 5 — (iii) New pose (mid-point integration, $\theta_{\text{prev}} = 0$):**

$$
x_1 = 0 + 0.01414 \cdot \cos(0 + 0.01047/2) = 0.01414 \cdot \cos(0.005235) = 0.01414 \cdot 0.99999 = 0.01414 \text{ m}
$$

$$
y_1 = 0 + 0.01414 \cdot \sin(0.005235) = 0.01414 \cdot 0.005235 = 7.40 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}
$$

$$
\theta_1 = 0 + 0.01047 = 0.01047 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 6 — Result:**

$$
\boxed{(x_1,\; y_1,\; \theta_1) = (0.01414 \text{ m},\; 7.40 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m},\; 0.01047 \text{ rad})}
$$

The robot moved 14.14 mm forward and turned by 0.6° counter-clockwise (assuming positive $\Delta\theta$ is counter-clockwise per REP-103).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Order of operations in mid-point integration:** Students often compute $x_1 = x_0 + \Delta s \cos\theta_0$ instead of using the *mid-point angle* $\theta_0 + \Delta\theta/2$. The former is only 1st-order accurate; the latter is 2nd-order accurate. Both give the same answer for $\theta_0 = 0$, but diverge rapidly over long trajectories. **Lose 1 mark if you use the wrong one in the general case.**
> 2. **Units confusion:** $r$ is in **metres**, $\Delta\phi$ is in **radians**, $\Delta s$ is in **metres**, $b$ is in **metres**, $\theta$ is in **radians**. Do NOT use degrees anywhere in the formula. **Lose 0.5 mark per unit error.**
> 3. **Forgetting the +1 in the beam count:** When $\theta_{\max} - \theta_{\min}$ is exactly divisible by $\alpha$, the floor function drops the last beam unless you add 1. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 4. **CameraInfo vs Image confusion:** Stereo cameras publish *two* `Image` topics (left and right) but only *one* `CameraInfo` per eye. The CameraInfo is NOT the image — it is the *intrinsic calibration matrix*. **Lose 1 mark if you swap them in a Part-A question.**
> 5. **TF frame_id omission:** Every `sensor_msgs` message has a `header.frame_id`. If you write a driver node without setting it, the message is unrenderable in RViz. **Always mention frame_id in your launch-file design.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Sensors)**

- ☐ **REP-103** (Standard Units & Coordinate Conventions): SI units everywhere, x-forward, y-left, z-up for mobile robots, x-right, y-down, z-forward for camera optical frames.
- ☐ **REP-105** (Coordinate Frames): `map` → `odom` → `base_link` → sensors. The TF tree must be a connected graph, no cycles.
- ☐ **`sensor_msgs/LaserScan`** fields: `angle_min`, `angle_max`, `angle_increment`, `range_min`, `range_max`, `ranges[]`, `intensities[]`, `header.frame_id`.
- ☐ **`sensor_msgs/Imu`** fields: `orientation` (quaternion), `angular_velocity`, `linear_acceleration` — **each with its own covariance matrix**.
- ☐ **`sensor_msgs/Image`** fields: `width`, `height`, `encoding` (`bgr8`, `mono8`, `16UC1`), `step`, `data[]`.
- ☐ **`sensor_msgs/CameraInfo`** fields: `K` (intrinsics), `D` (distortion), `R`, `P` (projection), `distortion_model` (e.g., `plumb_bob`).
- ☐ **Sensor Classification Axes:** Proprioceptive vs Exteroceptive × Active vs Passive = 4 canonical categories.
- ☐ **LiDAR Scan-to-Cart:** $x_i = r_i \cos\theta_i$, $y_i = r_i \sin\theta_i$, $\theta_i = \theta_{\min} + i\alpha$.
- ☐ **Pinhole Projection:** $u = f_x X/Z + c_x$, $v = f_y Y/Z + c_y$.
- ☐ **Stereo Depth:** $Z = fB/d$ where $d$ is disparity, $B$ is baseline.
- ☐ **Differential-Drive Odometry:** $\Delta s = (r/2)(\Delta\phi_R + \Delta\phi_L)$, $\Delta\theta = (r/b)(\Delta\phi_R - \Delta\phi_L)$.
- ☐ **Mid-point integration** is 2nd-order accurate; naive $x + \Delta s\cos\theta$ is only 1st-order.
- ☐ **Kalman Gain:** $K = P H^\top (H P H^\top + R)^{-1}$ — minimizes posterior covariance.
- ☐ **Process noise** $Q$ (from odometry model) and **measurement noise** $R$ (from sensor datasheet) are both required to configure `robot_localization`.
- ☐ **URDF + Gazebo sensors** use the same `sensor_msgs` topics as real hardware — this enables sim-to-real transfer.
- ☐ **TF static_transform_publisher** must be in every sensor launch file, or RViz will display the data in an arbitrary frame.
- ☐ **Active sensors** (LiDAR, sonar) emit energy and measure the echo; **passive sensors** (camera, GPS) only receive.
- ☐ **Encoder resolution** $= 2\pi / T$ rad/tick, where $T$ is ticks per revolution.
- ☐ **Frame rate vs angular resolution** trade-off in LiDAR: more beams at higher frequency = more USB bandwidth.
- ☐ **Multi-sensor fusion** is a KTU-mandated learning outcome — know the EKF predict-update cycle by heart.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computation Complete Robots - PR2
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT525 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Computation Complete Robots - PR2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PR2 (Personal Robot 2)** is a *computation-complete* mobile manipulation research robot developed by **Willow Garage** (founded in 2006 by Scott Hassan, with funding from the Google co-founder's early-stage ventures). Released commercially in **2010**, it is the canonical reference platform upon which the entire **Robot Operating System (ROS)** ecosystem was conceived, validated, and matured.

A *computation-complete robot*, in KTU 2024 Scheme ROS terminology, is defined as an autonomous robotic platform that:
1. Hosts **all onboard computational resources** (processors, memory, storage) required for full sensor-fusion, planning, control, and perception without relying on an external tethered workstation for real-time execution.
2. Provides **standardized hardware abstractions** through a unified middleware (ROS), enabling portable and reproducible research.

The PR2 satisfies this definition by integrating:
- **2 × Intel Xeon E3-1260L** processors (quad-core, 2.4 GHz)
- **24 GB DDR3 ECC RAM**
- **2 × 500 GB SATA HDDs** in mirrored RAID
- **Onboard gigabit Ethernet switch** for internal component networking

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** The PR2 was the *first* robot shipped with ROS pre-installed and is the platform on which the canonical ROS tutorials (`ros_tutorials` metapackage) were originally validated.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you hire a **genius butler** who lives *inside* your house:
- He carries his **own brain** (the two Xeon servers tucked in his "torso").
- He has **two fully articulated arms** (7-DOF each) — like a human with elbows and wrists.
- He moves in **any direction on the floor** using special omni-wheels (no need to turn before moving sideways).
- He "sees" using **multiple cameras**, including a pair that mimics human stereo vision.
- He was trained using a **single, universal playbook** (ROS) — meaning any chef (researcher) worldwide who knows this playbook can give him new tasks.

So, just as a smartphone is "computation complete" because it doesn't need a desktop computer to function, the PR2 is "computation complete" because it doesn't need a laptop plugged into its back to think.

## 1.3 Why "Computation Complete" Matters in ROS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Before PR2, most research robots were *tethered* — they relied on an off-board PC for computation via Ethernet or USB cables. This made real-world deployment nearly impossible because:
> - Cables restrict mobility.
> - Latency over cables is non-deterministic.
> - The robot cannot operate in real environments outside the lab.

The PR2 broke this paradigm by making the robot **self-contained**, which directly enabled modern applications like **fetch-and-carry robots, autonomous hospital couriers, and home assistants**.

| Specification | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Original Price (2010) | ~USD 400,000 |
| Total Degrees of Freedom | ~**20+** (arms: 7×2, base: 3, neck/torso: 4, grippers: 3×2) |
| Onboard Compute | 2 × Xeon (8 cores total), 24 GB RAM |
| Reach (each arm) | ~**1.0 m** |
| Payload (each arm) | ~**1.5 kg** (full extension) |
| Battery | 1.3 kWh Li-ion, ~2 hrs operation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 System Architecture Breakdown

The PR2 is structured into **four logical subsystems**, each mapped to distinct ROS namespaces to enforce modularity:

### 2.1.1 Mobile Base Subsystem (`/base` namespace)
- Equipped with a **four-wheel omni-directional drive**.
- Kinematic model: a **4-wheel Swedish 90° configuration** providing full 3-DOF planar motion (x, y, θ).
- Encoders on each wheel provide **odometry** published via `/base/odometry` topic.
- Contains **two safety laser scanners** (front + rear) publishing `/base/scan` topics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why omni-wheels?** A car-like differential drive cannot translate sideways. For manipulation tasks (e.g., approaching a drawer sideways), the PR2 needs holonomic mobility.

### 2.1.2 Manipulation Subsystem (`/right_arm`, `/left_arm` namespaces)
Each arm has **7 Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**, exceeding the minimum 6 DOF needed for arbitrary end-effector pose. This redundancy provides:
- **Obstacle avoidance** in joint space.
- **Singularity avoidance** during inverse kinematics (IK).
- **Optimized manipulability** for force-controlled tasks.

**Arm joint enumeration (per arm):**
1. `shoulder_pan` (base rotation)
2. `shoulder_lift` (shoulder pitch)
3. `upper_arm_roll` (humerus roll)
4. `elbow_flex` (elbow)
5. `forearm_roll` (forearm roll)
6. `wrist_flex` (wrist pitch)
7. `wrist_roll` (wrist rotation)

### 2.1.3 Sensor Subsystem (`/head`, `/laser`, `/kinect` namespaces)
- **Head:** Stereo camera pair (Prosilica) + 5-MP texture camera.
- **Tilt laser:** Hokuyo on tilting servo (legacy sensor).
- **Microsoft Kinect** (later generation) for RGB-D perception.
- **IMU** on the torso for orientation estimation.
- **Force/Torque sensors** at each wrist for compliant manipulation.

### 2.1.4 Compute Subsystem (`/compute` namespace)
- Hosts the **roscore (master)** locally.
- Runs **rosbag** for offline data recording.
- Hosts **MoveIt!** motion planning server.
- Runs **Gazebo** simulation bridge when not on real hardware.

## 2.2 Why PR2 is Considered the "Mothership" of ROS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The PR2 is sometimes called the **"Ros-Tutorial Robot"** because virtually every ROS fundamental was first demonstrated on it. The TF tree, `actionlib`, MoveIt!, `rosbag`, `diagnostics`, and `robot_state_publisher` were all born from PR2 development.

### 2.2.1 The TF Tree of PR2 (Conceptual Hierarchy)
The PR2's coordinate frame tree is the **canonical example** in every ROS textbook:

```
/base_footprint
    └── /base_link
            ├── /torso_lift_link
            │       ├── /head_pan_link
            │       │       └── /head_tilt_link
            │       │               ├── /wide_stereo_optical_frame
            │       │               └── /narrow_stereo_optical_frame
            │       └── /imu_link
            ├── /laser_tilt_link
            └── /wheel_rear_left_link (× 4 wheels)
                    ├── /right_arm_shoulder_link
                    │       └── ... (7 joints) ...
                    │               └── /right_gripper_tool_frame
                    └── /left_arm_shoulder_link
                            └── ... (7 joints) ...
                                    └── /left_gripper_tool_frame
```

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Subsystem | Key Hardware | ROS Topic Convention | Rate (Hz) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Base Odometry | 4 wheel encoders | `/base/odometry` | 50 |
| Base Lasers | 2 × Hokuyo UTM-30LX | `/base_scan` | 40 |
| Head Stereo | 2 × Prosilica GC2450 | `/wide_stereo/left/image_raw` | 25 |
| Tilt Laser | Hokuyo + servo | `/tilt_scan` | 40 |
| Joint States | All arm/wheel motors | `/joint_states` | 100 |
| Force/Torque | ATI Mini45 sensors | `/ft/...` | 1000 |
| IMU | MicroStrain 3DM-GX2 | `/imu/data` | 100 |
| TF Tree | Static + dynamic | `/tf`, `/tf_static` | 100 |

| ROS Concept | PR2's Pioneering Use |
| :--- | :--- |
| Topics | Joint state publishing at 100 Hz |
| Services | Calibrated servo reset calls |
| Actionlib | Long-running motion primitives |
| TF | Canonical URDF-derived transform tree |
| URDF | First standardized full-body model |
| rosbag | Million-iteration demos (towel folding) |
| Diagnostics | Motor temperature, battery, CPU health |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The high joint-state publishing rate (100 Hz) on the PR2 is why ROS was designed with UDP-friendly, low-overhead message passing rather than heavy serialization like CORBA — a deliberate co-design decision.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The PR2's design decisions directly seeded the modern robotics industry:
- **TurtleBot 1, 2, 3, 4** (Open Robotics) — the desktop-scale descendant of PR2, used in education worldwide.
- **Fetch Robotics Freight & Fetch robots** — commercial warehouse robots created by ex-Willow-Garreage founders.
- **Stretch (Hello Robot)** — a low-cost mobile manipulator inspired by PR2's research pedigree.

The pattern of *"one powerful onboard computer + ROS + mobile manipulation base"* is the standard architecture taught in KTU PERUT525 labs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivation & Kinematic Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> This section provides the *exhaustive* kinematic derivation of the PR2 arm — a KTU high-yield analytical topic. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly.

## 3.1 Forward Kinematics of a Single PR2 Arm (Denavit-Hartenberg Analysis)

### 3.1.1 Setting Up the Standard DH Convention
For a serial chain with $n$ revolute joints, the homogeneous transformation from joint $i-1$ to joint $i$ is given by:

$$
^{i-1}T_{i}(\theta_i) = \begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\cos\theta_i \\
\sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\sin\theta_i \\
0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.1.2 Extracting DH Parameters for PR2 Right Arm

The standard PR2 URDF defines the following **DH parameters** for the right arm (convention adopted from Corke, *Robotics, Vision and Control*):

| Joint $i$ | $a_i$ (m) | $\alpha_i$ (rad) | $d_i$ (m) | $\theta_i$ offset (rad) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 (shoulder\_pan) | 0.0 | $-\pi/2$ | 0.0 | 0.0 |
| 2 (shoulder\_lift) | 0.0 | $\pi/2$ | 0.0 | $\pi/2$ |
| 3 (upper\_arm\_roll) | 0.4 | $-\pi/2$ | 0.0 | 0.0 |
| 4 (elbow\_flex) | 0.0 | $\pi/2$ | 0.0 | $-\pi/2$ |
| 5 (forearm\_roll) | 0.4 | $-\pi/2$ | 0.0 | 0.0 |
| 6 (wrist\_flex) | 0.0 | $\pi/2$ | 0.0 | 0.0 |
| 7 (wrist\_roll) | 0.0 | 0.0 | 0.0 | 0.0 |

> **Step 1 — Compute individual $T$ matrices.** We substitute each row into the DH formula. We show the *first two* transformations explicitly as exemplars; the remaining joints follow the identical pattern.

**Transformation from frame 0 to frame 1** (substituting $\theta = \theta_1$, $\alpha = -\pi/2$, $a = 0$, $d = 0$):

$$
^{0}T_{1} = \begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_1 & 0 & -\sin\theta_1 & 0 \\
\sin\theta_1 & 0 & \cos\theta_1 & 0 \\
0 & -1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Step 2 — Compute frame 1 to frame 2 transformation** (substituting $\theta = \theta_2 + \pi/2$, $\alpha = \pi/2$, $a = 0$, $d = 0$):

$$
^{1}T_{2} = \begin{bmatrix}
-\sin\theta_2 & 0 & \cos\theta_2 & 0 \\
\cos\theta_2 & 0 & \sin\theta_2 & 0 \\
0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Step 3 — Multiply matrices sequentially.** The full forward kinematics is:

$$
^{0}T_{7}(\mathbf{q}) = {^{0}T_{1}}(\theta_1) \cdot {^{1}T_{2}}(\theta_2) \cdot {^{2}T_{3}}(\theta_3) \cdot {^{3}T_{4}}(\theta_4) \cdot {^{4}T_{5}}(\theta_5) \cdot {^{5}T_{6}}(\theta_6) \cdot {^{6}T_{7}}(\theta_7)
$$

where $\mathbf{q} = [\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3, \theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6, \theta_7]^{T}$ is the joint configuration vector.

> **Step 4 — Extract end-effector pose.** The position of the gripper in the shoulder frame is the translation column of $^{0}T_{7}$:

$$
\mathbf{p}_{gripper}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix}
p_x \\ p_y \\ p_z
\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}
^{0}T_{7}(1,4) \\ ^{0}T_{7}(2,4) \\ ^{0}T_{7}(3,4)
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Step 5 — Reach verification.** The maximum reach of each arm is computed by summing the upper-arm length (0.4 m) and forearm length (0.4 m):

$$
L_{reach} = a_3 + a_5 = 0.4 + 0.4 = 0.8 \text{ m}
$$

However, the PR2's actual reachable workspace is augmented by the shoulder-pan and shoulder-lift joints, giving an *effective* workspace radius of approximately **1.0 m** from the shoulder origin (accounting for the wrist extending past the forearm link).

## 3.2 Workspace Volume (Spherical Approximation)

> **Step 6 — Compute the arm's reachable spherical shell volume.** For a 7-DOF arm with effective reach $R = 1.0$ m and minimum reach $r \approx 0.1$ m (when fully folded):

$$
V_{shell} = \frac{4}{3}\pi \left(R^3 - r^3\right)
$$

$$
V_{shell} = \frac{4}{3}\pi \left(1.0^3 - 0.1^3\right) = \frac{4}{3}\pi (0.999) \approx 4.18 \text{ m}^3
$$

> **Step 7 — Total bimanual workspace overlap.** With two arms, the *intersection* of the workspaces is the region reachable by *both* arms simultaneously (critical for bimanual tasks):

$$
V_{bimanual} = 2 \cdot V_{shell} - V_{union} \approx 2.5 \text{ m}^3 \text{ (typical)}
$$

## 3.3 Velocity Kinematics — The Jacobian

> **Step 8 — Derive the geometric Jacobian.** For a chain of revolute joints, the $i^{th}$ column of the Jacobian $J(\mathbf{q})$ is:

$$
J_i(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix}
\mathbf{z}_{i-1} \times (\mathbf{p}_{end} - \mathbf{p}_{i-1}) \\
\mathbf{z}_{i-1}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

where $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$ is the rotation axis of joint $i$ and $\mathbf{p}_{i-1}$ is its origin (both in the base frame).

> **Step 9 — Relate joint velocities to end-effector twist.** For the PR2 gripper:

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
\mathbf{v}_{gripper} \\
\boldsymbol{\omega}_{gripper}
\end{bmatrix} = J(\mathbf{q}) \, \dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

This 6×7 matrix has **one redundant DOF** ($6 < 7$), which is exploited for null-space motion (avoiding obstacles without moving the gripper):

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}}_{null} = (I - J^{+}J) \, \mathbf{z}_{0}
$$

where $J^{+}$ is the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse and $\mathbf{z}_0$ is any gradient (e.g., toward a manipulability-maximizing posture).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.4 Data Flow During a Typical Pick-and-Place Task

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant USER as User Voice Command
    participant BT as Behavior Tree Node
    participant PL as MoveIt Planner
    participant CTRL as ros_control
    participant HW as PR2 Hardware
    participant CAM as Stereo Camera

    USER->>BT: Fetch the bottle
    BT->>CAM: Request RGB-D image
    CAM-->>BT: Point cloud of scene
    BT->>PL: Plan trajectory to bottle
    PL->>CTRL: Send JointTrajectory action
    CTRL->>HW: Actuate arm motors
    HW-->>CTRL: Joint state feedback
    CTRL-->>BT: Action result SUCCESS
    BT->>PL: Plan trajectory to drop zone
    PL->>CTRL: Execute place motion
    CTRL-->>BT: Task complete
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1.1  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "computation-complete robot" in the context of ROS. Explain why the PR2 is considered a canonical example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *computation-complete robot* is one that hosts all required processing (CPU, memory, storage) onboard, eliminating dependence on an external tethered workstation for real-time operation. **The PR2 (Personal Robot 2)**, developed by Willow Garage in **2010**, is considered canonical because it integrates 2× Intel Xeon processors, 24 GB RAM, and RAID storage directly into the robot chassis while running ROS natively. It pioneered the architecture used in all subsequent ROS-based platforms, including TurtleBot. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for PR2 specs, 1 mark for canonical significance).

### Question A1.2  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any three major sensor subsystems of the PR2 and their corresponding ROS topic names.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Stereo head cameras → `/wide_stereo/left/image_raw`, `/wide_stereo/right/image_raw` (1 mark)
2. Two base laser scanners → `/base_scan`, `/rear_scan` (1 mark)
3. IMU → `/imu/data` (or wrist Force/Torque sensors → `/r_ft/...` and `/l_ft/...`) (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In 14-mark questions, students commonly lose marks by:
> 1. Failing to **draw the URDF tree structure** when asked about PR2 architecture — examiners allocate 2-3 marks purely for the diagram.
> 2. Confusing **DH parameters vs. URDF joint definitions** — these are *different* conventions and examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 3. Skipping **units** in numerical problems (especially meters and radians).
> 4. Not mentioning the **namespace convention** (`/base`, `/right_arm`, etc.) used by PR2.

---

### Question B1 — Option A  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, 14 marks]`

**(a)** Describe in detail the **hardware architecture** of the PR2 robot. Include its compute subsystem, manipulation subsystem, mobile base, and sensor suite. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **software architecture** of PR2 in terms of ROS layers. How does the PR2's TF tree and URDF contribute to its modularity? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Hardware Architecture (7 marks):**
- **Compute Subsystem (2 marks):** Two Intel Xeon E3-1260L processors (8 cores total), 24 GB DDR3 ECC RAM, dual 500 GB HDDs in RAID-1, internal gigabit Ethernet switch. Runs ROS master (roscore) locally.
- **Mobile Base (1.5 marks):** 4-wheel Swedish-90 omni-drive with 2× Hokuyo laser scanners. Provides holonomic 3-DOF motion (x, y, θ).
- **Manipulation Subsystem (2 marks):** Two 7-DOF arms (shoulder pan/lift/roll, elbow, forearm roll, wrist flex/roll). Each wrist has a 1-DOF gripper and an ATI Mini45 force/torque sensor. Liftable torso adds a 4th prismatic joint.
- **Sensor Suite (1.5 marks):** Head-mounted stereo Prosilica cameras, narrow-stereo option, tilting Hokuyo laser, MicroStrain IMU on torso, Kinect RGB-D on later revisions.

**(b) Software Architecture (7 marks):**
- **Layered ROS Stack (3 marks):** Application Layer (perception/planning/behavior), Middleware Layer (topics, services, actionlib, parameter server, TF), Hardware Abstraction Layer (ros_control-based drivers), Physical Hardware.
- **URDF Contribution (2 marks):** The PR2's URDF defines ~30+ links and joints in an XML hierarchy, allowing the robot_state_publisher to broadcast TF transforms automatically at every joint state update.
- **TF Tree Modularity (2 marks):** Each subsystem (base, arms, head, torso) is rooted under `/base_link` with its own sub-tree. This modular TF allows MoveIt! to plan *per-arm* trajectories while the rest of the body remains stationary in the TF graph.

---

### Question B1 — Option B  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, 14 marks]`

**(a)** Explain why the PR2 is called a **"computation-complete"** robot. Compare it with the older tethered robot paradigm and explain the engineering advantages. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Using standard DH convention, derive the **forward kinematics** of a 7-DOF PR2 arm. Show how the end-effector position is computed from joint angles $\theta_1$ to $\theta_7$. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) "Computation-Complete" Paradigm (7 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** A computation-complete robot carries all necessary onboard computational resources, eliminating the need for external tethered workstations for real-time control and perception.
- **Tethered Limitations (3 marks):** Older robots (e.g., 1990s research arms) used parallel/USB/Ethernet cables to off-board PCs, causing (i) restricted mobility, (ii) non-deterministic latency affecting real-time loops, (iii) vulnerability to cable damage, and (iv) inability to operate outside controlled lab environments.
- **PR2's Advantages (2 marks):** Onboard Xeon processors allow MoveIt!, rosbag, perception pipelines, and even Gazebo simulations to run *concurrently*. This enables real-world deployments (e.g., fetch-and-carry in offices) and powers the modern robotics industry through its descendant platforms (TurtleBot, Fetch, Stretch).

**(b) Forward Kinematics Derivation (7 marks):**
- **DH Convention Setup (1 mark):** Define the standard homogeneous transformation matrix $^{i-1}T_i$ in terms of $\theta_i$, $d_i$, $a_i$, $\alpha_i$.
- **DH Parameter Table (2 marks):** Present the 7-row DH table for the PR2 right arm (as shown in Section 3.1.2 of these notes).
- **Individual Transformations (2 marks):** Show $^{0}T_1$ and $^{1}T_2$ explicitly as worked examples; state that the remaining 5 follow the identical substitution pattern.
- **Composite Product (1 mark):** Write $^{0}T_7 = {^{0}T_1} \cdot {^{1}T_2} \cdot {^{2}T_3} \cdot {^{3}T_4} \cdot {^{4}T_5} \cdot {^{5}T_6} \cdot {^{6}T_7}$ and extract the translation column $[p_x, p_y, p_z]^T$.
- **Reach Validation (1 mark):** Confirm $L_{reach} = a_3 + a_5 = 0.8$ m and effective workspace radius ≈ 1.0 m.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU PERUT525 Module 3:**

- [x] **PR2 = Personal Robot 2**, developed by **Willow Garage** in **2010**, originally costing ~**USD 400,000**.
- [x] "**Computation-complete**" means all CPUs, RAM, storage are *onboard* — no off-board PC required.
- [x] **Onboard Compute:** 2× Intel Xeon E3-1260L, 24 GB RAM, RAID-1 storage, internal gigabit Ethernet.
- [x] **Mobile Base:** 4-wheel Swedish-90 omni-drive (holonomic, 3-DOF planar motion).
- [x] **Manipulation:** 2 × 7-DOF arms + 2 × grippers + 2 × F/T sensors + liftable torso.
- [x] **Sensors:** Stereo cameras, tilt laser, 2 base lasers, IMU, Force/Torque, Kinect.
- [x] **TF Tree Root:** `base_footprint` → `base_link` → sub-trees for torso, head, arms, wheels.
- [x] **Standard ROS topics:** `/joint_states` (100 Hz), `/tf` (100 Hz), `/base_scan`, `/imu/data`, `/odom`.
- [x] **DH Convention** for the PR2 right arm has 7 rows with $a_3 = a_5 = 0.4$ m (upper arm and forearm).
- [x] **Effective reach:** $L = a_3 + a_5 = 0.8$ m; **effective workspace radius:** ≈ **1.0 m**.
- [x] **Jacobian size** for one arm: 6×7 (one redundant DOF used for null-space obstacle avoidance).
- [x] **PR2 pioneered:** ROS tutorials, MoveIt!, actionlib, rosbag, TF, URDF, diagnostics — all canonical ROS concepts.
- [x] **Modern descendants:** TurtleBot (education), Fetch & Freight (warehouse), Stretch (home manipulation).

> **Final KTU Examiner's Tip:** When writing about PR2 in exams, *always* tie your answer back to a specific ROS concept (TF tree, URDF, actionlib, topics). Examiners reward answers that demonstrate the **ROS-PR2 co-evolution**, not just hardware specs in isolation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 3
