<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fetch** is a commercially developed, integrated **mobile manipulator robot platform** manufactured by **Fetch Robotics** (now part of Zebra Technologies), extensively used in academic research, warehouse automation, and as a canonical reference platform for teaching **Robot Operating System (ROS)** concepts. Within the KTU PERUT525 syllabus (Module 3), "Fetch" refers specifically to the **ROS-empowered software ecosystem** surrounding the Fetch robot — including its **URDF/Xacro description**, **Gazebo simulation model**, **MoveIt! configuration**, **navigation stack integration**, and **manipulation pipelines**.

The Fetch robot is typically paired with its mobile base called **Freight**. While *Freight* is a holonomic mobile base used purely for transportation, *Fetch* adds a **7-degree-of-freedom (7-DOF) manipulator arm**, a **torso lift (prismatic joint)**, a **head with pan-tilt and RGB-D sensors**, and onboard computing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Examiner-Wording):** *Fetch is an open-platform mobile manipulator with a 7-DOF arm, telescoping spine, and pan-tilt head, fully supported by ROS-native packages, URDF descriptions, and MoveIt! configurations for research and simulation in service robotics.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **tall, wheeled office assistant** that can:
- **Move around the office freely** (like an autonomous wheelchair).
- **Stretch its spine** to reach high shelves or bend down to pick things off the floor (just like a human reaching up or crouching).
- **Rotate its "head"** to look around with cameras, similar to a CCTV camera mounted on a swivel mount.
- **Pick up objects** using a 7-jointed arm — the same way a human arm has shoulder, elbow, and wrist joints.

In essence, **Fetch is a "robot butler on wheels."**

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** Fetch = Mobile Base (Freight) + 7-DOF Arm + Telescopic Spine + Sensor-Rich Head. It is the **"Hello World" mobile manipulator** of the ROS ecosystem.

## 1.3 Key Hardware Specifications (KTU-Relevant Metrics)

| Component | Specification |
|---|---|
| Mobile Base (Freight) | Differential drive with **2 casters**, max speed **1.5 m/s** |
| Manipulator Arm | **7-DOF**, payload **6 kg** |
| Torso Spine | Prismatic lift, range **~0.9 m to 1.5 m** |
| Head | Pan-tilt with **Asus Xtion / Intel RealSense** RGB-D camera |
| Onboard PC | Intel i7 CPU, 16 GB RAM (typical research variant) |
| Battery | Lithium-ion, ~8–10 hours runtime |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All ROS packages for Fetch are open-source and hosted under `fetchrobotics/fetch_ros`, making it the **de facto reference mobile manipulator** for ROS education globally.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Breakdown of the Fetch ROS Ecosystem

The Fetch robot in ROS is decomposed into several logically separated software layers. Understanding each layer is essential for KTU Module 3.

### Layer 1: Robot Description (URDF / Xacro)
- The physical Fetch robot is described using a **Unified Robot Description Format (URDF)** file, often composed via **Xacro (XML Macros)** for modularity.
- Key files: `fetch.urdf.xacro`, `fetch_description/robots/fetch.xtion.urdf.xacro`.
- Contains **kinematic chains**, **joint limits**, **inertial properties**, and **visual/collision geometries**.

### Layer 2: Hardware Abstraction (`fetch_drivers`)
- Provides real-time interfaces to motors, sensors, and actuators.
- Publishes **JointStates**, **IMU**, **laser scans**, **point clouds**, **RGB images**, and **depth images** as ROS topics.

### Layer 3: Simulation (Gazebo)
- A **Gazebo-compatible model** of Fetch is provided (`fetch_gazebo`).
- Simulates physics, sensors, and collisions identically to the real robot.
- Enables testing navigation, manipulation, and perception without the physical hardware.

### Layer 4: MoveIt! Configuration
- Fetch ships with a pre-configured **MoveIt! package** (`fetch_moveit_config`).
- Defines the **planning group** (typically `arm`), **end-effector** (gripper), and **named poses**.
- Supports **OMPL** planners (RRTConnect, PRMstar, etc.) and **CHOMP / STOMP** optimization-based planners.

### Layer 5: Navigation Stack
- Uses **`move_base`** with the **AMCL** localization and **SLAM** (gmapping/cartographer).
- The costmap layers include the **manipulator's reach footprint** since Fetch is taller than a typical TurtleBot.

### Layer 6: Perception & Manipulation Pipelines
- Built on **`perception`** pipelines using **PCL** and **OpenCV**.
- Object detection, 6D pose estimation (e.g., **Furniture-FLD**, **YCB object set**), and grasp planning using **Grasp Pose Detection (GPD)** or **Dex-Net**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Takeaway:** The Fetch stack is a vertically integrated reference architecture — every ROS concept (URDF, topics, services, actions, TF tree, MoveIt!, nav stack) can be demonstrated on a single platform.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Equation / Definition |
|---|---|---|
| $n_{DOF}$ | Degrees of freedom of Fetch arm | $n_{DOF} = 7$ |
| $\theta_i$ | Joint $i$ angle of arm | $\theta_i \in [\theta_{i,min}, \theta_{i,max}]$ |
| $T_{base}^{ee}$ | End-effector pose w.r.t. base | $T_{base}^{ee} = \prod_{i=1}^{n_{DOF}} A_i(\theta_i)$ |
| $v_{max}$ | Max base linear velocity | $v_{max} = 1.5 \text{ m/s}$ |
| $\omega_{max}$ | Max base angular velocity | $\omega_{max} \approx 1.0 \text{ rad/s}$ |
| $h_{spine}$ | Spine height (prismatic) | $h_{spine} \in [0, h_{max}]$, $h_{max} \approx 0.6$ m |
| $m_{payload}$ | Arm payload | $m_{payload} \leq 6 \text{ kg}$ |
| $f_{pub}$ | Typical JointState publish rate | $f_{pub} \approx 100 \text{ Hz}$ |
| $\Delta t_{control}$ | Control loop timestep | $\Delta t_{control} = 0.01 \text{ s}$ |

## 2.3 Why Fetch is the KTU-Mandated Reference Platform

1. **Open-Source End-to-End:** From low-level drivers to high-level AI perception, Fetch exposes **every layer** of the ROS stack.
2. **Hardware-Software Co-design:** The URDF, controllers, and planners are written by the *same team* that designed the hardware → no model mismatches.
3. **Reproducibility:** A KTU student can run **identical experiments** on a simulation and on real hardware — a property critical for academic publishing.
4. **Industrial Relevance:** Fetch Robotics was acquired by Zebra Technologies for **\$290 million (2017)**, validating the platform's commercial viability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Forward Kinematics of the Fetch Manipulator Arm

The Fetch arm consists of **7 revolute joints**. We denote the joints as $J_1$ through $J_7$, where $J_1$ is the shoulder pan, $J_2$ shoulder lift, $J_3$ upper arm roll, $J_4$ elbow flex, $J_5$ forearm roll, $J_6$ wrist flex, and $J_7$ wrist roll.

### Derivation: Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) Parameter Approach

The homogeneous transformation matrix $A_i$ for joint $i$ using the **modified DH convention** is:

$$
A_i = \begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i & 0 & a_{i-1} \\
\sin\theta_i \cos\alpha_{i-1} & \cos\theta_i \cos\alpha_{i-1} & -\sin\alpha_{i-1} & -d_i \sin\alpha_{i-1} \\
\sin\theta_i \sin\alpha_{i-1} & \cos\theta_i \sin\alpha_{i-1} & \cos\alpha_{i-1} & d_i \cos\alpha_{i-1} \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

For the Fetch arm, the **modified DH parameters** (approximated from `fetch_description`) are:

| Joint $i$ | $a_{i-1}$ (m) | $\alpha_{i-1}$ (rad) | $d_i$ (m) | $\theta_i$ offset (rad) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 0.119 | 0 |
| 2 | 0 | $-\pi/2$ | 0 | $\pi/2$ |
| 3 | 0.35 | 0 | 0.425 | 0 |
| 4 | 0.075 | $\pi/2$ | 0 | 0 |
| 5 | 0 | $-\pi/2$ | 0.395 | 0 |
| 6 | 0 | $\pi/2$ | 0 | 0 |
| 7 | 0 | 0 | 0.102 | 0 |

### Step-by-Step Computation

The end-effector pose relative to the base is computed as:

$$
T_{base}^{ee} = A_1 \cdot A_2 \cdot A_3 \cdot A_4 \cdot A_5 \cdot A_6 \cdot A_7
$$

Substituting step by step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_3 &= A_1 \cdot A_2 \cdot A_3 \\
T_5 &= T_3 \cdot A_4 \cdot A_5 \\
T_{base}^{ee} &= T_5 \cdot A_6 \cdot A_7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of each step:**
- **Step 1:** $A_1$ rotates the base about the shoulder pan axis; $A_2$ tilts the shoulder lift by $-\pi/2$ — this orients the first arm segment vertically downward.
- **Step 2:** $A_3$ extends along $0.425$ m to the elbow. $A_4$ rotates at the elbow flex (the dominant bending joint).
- **Step 3:** $A_5$ rolls the forearm; $A_6$ flexes the wrist; $A_7$ rolls the wrist — aligning the gripper with the target object.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In ROS, this DH-based computation is automatically performed by the **KDL** or **IKFast** kinematics plugin configured in `fetch_moveit_config`. The student does **NOT** need to code this manually — but explaining the derivation earns full marks in a KTU 14-mark question.

## 3.2 Workspace Analysis

The reachable workspace of Fetch's arm is the union of spheres traced by the wrist:

$$
W = \bigcup_{\theta \in \Theta} \; \{ p \in \mathbb{R}^3 \mid \|p - p_{base}\| \leq L_{total} \}
$$

where $L_{total} = 0.35 + 0.425 + 0.395 + 0.102 = 1.272 \text{ m}$ (sum of link lengths), and $\Theta$ is the feasible joint configuration set.

$$
\boxed{L_{total} = 1.272 \text{ m}}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Task-to-Package Mapping

| ROS Task | Required Fetch Package | Key Topics / Services |
|---|---|---|
| View joint states | `fetch_driver` | `/joint_states` |
| Move base | `fetch_navigation` | `/cmd_vel`, `/move_base` |
| Plan arm motion | `fetch_moveit_config` | `/move_group` |
| Grasp object | `fetch_gazebo_demo` + `grasp_msgs` | `/gripper_controller/command` |
| Visualize robot | `rviz` + `fetch_description` | TF, RobotModel |
| Simulate | `fetch_gazebo` | `gazebo.launch` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`
**What is the Fetch robot, and what are its key hardware components?**

**Model Answer:**
Fetch is an open-source mobile manipulator robot platform used as a reference design in ROS. Its key hardware components are:
- **Mobile Base (Freight):** Differential-drive base for holonomic-like motion.
- **7-DOF Manipulator Arm:** 7 revolute joints for human-like dexterity.
- **Telescopic Spine:** Prismatic joint allowing vertical reach adjustment.
- **Pan-Tilt Head:** With RGB-D camera for perception.
- **Onboard Computer:** Intel i7 running ROS Master and nodes.

*(Examiner expects keywords: mobile manipulator, 7-DOF, ROS, RGB-D, Freight — 3 marks for completeness.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the main ROS packages associated with the Fetch robot.**

**Model Answer:**
1. `fetch_description` — URDF/Xacro robot model
2. `fetch_driver` — Hardware drivers and JointState publisher
3. `fetch_gazebo` — Gazebo simulation launchers
4. `fetch_moveit_config` — MoveIt! planning configuration
5. `fetch_navigation` — 2D navigation stack integration
6. `fetch_ros` — Meta-package tying everything together

*(3 marks for naming ≥4 packages with brief roles.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **software architecture of the Fetch ROS stack**. List all major layers and their functions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **forward kinematics equation** for the Fetch 7-DOF arm using the **modified Denavit-Hartenberg convention**. State the DH parameters and show at least three intermediate transformation steps. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The Fetch ROS architecture consists of **six vertically stacked layers**:

1. **Robot Description Layer** (`fetch_description`): Provides URDF/Xacro files describing the kinematic structure, joint limits, and sensor placements.

2. **Hardware Driver Layer** (`fetch_driver`): Real-time interfaces to motors and sensors; publishes `/joint_states`, `/imu`, `/scan`, `/head_camera/depth/image_raw` at fixed rates.

3. **Simulation Layer** (`fetch_gazebo`): Provides a Gazebo-compatible model for offline testing, including physics simulation and sensor noise models.

4. **Perception Layer**: Uses PCL, OpenCV, and optionally deep-learning models (YOLO, Mask-RCNN) for object detection and pose estimation.

5. **Motion Planning Layer** (`fetch_moveit_config`): MoveIt!-based planning for the 7-DOF arm with OMPL planners; supports collision-aware planning.

6. **Navigation Layer** (`fetch_navigation`): 2D SLAM using gmapping/Cartographer, AMCL localization, and `move_base` for path planning.

```
User Task → MoveIt! / move_base → Perception (PCL/YOLO) → Gazebo/Sim → Drivers → Robot
                                              ↑                                              ↓
                                          TF tree / JointStates ←──────── Sensors ←──── Hardware
```

*(7 marks: 4 marks for layers + 3 marks for diagram.)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

Using the **modified DH convention**, each joint's transformation matrix is:

$$
A_i = R_z(\theta_i) \cdot T_z(d_i) \cdot T_x(a_{i-1}) \cdot R_x(\alpha_{i-1})
$$

The DH parameters for Fetch are tabulated in Section 3.1 above. The end-effector pose is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{base}^{ee} &= \prod_{i=1}^{7} A_i(\theta_i) \\
&= A_1 \cdot A_2 \cdot A_3 \cdot A_4 \cdot A_5 \cdot A_6 \cdot A_7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Intermediate computations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_3 &= A_1 A_2 A_3 \\
&= \begin{bmatrix}
c_1 c_{23} & -c_1 s_{23} & s_1 & c_1(0.35 c_2 + 0.425 c_{23}) \\
s_1 c_{23} & -s_1 s_{23} & -c_1 & s_1(0.35 c_2 + 0.425 c_{23}) \\
s_{23} & c_{23} & 0 & 0.35 s_2 + 0.425 s_{23} + 0.119 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $c_i = \cos\theta_i$, $s_i = \sin\theta_i$, and $c_{23} = \cos(\theta_2 + \theta_3)$, etc.

Continuing similarly for $T_5$ and $T_7$, the final $T_{base}^{ee}$ gives the end-effector position and orientation.

*(7 marks: 2 for DH table + 3 for matrix multiplication + 2 for final pose expression.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Describe the **MoveIt! configuration** for the Fetch robot. Explain the role of the planning group, end-effector, and named poses. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the procedure to **launch Fetch in Gazebo**, navigate it to a target, and execute a **pick-and-place task**. Mention the relevant launch files and ROS topics. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The `fetch_moveit_config` package contains:

1. **`config/kinematics.yaml`:** Specifies the kinematics solver — typically **KDL** with a timeout of 0.05 s, attempting up to 5 solutions.

2. **`config/ompl_planning.yaml`:** Defines planners such as **RRTConnect** and **PRMstar** with default planning time 5 s.

3. **`config/fetch.srdf`:** Semantic Robot Description Format — defines:
   - **Planning Group:** `arm` (7 joints: `shoulder_pan_joint` to `wrist_roll_joint`).
   - **End-Effector:** `gripper` — linked to `wrist_roll_link`.
   - **Named Poses:** `home`, `sleep`, `stow` — pre-defined configurations for common tasks.

4. **Launch Files:** `demo.launch` opens RViz with the MoveIt! MotionPlanning plugin.

*(7 marks: 2 planning group + 2 end-effector + 2 named poses + 1 launch file.)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Launch Gazebo Simulation:**

```bash
roslaunch fetch_gazebo simulation.launch
```

This spawns the Fetch robot in an empty world with all sensor plugins active.

**Step 2 — Launch MoveIt! and RViz:**

```bash
roslaunch fetch_moveit_config demo.launch
```

This opens RViz with the MoveIt! plugin enabled for interactive planning.

**Step 3 — Navigate to Target (using `fetch_navigation`):**

```bash
roslaunch fetch_navigation explore.launch   # for SLAM
roslaunch fetch_navigation navigation.launch # for move_base
rosrun rviz rviz                            # visualize
```

A 2D Nav Goal is set in RViz, sending a `geometry_msgs/PoseStamped` to `/move_base_simple/goal`.

**Step 4 — Pick-and-Place Pipeline:**

Relevant topics and actions:
- `/arm_controller/follow_joint_trajectory` — Action server for arm motion.
- `/gripper_controller/gripper_cmd` — Topic to open/close gripper.
- `/head_controller/point_head` — Action to direct the head camera.

**Pseudocode Flow:**

1. Localize target via head camera.
2. Plan arm trajectory using `move_group` MoveIt! API.
3. Execute `FollowJointTrajectory` action.
4. Close gripper via `gripper_cmd`.
5. Retreat arm, navigate to drop location.
6. Open gripper.

*(7 marks: 2 for launch + 2 for navigation + 3 for pick-and-place pipeline.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **omit the DH parameter table** — losing 2 marks in 14-mark derivations. Always include the table *before* the matrix multiplication.
> - **Do NOT confuse Fetch (manipulator) with Freight (mobile base alone).** Examiners deduct 1 mark for this mix-up.
> - When asked about MoveIt!, **mentioning SRDF** is essential — many students skip this.
> - Avoid writing `KDL kinematics` without specifying the **timeout and attempts** — the YAML configuration is part of the mark scheme.
> - In pick-and-place questions, the **sequence (localize → plan → execute → grasp → retreat)** is critical; reversing steps loses coherence marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Fetch** = Mobile manipulator with **7-DOF arm**, telescopic spine, and pan-tilt head.
- ✅ **Freight** is the mobile base alone — Fetch = Freight + Manipulator.
- ✅ ROS packages: `fetch_description`, `fetch_driver`, `fetch_gazebo`, `fetch_moveit_config`, `fetch_navigation`.
- ✅ URDF is described using **Xacro macros** for modularity.
- ✅ Forward kinematics uses **modified DH parameters** — total arm reach ≈ **1.272 m**.
- ✅ MoveIt! planning group: `arm`; end-effector: `gripper`; named poses: `home`, `sleep`, `stow`.
- ✅ Gazebo launch: `roslaunch fetch_gazebo simulation.launch`.
- ✅ Navigation uses **AMCL + move_base**; manipulation uses **MoveIt! + OMPL**.
- ✅ Pick-and-place: localize → plan → execute → grasp → retreat → drop.
- ✅ Head-mounted **RGB-D camera** (Asus Xtion / Intel RealSense) is the primary perception sensor.
- ✅ Fetch is an **open-source** platform — URDF, drivers, and MoveIt! configs are all publicly available.
- ✅ Joint states published at **~100 Hz**; control loop runs at **100 Hz** ($\Delta t = 0.01$ s).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robonaut 2
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT525 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS)
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Robonaut 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Robonaut 2 (R2)** is a state-of-the-art, dexterous, humanoid robot developed collaboratively by **NASA's Johnson Space Center (JSC)** and **General Motors (GM)**. It is a direct successor to the original Robonaut (R1) and represents one of the first humanoid robotic systems specifically engineered to operate safely in close proximity to humans, both in terrestrial settings and in the microgravity environment of the International Space Station (ISS). Within the context of the **Robot Operating System (ROS)**, Robonaut 2 is a flagship open-source reference platform, with its entire control stack, message definitions, and manipulation pipelines built natively on ROS.

From a KTU 2024 perspective, Robonaut 2 is studied as a benchmark case for **human-safe humanoid manipulation**, **bimanual task execution**, **force-torque compliant control**, and **ROS-based hardware abstraction** for complex multi-DOF (Degrees of Freedom) systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PERUT525 / Module 3):** Robonaut 2 is a mandatory study item because it bridges the gap between *abstract ROS middleware concepts* (topics, services, actions, nodelets) and *real-world deployment* on a 42-DOF humanoid with skin-like tactile sensing.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **sophisticated puppet master** standing inside a spacecraft. The puppet (Robonaut 2) has hands almost as nimble as a human astronaut wearing a pressurized spacesuit glove. The puppeteer (the ground controller, or an autonomous ROS planner) sends high-level commands like "unscrew this bolt" or "hold this wrench" — and the puppet executes these commands with millimetre-level precision, while continuously reporting back the forces it feels at its fingertips.

**Plain-English Breakdown:**
- The **robot body** = a human-shaped machine with two arms, two hands, a head, and a torso.
- The **"brain"** = a distributed set of ROS nodes running across onboard and ground computers.
- The **"nervous system"** = ROS topics and services shuttling sensor data (camera images, joint angles, fingertip forces) at high frequency.
- The **"senses"** = cameras in the head, position sensors in every joint, and force/torque sensors in the hands.
- The **"muscles"** = brushless DC motors in the arms, hands, and neck.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric to Remember:** Robonaut 2 possesses **42 active Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** distributed across its upper body, with each hand alone containing **12 DOF** — making it one of the most dexterous humanoid manipulators ever flown in space.

### 1.3 Why Robonaut 2 Matters in ROS

Robonaut 2 is significant for KTU students because it was one of the **earliest production-grade humanoid robots to open-source its ROS interface**, providing reusable packages such as:
- `r2_msgs` — custom message types for joint control, hand pose, and force feedback.
- `r2_controllers` — hardware-interface plugins that talk to EtherCAT motor drivers via the `ros_control` framework.
- Integration with **MoveIt!** for motion planning of dual-arm manipulation.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Before Robonaut 2, most industrial robots used proprietary controllers. R2's adoption of ROS demonstrated that **open-source middleware** could meet the safety, real-time, and reliability demands of human-rated spaceflight hardware.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Pillars of Robonaut 2

The Robonaut 2 system can be decomposed into **four tightly coupled architectural layers**. Each layer maps cleanly onto ROS concepts, which is why it is featured in PERUT525.

**Layer 1 — Mechanical Skeleton (Hardware Layer)**
- Upper-body humanoid: head, torso, two 7-DOF arms, two 12-DOF hands, 3-DOF neck.
- Compliant joints with series elastic elements for safe human contact.
- Force-torque (F/T) sensors mounted at the wrists and fingertips.
- Stereo vision cameras in the head for depth perception.

**Layer 2 — Low-Level Real-Time Control (Hardware Abstraction Layer)**
- Onboard real-time controller running **EtherCAT** communication at **1 kHz** (1,000 Hz update rate).
- The `ros_control` package provides the standard interface between ROS commands and the real-time controller.
- Joint state feedback is published as `sensor_msgs/JointState` messages.
- Each joint is commanded via `trajectory_msgs/JointTrajectory` or `std_msgs/Float64` arrays.

**Layer 3 — High-Level Autonomy & Planning (ROS Middleware Layer)**
- Runs on ground computers or onboard auxiliary processors.
- Uses **MoveIt!** for collision-aware motion planning of both arms simultaneously.
- Perception pipeline uses OpenCV and PCL (Point Cloud Library) wrapped in ROS nodes.
- Task-level scripting uses **SMACH** (State Machine) or **Behavior Trees** (BT).

**Layer 4 — Teleoperation & Supervisory Interface (User Layer)**
- Supports virtual-reity-based telepresence where a human operator wearing a haptic vest and motion-capture gloves controls R2.
- Haptic feedback flows back through custom ROS topics from R2's F/T sensors to the operator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this layering matters for ROS:** R2's design proves the **separation-of-concerns** principle of ROS — the real-time motor loop is *isolated* from the slower planning/AI loop, communicating only via well-defined message contracts. This is exactly the **nodelet + topic** pattern taught in Module 3.

### 2.2 ROS Software Stack Mapping

Below is the mapping between R2's subsystems and their corresponding ROS packages:

| R2 Subsystem | ROS Package / Concept | Message Type / Topic | Update Rate |
|---|---|---|---|
| Joint State Reporting | `ros_control` + `controller_manager` | `sensor_msgs/JointState` | 1000 Hz |
| Arm Trajectory Execution | `joint_trajectory_controller` | `trajectory_msgs/JointTrajectory` | 1000 Hz |
| Hand Pose Control | `r2_hand_controller` | `r2_msgs/HandCommand` | 500 Hz |
| Stereo Vision | `stereo_image_proc`, `image_transport` | `sensor_msgs/Image` | 30 Hz |
| Force/Torque Feedback | `geometry_msgs/WrenchStamped` | Custom `r2_msgs/ForceSensor` | 1000 Hz |
| Motion Planning | `moveit_core`, `moveit_planners` | `moveit_msgs/MoveGroupAction` | 10–50 Hz |
| Teleoperation | Custom VR nodes + `tf2` | `geometry_msgs/PoseStamped` | 100 Hz |
| Task Sequencing | `smach_ros`, `behavior_tree` | Actionlib goals | Event-driven |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

Although Robonaut 2 is a hardware-centric system, several **kinematic and control equations** are essential for KTU exams. These govern its motion planning and force-controlled manipulation.

| Concept | Equation | Variable Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forward Kinematics (FK) | $T(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i(\theta_i)$ | $T$ = end-effector pose, $A_i$ = DH matrix, $\theta_i$ = joint angle | $T$ dimensionless pose, $\theta$ in **rad** |
| Denavit–Hartenberg Matrix | $A_i = \begin{bmatrix} c_{\theta_i} & -s_{\theta_i} c_{\alpha_i} & s_{\theta_i} s_{\alpha_i} & a_i c_{\theta_i} \\ s_{\theta_i} & c_{\theta_i} c_{\alpha_i} & -c_{\theta_i} s_{\alpha_i} & a_i s_{\theta_i} \\ 0 & s_{\alpha_i} & c_{\alpha_i} & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $a_i, d_i, \alpha_i, \theta_i$ = DH parameters | rad, m |
| Inverse Kinematics (IK) | $\theta = f^{-1}(x_{desired}, y_{desired}, z_{desired})$ | Non-linear, solved via Jacobian or numerical IK | rad |
| Jacobian Relation | $\dot{x} = J(\theta) \cdot \dot{\theta}$ | $J$ = Jacobian, $\dot{x}$ = end-effector velocity, $\dot{\theta}$ = joint rates | m/s, rad/s |
| Impedance Control Law | $M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Kx = F_{ext}$ | $M$ = virtual mass, $B$ = damping, $K$ = stiffness, $F_{ext}$ = external force | N, m, kg |
| Force-Torque Sensor Output | $\tau = J^T F_{ext}$ | $\tau$ = joint torque, $J^T$ = Jacobian transpose, $F_{ext}$ = Cartesian force | Nm, N |
| Total DOF (Bimanual R2) | $DOF_{total} = 2 \cdot DOF_{arm} + 2 \cdot DOF_{hand} + DOF_{head} + DOF_{neck}$ | R2: $2(7) + 2(12) + 2 + 3 = 42$ | count |
| Communication Latency Target | $t_{latency} \leq 10$ ms | Maximum acceptable round-trip for teleoperation | ms |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Note:** KTU frequently tests the **Jacobian transpose method** for converting Cartesian F/T sensor readings back into joint torques, because this is the mathematical heart of R2's compliant manipulation.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Robonaut 2's design has had cascading influence across the robotics industry:
- **Automotive assembly lines:** GM deploys R2-derived双臂 manipulators for tasks requiring human-like dexterity (e.g., soft-material handling).
- **Space exploration:** The successor **Robonaut 5 ( Valkyrie / R5 )** is being developed for the **Artemis lunar program** using the same ROS-2 architecture.
- **Disaster response:** R2's compliance control inspired robots used in Fukushima-style hazardous environments.
- **Medical robotics:** The impedance-control law $M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Kx = F_{ext}$ is now standard in surgical robots like the **Da Vinci Research Kit (DVRK)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown: ROS-Based Motion Pipeline of Robonaut 2

> This section provides an *exhaustive, step-by-step* analytical walk-through of how a ROS command travels from a high-level task planner all the way down to Robonaut 2's motor windings. Every transition is explicitly shown — no logical steps are skipped.

### 3.1 Problem Setup — Reaching for a Target Object

**Scenario:** Robonaut 2 is aboard the ISS. A ground operator (or autonomous planner) issues a ROS action goal: *"Move the right hand to the air-filter panel located at coordinates (x, y, z) = (0.45 m, -0.12 m, 0.30 m) with a desired end-effector orientation given by a quaternion."*

The robot must:
1. Plan a collision-free trajectory.
2. Convert the Cartesian goal into joint angles.
3. Stream the trajectory to the low-level controller.
4. Apply force feedback corrections if the hand contacts an unexpected obstacle.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Pipeline

**Step 1 — Action Goal Submission (User Layer)**
A client node sends an action goal to the MoveIt! `MoveGroup` action server.

$$
G_{goal} = \{\, x_{ee},\, y_{ee},\, z_{ee},\, q_x,\, q_y,\, q_z,\, q_w,\, \text{frame}_{ee} \,\}
$$

where the quaternion $(q_x, q_y, q_z, q_w)$ encodes orientation, and $\text{frame}_{ee}$ is the target end-effector frame (e.g., `right_hand_palm`).

**Step 2 — Scene Perception (Perception Layer)**
Point cloud from the stereo cameras is segmented to identify the air-filter panel. The object's pose is published on a ROS topic:

$$
P_{object} \rightarrow \text{topic: } /detected\_objects \rightarrow \text{type: } \vert geometry\_msgs \vert /PoseStamped
$$

**Step 3 — Motion Planning (Planning Layer)**
MoveIt!'s OMPL plugin (Open Motion Planning Library) runs an RRT* (Rapidly-exploring Random Tree) search in the robot's **configuration space** (C-space). The dimensionality of this space equals the number of active arm joints:

$$
n_{C} = 7 \quad \text{(for one 7-DOF R2 arm)}
$$

The planner returns a dense trajectory of waypoints:

$$
\tau_{plan} = [\, q(t_0),\, q(t_1),\, \dots,\, q(t_N) \,]
$$

where each $q(t_i) \in \mathbb{R}^{7}$ is a 7-D joint configuration vector.

**Step 4 — Trajectory Smoothing (Optimization Layer)**
Raw RRT* output is jerky. A time-parameterization algorithm (TrajOpt/CHOMP) smooths the path:

$$
\min_{\tau} \int_{0}^{T} \left[ \alpha \cdot \vert \ddot{\tau}(t) \vert^{2} + \beta \cdot \vert \tau_{obstacle}(t) \vert^{2} \right] dt
$$

subject to:

$$
q_{min} \leq \tau(t) \leq q_{max} \quad \text{and} \quad \vert \dot{\tau}(t) \vert \leq \dot{q}_{max}
$$

The output is a `JointTrajectory` message with smooth position, velocity, and acceleration profiles.

**Step 5 — Inverse Kinematics (IK Layer) — Pre-Planning Check**
Before execution, MoveIt! uses **KDL** (Kinematics and Dynamics Library) or **TRAC-IK** to verify that the start and goal poses are reachable.

The IK equation for a 7-DOF arm has redundancy (7 unknowns, 6 Cartesian constraints), so the **null-space** is exploited to optimize secondary criteria like joint-limit avoidance:

$$
\theta = \theta_{primary} + (I - J^{\dagger}J)\, z
$$

where $J^{\dagger} = J^T(JJ^T)^{-1}$ is the **Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse** of the Jacobian, and $z$ is an arbitrary vector projected into the null space.

**Step 6 — Hardware Interface Translation (Hardware Abstraction Layer)**
The `ros_control` `JointTrajectoryController` samples the smooth trajectory at **1 kHz** and converts each joint angle to a motor command. For a typical brushless DC motor, the command voltage is:

$$
V_{cmd}(t) = K_p \cdot (q_{desired}(t) - q_{actual}(t)) + K_d \cdot (\dot{q}_{desired}(t) - \dot{q}_{actual}(t)) + \int K_i \cdot e(t) \, dt
$$

This is the classical **PID control law**, with $K_p, K_d, K_i$ as the proportional, derivative, and integral gains respectively.

**Step 7 — Force-Torque Compliance (Sensing Layer)**
If the hand contacts an obstacle unexpectedly, the wrist F/T sensor measures:

$$
F_{ext} = [\, F_x, F_y, F_z, \tau_x, \tau_y, \tau_z \, ]^T
$$

The impedance-control law computes a Cartesian correction:

$$
\Delta x = \frac{F_{ext}}{K_{virtual}}
$$

and this displacement is converted back to joint-space via the Jacobian transpose:

$$
\Delta \theta = J^T(\theta) \cdot F_{ext}
$$

The controller then blends this $\Delta \theta$ into the ongoing trajectory, achieving **soft contact behaviour**.

**Step 8 — Feedback Publication (ROS Middleware Layer)**
Every cycle, the controller publishes the actual joint state:

$$
\text{topic: } /r2/joint\_states \rightarrow \text{type: } \vert sensor\_msgs \vert /JointState
$$

containing $\{ name[i],\, position[i],\, velocity[i],\, effort[i] \}$ for all 42 joints. Higher-level nodes (visualization in RViz, monitoring dashboards) subscribe to this topic in a **decoupled** manner — the very essence of ROS's publish-subscribe architecture.

### 3.3 Summary Equation — Closed-Loop Control Block

The full closed-loop dynamics of one R2 joint can be written compactly as:

$$
\ddot{q}_{actual} = M^{-1}(\theta)\left[\, \tau_{motor} - C(\theta,\dot{\theta})\dot{\theta} - G(\theta) + J^T F_{ext} \,\right]
$$

where:
- $M(\theta)$ = mass/inertia matrix (configuration-dependent)
- $C(\theta, \dot{\theta})\dot{\theta}$ = Coriolis and centrifugal terms
- $G(\theta)$ = gravitational torque vector (important in 1-g testing on Earth)
- $J^T F_{ext}$ = external torque from contact forces
- $\tau_{motor}$ = commanded motor torque from Step 6

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Takeaway:** Notice how every block in this pipeline is a **ROS node or nodelet** communicating over well-defined topics. This is the textbook example of how ROS enables *modular, distributed, real-time humanoid control* — a core learning outcome of PERUT525 Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> The following Mermaid diagrams map the **physical subsystems** and the **ROS software architecture** of Robonaut 2. Per the protocol, all node IDs are alphanumeric and labels are kept clean (no markdown formatting inside quoted labels).

### 4.1 Robonaut 2 Physical Architecture (Block-Level View)

```mermaid
graph TD
    HeadBlock[HEAD STEREO CAMERAS 2X]
    NeckBlock[NECK PAN TILT 3DOF]
    TorsoBlock[TORSO CENTRAL SPINE]
    LArmBlock[LEFT ARM 7DOF]
    RArmBlock[RIGHT ARM 7DOF]

    RHandBlock[RIGHT HAND 12DOF]

    WristFTR[RIGHT WRIST F T SENSOR]
    EThercatBlock[ETHERCAT REAL TIME BUS 1KHZ]
    OnboardRT[ONBOARD REAL TIME CONTROLLER]
    AuxComp[AUXILIARY LINUX COMPUTER]
    GroundOp[GROUND OPERATOR VR STATION]
    ISSLink[ISS KU BAND COMMUNICATION LINK]

    HeadBlock --> NeckBlock
    NeckBlock --> TorsoBlock
    TorsoBlock --> LArmBlock
    TorsoBlock --> RArmBlock
    LArmBlock --> LHandBlock
    RArmBlock --> RHandBlock
    LArmBlock --> WristFTL
    RArmBlock --> WristFTR
    LArmBlock --> EThercatBlock
    RArmBlock --> EThercatBlock
    LHandBlock --> EThercatBlock
    RHandBlock --> EThercatBlock
    WristFTL --> EThercatBlock
    WristFTR --> EThercatBlock
    EThercatBlock --> OnboardRT
    OnboardRT --> AuxComp
    AuxComp --> ISSLink
    ISSLink --> GroundOp
```

### 4.2 Robonaut 2 ROS Software Node Graph

```mermaid
graph TD
    CamNode[STEREO CAMERA NODE]
    PclNode[POINT CLOUD NODE]

    MoveitNode[MOVEIT MOVE GROUP ACTION SERVER]

    IkNode[INVERSE KINEMATICS NODE KDL]
    JtcNode[JOINT TRAJECTORY CONTROLLER]
    HWIntNode[HARDWARE INTERFACE ETHERCAT]
    FbNode[FORCE TORQUE FEEDBACK NODE]
    VizNode[RVIZ VISUALIZATION NODE]

    TaskNode[SMACH TASK SUPERVISOR]
    StatePub[JOINT STATE PUBLISHER]

    CamNode --> PclNode
    PclNode --> ObjDetNode
    ObjDetNode --> MoveitNode
    TeleopNode --> MoveitNode
    TaskNode --> MoveitNode

```

### 4.3 ROS Topic & Message Flow (End-to-End Command Cycle)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[OPERATOR GOAL POSE] --> B[MOVEIT ACTION CLIENT]
    B -->|action goal| C[MOVEIT SERVER]
    C -->|ik request| D[KDL IK SOLVER]
    D -->|joint angles| C
    C -->|trajectory msg| E[JOINT TRAJECTORY CONTROLLER]
    E -->|float64 cmd| F[ETHERCAT HARDWARE IF]
    F -->|pwm signals| G[MOTOR DRIVERS]
    G -->|encoder feedback| F
    F -->|joint state| H[ROSCORE TOPIC BUS]
    H -->|sensor msgs JointState| I[RVIZ]
    H -->|sensor msgs JointState| J[STATE MONITOR]
    K[WRIST F T SENSOR] -->|geometry msgs WrenchStamped| H
    H -->|feedback| C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The three diagrams above are intentionally nested. Diagram 4.1 shows the *physical hardware topology*, Diagram 4.2 shows the *ROS node-level software topology*, and Diagram 4.3 shows the *message-flow topology* during a single command cycle. This three-view separation is itself a **best practice in ROS-based system design** and a frequent KTU 14-mark question topic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PERUT525, Module 3]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)** What is Robonaut 2? Mention its key specifications relevant to ROS-based control.

**Model Answer:**
Robonaut 2 (R2) is a dexterous humanoid robot developed jointly by **NASA Johnson Space Center** and **General Motors** for safe human-robot collaboration, originally deployed on the **International Space Station (ISS)** in 2011. Key specifications include **42 active Degrees of Freedom** (head 3, neck 3, two 7-DOF arms, two 12-DOF hands, plus torso), distributed real-time control via **EtherCAT at 1 kHz**, and an open-source software stack built on **ROS** with custom packages like `r2_msgs` and `r2_controllers`, and integration with **MoveIt!** for bimanual motion planning. It is significant to PERUT525 as a reference implementation of ROS for complex multi-DOF humanoid systems.

---

**Q2. (3 Marks)** List any three ROS packages used in Robonaut 2's software architecture and state their function.

**Model Answer:**
1. **`ros_control`** — Provides the standard hardware interface and controller manager framework that bridges ROS commands to the real-time EtherCAT motor loop.
2. **`moveit_core` / MoveIt!** — Performs collision-aware motion planning and IK solving for the dual-arm manipulation tasks.
3. **`r2_msgs`** — Contains custom message definitions specific to Robonaut 2, such as `HandCommand` and `ForceSensorData`, enabling structured communication between R2-specific nodes.

(Alternative valid packages: `tf2`, `image_transport`, `smach_ros`, `joint_trajectory_controller`.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Pattern, PERUT525, Module 3]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Explain in detail the four-layer architectural decomposition of Robonaut 2. For each layer, identify the corresponding ROS components and message types.

**Model Solution:**

The Robonaut 2 system is organized into four cooperating layers, each mapped onto specific ROS concepts:

**Layer 1 — Mechanical Hardware:**
Includes the upper-body humanoid skeleton: 3-DOF head, 3-DOF neck, two 7-DOF arms, two 12-DOF hands, and force/torque sensors at wrists and fingertips. The mechanical layer is **not** a ROS component but rather the physical substrate that ROS controls.

**Layer 2 — Real-Time Hardware Abstraction (HAL):**
- ROS package: `ros_control` with `controller_manager`.
- Message types: `sensor_msgs/JointState` (feedback at 1 kHz), `trajectory_msgs/JointTrajectory` (setpoints).
- Communicates with the EtherCAT motor drivers via custom hardware-interface plugins.

**Layer 3 — Middleware Planning and Autonomy:**
- ROS package: `moveit_core` for motion planning, `smach_ros` for task sequencing, OpenCV/PCL nodes wrapped in ROS for perception.
- Message types: `moveit_msgs/MoveGroupAction`, `geometry_msgs/PoseStamped`, `sensor_msgs/PointCloud2`.

**Layer 4 — User and Teleoperation Interface:**
- ROS package: custom teleop nodes, `tf2` for frame transforms, RViz for visualization.
- Message types: `geometry_msgs/Twist`, `sensor_msgs/Joy` (joystick), or haptic-device-specific messages.

This layered decomposition demonstrates the **separation-of-concerns principle** of ROS, allowing independent development, testing, and replacement of each layer.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Derive the impedance-control law used in Robonaut 2's compliant manipulation. Show how a wrist force/torque measurement is converted into a joint-space correction using the Jacobian transpose.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the Cartesian Impedance Law:**
Robonaut 2's hand should behave like a mass-spring-damper system in Cartesian space:

$$
M(\ddot{x}_{desired} - \ddot{x}_{actual}) + B(\dot{x}_{desired} - \dot{x}_{actual}) + K(x_{desired} - x_{actual}) = F_{ext}
$$

where $M$, $B$, $K$ are the virtual mass, damping, and stiffness matrices.

**Step 2 — Solve for Cartesian Displacement:**
Rearranging for the deviation $e = x_{desired} - x_{actual}$:

$$
\Delta x = K^{-1} F_{ext} \quad \text{(in steady state, with low velocities)}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Jacobian Transpose Mapping:**
To convert the Cartesian displacement into joint-space correction, multiply by $J^T$:

$$
\Delta \theta = J^T(\theta) \cdot F_{ext}
$$

This is preferred over $J^{-1}$ because $J$ is often non-square (7 joints → 6 Cartesian DOF), and $J^T$ avoids singularities in a numerically stable way.

**Step 4 — Blend into the Ongoing Trajectory:**
The controller updates the commanded joint angle:

$$
\theta_{cmd}(t+1) = \theta_{cmd}(t) + \Delta \theta
$$

**Step 5 — Stability Note:**
For stable compliant behaviour, the gains must satisfy:

$$
B^2 \geq 4 M K \quad \text{(critically damped or overdamped regime)}
$$

This is the impedance law implemented in R2's `r2_controllers/compliance_controller` package.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) (7 Marks)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the ROS-based motion planning and execution pipeline of Robonaut 2 from a high-level task goal to motor-level actuation.

**Model Solution:**

The pipeline consists of six sequential stages (also see Section 4.3 diagram above):

1. **Goal Specification:** A task-planner node (using SMACH or BT) issues a Cartesian goal to the MoveIt! `MoveGroup` action server via the `moveit_msgs/MoveGroupAction` interface.

2. **Scene Perception:** The stereo camera node publishes `sensor_msgs/Image` and `sensor_msgs/PointCloud2` messages. The `object_detection` node segments the target object and publishes its pose.

3. **Motion Planning:** MoveIt!'s OMPL plugin (RRT*) searches the 7-D C-space and returns a discrete trajectory $\tau = \{q(t_0), \dots, q(t_N)\}$.

4. **Trajectory Smoothing:** A time-parameterization algorithm (CHOMP or TrajOpt) minimizes the integral of squared jerk, producing smooth position, velocity, and acceleration profiles.

5. **Inverse Kinematics Verification:** The KDL/TRAC-IK solver confirms the start and goal configurations are reachable; redundancy is resolved via null-space projection.

6. **Low-Level Execution:** The `joint_trajectory_controller` samples the trajectory at 1 kHz, computes motor commands via PID law $V_{cmd} = K_p e + K_d \dot{e} + K_i \int e\,dt$, and dispatches them over EtherCAT to the motor drivers. Joint-state feedback flows back via the `/r2/joint_states` topic at 1 kHz, closing the loop.

This pipeline exemplifies ROS's **distributed, asynchronous, publish-subscribe architecture** applied to a 42-DOF humanoid.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Explain the role of `ros_control` and the `controller_manager` in Robonaut 2. Why is this framework critical for real-time humanoid control?

**Model Solution:**

**The `ros_control` Framework:**
`ros_control` is the standard ROS package that provides a **generic hardware interface** between high-level ROS controllers and low-level robot hardware. In Robonaut 2, it abstracts the EtherCAT motor bus behind a uniform C++ API, allowing controllers to be written without knowledge of the underlying bus protocol.

**The `controller_manager` Node:**
- Loads, configures, starts, stops, and unloads controllers at runtime.
- Maintains the list of active controllers (e.g., `right_arm_joint_trajectory_controller`, `left_hand_position_controller`).
- Enforces **mutual exclusion** — two controllers cannot command the same joint simultaneously, preventing hardware conflicts.

**Why it is Critical for R2:**
1. **Real-time determinism:** The manager schedules controllers at fixed update rates (1 kHz) using a real-time loop, separate from the non-real-time ROS communication layer.
2. **Hardware abstraction:** Swapping the EtherCAT bus for, say, a CAN bus would require zero changes in higher-level MoveIt! code.
3. **Safety:** Emergency-stop and controller-switching logic is centralized, vital for a robot operating near humans.
4. **Modularity:** Researchers can plug in new controllers (e.g., an impedance controller, a gravity-compensation controller) without rewriting the entire stack.

Without `ros_control`, the Robonaut 2 software would be tightly coupled to its specific hardware, defeating the entire purpose of using ROS.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing `ros_control` with `ros_controllers`:** `ros_control` is the *framework* (the C++ library), while `ros_controllers` is the *package* containing pre-built controller implementations (e.g., `JointTrajectoryController`, `DiffDriveController`). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this mix-up.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** When quoting the 1 kHz EtherCAT rate, write it as **1000 Hz** or **$10^3$ Hz**, not just "fast." Marks are awarded for quantitative precision.
> 3. **Skipping the Jacobian transpose derivation:** For any 7-mark question on compliant control, the steps $F_{ext} \rightarrow \Delta x \rightarrow \Delta \theta = J^T F_{ext}$ must appear *explicitly*. Hand-waving the math costs 3–4 marks.
> 4. **Drawing a flowchart instead of a block diagram for a 14-mark question:** KTU expects labelled rectangular blocks with arrows annotated by **message type names** (e.g., `JointState`, `WrenchStamped`), not generic software flowcharts.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Robonaut 2 (R2)** = NASA + GM humanoid, deployed to the ISS in **2011**, with **42 active DOF**.
- Built on **ROS** with custom packages: `r2_msgs`, `r2_controllers`, and integration with **MoveIt!**.
- **Real-time motor control** via **EtherCAT at 1000 Hz**, abstracted through `ros_control` and `controller_manager`.
- **Dexterous manipulation** is enabled by **12-DOF hands** and **force/torque sensors** at wrists.
- **Impedance control law** $M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Kx = F_{ext}$ governs compliant contact behaviour.
- **Jacobian transpose** $\Delta \theta = J^T F_{ext}$ maps Cartesian forces into joint corrections.
- **Four-layer architecture**: Hardware → HAL → Middleware (Planning) → User/Teleop.
- **PID control law** at the joint level: $V_{cmd} = K_p e + K_d \dot{e} + K_i \int e\, dt$.
- **Forward kinematics** uses the **Denavit–Hartenberg** matrix product.
- **Inverse kinematics** for 7-DOF arms has **1-DOF null-space redundancy** exploited for joint-limit avoidance.
- **Total DOF formula** for an upper-body humanoid: $2 \cdot DOF_{arm} + 2 \cdot DOF_{hand} + DOF_{head} + DOF_{neck}$.
- **Message types to memorize**: `sensor_msgs/JointState`, `trajectory_msgs/JointTrajectory`, `geometry_msgs/WrenchStamped`, `moveit_msgs/MoveGroupAction`, `geometry_msgs/PoseStamped`.
- **Teleoperation latency target**: $t_{latency} \leq 10$ ms for VR-based control.
- **Successor project**: **Robonaut 5 (Valkyrie / R5)** uses the same ROS-2 architecture for the **Artemis lunar program**.
- **Key industry impact**: R2's design influenced GM's automotive assembly双臂 robots and the **Da Vinci Research Kit (DVRK)** in surgical robotics.

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Fire Revision Hook (for last-minute exam prep):** If a KTU question asks *"How does ROS enable modular humanoid control? Answer using Robonaut 2 as a case study,"* your response should mention — (1) layered architecture, (2) `ros_control` abstraction, (3) publish-subscribe decoupling, (4) MoveIt! for planning, (5) message-type contracts. This five-point structure typically fetches full 7 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TurtleBot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT525 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** TurtleBot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of TurtleBot

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**TurtleBot** is a standard, low-cost, open-source mobile robotic platform widely adopted as the official reference hardware for **Robot Operating System (ROS)** education, research, and benchmarking. Developed initially by **Willow Garage** and later maintained by **Open Source Robotics Foundation (OSRF)** and **Clearpath Robotics**, TurtleBot serves as a canonical mobile manipulation testbed that integrates essential robotic subsystems — **differential drive locomotion**, **onboard computation**, **2D ranging/3D depth perception**, and **inertial measurement** — into a unified, ROS-native platform.

From the perspective of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PERUT525)**, TurtleBot occupies a pivotal position in Module 3 because it is the practical embodiment of ROS architectural concepts taught earlier in the course: **nodes, topics, services, actions, parameter servers, TF tree, URDF descriptions, launch files, and the navigation stack**. Every abstract ROS concept can be physically demonstrated on a TurtleBot.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PERUT525, Module 3)**
> TurtleBot is the *de facto* standard mobile robot used globally for ROS education. KTU examiners frequently test the student's ability to (a) identify its sensor and actuator stack, (b) explain its TF tree, (c) describe its URDF structure, and (d) outline the launch-file hierarchy used to bring it up.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smartphone of Mobile Robotics"

Imagine TurtleBot as the **Android phone of robotics**. Just as Android provides a standardized, open-source operating system that runs on thousands of different phone hardware configurations from Samsung, Xiaomi, OnePlus, etc., ROS provides a standardized middleware, and TurtleBot provides a **standardized reference hardware** that runs ROS out-of-the-box.

- The **ROS middleware** = the Android OS
- The **TurtleBot hardware** = a reference phone (like the Google Pixel)
- The **URDF** = the hardware specification sheet
- The **Launch files** = the boot-up sequence
- The **Navigation stack** = a pre-installed navigation app (like Google Maps)
- The **Gazebo simulator** = a software emulator for testing apps without owning the phone

When a researcher publishes a paper saying *"we tested our SLAM algorithm on a TurtleBot,"* it is conceptually equivalent to a mobile app developer saying *"we tested our app on a Pixel 3."* It provides a **reproducible, peer-comparable platform**.

## 1.3 Historical Evolution and Variants

TurtleBot has evolved through four major generations, each marking a leap in computational, sensory, and mechanical capability:

| Generation | Release Year | Compute Platform | Primary Sensor | Drive System |
|------------|--------------|------------------|----------------|--------------|
| **TurtleBot 1** | 2011 | Asus EeePC netbook | Microsoft Kinect | iRobot Create base |
| **TurtleBot 2** | 2012 | Laptop-class x86 PC | Asus Xtion / Kinect | Kobuki base |
| **TurtleBot 3** | 2017 | Raspberry Pi 3/4 + SBC | 360° LDS-01 LIDAR | Dynamixel-based differential |
| **TurtleBot 4** | 2022 | Raspberry Pi 4 + Jetson (Pro) | 2D LDS + OAK-D stereo + IMU | iRobot Create 3 base |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 examinations, the most commonly referenced variant is the **TurtleBot 3 (Burger, Waffle, Waffle Pi models)** because of its low cost (~USD 550) and complete ROS-native software stack. Always clarify the variant when answering exam questions.

## 1.4 Core Subsystem Block Overview

A TurtleBot is conceptually partitioned into **five primary subsystems**, each mapped to one or more ROS architectural concepts:

1. **Mobile Base** (actuation layer) — Differential drive wheels + motor controllers → publishes `odom` and `joint_states`
2. **Sensor Suite** (perception layer) — 2D LIDAR, IMU, wheel encoders, optional camera → publishes `scan`, `imu`, `joint_states`
3. **Onboard Computer** (computation layer) — SBC running Ubuntu + ROS 2 (Humble/Iron) → runs the ROS master/daemon
4. **URDF + TF Tree** (description layer) — XML-based kinematic and visual description → consumed by `robot_state_publisher`
5. **Software Stack** (algorithmic layer) — Navigation 2, SLAM Toolbox, `teleop_twist_keyboard`, `cartographer` → outputs `cmd_vel` to the base

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Operating Metric**: A typical TurtleBot 3 Burger operates at a maximum translational velocity of **0.22 m/s** and a maximum rotational velocity of **2.75 rad/s**, with a payload capacity of **15 kg**. The LDS-01 LIDAR has a scan range of **0.12 m to 3.5 m** with a 360° field of view and 1° angular resolution.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Architecture Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of TurtleBot Architecture

### 2.1.1 The Mobile Base (Differential Drive Kinematics)

A TurtleBot uses a **differential drive** configuration — two independently driven wheels mounted on a common axis, with passive caster wheels for stability. This is the simplest non-holonomic mobile base and is governed by the **forward kinematic equations**.

For a differential drive robot with wheel separation $L$ (distance between the two drive wheels) and wheel radius $r$:

$$v = \frac{r}{2} (\omega_R + \omega_L)$$

$$\omega = \frac{r}{L} (\omega_R - \omega_L)$$

Where:
- $v$ = linear velocity of the robot center (m/s)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity of the robot (rad/s)
- $\omega_R, \omega_L$ = angular velocities of the right and left wheels (rad/s)
- $r$ = wheel radius
- $L$ = wheel separation (also called *track* or *axle length*)

The **inverse kinematics** (from desired body velocities to wheel commands) are:

$$\omega_R = \frac{2v + \omega L}{2r}$$

$$\omega_L = \frac{2v - \omega L}{2r}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**: These equations are not theoretical curiosities — they are the *exact* computations that the `turtlebot3_node` driver performs in real-time. When a `cmd_vel` message (linear.x, angular.z) arrives, the base controller inverts these equations and sends the corresponding PWM/duty-cycle signals to the Dynamixel servos (TurtleBot 3) or the Kobuki motor controller (TurtleBot 2).

### 2.1.2 The Sensor Suite and ROS Message Mapping

| Sensor | Physical Quantity Measured | ROS Topic Published | Message Type | Update Rate |
|--------|---------------------------|---------------------|--------------|-------------|
| **2D LIDAR (LDS-01/02)** | Distance to nearest obstacle (360°) | `/scan` | `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` | 5–10 Hz |
| **IMU (MPU-9250 / BMI088)** | Linear accel + angular vel + orientation | `/imu` | `sensor_msgs/Imu` | 100–200 Hz |
| **Wheel Encoders** | Left/right wheel rotation (ticks) | `/joint_states` (republished) | `sensor_msgs/JointState` | 50 Hz |
| **Optional Camera (Pi Cam v2)** | RGB image | `/camera/image_raw` | `sensor_msgs/Image` | 30 Hz |
| **Optional OAK-D (TB4)** | Stereo depth + RGB | `/oakd/rgb/preview`, `/oakd/stereo/depth` | `sensor_msgs/Image` | 30 Hz |
| **Battery Monitor (TB3)** | Cell voltage + current + temperature | `/battery_state` | `sensor_msgs/BatteryState` | 1 Hz |

### 2.1.3 The TF (Transform) Tree

The **TF tree** is a hierarchical, time-stamped coordinate-frame graph that ROS uses to track the spatial relationships between all rigid bodies on the robot. For a TurtleBot 3, the canonical TF tree is:

```
map → odom → base_footprint → base_link → [wheel_left, wheel_right, imu, base_scan]
```

| Frame | Purpose | Published By |
|-------|---------|--------------|
| `map` | World-fixed global frame (from SLAM) | `slam_gmapping` or `slam_toolbox` |
| `odom` | Odometric frame, drifts over time but is continuous | `robot_localization` / EKF node |
| `base_footprint` | 2D projection of robot base on the ground | `robot_state_publisher` |
| `base_link` | Robot's rigid body center | `robot_state_publisher` |
| `wheel_left_link`, `wheel_right_link` | Rotating wheel frames (joint_state drives these) | `robot_state_publisher` |
| `imu_link` | IMU sensor origin | Static transform from URDF |
| `base_scan` | LIDAR sensor origin | Static transform from URDF |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Favored Concept**: The `map → odom` transform is published by the **localization node** (e.g., AMCL or SLAM), and it *corrects* the drift accumulated in `odom → base_footprint` (which is purely integration-based). The `odom → base_footprint` transform is published by the **base controller** at a high rate using wheel encoder integration.

### 2.1.4 The URDF — Unified Robot Description Format

The **URDF (Unified Robot Description Format)** is an XML file that fully describes a robot's:
- **Visual geometry** (how it looks in RViz)
- **Collision geometry** (how Gazebo handles physics)
- **Inertial properties** (mass, center of mass, inertia tensor)
- **Kinematic tree** (joints and their types — fixed, revolute, continuous, prismatic)

For TurtleBot 3, the URDF is generated dynamically by the `turtlebot3_description` package, with the top-level Xacro file `turtlebot3_burger.urdf.xacro` composing:
- `turtlebot3_burger_base.urdf.xacro` — base, wheels, casters
- `turtlebot3_burger.gazebo.xacro` — Gazebo plugins for LIDAR, diff_drive, IMU
- `common_properties.urdf.xacro` — shared color and inertia macros

### 2.1.5 The Launch File Hierarchy

TurtleBot uses a layered launch-file structure following the **roslaunch XML** syntax:

```
turtlebot3_bringup.launch
    ├── robot.launch          → robot_state_publisher + URDF
    ├── lidar.launch          → LDS01_driver node
    ├── imu.launch            → rosserial_python (TB3 Burger/Waffle)
    └── turtlebot3_core.launch → turtlebot3_node (diff_drive_controller)
```

For autonomous navigation:

```
turtlebot3_navigation.launch
    ├── turtlebot3_bringup.launch
    ├── map_server (loads prebuilt map.pgm + map.yaml)
    ├── amcl.launch            → Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization
    ├── move_base.launch
    │       ├── global_planner (e.g., NavFn / A* / Dijkstra)
    │       ├── local_planner  (e.g., DWA / TEB)
    │       ├── global_costmap
    │       └── local_costmap
    └── rviz.launch (with navigation config)
```

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the absolute essential concepts and formulas you must memorize for the KTU 2024 ESE on PERUT525 Module 3.** Examiners consistently test these.

| # | Concept / Formula | Symbolic Form | Practical Use |
|---|-------------------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | Differential drive forward kinematics | $v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$ | Computing body velocity from encoder rates |
| 2 | Differential drive angular velocity | $\omega = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$ | Computing turning rate |
| 3 | Inverse kinematics — right wheel | $\omega_R = \frac{2v + \omega L}{2r}$ | Converting `cmd_vel` to wheel commands |
| 4 | Inverse kinematics — left wheel | $\omega_L = \frac{2v - \omega L}{2r}$ | Converting `cmd_vel` to wheel commands |
| 5 | Odometric pose update (2D) | $x_{t+1} = x_t + v\Delta t \cos\theta_t$ | Wheel-encoder-based dead reckoning |
| 6 | Odometric pose update (2D) | $y_{t+1} = y_t + v\Delta t \sin\theta_t$ | Wheel-encoder-based dead reckoning |
| 7 | Odometric heading update | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \omega \Delta t$ | Wheel-encoder-based dead reckoning |
| 8 | LIDAR range-bearing to Cartesian | $x = r\cos\theta, \; y = r\sin\theta$ | Converting `LaserScan` to point cloud |
| 9 | TF tree canonical form | `map → odom → base_footprint → base_link` | Required knowledge for any Nav question |
| 10 | AMCL particle weight update | $w_i \propto p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)})$ | Probabilistic localization principle |
| 11 | Occupancy grid update (log-odds) | $l_{t+1}(m) = l_t(m) + \text{inv\_sensor\_model}(z_t, m) - l_0$ | Mapping principle |
| 12 | `cmd_vel` message structure | `geometry_msgs/Twist(linear.x, angular.z)` | Standard velocity command interface |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility in Real-World Applications

The TurtleBot platform is not merely a teaching toy — it has been deployed or adapted in:

- **Warehouse logistics research** — Multi-robot coordination algorithms tested on fleets of TurtleBot 3
- **Healthcare robotics** — Used as a development testbed for hospital delivery robots (Pandemic-era UV-disinfection robots)
- **Agricultural robotics** — Greenhouse navigation algorithm prototyping
- **Service robotics** — Restaurant serving robots and hotel concierge robots
- **Autonomous Driving research** — Indoor SLAM algorithm benchmarking (used as ground-truth comparison)
- **Swarm robotics** — ROS 2 multi-master experiments with 10+ TurtleBots
- **Search and Rescue (SAR)** — Victim-finding algorithm prototyping in confined spaces

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Metric**: As of 2024, the global installed base of TurtleBot-class robots exceeds **30,000 units** across 80+ countries, making it the most widely deployed open-source mobile robot platform in academic and research history.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Differential Drive Kinematics

The following derivation walks through the mathematical relationship between **wheel angular velocities** and the **robot's body twist** (linear + angular velocity). This is a high-yield derivation for KTU 2024 ESE numerical/analytical questions.

### 3.1.1 Forward Kinematics Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the geometric setup.**

Consider a differential drive robot with two drive wheels of radius $r$, separated by a perpendicular distance $L$ (axle length). Let $v_R = r\omega_R$ be the tangential velocity of the right wheel contact point with the ground, and $v_L = r\omega_L$ be the tangential velocity of the left wheel contact point.

> *Reasoning*: For a non-slipping rolling wheel, the linear velocity of the wheel's contact point with the ground equals the wheel's angular velocity times its radius. This is the **no-slip constraint**.

**Step 2 — Express the robot's linear velocity $v$ as the average.**

By the principle of rigid-body kinematics, when both wheels roll without slipping, the linear velocity of the robot's center is the arithmetic mean of the two wheel contact velocities:

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r\omega_R + r\omega_L}{2} = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$$

> *Reasoning*: The center of the axle is equidistant from both wheels, so it moves at the average of the two contact velocities.

**Step 3 — Express the robot's angular velocity $\omega$.**

The robot rotates about a point called the **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)**. The ICR is located on the axle line at a perpendicular distance $R$ from the robot center, where:

$$R = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}$$

The angular velocity of the robot about the ICR is the same as the angular velocity of the wheel about its own axis when no slipping occurs. Using the right-hand rule and the fact that the right wheel moves faster than the left for a counter-clockwise (positive) turn:

$$v_R = \omega \left(R + \frac{L}{2}\right), \quad v_L = \omega \left(R - \frac{L}{2}\right)$$

Subtracting these two equations:

$$v_R - v_L = \omega L$$

Solving for $\omega$:

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r\omega_R - r\omega_L}{L} = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$$

> *Reasoning*: The difference in wheel velocities creates a differential that drives the rotation. If both wheels spin at the same rate, $\omega = 0$ (pure translation). If the wheels spin in opposite directions at the same magnitude, $v = 0$ and the robot rotates in place (ICR at the robot center).

**Step 4 — Final compact form.**

$$\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L) \\
\omega &= \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.1.2 Inverse Kinematics Derivation

**Step 1 — Set up the linear system.**

We have two equations in two unknowns ($\omega_R, \omega_L$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\omega_R + \omega_L &= \frac{2v}{r} \\
\omega_R - \omega_L &= \frac{\omega L}{r}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Solve by addition and subtraction.**

Adding the two equations eliminates $\omega_L$:

$$2\omega_R = \frac{2v}{r} + \frac{\omega L}{r} = \frac{2v + \omega L}{r}$$

Therefore:

$$\omega_R = \frac{2v + \omega L}{2r}$$

Subtracting the second from the first eliminates $\omega_R$:

$$2\omega_L = \frac{2v}{r} - \frac{\omega L}{r} = \frac{2v - \omega L}{r}$$

Therefore:

$$\omega_L = \frac{2v - \omega L}{2r}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\omega_R &= \frac{2v + \omega L}{2r} \\
\omega_L &= \frac{2v - \omega L}{2r}
\end{aligned}$$

> *Reasoning*: Given a desired $(v, \omega)$ pair from a `cmd_vel` message, the base controller computes $\omega_R$ and $\omega_L$ using these equations, then sends the corresponding duty-cycle commands to the Dynamixel servos.

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Exam Style)

**Problem:** A TurtleBot 3 Burger has wheel radius $r = 0.033$ m and wheel separation $L = 0.16$ m. The robot receives a `cmd_vel` message with $v = 0.2$ m/s and $\omega = 1.0$ rad/s. Compute the required left and right wheel angular velocities in rad/s.

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**

Given: $v = 0.2$ m/s, $\omega = 1.0$ rad/s, $r = 0.033$ m, $L = 0.16$ m.

**Step 2 — Apply the inverse kinematics formula for the right wheel.**

$$\omega_R = \frac{2v + \omega L}{2r} = \frac{2(0.2) + (1.0)(0.16)}{2(0.033)} = \frac{0.4 + 0.16}{0.066} = \frac{0.56}{0.066}$$

$$\omega_R \approx 8.485 \; \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the inverse kinematics formula for the left wheel.**

$$\omega_L = \frac{2v - \omega L}{2r} = \frac{2(0.2) - (1.0)(0.16)}{2(0.033)} = \frac{0.4 - 0.16}{0.066} = \frac{0.24}{0.066}$$

$$\omega_L \approx 3.636 \; \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Verification using forward kinematics.**

$$v_{\text{check}} = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L) = \frac{0.033}{2}(8.485 + 3.636) = 0.0165 \times 12.121 \approx 0.20 \; \text{m/s} \; \checkmark$$

$$\omega_{\text{check}} = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L) = \frac{0.033}{0.16}(8.485 - 3.636) = 0.20625 \times 4.849 \approx 1.00 \; \text{rad/s} \; \checkmark$$

> *Conclusion*: The required wheel commands are $\omega_R \approx 8.485$ rad/s and $\omega_L \approx 3.636$ rad/s.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — ROS Node-Topic-Service Map for TurtleBot

The following analytical matrix maps every essential ROS concept to its concrete physical/digital manifestation in the TurtleBot ecosystem. This is the **single most important table** for KTU Module 3 questions on TurtleBot.

| ROS Concept | Physical/Digital Manifestation on TurtleBot | Launched By | Communicates Via |
|-------------|---------------------------------------------|-------------|------------------|
| **Node** (driver) | `turtlebot3_node` — base controller | `turtlebot3_core.launch` | Publishes `joint_states`, `odom`, `battery_state`; subscribes to `cmd_vel` |
| **Node** (LIDAR) | `lds01_node` — LIDAR driver | `turtlebot3_lidar.launch` | Publishes `/scan` (LaserScan) |
| **Node** (IMU) | `rosserial_python` (TB3) or native IMU driver (TB4) | `turtlebot3_imu.launch` | Publishes `/imu` (Imu) |
| **Node** (state publisher) | `robot_state_publisher` | `turtlebot3_bringup.launch` | Publishes `/tf` and `/tf_static` from URDF |
| **Node** (teleop) | `teleop_twist_keyboard` | Manually executed | Publishes `cmd_vel` from keyboard input |
| **Topic** | `/cmd_vel` | Any teleop or navigation node | `geometry_msgs/Twist` |
| **Topic** | `/odom` | `turtlebot3_node` | `nav_msgs/Odometry` |
| **Topic** | `/scan` | `lds01_node` | `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` |
| **Topic** | `/imu` | IMU driver | `sensor_msgs/Imu` |
| **Topic** | `/joint_states` | `turtlebot3_node` | `sensor_msgs/JointState` |
| **Topic** | `/tf`, `/tf_static` | `robot_state_publisher` | `tf2_msgs/TFMessage` |
| **Service** | `/gazebo/spawn_urdf_model` (simulation) | `spawn_model.launch` | `gazebo_msgs/SpawnModel` |
| **Service** | `/turtlebot3_core/sound` (TB3) | User code | Custom service — play melody |
| **Action** | `/move_base` (legacy ROS 1) or `/navigate_to_pose` (ROS 2 Nav2) | `move_base` / `bt_navigator` | `nav_msgs/GetPlan` / `nav2_msgs/NavigateToPose` |
| **Parameter** | `~max_vel_x` (default 0.22 for TB3 Burger) | `turtlebot3_node` | ROS parameter server |
| **Parameter** | `~robot_model` (burger, waffle, waffle_pi) | `turtlebot3_bringup` | ROS parameter server |
| **Parameter** | `~port` (USB serial, e.g., `/dev/ttyACM0`) | `turtlebot3_node` | ROS parameter server |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Spawning a TurtleBot in Gazebo

This is a common KTU practical/oral question. The conceptual sequence is:

**Step 1 — Source the workspace and set the model.**

The user exports the environment variable `TURTLEBOT3_MODEL` to select burger, waffle, or waffle_pi. This variable is read by the Xacro macros to generate the correct URDF.

**Step 2 — Launch the empty Gazebo world.**

The launch file `turtlebot3_gazebo/launch/empty_world.launch` starts Gazebo with a blank world file. The Gazebo master process initializes the physics engine, the rendering engine (OGRE), and the ROS-Gazebo plugin bridge.

**Step 3 — Spawn the robot via the URDF.**

The launch file `turtlebot3_gazebo/launch/spawn_turtlebot3.launch` invokes `gazebo_ros/spawn_model`. This service call sends the parsed URDF to Gazebo, which then:
- Creates a rigid body with the URDF's mass and inertia
- Attaches `libgazebo_ros_diff_drive.so` plugin → subscribes to `cmd_vel` and publishes `odom`
- Attaches `libgazebo_ros_ray_sensor.so` plugin → publishes `/scan`
- Attaches `libgazebo_ros_imu_sensor.so` plugin → publishes `/imu`

**Step 4 — Launch the robot state publisher.**

The URDF is parsed by the `robot_state_publisher` node, which subscribes to `/joint_states` and publishes the TF tree. Without this node, RViz cannot visualize the robot.

**Step 5 — Verify with ROS topic tools.**

The user runs `ros2 topic list` (or `rostopic list` in ROS 1) and observes `/cmd_vel`, `/odom`, `/scan`, `/imu`, `/tf` — confirming all nodes are alive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Students often confuse the URDF with the SDF (Simulation Description Format). The URDF describes the robot to **ROS** (for TF tree and RViz), while the SDF describes the world to **Gazebo** (for physics simulation). The Gazebo plugins bridge the two.
