# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part A**
> Examiners allocate marks strictly as: **1 mark for correct keyword identification**, **1 mark for diagrammatic/symbolic support**, **1 mark for concise yet complete answer**. Students often lose the third mark by giving one-line answers without depth. Always include a relevant formula, node name, or topic name.

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is TurtleBot? List its major hardware components.**

**Model Answer:**

TurtleBot is a low-cost, open-source mobile robotic platform developed as the reference hardware for the Robot Operating System (ROS). It serves as a standardized testbed for mobile robotics research and education.

The major hardware components of a **TurtleBot 3 Burger** are:

1. **Mobile base** — Two Dynamixel XL430-W250 servo motors driving two wheels, with two passive caster wheels for stability (differential drive configuration).
2. **Onboard computer** — Raspberry Pi 4 Model B (1.5 GHz quad-core ARM Cortex-A72, 4 GB RAM), running Ubuntu 22.04 with ROS 2 Humble.
3. **2D LIDAR sensor** — LDS-01 (or LDS-02), 360° field of view, 0.12–3.5 m range, 1° angular resolution, 5 Hz scan rate.
4. **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** — MPU-9250 9-axis (3-axis accelerometer + 3-axis gyroscope + 3-axis magnetometer), connected via serial.
5. **Wheel encoders** — Built into the Dynamixel servos, providing 4096 ticks per revolution.
6. **Battery** — 11.1 V 1800 mAh Li-Po, providing approximately 2.5 hours of operation.
7. **Optional camera module** — Raspberry Pi Camera v2 (8 MP Sony IMX219).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain the TF tree of a TurtleBot and list all coordinate frames in order.**

**Model Answer:**

The TF (transform) tree of a TurtleBot is a hierarchical structure of coordinate frames that defines the spatial relationship between all rigid bodies on the robot. The canonical TF tree is:

$$\text{map} \rightarrow \text{odom} \rightarrow \text{base\_footprint} \rightarrow \text{base\_link} \rightarrow [\text{wheel\_left\_link}, \text{wheel\_right\_link}, \text{imu\_link}, \text{base\_scan}]$$

| Frame | Description | Published By |
|-------|-------------|--------------|
| `map` | World-fixed frame from SLAM | SLAM node |
| `odom` | Odometric frame, continuous but drifts | `turtlebot3_node` |
| `base_footprint` | 2D ground projection of base | URDF + state publisher |
| `base_link` | Robot's rigid body center | URDF + state publisher |
| `wheel_left_link` / `wheel_right_link` | Wheel frames (rotating) | Joint states from encoders |
| `imu_link` | IMU origin | Static URDF transform |
| `base_scan` | LIDAR origin | Static URDF transform |

The `map → odom` transform is provided by the localization node (AMCL or SLAM), and the `odom → base_footprint` transform is provided by the wheel-encoder integration in the base controller.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B (14 Marks)**
> For 14-mark questions, the standard split is **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Within each 7-mark sub-question, examiners allocate marks across: *diagram/first-principle derivation (~2 marks)*, *mid-stage working (~3 marks)*, *final result/conclusion (~2 marks)*. Skipping a derivation step costs **0.5 to 1 mark per skipped step**. Always show the substitution step into the formula explicitly.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the hardware and software architecture of the TurtleBot 3 platform. Describe the role of each major ROS node in its operation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the forward and inverse kinematics equations for the differential drive base of a TurtleBot. A TurtleBot 3 Burger has wheel radius $r = 0.033$ m and wheel separation $L = 0.16$ m. If the right and left wheels rotate at $\omega_R = 7$ rad/s and $\omega_L = 5$ rad/s respectively, compute the robot's linear and angular velocities. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The TurtleBot 3 architecture can be partitioned into **three layers**:

**Layer 1 — Hardware Layer:**

The hardware layer consists of:
- A **Raspberry Pi 4** SBC running Ubuntu + ROS 2
- A **LDS-01 LIDAR** for 2D environment scanning
- A **9-axis IMU (MPU-9250)** for orientation sensing
- Two **Dynamixel XL430-W250** smart servos acting as wheel actuators with built-in encoders
- A **Li-Po battery** (11.1 V, 1800 mAh)

**Layer 2 — Driver/Abstraction Layer (ROS Nodes):**

| Node | Role |
|------|------|
| `turtlebot3_node` | Subscribes to `cmd_vel`, computes wheel commands, publishes `/odom` and `/joint_states` |
| `lds01_node` | Reads LIDAR data, publishes `/scan` as `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` |
| `imu_node` (rosserial) | Reads IMU data, publishes `/imu` as `sensor_msgs/Imu` |
| `robot_state_publisher` | Parses URDF, subscribes to `/joint_states`, publishes `/tf` |

**Layer 3 — Application Layer (Navigation and User Code):**

Includes `slam_toolbox`, `amcl`, `nav2_bt_navigator`, `teleop_twist_keyboard`, and custom user nodes for tasks like person-following, pick-and-place, etc.

**ROS Communication Flow:**

The `cmd_vel` topic (typed `geometry_msgs/Twist`) is the standard interface for issuing velocity commands. The `/scan`, `/imu`, and `/odom` topics are the standard perception feedback. The `/tf` topic carries all coordinate transforms. The diagnostic data flows through `/diagnostics` and `/battery_state`.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Forward Kinematics Derivation:**

Given $\omega_R$ and $\omega_L$, the robot's linear and angular velocities are:

$$v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L), \quad \omega = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$$

**Step 1 — Compute $v$:**

$$v = \frac{0.033}{2}(7 + 5) = 0.0165 \times 12 = 0.198 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\omega$:**

$$\omega = \frac{0.033}{0.16}(7 - 5) = 0.20625 \times 2 = 0.4125 \; \text{rad/s}$$

**Inverse Kinematics Derivation** (for completeness, though not numerically tested in this sub-question):

$$\omega_R = \frac{2v + \omega L}{2r}, \quad \omega_L = \frac{2v - \omega L}{2r}$$

**Final Answer:** $v = 0.198$ m/s, $\omega = 0.4125$ rad/s (turning left since $\omega_R > \omega_L$).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the URDF structure of TurtleBot 3 with reference to its kinematic tree, joint types, and the role of Xacro macros. How does the `robot_state_publisher` node consume the URDF? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the ROS Navigation stack (Nav2) used with TurtleBot. List the major server nodes, the role of costmaps, and the difference between global and local planners. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**URDF Structure of TurtleBot 3:**

The **URDF (Unified Robot Description Format)** is an XML representation of a robot model. For TurtleBot 3, it is composed using **Xacro** (XML Macros), which allows modular, parameterized XML files.

The top-level file is `turtlebot3_burger.urdf.xacro`, which includes:

- `turtlebot3_burger_base.urdf.xacro` — Defines the base link, two drive wheels, two casters, IMU link, and LIDAR link
- `turtlebot3_burger.gazebo.xacro` — Defines Gazebo plugins (`libgazebo_ros_diff_drive.so`, `libgazebo_ros_ray_sensor.so`, `libgazebo_ros_imu_sensor.so`)
- `common_properties.urdf.xacro` — Reusable Xacro macros for colors, mass, and inertia

**Kinematic Tree:**

The kinematic tree is rooted at `base_link`, with child links connected via joints:

| Joint Name | Type | Parent | Child | Motion |
|------------|------|--------|-------|--------|
| `wheel_left_joint` | `continuous` | `base_link` | `wheel_left_link` | Unlimited rotation around z-axis |
| `wheel_right_joint` | `continuous` | `base_link` | `wheel_right_link` | Unlimited rotation around z-axis |
| `caster_back_joint` | `fixed` | `base_link` | `caster_back_link` | None |
| `imu_joint` | `fixed` | `base_link` | `imu_link` | None |
| `base_scan_joint` | `fixed` | `base_link` | `base_scan` | None |
| `scan_joint` | `fixed` | `base_scan` | `laser_frame` | None |

The joint types `continuous` (unlimited revolute), `revolute` (limited revolute), `prismatic` (linear), and `fixed` (no motion) are the four primary joint types in URDF.

**Role of Xacro Macros:**

Xacro reduces duplication via `<xacro:macro>` definitions and parameter substitution. For example, the wheel macro is invoked once for the left wheel and once for the right wheel with different `reflect` parameters.

**Role of `robot_state_publisher`:**

The `robot_state_publisher` node:
1. Parses the URDF upon startup
2. Subscribes to `/joint_states` (published by `turtlebot3_node`)
3. For each joint in the URDF, computes the transform from parent to child link
4. Publishes these transforms to `/tf` and `/tf_static`

This allows RViz to display the robot's 3D model and the TF tree to be queried by other nodes (e.g., Nav2).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**ROS 2 Navigation 2 (Nav2) Stack:**

The Nav2 stack is the ROS 2 successor to the ROS 1 `move_base`. It uses **Behavior Trees (BT)** for high-level task orchestration.

**Major Server Nodes:**

1. **bt_navigator** — Executes the Behavior Tree XML to coordinate the overall navigation task
2. **planner_server** — Global path planning (using algorithms like NavFn, SmacPlanner, Theta*)
3. **controller_server** — Local trajectory execution (DWA, TEB, RPP)
4. **recoveries_server** — Recovery behaviors (rotate-in-place, back-up, wait)
5. **smoother_server** — Path smoothing (e.g., for human-like trajectories)
6. **waypoint_follower** — Sequential waypoint navigation
7. **velocity_smoother** — Smooths `cmd_vel` output to respect acceleration limits
8. **lifecycle_manager** — Manages node activation/deactivation

**Costmaps:**

A **costmap** is a 2D grid map where each cell has a cost value from 0 (free) to 254 (lethal obstacle), with 255 (unknown). Two costmaps are maintained:

| Costmap | Type | Update | Purpose |
|---------|------|--------|---------|
| **Global costmap** | Static + dynamic obstacles | Loaded from `map_server` + LIDAR updates | Long-range planning |
| **Local costmap** | Rolling window around the robot | High-frequency LIDAR updates | Short-range obstacle avoidance |

**Global vs Local Planner:**

- **Global planner** operates on the global costmap and produces a complete path from start to goal using algorithms like A* or Dijkstra. It does not consider robot dynamics.
- **Local planner** operates on the local costmap and produces velocity commands in real-time, considering the robot's kinematics, dynamic obstacles, and the current portion of the global path. Algorithms include **DWA (Dynamic Window Approach)** and **TEB (Timed Elastic Band)**.

The local planner continuously tracks the global plan while reacting to dynamic obstacles, which is essential for safe navigation in a changing environment.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist for TurtleBot (Module 3)**

- ✅ **Definition**: TurtleBot is the reference open-source mobile robot platform for ROS, with a differential drive base, 2D LIDAR, IMU, and onboard SBC.
- ✅ **Variants**: TurtleBot 1 (Create base), 2 (Kobuki), 3 (Raspberry Pi + LDS-01), 4 (Create 3 + OAK-D). KTU most often tests **TurtleBot 3 Burger**.
- ✅ **Differential drive kinematics** — memorize both forward and inverse formulas and the *derivation logic* (no-slip constraint + ICR).
- ✅ **TF tree canonical form** — `map → odom → base_footprint → base_link → [wheels, IMU, LIDAR]`. Know which node publishes which transform.
- ✅ **URDF = ROS format**, **SDF = Gazebo format**. URDF is consumed by `robot_state_publisher`. Xacro enables modularity.
- ✅ **Joint types**: `fixed`, `revolute`, `continuous`, `prismatic`, `floating`, `planar`. TurtleBot 3 wheels use `continuous`.
- ✅ **Sensor topics**: `/scan` (LaserScan), `/imu` (Imu), `/odom` (Odometry), `/joint_states` (JointState), `/cmd_vel` (Twist).
- ✅ **Command interface**: `cmd_vel` of type `geometry_msgs/Twist` with fields `linear.x` (m/s) and `angular.z` (rad/s).
- ✅ **Nav2 servers**: `bt_navigator`, `planner_server`, `controller_server`, `recoveries_server`, `smoother_server`, `velocity_smoother`, `lifecycle_manager`.
- ✅ **Costmaps**: Global (static map + obstacles) and Local (rolling window). Global planner uses A*/Dijkstra; local planner uses DWA/TEB.
- ✅ **Launch file hierarchy**: `bringup` → `core` + `lidar` + `imu` + `robot_state_publisher`. Layered modular structure.
- ✅ **AMCL** = Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization — particle filter for pose estimation in a known map.
- ✅ **SLAM Toolbox** / **Cartographer** = simultaneous localization and mapping — generates the `/map` topic.
- ✅ **Gazebo plugins** for TurtleBot: `gazebo_ros_diff_drive`, `gazebo_ros_ray_sensor`, `gazebo_ros_imu_sensor` — these are the bridges between URDF and the physics simulator.
- ✅ **Common numerical values for TurtleBot 3 Burger**: $r = 0.033$ m, $L = 0.16$ m, max $v = 0.22$ m/s, max $\omega = 2.75$ rad/s, LDS-01 range = 0.12–3.5 m.
- ✅ **Real-world deployment**: Warehouse logistics, healthcare delivery, agricultural robots, swarm research, service robotics.
- ✅ **KTU Pitfall**: Don't confuse ROS 1 `move_base` (with action `MoveBase`) with ROS 2 Nav2's `navigate_to_pose` action — they have different message types and BT-based orchestration.
- ✅ **Common mark-loss areas**: Skipping derivation steps, not drawing the TF tree, failing to mention the message type, confusing `odom` with `map` frame.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulators - Stage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT525 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS) > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Simulators - Stage # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Stage** (also known as **Player/Stage** when paired with the Player robot device server) is a **2D multi-agent robotic simulator** that simulates a population of mobile robots, sensors and environmental objects within a two-dimensional bitmapped world. It is designed primarily for **multi-robot research**, swarm intelligence studies, and rapid prototyping of robot control algorithms. In the KTU 2024 ROS syllabus context, Stage is studied as a **lightweight, scalable alternative** to 3D physics simulators (like Gazebo), especially when computational efficiency and large-scale multi-agent simulations (hundreds to thousands of robots) are required. The current implementation is **libstage**, a C++ library that can be embedded in custom applications, and the ROS integration is provided by the **`stage_ros`** package. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Syllabus Highlight:** Stage operates on a **2D bitmap occupancy grid** model (top-down view) and supports the **ODE (Open Dynamics Engine)** for fast rigid-body physics. It is **header-only / library-based**, meaning the simulator runs *inside* your controller process, eliminating the need for inter-process RPC calls. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Theatre Stage" Imagine a **school play performed on a stage**: - The **stage floor** is your **2D bitmap world** in Stage — it is a flat, top-down surface painted with black (free space) and white (occupied) pixels. - The **actors (robots)** move around on the stage according to the **script (your control code)**. - The **eyes of the audience (sensors)** are placed at fixed seats in the auditorium (fixed sensors) or move with the actors (robot-mounted sensors), each producing a particular kind of "view" of the play. - The **stage manager (libstage)** coordinates everything — no actor collides with the set, and the lighting (sensor models, noise) is consistent. Just as a theatre stage is **inexpensive, fast to set up, and can host many actors simultaneously**, **Stage** is computationally cheap, simple to configure (a single `.world` text file), and can simulate **hundreds of robots in real-time on a standard laptop**. ## 1.3 Core Characteristics of Stage | Characteristic | Value / Description | |---|---| | **Dimensionality** | 2.5D (2D world + simple 3D height info for sensors) | | **Underlying Engine** | ODE (Open Dynamics Engine) for dynamics | | **Maximum Recommended Robots** | Up to **10,000+ agents** in published research | | **World Format** | Plain-text `.world` file | | **ROS Interface** | `stage_ros` package, ROS 1 (Noetic) and ROS 2 (Foxy+) ports | | **Coordinate System** | Right-handed, X-east, Y-north, Z-up (meters) | | **Time Resolution** | Configurable, typically **100 Hz** (10 ms) simulation step | | **License** | Open source (GPLv2+) | > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Distinction:** While **Gazebo** is the 3D high-fidelity simulator, **Stage** is the **2D lightweight simulator**. The KTU syllabus lists both, and examiners often ask for a *comparative analysis* — remember: **Gazebo = 3D, accurate physics, fewer agents; Stage = 2D, fast, many agents**. ## 1.4 Why Stage Matters in ROS In ROS, Stage solves three critical problems: 1. **Algorithm Testing Without Hardware** — Test SLAM, path-planning (A*, D*), and swarm algorithms in minutes instead of days. 2. **Reproducible Benchmarks** — Bit-exact deterministic playback of scenarios for academic publications. 3. **Scalability Studies** — Evaluate how a coordination algorithm (e.g., PSO, flocking) degrades as agent count grows from 10 to 1000. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference ## 2.1 The Stage Architecture — Inside `libstage` Stage is structured as a **layered simulation kernel**. Understanding this layer cake is essential for both exam answers and real-world Stage development. ### 2.1.1 Layer 1 — World (`Stg::World`) The `World` class is the **root container**. It owns: - The **bitmap occupancy grid** (a 2D matrix of pixels, each cell storing probability of occupancy). - The **global simulation clock** (`World::sim_time`, double precision seconds). - A registry of all models (`World::models`), positionables, and powerpacks. - The **physics world** (an ODE `dWorldID`). > [!IMPORTANT] > The `World::Update()` method is called once per simulation tick. It cascades updates to all models in a fixed order: **consume power → read sensors → run controllers → write actuators → integrate physics → render**. ### 2.1.2 Layer 2 — Models (`Stg::Model`) Every entity in the world is a `Model`. There are two base subclasses: - **Blinkenlight** — a visual indicator (LED). - **Position` models** — anything with a 2D pose `(x, y, theta)`. From `Model`, the most important derived classes are: - **Robot** — mobile agents that subscribe to commands. - **Sensor** — abstract base for all sensor models. - **Actuator** — abstract base for motors and positioners. ### 2.1.3 Layer 3 — Sensors Stage provides a rich sensor library. Each sensor subclass defines its own update rule and noise model. ### 2.1.4 Layer 4 — Actuators Actuators transform abstract commands (e.g., a velocity `(v, ω)`) into low-level effects (e.g., wheel speeds). ## 2.2 Stage Sensors — Complete Reference | Sensor Model | Stage Class | Output Data | Common Use | |---|---|---|---| | **Laser Range Finder (Lidar)** | `ModelLaser` | `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` | SLAM, obstacle avoidance | | **Sonar / IR Ranger** | `ModelRanger` (legacy `ModelSonar`) | Ranges array | Low-cost proxemics | | **Blob Finder** | `ModelBlobfinder` | (x,y, color, area) blobs | Color-based tracking, ball-catching | | **Fiducial Finder** | `ModelFiducial` | Tagged robot IDs + bearing | Multi-robot identification | | **Camera (2D overhead)** | `ModelCamera` | Image of ground plane | Visual servoing, line-following | | **GPS** | `ModelGps` | `(latitude, longitude, altitude)` | Outdoor localization | | **IMU (limited)** | `ModelImu` (newer Stage) | Orientation, angular rates | Attitude estimation | | **Odometry** | (derived from `ModelPosition`) | `(x, y, θ, vx, vy, ω)` | Dead reckoning | | **Bumper / Whisker** | `ModelBumper` (custom subclass) | Contact boolean | Reactive control | ## 2.3 Actuators in Stage | Actuator Class | Command Interface | Physical Realization | |---|---|---| | `ModelPosition` | Pose target `(x*, y*, θ*)` or velocity `(vx, vy, ω)` | Holonomic or non-holonomic drive | | `ModelWheeled` (skid-steer, diff, ackermann variants) | Wheel speeds | Car-like or tank-like robot | | `ModelBlinkenlight` | On/Off | Visual LED | | `ModelGripper` | Open/Close/Detect | Manipulation (limited) | ## 2.4 The `.world` File — Stage's Configuration Heart The `.world` file is a **declarative specification** parsed by `WorldGui` (the standalone viewer) or the `stage_ros` node. Its structure follows the inheritance tree. ``` worldfile_root ├── include # Reusable sub-configurations ├── macros # Reusable snippets (e.g., "pioneer2dx") ├── bitmap # Floor plan image (PBM/PNG) ├── resolution # meters per pixel ├── interval_sim # simulation step size (ms) └── model (recursive) ├── name ├── pose # (x, y, z, yaw, pitch, roll) ├── size # bounding box ├── color ├── body # physics type ├── shape # geometry (circle, rectangle, bitmap) ├── sensor # sub-models └── actuator ``` ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metrics Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following equations are the most-tested Stage/Robot-Simulator formulas in KTU exams.** Memorize the units and the underlying assumptions. | # | Concept | Equation | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Simulation speed-up factor | $S = \dfrac{T_{real}}{T_{sim}}$ | dimensionless; $S>1$ is faster than real-time | | 2 | CPU time per agent | $C_n = \dfrac{T_{total}}{N_{agents}}$ | ms / agent — measures scalability | | 3 | Sensor noise — Gaussian | $\tilde{z} = z + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ | $z$ = true range, $\sigma$ = std dev | | 4 | Sensor noise — uniform quantization | $\tilde{z} = \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{z}{\Delta}\right) \cdot \Delta$ | $\Delta$ = range resolution | | 5 | Laser scan angular step | $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{\theta_{fov}}{N_{samples}-1}$ | radians; $\theta_{fov}$ = field of view | | 6 | Laser maximum range | $z_{max} = f \cdot R_{max}$ | $f$ = configured factor (usually 1.0) | | 7 | Vehicle non-holonomic update | $x_{k+1} = x_k + v \cos\theta_k \cdot \Delta t$ | forward kinematics | | 8 | Vehicle non-holonomic update | $y_{k+1} = y_k + v \sin\theta_k \cdot \Delta t$ | — | | 9 | Vehicle non-holonomic update | $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega \cdot \Delta t$ | — | | 10 | Odometry drift (linear) | $e_{drift} = \alpha_1 \vert v \vert + \alpha_2 \vert \omega \vert$ | $\alpha_i$ = error coefficients | | 11 | Odometry drift (angular) | $\sigma_{\theta}^2 = \alpha_3 \vert v \vert^2 + \alpha_4 \vert \omega \vert^2$ | variance | | 12 | Bitmap resolution | $r = \dfrac{L_{world}}{N_{pixels}}$ | meters/pixel | | 13 | Fiducial ID encoding | $id = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} b_i \cdot 2^i$ | $b_i$ = barcode bit, $n$ ≤ 12 | | 14 | Collision penalty (energy) | $E_{coll} = \frac{1}{2} m \Delta v^2$ | kinetic energy lost | | 15 | World tick time | $T_{tick} = n_{interval\_sim} \times 10^{-3}$ | seconds; $n$ in ms | > **Critical exam tip:** When asked to derive the "**update rule of a differential drive robot in Stage**", always show **equations 7, 8, 9** together. They form a coupled system that the Stage integrator solves using **Euler integration** (or RK4 in modern versions). ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | How Stage is Used | |---|---| | **Swarm Robotics Research** | Sheffield, EPFL, MIT labs use Stage for 100+ agent studies | | **Education** | Courses in multi-agent systems (e.g., ETH Zürich) | | **Algorithm Benchmarking** | Comparing A*, D*, RRT* in controlled identical scenarios | | **ROS Tutorials** | Official `turtlebot_stage` for offline SLAM tutorials | | **Embedded AI Testing** | Deploying trained RL policies in safe 2D sandboxes | --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Differential-Drive Kinematics Update (Stage's `ModelPosition` Engine) The most frequently asked derivation in KTU exams. We derive the discrete-time update rule for a **non-holonomic differential-drive robot**, which is the default `position` model in Stage. ### Given A robot at time $t_k$ has pose $(x_k, y_k, \theta_k)$ in the world frame. It receives a velocity command $(v, \omega)$ in its body frame. The simulation step is $\Delta t = t_{k+1} - t_k$. ### Step 1 — Express the body-frame velocity in the world frame The robot's body frame is rotated by $\theta_k$ relative to the world. The linear velocity vector in the world frame is: $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{x}_k &= v \cos\theta_k \\ \dot{y}_k &= v \sin\theta_k \\ \dot{\theta}_k &= \omega \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** A robot facing heading $\theta_k$ moves forward along its longitudinal axis. Projecting this axis onto world X and Y yields $\cos\theta_k$ and $\sin\theta_k$ respectively. The angular rate $\omega$ directly changes the heading. ### Step 2 — Apply Euler integration Stage's `World::Update()` uses **forward Euler integration** (semi-implicit variant) to advance the state by $\Delta t$: $$ \begin{aligned} x_{k+1} &= x_k + \dot{x}_k \cdot \Delta t = x_k + v \cos\theta_k \cdot \Delta t \\ y_{k+1} &= y_k + \dot{y}_k \cdot \Delta t = y_k + v \sin\theta_k \cdot \Delta t \\ \theta_{k+1} &= \theta_k + \dot{\theta}_k \cdot \Delta t = \theta_k + \omega \cdot \Delta t \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** Euler integration assumes the velocity is constant over the small interval $\Delta t$. With Stage's typical $\Delta t = 10\,\text{ms}$, this is highly accurate for wheel speeds below saturation. ### Step 3 — Add the odometry noise model (Stage's `ModelPosition::Update`) Stage adds Gaussian noise to each update, modelling real encoder slip: $$ \begin{aligned} x_{k+1} &= x_k + v \cos\theta_k \cdot \Delta t + \epsilon_x, \quad \epsilon_x \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \alpha_2 v^2 + \alpha_4 \omega^2 + \alpha_1) \\ y_{k+1} &= y_k + v \sin\theta_k \cdot \Delta t + \epsilon_y, \quad \epsilon_y \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \alpha_2 v^2 + \alpha_4 \omega^2 + \alpha_1) \\ \theta_{k+1} &= \theta_k + \omega \cdot \Delta t + \epsilon_\theta, \quad \epsilon_\theta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \alpha_3 v^2 + \alpha_4 \omega^2 + \alpha_1) \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** The error coefficients $\alpha_i$ are configured in the `.world` file (e.g., `position [ "odom_error" 0.05 0.05 0.05 0.05 ]`). This mirrors the **odometry error model** proposed by Borenstein & Feng (1996) for real robots. ### Step 4 — Final form (compact matrix form) $$ \mathbf{p}_{k+1} = \mathbf{p}_k + \begin{bmatrix} v \cos\theta_k \\ v \sin\theta_k \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} \Delta t + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_k, \quad \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_k \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{Q}_k) $$ where $\mathbf{Q}_k$ is the **process noise covariance matrix** determined by the $\alpha_i$ parameters. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Laser Range Sensor Model in Stage A laser emits $N$ rays spanning field-of-view $\theta_{fov}$ with angular step $\Delta\theta$. Each ray's true range is $z_i^*$, but Stage produces a noisy measurement $\tilde{z}_i$. ### Step 1 — Angular discretization $$ \Delta\theta = \frac{\theta_{fov}}{N - 1}, \quad \theta_i = \theta_{robot} + \theta_{start} + i \cdot \Delta\theta, \quad i \in \{0, 1, \ldots, N-1\} $$ > **Logic:** $N-1$ intervals create $N$ sample directions starting from $\theta_{start}$ (e.g., $-\pi/2$ for a forward-facing scan). ### Step 2 — Ray tracing to find the hit point Stage uses an **incremental 2D line algorithm** (Bresenham's variant) to walk through the bitmap from the sensor origin $(x_s, y_s)$ along direction $(\cos\theta_i, \sin\theta_i)$ until: - a non-zero (occupied) pixel is hit → $z_i^* = d_{hit}$, - or the maximum range $z_{max}$ is reached → $z_i^* = z_{max}$. The hit distance is: $$ z_i^* = \min\!\left(\, d_{hit}^{(i)},\; z_{max} \,\right) $$ ### Step 3 — Add noise Stage supports two noise models. The **additive Gaussian** model is: $$ \tilde{z}_i = z_i^* + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2) $$ With optional **range clamping**: $$ \tilde{z}_i = \text{clip}\!\left(\tilde{z}_i,\; 0,\; z_{max}\right) $$ > **Logic:** Real lidars exhibit near-constant range noise at short distances and range-proportional noise at long distances. Stage approximates this with a single $\sigma$ that the user can tune. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Simulation Speed-Up Factor (S) and Per-Agent Cost > [!IMPORTANT] > This is a classic 14-mark KTU question: *"A Stage simulation runs 50 robots for 100 seconds of real time and completes 200 seconds of simulated time. Compute the speed-up factor S and the per-agent CPU cost, given 8 logical cores."* ### Step 1 — Compute S $$ S = \frac{T_{sim}}{T_{real}} = \frac{200\,\text{s}}{100\,\text{s}} = 2.0 $$ > **Logic:** The simulator runs **twice as fast** as reality. ### Step 2 — Compute per-agent cost (linear model) Assuming linear scaling and equal load on $N_c = 8$ cores: $$ C_n = \frac{T_{real}}{N_{agents}} = \frac{100\,\text{s}}{50} = 2.0\,\text{s/agent} $$ Effective parallel cost: $$ C_{eff} = \frac{T_{real} \cdot N_c}{N_{agents}} = \frac{100 \times 8}{50} = 16.0\,\text{core\cdot s/agent} $$ > **Logic:** On a single core the work would take $100 \times 8 = 800\,\text{s}$ for the equivalent sequential load, giving $16\,\text{s}$ per agent. ### Step 3 — Compute the maximum sustainable agent count If the deadline is real-time ($S \geq 1$) and the budget is $T_{deadline} = 60\,\text{s}$: $$ N_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{T_{deadline}}{C_{eff}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{60}{16} \right\rfloor = 3 \text{ agents} $$ > **Logic:** Stage's *actual* performance is super-linear due to pairwise collision checks; the true ceiling is closer to $\sqrt{N_c \cdot N_{ref}}$ for collision-heavy scenarios. ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — "When to Use Stage vs. Gazebo" | Criterion | Stage | Gazebo | |---|---|---| | **Dimensionality** | **2D** (top-down) | **3D** (full) | | **Agents** | **Hundreds – thousands** | 1 – 20 typically | | **CPU** | Lightweight | Heavy (3D physics + rendering) | | **Sensors** | 2D-only (no RGB-D depth) | RGB-D, stereo, IMU, force/torque | | **Use Case** | Swarm, coverage, path planning | Manipulation, legged, drone | | **Real-time** | Easily real-time for 100+ | Often slows down | | **File Format** | `.world` (text) | `.sdf` / `.urdf` (XML) | | **ROS 2 Support** | Via `stage_ros2` | Native (`ros_gz`) | > **Exam-ready one-liner:** *"Use Stage for large-scale 2D multi-agent simulations where computational efficiency matters; use Gazebo for high-fidelity 3D single-robot scenarios involving manipulation or aerial dynamics."* --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Stage's Layered Software Architecture The following Mermaid block depicts how a ROS controller node interacts with Stage's `libstage` library through the `stage_ros` adapter. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph USERSPACE["User Space"] CTRL["ROS Controller Node
C++ / Python"] GUI["Stage GUI
stageros view"] end subgraph STAGEROS["stage_ros Adapter Layer"] SR_NODE["stageros Node"] SR_TOPIC["Topic Bridge
cmd_vel scan odom"] SR_TF["TF Broadcaster
odom to base_link"] end subgraph LIBSTAGE["libstage Simulation Kernel"] WORLD["Stg World
Global Clock Bitmap"] MODEL_R["Stg Model Robot
Differential Drive"] MODEL_S["Stg Model Sensor
Laser / Sonar / Camera"] MODEL_A["Stg Model Actuator
Position Wheeled"] PHYS["ODE Physics Engine
Rigid Body Solver"] end CTRL -- "/cmd_vel ROS Msg" --> SR_TOPIC SR_TOPIC -- "Velocity Callback" --> SR_NODE SR_NODE -- "Set Velocity v w" --> MODEL_A MODEL_A -- "Apply Forces" --> PHYS PHYS -- "Integrated Pose" --> MODEL_R MODEL_R -- "Sensor Trigger" --> MODEL_S MODEL_S -- "Raw Scan Data" --> SR_TOPIC SR_TOPIC -- "/scan /odom ROS Msg" --> CTRL MODEL_R -- "Pose Update" --> SR_TF SR_TF -- "TF Transform" --> CTRL GUI -. "Debug Visualization" .- WORLD ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows are *data flow*; dashed arrows are *optional/debug*. Note that **`stage_ros` runs in the same process as your controller** (unlike Gazebo, which uses a separate `gzserver`). ## 4.2 The `.world` File Object Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TB WORLD["World File .world"] WORLD --> INCLUDE["include other.world"] WORLD --> MACROS["macros pioneer2dx"] WORLD --> BITMAP["bitmap floorplan.pgm"] WORLD --> RES["resolution 0.02"] WORLD --> INTERVAL["interval_sim 100"] WORLD --> MODELS["Model Definitions"] MODELS --> M1["Model Pioneer2dx"] M1 --> POSE1["pose 0 0 0 0 0 0"] M1 --> SIZE1["size 0.4 0.26 0.2"] M1 --> BODY1["body type polygon"] M1 --> DRIVE1["drive differential"] M1 --> SENSORS["Sensors"] SENSORS --> L1["Model Laser
samples 180 fov 6.28"] SENSORS --> S1["Model Sonar x 5"] M1 --> ACTUATORS["Actuators"] ACTUATORS --> A1["Model Position
odom_error a1..a4"] MODELS --> M2["Model Wall Static"] M2 --> SHAPE2["shape box"] M2 --> COLOR2["color red"] MODELS --> M3["Model Puck Movable"] M3 --> BODY3["body cylinder mass 0.2"] ``` ## 4.3 Sensor Update Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A["World Tick
interval_sim"] --> B["Model Update
Read Sensor"] B --> C{"Sensor Type"} C -- Laser --> D["Ray Trace Bitmap"] C -- Sonar --> E["Cone Trace
5 rays"] C -- Fiducial --> F["ID Match
Barcode"] C -- Camera --> G["Rasterize
Color Buffer"] D --> H["Add Gaussian Noise
sigma"] E --> H F --> I["Publish ID + Pose"] G --> J["Publish Image"] H --> K["Publish sensor_msgs
LaserScan"] I --> K J --> K K --> L["ROS Topic
or Stage Callback"] ``` ## 4.4 Multi-Robot Simulation Topology ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph STAGE_WORLD["Single Stage World Process"] R1["Robot 1
namespace /robot1"] R2["Robot 2
namespace /robot2"] R3["Robot 3
namespace /robot3"] RN["Robot N
namespace /robotN"] end R1 -- "/robot1/scan" --> MUX["ROS Multiplexer"] R2 -- "/robot2/scan" --> MUX R3 -- "/robot3/scan" --> MUX RN -- "/robotN/scan" --> MUX MUX --> CTRL["Centralized Planner
or Federated Controller"] CTRL -- "/robot1/cmd_vel" --> R1 CTRL -- "/robot2/cmd_vel" --> R2 CTRL -- "/robotN/cmd_vel" --> RN ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style] **Q: Differentiate between the Stage and Gazebo simulators used in ROS. List three advantages of Stage for multi-robot simulations.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Stage | Gazebo | |---|---|---| | Dimensionality | 2D (top-down) | 3D | | Physics | ODE, lightweight | ODE / Bullet / DART, full | | Agents | 100s – 1000s | 1 – ~20 | **Three advantages of Stage for multi-robot simulations:** 1. **Scalability** — Can simulate thousands of agents in real-time on commodity hardware due to its 2D bitmap world representation. 2. **Low computational footprint** — No GPU required, runs on embedded platforms; ideal for headless servers. 3. **In-process integration** — `libstage` embeds into the controller process, eliminating network/IPC latency and enabling **deterministic, bit-exact reproducibility** crucial for academic benchmarks. ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style] **Q: Explain the role of the `.world` file in Stage. Mention any four entities that can be defined in it.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The `.world` file is a **declarative, plain-text configuration** that completely specifies the simulated environment for Stage. It is parsed at startup by `libstage` to construct the simulation world. It controls: - The **bitmap floor plan** (the 2D world geometry). - The **resolution** (meters per pixel) and the **simulation timestep**. - The **robots**, **static obstacles**, **sensors**, and **actuators** placed in the environment. **Four entities that can be defined in a `.world` file:** 1. **World** — global settings, bitmap, resolution, interval. 2. **Robot models** — `model "pioneer2dx"` with pose, size, drive type. 3. **Sensors** — `model laser`, `model sonar`, `model camera`, `model fiducial`. 4. **Obstacles / Objects** — static walls, movable pucks, cylinders, boxes. ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > KTU's ESE pattern: *Answer any one full question from this module.* Each question carries **14 marks** and is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Architecture & Implementation Focus* **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **layered architecture of `libstage`**. Explain the role of `Stg::World`, `Stg::Model`, and the physics engine in the simulation loop. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Diagram (3 marks):** Draw a 4-layer pyramid (World → Model → Sensor/Actuator → Physics). Show the simulation tick arrow cascading from top to bottom. **Explanation (4 marks):** - **`Stg::World`** is the root container. It owns the **bitmap occupancy grid** which represents the 2D environment, the **global simulation clock** (`sim_time`), and a registry of all models. The `interval_sim` parameter (in milliseconds) controls how often `World::Update()` is called. - **`Stg::Model`** is the abstract base for every entity in the world. Subclasses include `ModelRobot` (mobile agents), `ModelLaser`, `ModelSonar`, `ModelCamera`, `ModelFiducial`, etc. Each model has a pose $(x, y, z, \theta)$ and may have a body (physics representation). - **The simulation loop** proceeds as follows: (i) read actuator commands, (ii) read sensor data, (iii) advance physics via **ODE (`dWorldStep`)**, (iv) update all model poses, (v) publish sensor data via the ROS bridge. - The **physics engine (ODE)** handles rigid-body dynamics, collision detection, and friction. It operates in the 2D plane with optional Z-axis for sensor height. **(b)** A Stage simulation runs **40 robots** for **30 seconds of real time** and completes **90 seconds of simulated time** on a **quad-core (4-thread)** machine. Compute: (i) The speed-up factor $S$. (ii) The per-agent CPU time. (iii) The maximum number of agents that can run **in real-time** on this machine. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $N_{agents} = 40$, $T_{real} = 30\,\text{s}$, $T_{sim} = 90\,\text{s}$, $N_c = 4$ threads. **(i) Speed-up factor (2 marks):** $$ S = \frac{T_{sim}}{T_{real}} = \frac{90}{30} = 3.0 $$ The simulator runs **3× faster** than real time. **(ii) Per-agent CPU time (2 marks):** $$ C_n = \frac{T_{real}}{N_{agents}} = \frac{30}{40} = 0.75\,\text{s/agent} $$ **(iii) Maximum real-time agents (3 marks):** For real-time, we need $S \geq 1$, i.e., $T_{sim} \geq T_{real}$. The total CPU work for 40 agents in 30 s is $30 \times 40 = 1200\,\text{agent\cdot s}$ of work. With 4 threads doing this work in $T_{real} = 30\,\text{s}$: $$ \text{Total parallel work capacity} = 4 \times 30 = 120\,\text{thread\cdot s} $$ So per-agent cost in the parallel regime: $$ C_{eff} = \frac{120}{40} = 3.0\,\text{thread\cdot s/agent} $$ To stay in real-time ($S = 1$), total agent-time work must equal thread-time capacity: $$ N_{max} \times C_n = N_c \times 1 \text{ second per real second} $$ $$ N_{max} = \frac{N_c \times T_{real}}{C_n \times T_{real}} = \frac{4}{0.75 \times \frac{1}{30}} = \frac{4 \times 30}{0.75} = 160 \text{ agents} $$ > **Sanity check:** With $S = 3$ for 40 agents, the system has headroom. Linear extrapolation gives $40 \times 3 = 120$ agents at $S = 1$, but with the thread count factored in, **$N_{max} \approx 160$ agents** can be sustained in real time. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Theory & Sensor-Model Focus* **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the **structure of a `.world` file** in Stage with a suitable example. Show how a **Pioneer 2DX robot equipped with a SICK laser range finder** would be declared. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Structure (3 marks):** A `.world` file is a hierarchy of nested `define` blocks starting with the world settings (`resolution`, `interval_sim`, `bitmap`). The keyword `model` introduces any entity, and it can contain sub-blocks for `sensors`, `actuators`, `body`, `shape`, etc. The keyword `include` allows reuse of common configurations, and `macros` defines reusable parameter sets. **Example (4 marks):** ``` # world_file.world resolution 0.02 interval_sim 100 # 100 ms = 10 Hz bitmap ( size [40 20] # 40 m × 20 m type "pgm" pgm "./floorplan.pgm" ) # --- Define a Pioneer 2DX with a SICK laser --- define pioneer_sick model ( size [0.50 0.26 0.40] drive "differential" localization "gps" localization_origin [0 0 0 0] model ( name "laser0" pose [0 0 0 0 0 0] model laser ( sensor ( range_max 8.0 fov 6.283 samples 361 noise_std 0.02 ) # define a body to render size [0.05 0.05 0.05] color "blue" ) ) ) # --- Place 2 instances in the world --- pioneer_sick( pose [ -2 0 0 0 0 0 ] name "robot1" ) pioneer_sick( pose [ 2 0 0 0 0 0 ] name "robot2" ) ``` **Explanation of key parameters:** - `resolution 0.02` — 2 cm per pixel. - `interval_sim 100` — simulation step of 100 ms. - `drive "differential"` — non-holonomic drive. - `samples 361` — number of laser rays. - `fov 6.283` — full 360° field of view (≈ $2\pi$). - `noise_std 0.02` — Gaussian noise standard deviation = 2 cm. **(b)** Derive the **discrete-time update rule for a differential-drive robot** as implemented by `ModelPosition` in Stage. Show the effect of **odometry noise** on the pose. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — System Model (2 marks):** A differential-drive robot has pose $(x, y, \theta)$ in the world frame and accepts linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$ in the body frame. $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{x} &= v \cos\theta \\ \dot{y} &= v \sin\theta \\ \dot{\theta} &= \omega \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Euler Integration (2 marks):** Stage integrates with timestep $\Delta t$ (typically $0.01$ s): $$ \begin{aligned} x_{k+1} &= x_k + v \cos\theta_k \cdot \Delta t \\ y_{k+1} &= y_k + v \sin\theta_k \cdot \Delta t \\ \theta_{k+1} &= \theta_k + \omega \cdot \Delta t \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Add Odometry Noise (2 marks):** Real encoders have slip. Stage adds Gaussian noise whose variance grows with commanded speed: $$ \begin{aligned} x_{k+1} &= x_k + v \cos\theta_k \Delta t + \mathcal{N}(0,\; \alpha_2 v^2 + \alpha_4 \omega^2 + \alpha_1) \\ \theta_{k+1} &= \theta_k + \omega \Delta t + \mathcal{N}(0,\; \alpha_3 v^2 + \alpha_4 \omega^2 + \alpha_1) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Practical implication (1 mark):** This model explains why **open-loop dead reckoning drifts** over time and why **Stage-based SLAM** algorithms must incorporate loop closure to correct accumulated error. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls that Cost Marks in Stage Questions:** > 1. **Confusing Stage with Gazebo** — Examiners explicitly deduct 1–2 marks if you call Stage a "3D simulator" or claim it has "RGB-D cameras". **Stage is strictly 2D; depth comes from ray-traced range finders.** > 2. **Forgetting units** — Equations 7, 8, 9 from the formula sheet *must* include $\Delta t$. Writing $x_{k+1} = x_k + v\cos\theta$ is worth **0 marks**. > 3. **Skipping the noise model** — A derivation of kinematics without the odometry noise term loses 2 marks. The noise model is a **KTU-mandated** part of the Stage syllabus. > 4. **Wrong field-of-view convention** — A laser `fov` of 6.28 rad is **360°** (a full circle), not 180°. Many students get this wrong. > 5. **Ignoring interval_sim** — The simulation tick size is part of the configuration. Always mention it in any question that discusses "real-time performance". > 6. **Mixing up "Player" and "Stage"** — Player is the *device server*; Stage is the *simulator*. They are different components of the same project but are not the same thing. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist ### 🔑 Key Terms to Memorize - [ ] **Stage** — 2D multi-agent simulator - [ ] **`libstage`** — C++ simulation library (kernel) - [ ] **`stage_ros`** — ROS adapter package - [ ] **`.world` file** — declarative configuration - [ ] **ODE** — Open Dynamics Engine (physics backend) - [ ] **`Stg::World`** — root container with bitmap + clock - [ ] **`Stg::Model`** — base class for all entities - [ ] **`ModelPosition`** — non-holonomic drive actuator - [ ] **`ModelLaser`** — 2D laser range finder - [ ] **`interval_sim`** — simulation timestep in ms - [ ] **Borenstein-Feng odometry error model** — Gaussian drift ### 📌 One-Line Summaries (for last-minute revision) 1. **Stage = 2D + lightweight + scalable**, Gazebo = 3D + heavy + few agents. 2. The **`.world` file** is the *only* configuration needed; no code required. 3. **`stage_ros` runs in-process** with your controller, enabling real-time multi-robot testing. 4. **Sensors supported**: laser, sonar/ranger, camera, blobfinder, fiducial, GPS, IMU (limited). 5. **The simulation loop** = read sensors → run controllers → write actuators → integrate physics. 6. **Differential-drive update** = $(x, y, \theta) \to (x + v\cos\theta\Delta t,\; y + v\sin\theta\Delta t,\; \theta + \omega\Delta t)$. 7. **Odometry noise** is Gaussian with variance proportional to $v^2$ and $\omega^2$. 8. **Speed-up factor** $S = T_{sim}/T_{real}$; for real-time, $S \geq 1$. 9. **Stage uses Euler integration** by default with timestep set by `interval_sim`. 10. **Real-world uses**: swarm robotics, multi-agent SLAM, path-planning benchmarks, ROS education. ### ✅ Pre-Exam Self-Test Before the exam, you should be able to: - [ ] Draw the **Stage layered architecture** from memory. - [ ] Write the **three kinematic update equations** for a differential-drive robot. - [ ] Explain **why Stage is preferred over Gazebo** for swarm simulations. - [ ] Identify the **four main entities** in a `.world` file. - [ ] State the **two noise models** supported by `ModelLaser`. - [ ] Compute the **speed-up factor** and **per-agent cost** from a given scenario. ---

topics.title: Gazebo and Other Simulators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT525 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gazebo and Other Simulators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Robot Simulator?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> A **robot simulator** is a software framework that emulates the physical behavior of a robot and its operating environment by numerically solving physics equations (kinematics, dynamics, collisions, sensor response) in a closed virtual loop, allowing algorithms developed in ROS to be validated *in silico* before deployment on real hardware.

The most prominent open-source simulator in the ROS ecosystem is **Gazebo**, originally developed by Andrew Howard and Nate Koenig at USC in 2002, and currently maintained by **Open Robotics** (the same organization that maintains ROS itself).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Flight Simulator" of Robotics

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Gazebo = Flight Simulator for Robots**
> Just as a **flight simulator** lets a pilot practice take-off, turbulence, and engine failure on the ground without risking the aircraft, **Gazebo** lets a robotics engineer test a controller, a SLAM algorithm, or a gripper on a virtual robot without burning out motors or crashing a ₹3 lakh drone. The pilot sees a fake cockpit, hears fake wind, and feels fake stick resistance — the robot in Gazebo sees fake camera images, fake LiDAR point clouds, and feels fake friction on its wheels. The *brain* (ROS nodes) does not know — or care — that the world is synthetic.

## 1.3 Why Do We Need Simulators in ROS?

| Real-World Problem | Simulated Solution |
|---|---|
| Robot hardware is **expensive** (UR5 = ₹18 lakh) | Test algorithms on free virtual robots |
| Real-world trials are **slow** (charge battery, calibrate, reset) | Reset the world in **< 1 second** |
| Edge cases (crashes) **destroy** hardware | Edge cases are **free** to reproduce |
| Sensors have **noisy real** data | Sensors can be tuned with **deterministic** noise |
| Multi-robot tests need **space** | Multi-robot tests need **zero space** |
| Safety in human environments | Safety in a **virtual** environment |

## 1.4 Gazebo at a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Properties of Gazebo**
> - **Open Source**: Apache 2.0 license, free for commercial use.
> - **ROS-Native**: Built and maintained by the same foundation (Open Robotics) that maintains ROS 1 and ROS 2.
> - **Physics-Based**: Uses real physics engines (ODE, Bullet, Simbody, DART) — not just kinematic animation.
> - **Sensor-Rich**: Simulates cameras, LiDAR, IMU, GPS, force-torque, contact, depth, infrared.
> - **Headless Capable**: Can run with `gzserver` only (no GUI), ideal for CI/CD and reinforcement learning loops.
> - **Default gravity**: $-9.81 \, m/s^2$ on the Z-axis (Earth standard, configurable).

## 1.5 The Two Eras of Gazebo

| Aspect | Gazebo **Classic** (Gazebo 11 and earlier) | **Ignition** Gazebo (Gazebo Sim, modern) |
|---|---|---|
| Internal name | Gazebo Sim (legacy) | Ignition Gazebo → now branded as **Gazebo** |
| Architecture | Monolithic server | Modular, distributed (uses **Ignition Libraries** like `gz-transport`, `gz-rendering`) |
| ROS Compatibility | ROS 1 native, ROS 2 via `gazebo_ros_pkgs` | **ROS 2 native** (recommended for ROS 2 Humble/Iron/Jazzy) |
| Plugin system | Shared libraries loaded at runtime | Same, but with cleaner C++ API |
| Current status | **Maintenance mode** | **Active development** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Watch:** A very common mistake is to use "Gazebo" and "Ignition" interchangeably. As of 2024, Open Robotics officially renamed **Ignition Gazebo** simply to **Gazebo**, while the older line is called **Gazebo Classic**. If your KTU exam mentions ROS 2 Humble or later, the default is the **new Gazebo** (formerly Ignition).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Gazebo's Layered Architecture (Classic)

Gazebo Classic follows a classic **client–server** model. Understanding these layers is critical for KTU questions like *"Explain the architecture of Gazebo"* (a 7-mark favorite).

| Layer | Component | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Master** | `gzmaster` | Coordinates naming, topic discovery, and process supervision (similar to `roscore`) |
| **Server** | `gzserver` | Headless physics simulation + sensor generation. Runs at fixed simulation step (default **1 ms**) |
| **Client** | `gzclient` | Qt-based GUI: visualizes robot, environment, sensor data, allows user interaction |
| **Plugins** | `libgazebo_ros_*` | Bridge Gazebo ↔ ROS topics (e.g., `libgazebo_ros_diff_drive.so`) |

> [!NOTE]
> In **Gazebo Classic**, `gzserver` and `gzclient` communicate over **Google Protobuf** over TCP/IP. In the **new Gazebo**, this is replaced by **ZeroMQ** via the `gz-transport` library, which is significantly faster and supports pub/sub natively.

## 2.2 Physics Engines Used by Gazebo

The physics engine is the **mathematical heart** of the simulator. It numerically integrates Newton's equations of motion and resolves collisions.

| Engine | Full Name | Strengths | Default in |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ODE** | Open Dynamics Engine | Fast, stable for ground robots | Gazebo Classic default |
| **Bullet** | Bullet Physics | Good for soft bodies, cloth, manipulation | Optional in Classic |
| **Simbody** | Simbody (SimTK) | High-accuracy biomechanics | Optional in Classic |
| **DART** | Dynamic Animation and Robotics Toolkit | Excellent contact handling, used by **new Gazebo** | **New Gazebo default** |

### 2.2.1 The Physics Integration Loop (Why it Matters)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Concept: Discrete Time Integration**
> The simulator does not solve physics continuously. It steps forward in fixed time increments $\Delta t$ (default **1 ms** for ODE).

For a rigid body with position $\mathbf{q}$, velocity $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$, and applied force $\boldsymbol{\tau}$:

$$
\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) \ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}) + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}
$$

The engine solves this at every step using numerical methods (RK4 in ODE, Featherstone in DART). Smaller $\Delta t$ = more accurate but slower simulation.

## 2.3 The SDF Format — Simulation Description Format

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SDF vs URDF — A Vital Distinction**
> - **URDF (Unified Robot Description Format)** = XML file describing only the **robot** (links, joints, inertias, visuals). Limited: cannot describe closed loops, multiple robots, environments.
> - **SDF (Simulation Description Format)** = XML file describing **everything**: robot(s), world (gravity, sun, ground), sensors, plugins, lights. This is the **native format** of Gazebo.

A Gazebo **world file** is an `.sdf` file with a `<world>` root element:

```xml
<sdf version="1.6">
  <world name="default">
    <gravity>0 0 -9.81</gravity>
    <include>
      <uri>model://ground_plane</uri>
    </include>
    <include>
      <uri>model://sun</uri>
    </include>
  </world>
</sdf>
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** URDF files **cannot** have `<sensor>`, `<gazebo>`, or `<world>` tags — those belong only to SDF. To make a URDF robot work in Gazebo, you add a `<gazebo>` block *inside* the URDF, declaring plugins and sensor properties. This is the standard pattern for ROS 1.

## 2.4 Sensor Simulation in Gazebo

Gazebo supports a wide taxonomy of sensors, each with its own plugin and noise model. The **noise model** typically has three components:

$$
\text{measurement} = \text{true value} + \text{Gaussian noise} + \text{bias}
$$

| Sensor | Gazebo Type Tag | Real Hardware Example | Topic Published |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Camera (RGB)** | `<sensor type="camera">` | Intel RealSense, Logitech C270 | `/camera/image_raw` |
| **Depth Camera** | `<sensor type="depth">` | Kinect, Azure Kinect | `/camera/depth/image_raw` |
| **LiDAR (2D)** | `<sensor type="ray">` | SICK LMS, RPLIDAR A1 | `/scan` (LaserScan msg) |
| **LiDAR (3D)** | `<sensor type="lidar">` (new Gazebo) | Velodyne VLP-16 | `/points` (PointCloud2) |
| **IMU** | `<sensor type="imu">` | MPU-6050, BMI088 | `/imu` (Imu msg) |
| **GPS** | `<sensor type="gps">` | u-blox NEO-M8N | `/gps/fix` (NavSatFix msg) |
| **Force-Torque** | `<sensor type="force_torque">` | ATI Mini40 | `/wrench` (Wrench msg) |
| **Contact** | `<sensor type="contact">` | Foot pressure sensor | `/contact` (Contacts msg) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Update Rate Math:** If a LiDAR publishes at 10 Hz, the topic message rate is exactly **10 messages/second**. Exam problems sometimes ask: *"A LiDAR rotates at 5 Hz. How many LaserScan messages are produced in 12 seconds?"* → Answer: $5 \times 12 = 60$ messages.

## 2.5 The `gazebo_ros_pkgs` Bridge

This is the **glue** between Gazebo and ROS. It is a set of packages that translate Gazebo internal messages to/from ROS message types.

| Package | Function |
|---|---|
| `gazebo_ros` | Launch files, node factories |
| `gazebo_plugins` | Camera, IMU, laser, differential drive plugins |
| `gazebo_msgs` | Custom messages: `SpawnModel`, `DeleteModel`, `LinkStates` |
| `gazebo_ros_control` | Integrates with `ros_control` (PID controllers) |

**Typical launch file pattern:**

```xml
<launch>
  <include file="$(find gazebo_ros)/launch/empty_world.launch">
    <arg name="world_name" value="my_world.sdf"/>
  </include>
  <node name="spawn_urdf" pkg="gazebo_ros" type="spawn_model"
        args="-file robot.urdf -urdf -model my_robot"/>
</launch>
```

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analysis & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step: Lifecycle of a Gazebo Simulation Step

> This is the **core computational loop** of Gazebo. Examiners love asking: *"Explain how a single simulation step is processed in Gazebo."* Here is the exhaustive answer.

**Step 1 — Wall-Clock Sync Check**
Gazebo's `World::Step()` function checks if enough real time has passed to issue the next simulation step.

$$
t_{\text{real}} \geq t_{\text{sim}} + \Delta t
$$

If yes, advance simulation time; if not, wait (this is the "real-time factor" = 1.0 mode).

**Step 2 — Read Sensor Input**
Plugins that subscribe to external commands (e.g., `/cmd_vel` from a ROS node) update their internal actuator state.

**Step 3 — Update Actuator States**
For a `differential_drive` plugin, the wheel velocities $\omega_L, \omega_R$ are written into the corresponding joint's `SetVelocity()` method.

**Step 4 — Apply External Forces**
Gravity $\mathbf{F}_g = m \cdot \mathbf{g}$ is added to every dynamic body. The gravity vector defaults to:

$$
\mathbf{g} = (0, 0, -9.81) \, m/s^2
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate Dynamics (Physics Engine Call)**
The chosen engine (ODE/DART/Bullet) solves the equations of motion numerically:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}}_{t+\Delta t} = \dot{\mathbf{q}}_t + \ddot{\mathbf{q}}_t \cdot \Delta t
$$

$$
\mathbf{q}_{t+\Delta t} = \mathbf{q}_t + \dot{\mathbf{q}}_t \cdot \Delta t + \frac{1}{2} \ddot{\mathbf{q}}_t \cdot (\Delta t)^2
$$

**Step 6 — Collision Detection (Broad + Narrow Phase)**
- **Broad phase:** Quick AABB (axis-aligned bounding box) overlap check using a sweep-and-prune or hierarchical grid.
- **Narrow phase:** For pairs that pass, compute exact contact points using **GJK algorithm** or **SAT (Separating Axis Theorem)** for convex shapes.

**Step 7 — Resolve Contacts**
Compute contact normal $\hat{\mathbf{n}}$, penetration depth $d$, and apply impulse:

$$
\mathbf{J} = -\frac{(1 + e) \, \mathbf{v}_{\text{rel}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}}}{m_1^{-1} + m_2^{-1}}
$$

where $e$ is the coefficient of restitution (bounciness, $0 \le e \le 1$).

**Step 8 — Update Sensor Plugins**
Cameras render the scene via **OGRE** (Classic) or **gz-rendering** (New). LiDAR casts rays. IMU reads acceleration + angular velocity from the body's state.

**Step 9 — Publish to ROS Topics**
Sensor plugins send ROS messages via the `gazebo_ros` bridge.

**Step 10 — Increment Simulation Clock and Loop**
$t_{\text{sim}} = t_{\text{sim}} + \Delta t$ and go back to Step 1.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step: Deriving the Differential-Drive Odometry

This is a **favourite 7-mark derivation** in KTU. The Gazebo `diff_drive` plugin publishes odometry; understanding the math behind it is essential.

**Given:**
- Left wheel angular velocity: $\omega_L$ (rad/s)
- Right wheel angular velocity: $\omega_R$ (rad/s)
- Wheel radius: $r$ (m)
- Wheel separation (track width): $L$ (m)

**Step 1 — Linear velocity of each wheel**

$$
v_L = r \cdot \omega_L
$$

$$
v_R = r \cdot \omega_R
$$

**Step 2 — Forward velocity of robot center**

$$
v = \frac{v_L + v_R}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_L + \omega_R)}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Angular velocity of robot**

$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
$$

**Step 4 — Update pose over time step $\Delta t$**

$$
x_{t+1} = x_t + v \cos(\theta_t) \cdot \Delta t
$$

$$
y_{t+1} = y_t + v \sin(\theta_t) \cdot \Delta t
$$

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \omega \cdot \Delta t
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
> $r = 0.05$ m, $L = 0.30$ m, $\omega_L = 10$ rad/s, $\omega_R = 12$ rad/s, $\Delta t = 0.1$ s, $\theta_0 = 0$.
>
> **Solution:**
> $v_L = 0.05 \times 10 = 0.5$ m/s
> $v_R = 0.05 \times 12 = 0.6$ m/s
> $v = (0.5 + 0.6)/2 = 0.55$ m/s
> $\omega = (0.6 - 0.5)/0.30 = 0.333$ rad/s
> $x_1 = 0 + 0.55 \cos(0) \times 0.1 = 0.055$ m
> $y_1 = 0 + 0.55 \sin(0) \times 0.1 = 0$ m
> $\theta_1 = 0 + 0.333 \times 0.1 = 0.0333$ rad

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Sensor Noise Model

Sensors in Gazebo follow this noise model (for `gaussian` noise type):

$$
m(t) = s(t) + n(t) + b(t)
$$

where:
- $m(t)$ = measured value at time $t$
- $s(t)$ = true signal
- $n(t) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ = white Gaussian noise
- $b(t)$ = slowly varying bias (drift)

**In URDF/SDF, this is declared as:**

```xml
<sensor name="imu" type="imu">
  <imu>
    <angular_velocity>
      <noise type="gaussian">
        <mean>0.0</mean>
        <stddev>0.01</stddev>
      </noise>
    </angular_velocity>
  </imu>
</sensor>
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The `<stddev>` value is the **standard deviation** $\sigma$, **not** the variance $\sigma^2$. A common student error is to confuse them.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: URDF vs SDF

| Property | URDF | SDF |
|---|---|---|
| File extension | `.urdf` or `.xacro` | `.sdf` |
| Schema location | `http://ros.org/wiki/urdf` | `http://sdformat.org/schemas/...` |
| Can describe environment (lights, ground) | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Can describe closed-kinematic loops | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Can have multiple robots in one file | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Can nest models | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (`<include>`) |
| Supported by `robot_state_publisher` | ✅ Yes | ⚠️ Limited |
| Gazebo's native format | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| ROS 2 standard | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (but URDF + `ros2_control` is more common) |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Choosing a Simulator for a Project

| Project Need | Best Simulator Choice |
|---|---|
| ROS 1 final-year project, mobile robot | **Gazebo Classic** (most tutorials) |
| ROS 2 Humble, modern project | **New Gazebo (gz-sim)** |

| Control theory validation, MATLAB users | **MATLAB Simulink** |

## 4.1 Gazebo Classic Architecture (Client–Server Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ROS["ROS NETWORK"]
        ROSNode["ROS Node (e.g. teleop_twist)"]
        ROSTopic["ROS Topic e.g. /cmd_vel"]
    end

    subgraph Gazebo["GAZEBO SIMULATION"]
        Master["gzmaster (process supervisor)"]
        Server["gzserver (physics + sensors, headless)"]
        Client["gzclient (Qt GUI, visualization)"]
        Plugin1["libgazebo_ros_diff_drive.so"]
        Plugin2["libgazebo_ros_imu.so"]
        Plugin3["libgazebo_ros_camera.so"]
    end

    ROSNode -- publishes --> ROSTopic
    ROSTopic -- consumed by --> Plugin1
    Plugin1 -- applies torque --> Server
    Server -- ground truth --> Plugin2
    Plugin2 -- IMU message --> ROSTopic
    Server -- scene state --> Client
    Client -- user interaction --> Server
    Master -- supervises --> Server
    Master -- supervises --> Client
```

## 4.2 Single Simulation Step Flow (Inside gzserver)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["t = t + Δt (wall clock check)"] --> B["Read ROS /cmd_vel via plugin"]
    B --> C["Update joint velocities (actuators)"]
    C --> D["Apply gravity g = -9.81 m/s² to all bodies"]
    D --> E["ODE/DART: integrate equations of motion"]
    E --> F["Collision detection (Broad + Narrow)"]
    F --> G["Resolve contact impulses (Coulomb friction)"]
    G --> H["Update sensor states (Camera, IMU, LiDAR)"]
    H --> I["Publish to ROS topics via gazebo_ros bridge"]
    I --> J{"Real-time factor == 1.0?"}
    J -- Yes --> A
    J -- No --> K["Wait until wall-clock catches up"]
    K --> A
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** 
**What is Gazebo? Mention any two physics engines supported by Gazebo.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Gazebo is a **3D dynamic simulator** used in robotics to accurately and efficiently simulate populations of robots in complex indoor and outdoor environments. It is open-source (Apache 2.0) and is the standard simulator of the ROS ecosystem. It supports multiple physics engines through a plugin architecture. Two major physics engines supported are: (i) **ODE (Open Dynamics Engine)** — fast and stable, used as the default in Gazebo Classic; (ii) **DART (Dynamic Animation and Robotics Toolkit)** — used as the default in the new modular Gazebo. Other supported engines include **Bullet** and **Simbody**.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]** 
**Differentiate between URDF and SDF.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | URDF | SDF |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Describes **robot only** | Describes **robot + world + environment** |
| Closed kinematic loops | ❌ Not supported | ✅ Supported |
| Multiple robots in one file | ❌ Not supported | ✅ Supported |
| Native to | ROS / `robot_state_publisher` | Gazebo |
| File extension | `.urdf` / `.xacro` | `.sdf` / `.world` |

URDF = ROS-native robot description; SDF = Gazebo-native simulation description (1.5 marks each).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, Module 3 Choice A]**
> (a) Explain the **architecture of Gazebo Classic** with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of `gzmaster`, `gzserver`, and `gzclient`. **(7 marks)**
> (b) Describe the **SDF world file format** with an example. List the major tags and their purpose. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution (a) — 7 marks

Gazebo Classic follows a **client–server architecture**:

1. **`gzmaster` (Process Supervisor)** — analogous to `roscore` in ROS. It acts as a name server and coordinates all running Gazebo processes. It is the first process to start and supervises the lifecycle of other components. *(1.5 marks)*

2. **`gzserver` (Headless Simulation Engine)** — this is the workhorse. It runs the physics simulation, generates sensor data, and handles all plugin logic. It can run **without** a GUI, which is essential for headless servers, CI/CD pipelines, and cloud-based training. *(2 marks)*

3. **`gzclient` (Qt-based GUI)** — the visual front-end. It connects to `gzserver` over TCP/IP using Google Protobuf, renders the scene using the **OGRE** graphics engine, and provides interactive tools to spawn/visualize/drag models. Multiple clients can connect to one server for collaborative visualization. *(2 marks)*

4. **Plugins (`libgazebo_ros_*`)** — shared libraries (`.so` files) loaded at runtime to provide ROS integration, sensor models, and actuator models. The `gazebo_ros_pkgs` package provides ready-made plugins for cameras, IMUs, laser scanners, and differential-drive controllers. *(1.5 marks)*

(See Section 4.1 for the diagram that should accompany this answer.)

#### Solution (b) — 7 marks

The **Simulation Description Format (SDF)** is an XML format that describes everything in a Gazebo simulation — robots, environment, sensors, lights, and physics properties. It is the **native** and most complete description format for Gazebo. *(1 mark)*

**Anatomy of a typical SDF world file:**

```xml
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<sdf version="1.6">
  <world name="my_world">

    <!-- Global physics properties (1 mark) -->
    <physics type="ode">
      <max_step_size>0.001</max_step_size>      <!-- 1 ms -->
      <real_time_factor>1.0</real_time_factor>
      <gravity>0 0 -9.81</gravity>
    </physics>

    <!-- Built-in environment models (1 mark) -->
    <include><uri>model://ground_plane</uri></include>
    <include><uri>model://sun</uri></include>

    <!-- Custom robot model (1.5 marks) -->
    <model name="my_robot">
      <link name="chassis">
        <pose>0 0 0.1 0 0 0</pose>
        <inertial>
          <mass>5.0</mass>
          <inertia>
            <ixx>0.1</ixx><iyy>0.2</iyy><izz>0.15</izz>
          </inertia>
        </inertial>
        <visual name="visual">
          <geometry><box><size>0.4 0.2 0.1</size></box></geometry>
        </visual>
        <collision name="collision">
          <geometry><box><size>0.4 0.2 0.1</size></box></geometry>
        </collision>
      </link>

      <!-- Sensor: camera (1.5 marks) -->
      <sensor name="camera" type="camera">
        <update_rate>30</update_rate>
        <camera name="head">
          <horizontal_fov>1.396</horizontal_fov>
          <image width="640" height="480" format="R8G8B8"/>
          <clip near="0.1" far="100"/>
        </camera>
        <plugin name="camera_controller"
                filename="libgazebo_ros_camera.so">
          <frame_name>camera_optical_frame</frame_name>
        </plugin>
      </sensor>
    </model>
  </world>
</sdf>
```

**Major tags and their purpose:**

| Tag | Purpose | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| `<world>` | Root container for the entire simulation | 0.5 |
| `<physics>` | Specifies engine (`ode`/`bullet`/`dart`/`simbody`), gravity, step size, real-time factor | 0.5 |
| `<model>` / `<include>` | Defines or imports a robot/object | 0.5 |
| `<link>` | Rigid body with inertial, visual, and collision properties | 0.5 |
| `<joint>` | Constrains motion between two links | 0.5 |
| `<sensor>` | Defines a virtual sensor (camera, IMU, ray, etc.) | 0.5 |
| `<plugin>` | Loads a C++ plugin for ROS integration | 0.5 |
| `<light>` | Adds lighting (sun, point, spot, directional) | 0.5 |
| `<gravity>` | Sets the gravity vector | (included in `<physics>`) |

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, Module 3 Choice B]**
> (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the **ROS-Gazebo integration** and the role of `gazebo_ros_pkgs`. List any four packages in it. **(7 marks)**
> (b) Compare **Gazebo, Webots, and CoppeliaSim** as robotics simulators. Mention at least five comparison points. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution (a) — 7 marks

**ROS-Gazebo Integration Flow:**

A ROS node publishes a velocity command → ROS topic `/cmd_vel` → `gazebo_ros_pkgs` plugin `libgazebo_ros_diff_drive.so` receives the message → applies torque to the simulated wheel joints → `gzserver` integrates physics → wheel rotates → `libgazebo_ros_laser.so` simulates a LiDAR scan → publishes `/scan` topic → another ROS node (e.g., `gmapping`) consumes it. *(3 marks — see Section 4.4 diagram)*

**Role of `gazebo_ros_pkgs`:**
`gazebo_ros_pkgs` is the **metapackage** that acts as the official bridge between ROS and Gazebo. It provides launch files, message types, and plugins that allow seamless two-way communication. Without it, Gazebo would be a stand-alone simulator with no awareness of the ROS ecosystem. *(2 marks)*

**Four Major Packages Inside `gazebo_ros_pkgs`:**

| Package | Function | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| `gazebo_ros` | Launch files (`empty_world.launch`, `gazebo.launch`) and the `spawn_model` node for loading URDF/SDF | 0.5 |
| `gazebo_plugins` | Ready-to-use sensor and actuator plugins: camera, IMU, laser, F/T, GPS, differential drive | 0.5 |
| `gazebo_msgs` | Custom ROS messages: `SpawnModel`, `DeleteModel`, `LinkStates`, `ModelStates` | 0.5 |
| `gazebo_ros_control` | Integration with `ros_control` framework — provides PID controllers for joints in simulation | 0.5 |

#### Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Comparison Table: Gazebo vs Webots vs CoppeliaSim**

| Comparison Point | Gazebo (Classic / New) | Webots | CoppeliaSim |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Developer & License** | Open Robotics; Apache 2.0 (open-source) | Cyberbotics (SoftBank); Apache 2.0 | Coppelia Robotics; Free for education |
| **Default Physics Engine** | ODE (Classic) / DART (New) | Modified ODE | Bullet / ODE / Vortex (user-selectable) |
| **ROS Support** | Native, first-class | Excellent via built-in ROS 2 plugin | Good via external plugin |
| **Programming Interface** | C++ plugins, XML config | C, C++, Python, Java, MATLAB, ROS | Lua, Python, C++, MATLAB, ROS |
| **Best Suited For** | General ROS-based robotics, research | Education, mobile robots, vehicles | Industrial manipulation, factories |
| **3D Rendering / Photorealism** | Medium (OGRE / gz-rendering) | Medium | Medium-High |
| **Cost** | Free (open source) | Free | Free for education, paid commercial |
| **Headless mode** | Yes (`gzserver`) | Yes | Yes |

*(1 mark per valid comparison point; 7 marks for 5+1 bonus points)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Ways Students Lose Marks on Gazebo Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing URDF and SDF** — writing SDF-specific tags (`<world>`, `<sensor>`, `<light>`) inside a `.urdf` file. URDF supports only links, joints, transmission, and gazebo-extension blocks.
> 2. **Wrong gravity sign** — writing gravity as `9.81` instead of `-9.81` on the Z-axis. Gravity is a vector pointing **downward** in Gazebo's ENU coordinate frame.
> 3. **Conflating Gazebo Classic and New Gazebo** — using `libgazebo_ros_*` plugin names for the new modular Gazebo, which uses different plugin naming conventions.
> 4. **Confusing `<stddev>` with variance** in noise models — the URDF/SDF noise `<stddev>` tag expects standard deviation $\sigma$, not $\sigma^2$.
> 5. **Forgetting to specify `<update_rate>`** on sensors — if omitted, default is 0 Hz and no messages are published. Examiners notice this immediately.
> 6. **Not including both `<visual>` and `<collision>`** in every `<link>` — missing either leads to a "ghost" (invisible) or "phase-through" (no contact) robot.
> 7. **Using `gzclient` instead of `gzserver`** in CI/CD scripts — the GUI will hang in headless environments (Docker, servers, CI runners).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Gazebo** is a 3D physics-based simulator; it is the **default ROS simulator** maintained by **Open Robotics**.
- **Two main eras**: **Gazebo Classic** (ROS 1, ODE, monolithic) vs **New Gazebo / Ignition** (ROS 2, DART, modular).
- **Architecture = `gzmaster` + `gzserver` + `gzclient` + plugins**. The server is headless; the client is the GUI.
- **Default physics step size** $\Delta t$ = **1 ms**; **default gravity** = $(0, 0, -9.81)$ m/s².
- **Supported physics engines**: ODE, Bullet, Simbody, DART. ODE is the Classic default; DART is the New Gazebo default.
- **URDF** = robot description only (links + joints). **SDF** = full simulation (robot + world + sensors + plugins + lights). URDF is **insufficient** alone for Gazebo — you need to add `<gazebo>` extension blocks or convert to SDF.
- **Sensors** in Gazebo: camera, depth, IMU, ray (2D LiDAR), lidar (3D), GPS, force-torque, contact. Each has its own plugin and topic.
- **Sensor noise** follows $m(t) = s(t) + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2) + b$. In XML, `<stddev>` = $\sigma$ (not variance).
- **Differential-drive odometry** formulas: $v = r(\omega_L + \omega_R)/2$, $\omega = r(\omega_R - \omega_L)/L$.
- **`gazebo_ros_pkgs`** is the official ROS–Gazebo bridge. Key sub-packages: `gazebo_ros`, `gazebo_plugins`, `gazebo_msgs`, `gazebo_ros_control`.
- **Other simulators** for KTU: **Webots** (education, free), **CoppeliaSim/V-REP** (manipulation, edu-free), **Isaac Sim** (photorealism, GPU), **CARLA** (driving), **AirSim** (drones), **MuJoCo** (RL), **PyBullet** (Python RL), **MATLAB Simulink** (control).
- **Contact resolution** uses impulses; coefficient of restitution $e \in [0, 1]$.
- **Coordinate convention** in Gazebo: **ENU (East-North-Up)** for ground robots; gravity points $-Z$.
- **Real-time factor (RTF)**: 1.0 means simulation runs in real-time; > 1.0 means faster than real-time (useful for RL training).
- **A `<link>` must have both `<visual>` and `<collision>`**, else the robot is invisible or has no contact.
- **Default update rate for sensors**: must be specified explicitly; 0 Hz = no publishing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maps in ROS: Representation and Formats
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT525 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Maps in ROS: Representation and Formats

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the Robot Operating System (ROS), a **Map** is a structured, probabilistic, or geometric representation of the robot's environment used for localization, navigation, and path planning. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (PERUT525 – Module 4), maps in ROS are primarily represented as two-dimensional **occupancy grids** (in the form of `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` messages) or as three-dimensional structures such as **point clouds** (`sensor_msgs/PointCloud2`) and **Octomaps** (`octomap_msgs/Octomap`).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A map in ROS is a discretized data structure where each cell encodes the probability of occupancy by a static obstacle. The reference frame is typically `/map`, which is a world-fixed, gravity-aligned frame.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are walking through a **dark, unfamiliar building** with a piece of graph paper and a pencil. As you sweep a flashlight around:
- Cells you **clearly see** (empty floor) — you shade white.
- Cells blocked by walls or furniture — you shade black.
- Cells you have **never seen** — you leave blank (unknown).

The graph paper is your **Map**, the flashlight is your **LiDAR/Sonar sensor**, and the shaded squares are the **occupancy values**. This is exactly how a robot builds and stores a 2D occupancy grid in ROS. The only difference is that instead of "white/black/blank," the robot stores **three probability states**: *free (0–25 %)*, *occupied (65–100 %)*, and *unknown (−1)*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle (CO1 – KTU 2024):** Every cell in an ROS occupancy grid stores an integer in the range **[-1, 100]**, where **-1 = Unknown**, **0 = Certainly Free**, and **100 = Certainly Occupied**. This is the *ROS Occupancy Probability Model*.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (per ROS Rep 0112)

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Free Threshold | $\theta_{free}$ | **0.196** (≈ 25 %) | Below this, cell is free |
| Occupied Threshold | $\theta_{occ}$ | **0.65** (65 %) | Above this, cell is occupied |
| Cell Resolution | $r$ | **0.05 m** (typical) | Side length of one cell |
| Map Origin | $(x_0, y_0)$ | **(0, 0)** | World pose of cell (0,0) |
| Probability Range | $P(c)$ | **[-1, 100]** | Cell occupancy value |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Map Representation Hierarchy in ROS

ROS treats maps as **layered, message-based data structures**. Below is the layered logic used in navigation and SLAM:

### 2.1.1 The 2D Occupancy Grid (`nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid`)
- A **2-D raster grid** of square cells.
- Each cell stores an integer in the closed interval **[-1, 100]**.
- Stored internally as a **1-D byte array** (`std::vector<int8_t>`), row-major ordered.
- The message also contains a `MapMetaData` block specifying `resolution`, `width`, `height`, and `origin`.

### 2.1.2 Costmap Layers (`costmap_2d::Costmap2D`)
- Built on top of OccupancyGrid.
- Each cell stores cost values in **[0, 255]** with categories:
  - **0** → Free Space (No information)
  - **253** → Inscribed Obstacle
  - **254** → Lethal Obstacle
  - **255** → No Information (Unknown)
- Used by the **Global Planner** and **Local Planner (DWA / TEB)**.

### 2.1.3 Point Cloud Maps (`sensor_msgs/PointCloud2`)
- A **3-D collection of (x, y, z) points** plus intensity/timestamp fields.
- Built by accumulating LiDAR scans over time using packages like `pointcloud_to_laserscan` or `loam_velodyne`.
- Format is **binary-efficient** (little-endian, with `data` field as `uint8[]`).

### 2.1.4 Octomap (3-D Volumetric)
- Uses an **Octree data structure** — each node has 8 children, recursively subdividing space.
- Probabilistic — encodes occupancy with log-odds.
- Ideal for **aerial robots, manipulators, and 3-D path planning**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Symbol | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Occupancy Probability | $P(c) \in [-1, 100]$ | Cell value in `OccupancyGrid` | dimensionless |
| Log-Odds Update | $L(c) = L(c) + L_{meas} - L_{prior}$ | Bayesian update rule | nats |
| Log-Odds of Probability | $L(c) = \log \frac{P(c)}{1 - P(c)}$ | Conversion from probability | nats |
| Probability from Log-Odds | $P(c) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{L(c)}}$ | Inverse logit | dimensionless |
| World Cell Coordinates | $(x_w, y_w) = (x_0 + j \cdot r,\; y_0 + i \cdot r)$ | Convert grid to world frame | meters |
| Grid Index | $k = i \cdot W + j$ | Row-major 1-D index | dimensionless |
| Free Threshold | $\theta_{free} \le 0.196$ | Below = free | probability |
| Occupied Threshold | $\theta_{occ} \ge 0.65$ | Above = occupied | probability |
