| Map Resolution | $r = 0.05$ (typical) | Cell side length | meters |
| Octree Volume | $V_{node} = r_0^3 / 8^d$ | Volume of node at depth $d$ | m³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** If asked "What is the value range of an OccupancyGrid cell?", always reply **−1 to 100** (NOT 0 to 255 — that is the **Costmap** range).

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

| Engineering Field | Use of ROS Maps |
|---|---|
| **Autonomous Vehicles (Self-Driving Cars)** | 2-D Costmaps + OpenStreetMap overlays for lane-level planning |
| **Warehouse AGVs (Amazon Kiva, Fetch)** | OccupancyGrid for path planning, topological maps for routing |
| **Aerial Drones (Indoor / Search & Rescue)** | Octomaps for 3-D obstacle avoidance in cluttered environments |
| **Underwater ROVs** | 2.5-D Voxel grids generated from multibeam sonar |
| **Service Robots (TurtleBot, Pepper)** | 2-D SLAM maps (gmapping / Cartographer) for home navigation |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: World ↔ Grid Coordinate Transformation

Let the map have:
- Origin in world frame: $(x_0, y_0)$ meters
- Resolution: $r$ meters/cell
- Width: $W$ cells, Height: $H$ cells

### 3.1.1 From World Coordinates → Grid Index

**Step 1 — Translate world coordinates relative to the map origin:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{rel} &= x_w - x_0 \\
y_{rel} &= y_w - y_0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to floating-point grid coordinates:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
j_{float} &= \frac{x_{rel}}{r} \\
i_{float} &= \frac{y_{rel}}{r}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Floor to obtain discrete integer grid indices $(i, j)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
j &= \lfloor j_{float} \rfloor \\
i &= \lfloor i_{float} \rfloor
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert $(i, j)$ to the 1-D data-array index $k$ (row-major):**

$$
k = i \cdot W + j
$$

### 3.1.2 From Grid Index → World Coordinates

**Step 1 — Reconstruct $(i, j)$ from $k$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i &= \left\lfloor \frac{k}{W} \right\rfloor \\
j &= k \bmod W
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert grid indices back to world meters using the cell-center convention:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_w &= x_0 + (j + 0.5) \cdot r \\
y_w &= y_0 + (i + 0.5) \cdot r
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **+0.5** offset is the *cell-center convention* — it places the cell's reported coordinate at its geometric center, not its corner. ROS follows this convention; some older stacks (e.g., Stage) do not.

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Given:** Resolution $r = 0.1$ m, Origin $(x_0, y_0) = (-5.0, -5.0)$ m, Width $W = 100$, Height $H = 100$. A sensor reports an obstacle at world coordinates $(x_w, y_w) = (-2.35, 1.20)$ m. Find the 1-D data index $k$.

**Step 1 — Translate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{rel} &= -2.35 - (-5.0) = 2.65 \text{ m} \\
y_{rel} &= 1.20 - (-5.0) = 6.20 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to grid floats:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
j_{float} &= 2.65 / 0.1 = 26.5 \\
i_{float} &= 6.20 / 0.1 = 62.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Floor:**

$$
j = 26, \quad i = 62
$$

**Step 4 — Compute 1-D index:**

$$
k = 62 \times 100 + 26 = 6226
$$

The obstacle is stored at `data[6226]` of the `OccupancyGrid` message. ✔

## 3.2 Derivation: Log-Odds Bayesian Update for Occupancy Grids

This is the math used by **gmapping, cartographer, and hector_slam** to fuse successive LiDAR scans.

### 3.2.1 The Log-Odds Function

For any probability $P \in (0, 1)$, define the **log-odds** $L$ as:

$$
L = \log \frac{P}{1 - P}
$$

This is well-defined for $P \in (0, 1)$ and maps it to $L \in (-\infty, +\infty)$.

### 3.2.2 Bayesian Update in Log-Odds Form

In standard probability form, the recursive update is:

$$
P(c \mid z_{1:t}) = \frac{P(z_t \mid c) \cdot P(c \mid z_{1:t-1})}{P(z_t \mid c) \cdot P(c \mid z_{1:t-1}) + P(z_t \mid \lnot c) \cdot (1 - P(c \mid z_{1:t-1}))}
$$

**Step 1 — Take the log of both sides:**

$$
\log \frac{P(c \mid z_{1:t})}{1 - P(c \mid z_{1:t})} = \log \frac{P(z_t \mid c)}{P(z_t \mid \lnot c)} + \log \frac{P(c \mid z_{1:t-1})}{1 - P(c \mid z_{1:t-1})}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the log-odds notation:**

$$
L_t(c) = L_{meas}(c) + L_{t-1}(c)
$$

**Step 3 — Add a clamping bound to prevent runaway values (KTU-required detail):**

$$
L_t(c) = \max\left( L_{min},\; \min\left( L_{max},\; L_{t-1}(c) + L_{meas}(c) \right) \right)
$$

where, by default in ROS, $L_{min} = -2.0$ and $L_{max} = 3.5$ (corresponding roughly to probabilities of 0.12 and 0.97).

**Step 4 — Convert back to probability (inverse logit) for the `OccupancyGrid` cell value:**

$$
P_t(c) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{L_t(c)}}
$$

**Step 5 — Map probability to integer `OccupancyGrid` value (scale to 0–100, with -1 reserved for unknown):**

$$
V_{cell} = \text{round}\big(100 \cdot P_t(c)\big)
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing a Map Format in KTU Lab Exams

| Scenario | Recommended Map Type | Reason | Resolution |
|---|---|---|---|
| Indoor 2-D SLAM (TurtleBot) | OccupancyGrid | Lightweight, fast, planner-native | 0.05 m |
| Differential drive in cluttered lab | Costmap2D (with inflation layer) | Includes robot radius + safety buffer | 0.05 m |
| Outdoor autonomous car | GridCells + OpenStreetMap overlay | Semantic + geometric hybrid | 0.20 m |
| Drone in 3-D forest | Octomap | Volumetric, allows dynamic pruning | 0.10 m (leaf) |
| Manipulator arm workspace | PointCloud2 | High-fidelity surface from depth camera | Sensor-native |
| Underwater sonar mapping | Octomap (probabilistic) | Handles sparse, noisy returns | 0.25 m |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an ROS Map Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["SENSOR LAYER: LIDAR AND DEPTH CAMERA AND SONAR"] --> node2["DRIVER NODE: SCAN AND CLOUD AND ODOM"]
    node2 --> node3["SLAM NODE: GMAPPING OR CARTOGRAPHER OR HECTOR"]
    node3 --> node4["MAP SERVER NODE: PUBLISHES NAV MSGS OCCUPANCY GRID"]
    node4 --> node5["COSTMAP 2D NODE: BUILDS LAYERED COST MAP"]
    node5 --> node6["GLOBAL PLANNER: GLOBAL PATH"]
    node5 --> node7["LOCAL PLANNER: DWA OR TEB"]
    node6 --> node8["NAVIGATION OUTPUT: VELOCITY CMD VEL"]
    node7 --> node8
    node4 --> node9["RVIZ VISUALIZATION: MAP AND MARKERS"]
    node5 --> node9
    node8 --> node10["MOBILE BASE: HARDWARE INTERFACE"]
    node2 --> node5
    node1 --> node5
    node9 --> node11["OPERATOR CONSOLE: HUMAN SUPERVISOR"]
    node8 --> node11
```

## 4.2 OccupancyGrid Message Structure (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["NAV MSGS OCCUPANCY GRID"] --> nodeB["HEADER: STAMP AND FRAME ID MAP"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["INFO MAP META DATA"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["DATA: VECTOR INT8 OF SIZE W TIMES H"]
    nodeC --> nodeC1["RESOLUTION: FLOAT32 METERS"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["WIDTH: UINT32 CELLS"]
    nodeC --> nodeC3["HEIGHT: UINT32 CELLS"]
    nodeC --> nodeC4["ORIGIN: GEOMETRY MSGS POSE"]
    nodeC4 --> nodeC4a["POSITION X Y Z"]
    nodeC4 --> nodeC4b["ORIENTATION QUATERNION"]
```

## 4.3 Map Data Flow: World Frame vs. Cell Index

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeW["WORLD FRAME: METERS"] --> nodeT1["TRANSLATION SUBTRACT ORIGIN X0 Y0"]
    nodeT1 --> nodeT2["DIVIDE BY RESOLUTION R"]
    nodeT2 --> nodeT3["FLOOR OPERATION TO INTEGER I J"]
    nodeT3 --> nodeT4["COMPUTE K EQUALS I TIMES W PLUS J"]
    nodeT4 --> nodeT5["OCCUPANCY GRID DATA VECTOR INDEX K"]
    nodeT5 --> nodeT6["CELL VALUE MINUS 1 TO 100"]
```

## 4.4 Costmap2D Layered Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeL1["STATIC LAYER: PRE BUILT MAP"] --> nodeM["MASTER COSTMAP 2D OBJECT"]
    nodeL2["OBSTACLE LAYER: SENSOR DATA"] --> nodeM
    nodeL3["INFLATION LAYER: SAFETY BUFFER"] --> nodeM
    nodeL4["VOXEL LAYER: 3D ELEVATION CHECK"] --> nodeM
    nodeL5["RANGE SENSOR LAYER: SONAR"] --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeP1["GLOBAL PLANNER CONSUMES: 253 AND 254 AND 255"]
    nodeM --> nodeP2["LOCAL PLANNER CONSUMES: PER STEP TRAJECTORY SCORE"]
```

## 4.5 Map File Format Layout (YAML + Image)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeF1["MAP YAML FILE"] --> nodeF1a["IMAGE KEY: PATH TO PGM OR PNG"]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF1b["RESOLUTION KEY: METERS PER PIXEL"]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF1c["ORIGIN KEY: 2D POSE X Y YAW"]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF1d["OCCUPIED THRESH KEY: 0.65"]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF1e["FREE THRESH KEY: 0.196"]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF1f["NEGATE KEY: 0 OR 1"]
    nodeF1a --> nodeF2["PORTABLE GREY MAP OR PNG IMAGE"]
    nodeF2 --> nodeF2a["WHITE PIXEL 255: FREE"]
    nodeF2 --> nodeF2b["BLACK PIXEL 0: OCCUPIED"]
    nodeF2 --> nodeF2c["GREY PIXEL: UNKNOWN"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "Occupancy Grid" in ROS. State the value range of a single cell and the meaning of each value.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **Occupancy Grid** is a 2-D probabilistic map representation in ROS, exchanged using the `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` message type, in which the environment is discretized into a uniform grid of square cells. Each cell stores an integer in the range **[-1, 100]**, where:
- **-1** → Unknown (no sensor has ever observed this cell)
- **0** → Definitely free
- **100** → Definitely occupied
- **1 to 99** → Probabilistic occupancy.

The message includes a `MapMetaData` block describing the **resolution (m/cell)**, **width**, **height**, and **origin pose** of the grid in the `/map` frame. *(3 marks: 1 + 2)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List and briefly explain the four standard parameters stored in a ROS map's YAML file.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The standard YAML metadata file accompanying an ROS map contains:
1. **image** — relative path to the PGM/PNG grayscale image (1 mark).
2. **resolution** — meters per pixel (cell side length), e.g., 0.05 (1 mark).
3. **origin** — the 2-D pose $(x, y, yaw)$ of the lower-left cell in the `/map` frame (0.5 mark).
4. **occupied_thresh**, **free_thresh**, and **negate** — thresholds used by `map_server` to convert grayscale intensity to occupancy probability (0.5 mark).

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module-Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the layered architecture of the ROS `costmap_2d` package. List any four distinct layers with their functions. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Given a ROS map with:
- Origin = $(0.0, 0.0, 0.0)$
- Resolution = $0.2$ m
- Width = 200, Height = 150
- An obstacle is stored at 1-D data index $k = 14\,738$

Compute:
- (i) The grid indices $(i, j)$ of the obstacle. **(2 marks)**
- (ii) The world coordinates $(x_w, y_w)$ of the obstacle's cell center. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) If `map_server` reports `occupied_thresh = 0.65` and the cell value is `82`, classify the cell as Free / Occupied / Unknown. Justify. **(2 marks)**
- (iv) The probability of occupancy in log-odds form if the prior log-odds is 0.0 and the sensor measurement log-odds is 1.5. **(1 mark)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Costmap 2D Layered Architecture (7 marks)**

The `costmap_2d` package in ROS Navigation stack is a layered abstraction that fuses multiple data sources (static map, sensor data, user-defined virtual obstacles) into a single 2-D grid of cost values in the range **0 to 255**. A *Master Costmap2D* object holds the combined grid, and individual *Layer Plugins* contribute their updates.

**Layered Architecture Block Diagram:**

```
        ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
        │       Master Costmap2D Object          │
        │     (Combined 2-D Cost Grid)           │
        └─────┬──────┬──────┬──────┬─────────────┘
              │      │      │      │
       ┌──────▼─┐ ┌──▼────┐ ┌▼─────┴────┐ ┌────▼──────┐
       │ Static │ │Obst.  │ │Inflation  │ │Voxel/     │
       │ Layer  │ │Layer  │ │Layer      │ │Range Layer│
       └────────┘ └───────┘ └───────────┘ └───────────┘
              │       │          │              │
        Pre-built    LiDAR/    Safety       3-D clear-
         Map         Depth     buffer       ance check
                     data      around       for foot-
                                obstacles   print
```

**Four Layers:**
1. **Static Layer** — Holds the pre-built SLAM map (`OccupancyGrid`) as the background. Read-only during runtime. *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Obstacle Layer** — Inserts live obstacles detected by LiDAR / depth camera / sonar into the costmap in real time. *(1.5 marks)*
3. **Inflation Layer** — Adds a conservative safety buffer around obstacles using exponential decay, ensuring the robot never grazes an obstacle. Cells closer to obstacles get higher cost (254 = inscribed, 253 = circumscribed). *(2 marks)*
4. **Voxel Layer / Range Sensor Layer** — Voxel layer tracks 3-D obstacles to determine if a 2-D cell is genuinely clear; range sensor layer handles sonar with cone-shaped sensing. *(2 marks)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Grid Indices $(i, j)$ (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i &= \left\lfloor \frac{k}{W} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{14738}{200} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 73.69 \rfloor = 73 \\
j &= k \bmod W = 14738 - (73 \times 200) = 14738 - 14600 = 138
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) World Coordinates (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_w &= 0.0 + (138 + 0.5) \times 0.2 = 138.5 \times 0.2 = 27.7 \text{ m} \\
y_w &= 0.0 + (73 + 0.5) \times 0.2 = 73.5 \times 0.2 = 14.7 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Cell Classification (2 marks)**

- Cell value = 82 → Probability $P = 82/100 = 0.82$
- Compare: $P = 0.82 > \theta_{occ} = 0.65$ → **Cell is Occupied** ✔
- This satisfies the upper threshold in `map_server`, so on `map_server` startup, this cell will be marked as a lethal obstacle.

**(iv) Log-Odds Update (1 mark)**

$$
L_t(c) = L_{t-1}(c) + L_{meas}(c) = 0.0 + 1.5 = 1.5 \text{ nats}
$$

Convert back to probability:

$$
P_t(c) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{1.5}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + 4.4817} = 1 - 0.1824 = 0.8176 \approx 82\%
$$

**Matches the given value of 82 → Self-consistent. ✔**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q. (a)** Compare 2-D `OccupancyGrid` maps with 3-D `Octomap` representations in ROS. Mention the message type, data structure, probability model, and one suitable application for each. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A ROS map has:
- Resolution = 0.05 m
- Origin = $(-10.0, -10.0, 0.0)$ in the `/map` frame
- Width = 400, Height = 400

A robot's LiDAR reports a free space reading at world coordinates $(2.30, 4.15)$ m.
- (i) Compute the 1-D array index $k$ where this measurement is stored. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) If the cell's *prior* log-odds was 1.0 (occupied-leaning) and the *measurement* log-odds for "free" is $-0.7$, compute the new log-odds and the updated probability. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) Write the new value in the `OccupancyGrid` `data[]` array. **(1 mark)**
- (iv) Mention the exact name of the ROS node and launch file responsible for serving this static map to other nodes. **(1 mark)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) 2-D OccupancyGrid vs. 3-D Octomap (7 marks)**

| Feature | 2-D OccupancyGrid | 3-D Octomap |
|---|---|---|
| **ROS Message Type** | `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` | `octomap_msgs/Octomap` |
| **Data Structure** | Flat 1-D byte array (raster) | Octree (recursive 8-child tree) |
| **Cell Value Range** | $[-1, 100]$ integer | Float log-odds, $[-2.0, 4.5]$ clamped |
| **Probability Model** | Discrete probability thresholds (0.196, 0.65) | Continuous log-odds Bayesian update |
| **Memory Footprint** | $O(W \times H)$ fixed | $O(N \log(1/r))$ adaptive |
| **Update Method** | Replace cell value | Insert/merge octree nodes |
| **Suitable Application** | Indoor mobile robot (TurtleBot) navigating flat floors | Aerial drone in cluttered 3-D environment |
| **Example Package** | `map_server`, `gmapping` | `octomap_server`, `octomap_rviz_plugins` |
| **Visualization** | `map` topic in RViz | `OccupancyGrid` projected in RViz |
| **Resolution** | Uniform, fixed at save | Adaptive, per-node depth |
| **Strength** | Fast, planner-friendly | Volumetric, handles overhangs |
| **Limitation** | Cannot represent multi-floor / overhangs | Higher CPU cost, requires 3-D sensor |

*(7 marks — 1 mark for each significant comparison row, with at least one row dedicated to the application.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Compute 1-D index $k$ (3 marks)**

**Step 1 — Translate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{rel} &= 2.30 - (-10.0) = 12.30 \text{ m} \\
y_{rel} &= 4.15 - (-10.0) = 14.15 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to floats:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
j_{float} &= 12.30 / 0.05 = 246.0 \\
i_{float} &= 14.15 / 0.05 = 283.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Floor (already integers):**

$$
j = 246, \quad i = 283
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $k$:**

$$
k = i \times W + j = 283 \times 400 + 246 = 113\,200 + 246 = 113\,446
$$

**(ii) Log-Odds Update (2 marks)**

$$
L_t(c) = L_{prior} + L_{meas} = 1.0 + (-0.7) = 0.3 \text{ nats}
$$

Convert to probability:

$$
P_t(c) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{0.3}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + 1.3499} = 1 - 0.4256 = 0.5744
$$

**(iii) OccupancyGrid `data[]` value (1 mark)**

$$
V_{cell} = \text{round}(100 \times 0.5744) = 57
$$

This means `data[113446] = 57` (a borderline cell — slightly occupied-leaning).

**(iv) ROS Node (1 mark)**

- **Node executable:** `map_server`
- **Launch file:** `map_server.launch` (from package `map_server`)
- **Command-line invocation:** `rosrun map_server map_server my_map.yaml`

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU Board Pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing `OccupancyGrid` range $[-1, 100]$ with `Costmap` range $[0, 255]$.** Examiners deduct up to 1 mark for this swap. Always state the **message type** before giving the range.
> 2. **Forgetting the +0.5 cell-center offset** when converting grid index to world coordinates. The cell's reported location is its center, not its bottom-left corner.
> 3. **Mixing row-major and column-major indexing.** ROS is **strictly row-major**: $k = i \cdot W + j$. Some textbooks state it as $k = j \cdot H + i$, which is *wrong* for ROS.
> 4. **Skipping the YAML file parameters** when asked to "list map metadata." Examiners expect *at least* 4 of: image, resolution, origin, occupied_thresh, free_thresh, negate.
> 5. **Not clamping the log-odds** when explaining the Bayesian update. Always state the bounds **$L_{min} = -2.0$ and $L_{max} = 3.5$** (or equivalent justification).
> 6. **Wrong frame ID:** The map's frame ID must be `/map`, not `/odom` or `/base_link`. Examiners may mark this as a conceptual error.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cell Value Rule:** An `OccupancyGrid` cell stores **$-1$ (Unknown), 0 (Free), or 1–100 (Probabilistic Occupancy)**. The integer 100 is the maximum, not 255.
- **Two Files, One Map:** Every ROS static map consists of (1) a **YAML metadata file** and (2) a **grayscale image** (PGM preferred, PNG acceptable).
- **Standard YAML Thresholds:** `occupied_thresh = 0.65`, `free_thresh = 0.196`, `negate = 0` (white = free).
- **The Map Server Node:** The single source of truth for serving a pre-built map is the `map_server` node from the package `map_server`.
- **Coordinate Convention:** $(i, j) = (row, col)$, and the **cell-center** is at $(x_0 + (j+0.5)r,\; y_0 + (i+0.5)r)$.
- **Indexing Formula:** $k = i \cdot W + j$ (row-major order, *not* column-major).
- **Bayesian Fusion:** Successive LiDAR scans are fused using log-odds addition — *not* simple probability multiplication — to avoid numerical underflow and to allow clamping.
- **Log-Odds Conversion:** $L = \log(P/(1-P))$; $P = 1 - 1/(1+e^L)$ is the inverse.
- **2-D vs 3-D:** Use `OccupancyGrid` for planar ground robots, `Octomap` for aerial / manipulator / 3-D environments.
- **Costmap Stacking:** `costmap_2d` stacks Static + Obstacle + Inflation (+optional Voxel/Range) layers into a single 0–255 cost grid for planners.
- **The `/map` Frame:** Always world-fixed and gravity-aligned. The robot localizes within this frame using `amcl` or `robot_localization`.
- **SLAM Packages (KTU Hot Topic):** `gmapping` (2-D laser), `cartographer` (2-D/3-D, Google's), `hector_slam` (no odometry needed), `slam_toolbox` (lifetime mapping).
- **Resolution Trade-off:** Smaller $r$ → finer map, higher memory, slower planning. Default indoor: $r = 0.05$ m; outdoor autonomous vehicles: $r = 0.20$ m.
- **Message Topics:** `/map` (OccupancyGrid), `/costmap` (custom), `/global_costmap`, `/local_costmap`, `/move_base/global_plan`, `/move_base/local_plan`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)**

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)** is the computational problem of constructing or updating a map of an unknown environment while simultaneously keeping track of an agent's location within that environment, using onboard sensor measurements (LiDAR, cameras, IMU, odometry) and probabilistic estimation techniques.

In the formal probabilistic framework adopted by KTU 2024 (Probabilistic Robotics, Thrun et al.), SLAM is defined as estimating the joint posterior probability density of the robot pose $x_t$ and the map $m$ given all sensor measurements $z_{1:t}$ and control inputs $u_{1:t}$:

$$p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

This single equation encapsulates the **"chicken-and-egg" paradox** of mobile robotics: to know *where you are* you need a *map*, but to *build a map* you need to know *where you are*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The PERUT525 (ROS) syllabus classifies SLAM as a core module-4 topic under *Perception & Navigation*, with explicit emphasis on ROS implementations: `gmapping`, `hector_slam`, `cartographer`, and `RTAB-Map`. Students must be able to articulate the difference between **filter-based** (online) and **graph-based** (full-SLAM) formulations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Blindfolded Explorer

Imagine you are blindfolded and dropped into an unknown building. You can:
- **Feel** walls with your left hand (range sensor),
- **Count** your footsteps (wheel odometry),
- **Hear** echoes when you clap (sonar/ultrasonic).

**You must draw a floor plan WHILE figuring out which room you are in.** Every step you take is uncertain; every wall you feel could be a new corridor or a hallway you simply forgot. Periodically, you may *recognize* a room you've been in before (loop closure), and your entire mental map must be corrected. **That continuous process of mapping + self-localization, under uncertainty, IS SLAM.**

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU-Standard)

| Metric | Typical Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **ATE (Absolute Trajectory Error)** | < 0.15 m | RMSE of estimated vs. ground-truth pose |
| **RPE (Relative Pose Error)** | < 0.02 m/m | Drift per unit distance traveled |
| **Map Resolution** | **0.05 m / cell** | Standard occupancy-grid resolution |
| **Update Rate** | **10–40 Hz** | For 2D LiDAR-based SLAM |
| **CPU Footprint** | < 2 cores | Embedded deployment target |

> [!NOTE]
> **Localization vs. Mapping vs. SLAM:** *Localization* assumes the map is known. *Mapping* assumes the pose is known. **SLAM solves both jointly** — this joint nature is what makes it computationally harder than either sub-problem in isolation.

### 1.4 The Three Pillars of SLAM

1. **Front-End (Sensor Processing & Data Association):** Ingests raw sensor data, extracts features, matches current observations to prior map entries.
2. **Back-End (State Estimation / Optimization):** Fuses motion + measurement models to produce a globally consistent estimate using techniques such as EKF, particle filters, or pose-graph optimization.
3. **Loop-Closure Detection:** Recognizes previously visited places and triggers global map correction — *the key step that converts odometry drift into a coherent map.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# **2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet**

## 2.1 Probabilistic Formulation — The Bayes Filter for SLAM

SLAM is fundamentally a **recursive Bayesian estimation** problem. The robot maintains a *belief* — a probability distribution over its state. Two operations alternate continuously:

### 2.1.1 The Prediction Step (Motion Update)

When the robot executes control $u_t$, the belief is propagated through the motion model $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$:

$$\overline{bel}(x_t, m) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}, m) \, dx_{t-1}$$

This step **increases uncertainty** because the motion model itself is noisy (wheel slip, encoder quantization error).

### 2.1.2 The Measurement Update (Correction Step)

When a sensor reading $z_t$ arrives, the belief is sharpened using Bayes' rule with the measurement model $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$:

$$bel(x_t, m) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t, m)$$

where $\eta$ is the normalizer:

$$\eta = \left[ \iint p(z_t \mid x_t', m') \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t', m') \, dx_t' \, dm' \right]^{-1}$$

This step **decreases uncertainty** because observations constrain the state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** For the 14-mark questions, students are expected to write these two equations verbatim and explain the role of each. Marks are awarded for correctly identifying which step *increases* vs. *decreases* uncertainty.

### 2.2 The Three Classical SLAM Algorithms

#### 2.2.1 EKF-SLAM (Extended Kalman Filter SLAM)

The earliest mature solution. The state vector stores the robot pose **and every landmark position**:

$$X = [x, y, \theta, l_1^x, l_1^y, l_2^x, l_2^y, \dots, l_N^x, l_N^y]^T$$

This produces a state of size $\dim(X) = 3 + 2N$ and a covariance matrix of size $(3+2N)^2$.

- **Pros:** Mathematically rigorous, optimal under Gaussian-linear assumptions.
- **Cons:** $O(N^2)$ per update, quadratic memory → **does not scale beyond a few hundred landmarks**.

#### 2.2.2 FastSLAM (Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter)

Exploits a key factorization discovered by Murphy (1999) and Montemerlo (2002):

$$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_{1:t} \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \cdot \prod_{n=1}^{N} p(m_n \mid x_{1:t}, z_{1:t})$$

The robot path is sampled with $M$ particles; each landmark is estimated with an independent 2×2 EKF (one per landmark, per particle). Complexity drops to **$O(M \log N)$** — a dramatic improvement.

#### 2.2.3 Graph-SLAM (Pose-Graph Optimization)

Reformulates SLAM as a sparse nonlinear least-squares problem. Nodes = poses; edges = spatial constraints (odometry or loop closure). Solved iteratively with **Gauss-Newton** or **Levenberg-Marquardt**, often via **g2o** or **GTSAM** libraries.

### 2.3 Occupancy Grid Maps (2D LiDAR SLAM)

For ROS-based 2D SLAM, the map is discretized into a grid of cells. Each cell $m_i$ holds the probability of being **occupied**. Updating uses the **inverse sensor model** $p(m_i \mid x_t, z_t)$ and log-odds representation:

$$l_{t,i} = l_{t-1,i} + \underbrace{\log \frac{p(m_i \mid x_t, z_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid x_t, z_t)}}_{\text{inverse sensor model}} - l_0$$

where $l_0$ is the prior log-odds (typically 0). A cell is **free** if $l < 0$, **occupied** if $l > 0$, and **unknown** at $l = 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why log-odds?** It converts the multiplicative Bayes update into a simple *additive* operation — extremely efficient for embedded processors and the standard trick used inside `gmapping`.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Name | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | SLAM Posterior | Definition |
| 2 | $\overline{bel}(x_t, m) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\, bel(x_{t-1}, m)\, dx_{t-1}$ | Prediction Step | EKF, Particle, Graph |
| 3 | $bel(x_t, m) = \eta\, p(z_t \mid x_t, m)\, \overline{bel}(x_t, m)$ | Measurement Update | All SLAM |
| 4 | $\dim(X_{EKF}) = 3 + 2N$ | EKF State Size | EKF-SLAM |
| 5 | $O(N^2)$ | EKF-SLAM Complexity | EKF-SLAM |
| 6 | $O(M \log N)$ | FastSLAM Complexity | FastSLAM |
| 7 | $l_{t,i} = l_{t-1,i} + \text{inv\_sensor}(x_t, z_t) - l_0$ | Log-Odds Update | Occupancy Grid |
| 8 | $\Sigma_{xy} \to 0$ as $N \to \infty$ | Map Convergence Thm. | EKF-SLAM Theory |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Throughout these notes, absolute value bars are written as `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` to keep markdown table syntax intact. Writing `\vert x \vert` in the exam answer sheet is acceptable; do **not** mix raw `\vert` characters with markdown table delimiters.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

SLAM is the cornerstone of **autonomous navigation** in:
- **Warehouse robots** (Amazon Kiva, Fetch Robotics) — use `gmapping` + `amcl` localization.
- **Autonomous vehicles** (Waymo, Cruise) — use LiDAR + visual SLAM (LOAM, LIO-SAM).
- **AR/VR headsets** (Meta Quest, Apple Vision Pro) — use VINS-Mono / ORB-SLAM3.
- **Disaster response UAVs** — use `RTAB-Map` and `hector_slam`.
- **Domestic vacuums** (iRobot, Roborock) — use simplified VSLAM with floor-plane priors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# **3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix**

## 3.1 Derivation: From Bayes' Rule to the SLAM Recursion

We start from the definition of conditional probability and chain rule. Every line is expanded below.

**Step 1 — Begin with the joint posterior using Bayes' theorem:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) 
&= \eta_t \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Bayes' rule states $p(A \mid B) = \frac{p(B \mid A) p(A)}{p(B)}$. Here $A = (x_t, m)$, $B = (z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$, and we condition on the latest measurement $z_t$ explicitly.

**Step 2 — Apply the Markov assumption (current measurement depends only on current state):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(z_t \mid x_t, m, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t, m)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Sensor readings are conditionally independent of past measurements given the current pose and map. This is the **static-world assumption** of classical SLAM.

**Step 3 — Apply the Markov assumption to the prediction term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) 
&= \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot p(x_{t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Marginalize over all possible previous poses; the motion model is Markovian (current pose depends only on previous pose and current control).

**Step 4 — Combine steps 1–3 to obtain the standard SLAM recursion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
bel(x_t, m) 
&= \eta_t \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m) 
   \cdot \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\, bel(x_{t-1}, m) \, dx_{t-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* This is the **complete SLAM Bayes filter** — prediction first (integral), then correction (multiplication by likelihood). The two operations alternate in a tight loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The KTU examiner expects the *integral* to appear in the prediction step and *no integral* in the measurement update. Mixing them up is one of the most common 2-mark deductions.

## 3.2 Derivation: Log-Odds Occupancy Update

Starting from the recursive Bayes update for a single cell:

**Step 1 — Write the cell-occupancy posterior at time $t$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) 
&= \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \cdot p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the odds ratio (more numerically stable than probability):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{odds}(x) = \frac{x}{1 - x}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the log of the odds ratio for *both* sides:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_{t,i} &= \log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute step 1 and split using $\log(ab) = \log a + \log b$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_{t,i} &= \log \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)}{1 - p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)} 
        + \log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})} 
        - \log \frac{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t})}{1 - p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the three terms:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_{t,i} &= \underbrace{\text{inv\_sensor}(x_t, z_t)}_{\text{from current scan}} 
        + l_{t-1,i} - l_0
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The first term is the *inverse sensor model* (what the scan tells us about this cell right now). The second is the *previous log-odds* (accumulated history). The third is the *prior* $l_0$, typically zero.

**Step 6 — Final additive form (no multiplication, no division):**

$$
\boxed{\,l_{t,i} = l_{t-1,i} + \text{inv\_sensor}(x_t, z_t) - l_0\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the exact equation implemented in `gmapping/src/sensor/map.cpp` (OpenSlam era) and replicated in modern ROS 2 ports.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choosing a SLAM Algorithm

| Property | EKF-SLAM | FastSLAM 2.0 | Graph-SLAM | ORB-SLAM3 (Visual) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Estimator** | Extended Kalman Filter | Rao-Blackwellized PF | Nonlinear LSQ | Bundle Adjustment + covisibility |
| **State Space** | $3 + 2N$ | $3 + 2M \cdot N$ | Pose graph nodes | Map points + keyframes |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(N^2)$ | $O(M \log N)$ | $O(C^2)$ for closure | $O(P^{1.5})$ sparse BA |
| **Memory** | $O(N^2)$ | $O(M N)$ | Sparse edges only | Sparse covisibility graph |
| **Scalability** | Poor ($N < 200$) | Good ($N < 10^4$) | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Sensor** | Range + odom | Range + odom | Any constraint | Mono / Stereo / RGB-D |
| **ROS Package** | `openslam_gmapping`* | `openslam_gmapping` | `cartographer`, `karto` | `orb_slam2_ros` |
| **Loop Closure** | Implicit (covariance) | Implicit (per-particle) | Explicit (factor graph) | DBoW2 place recognition |
| **Linearization** | First-order EKF | Particle + EKF | Iterative GN / LM | Second-order BA |

`*` Historical note: `gmapping` is technically a Rao-Blackwellized particle filter, not pure EKF-SLAM.

## 3.4 Convergence Property (Thrun's Theorem)

> **Thrun & Liu (2005) showed that the landmark-map covariance $\Sigma_{lm}$ in EKF-SLAM monotonically decreases as more landmarks are observed**, and that *all* landmark estimates become fully correlated in the limit: $\Sigma_{xy} \to 0$ as $N \to \infty$. This is why a globally consistent map *exists* in the limit.

*Practical implication:* Even though EKF-SLAM does not scale computationally, it gives the foundational intuition that **observations of any landmark help localize all other landmarks** — a beautiful result that motivates graph-based formulations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# **4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics**

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a ROS-Based SLAM Pipeline

This diagram maps the **data flow** between ROS nodes inside a typical 2D LiDAR SLAM stack. Note: physical drawings are not necessary for an algorithmic topic — a *functional architecture flow* is the academically correct schematic.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["SENSOR INPUT LAYER"] --> node2["ROS TOPIC /scan LaserScan"]
    node3["WHEEL ODOMETRY"] --> node4["ROS TOPIC /odom Odometry"]
    node5["IMU OPTIONAL"] --> node6["ROS TOPIC /imu data"]
    node2 --> node7["SLAM NODE gmapping OR cartographer"]
    node4 --> node7
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8["SCAN MATCHING FRONT END"]
    node8 --> node9["ODOMETRY CORRECTION"]
    node9 --> node10["PARTICLE FILTER OR POSE GRAPH"]
    node10 --> node11["LOOP CLOSURE DETECTION"]
    node11 --> node12["MAP BUILDER OCCUPANCY GRID"]
    node12 --> node13["ROS TOPIC /map OccupancyGrid"]
    node10 --> node14["ROS TOPIC /tf ROBOT POSE TRANSFORM"]
    node7 --> node15["RVIZ VISUALIZATION"]
    node13 --> node15
    node14 --> node15
    node7 --> node16["MAP SERVER SAVE / LOAD .pgm .yaml"]
```

*Reading the diagram:* Sensor data enters on the left, the SLAM core node in the middle performs scan matching + filtering + loop closure, and the outputs (map topic, transform, saved files) exit on the right. The double-back arrow at `LOOP CLOSURE DETECTION` reflects that loop closure is the *global correction signal* feeding back into the pose estimator.

## 4.2 Pose-Graph Representation of Graph-SLAM

This is the canonical graph-SLAM data structure. Nodes = robot poses; edges = relative-pose constraints. Loop closures appear as edges connecting non-adjacent nodes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pose1["POSE x0"] --- odom01["ODOM EDGE u1"]
    odom01 --- pose2["POSE x1"]
    pose2 --- odom12["ODOM EDGE u2"]
    odom12 --- pose3["POSE x2"]
    pose3 --- odom23["ODOM EDGE u3"]
    odom23 --- pose4["POSE x3"]
    pose4 --- odom34["ODOM EDGE u4"]
    odom34 --- pose5["POSE x4"]
    pose5 --- odom45["ODOM EDGE u5"]
    odom45 --- pose6["POSE x5"]
    pose1 -. LOOP CLOSURE .-> pose5
    pose2 -. LOOP CLOSURE .-> pose6
    pose3 -. LOOP CLOSURE .-> pose6
```

*Reading the diagram:* Solid edges are sequential odometry (high-confidence, locally consistent). Dashed edges are loop closures (low-confidence a priori but globally crucial). The optimizer (`g2o` or `GTSAM`) finds node positions that minimize the total squared error of all edges simultaneously.

## 4.3 ROS-SLAM Package Comparison Tree

This shows the **practical family tree** of ROS 1 / ROS 2 SLAM packages and their algorithmic lineage.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root["ROS SLAM ECOSYSTEM"]
    root --> filt["FILTER BASED ONLINE"]
    root --> graph["GRAPH BASED FULL SLAM"]
    root --> visual["VISUAL SLAM"]
    filt --> gmap["gmapping Rao Blackwellized PF"]
    filt --> hect["hector_slam Scan Matching EKF"]
    filt --> kf["karto Sparse Pose Adjustment"]
    graph --> carto["cartographer Google 2D and 3D"]
    graph --> slamtoolbox["slam_toolbox Lifelong Mapping"]
    visual --> orb["ORB SLAM2 ORB SLAM3"]
    visual --> rtab["RTAB Map Real Time Appearance Based"]
    visual --> lsd["LSD SLAM Large Scale Direct"]
```

*Reading the diagram:* Three algorithmic philosophies branch from the root. The same physical robot can be plugged into any of these packages by remapping the `/scan` and `/odom` topics — a beautiful demonstration of ROS's modular philosophy.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# **5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap**

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. Define SLAM. State the chicken-and-egg problem it solves. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)** is the computational problem of estimating a robot's pose $x_t$ **and** a map $m$ of an unknown environment from a stream of sensor measurements $z_{1:t}$ and control inputs $u_{1:t}$, expressed probabilistically as $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$. (2 marks)

**Chicken-and-egg problem:** To localize the robot, a map is required. To build a map, the robot's location must be known accurately. SLAM breaks this circular dependency by solving both problems simultaneously through recursive Bayesian estimation — using the current (incomplete, uncertain) estimate of one to refine the other, and iterating. (1 mark)

---

### Q2. What is loop closure detection? Why is it important? [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Loop closure detection** is the process of recognizing that the robot has re-visited a previously mapped location and identifying the correspondence between the current sensor observation and a stored map entry. (1 mark)

**Importance:** (i) It eliminates accumulated odometric drift by adding a strong global constraint. (ii) It produces a topologically and metrically consistent map. (iii) Without loop closure, SLAM degrades to dead-reckoning with linearly growing error. (2 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

### **Question A** (14 Marks)

**(a) Derive the recursive Bayes filter formulation of the SLAM posterior. Clearly separate the prediction and measurement update steps. (7 marks)**

**(b) With the aid of a block diagram, explain the architecture of a ROS-based 2D LiDAR SLAM system using the `gmapping` package. List the key ROS topics and parameters involved. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks

1. **State the posterior** $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ (1 mark).
2. **Apply Bayes' rule** to factor out the latest measurement $z_t$ and introduce normalizer $\eta_t$ (1 mark).
3. **Apply Markov assumption** for sensor: $p(z_t \mid x_t, m, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ (1 mark).
4. **Apply Markov assumption** for motion: marginalize over $x_{t-1}$ via the motion model $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ (2 marks).
5. **Write final recursion** with the integral in prediction and the multiplicative correction in update (1 mark).
6. **Conclude with a one-line interpretation** of how uncertainty grows in prediction and shrinks in update (1 mark).

**Final boxed equation to be written:**

$$
bel(x_t, m) = \eta_t \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\, bel(x_{t-1}, m)\, dx_{t-1}
$$

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks

1. **Overview (1 mark):** `gmapping` is a Rao-Blackwellized particle-filter SLAM; each particle carries its own occupancy-grid map.
2. **Block diagram (3 marks):** Draw the same architecture shown in Section 4.1 — sensor input → scan matcher → particle filter → map builder → `/map` topic and `/tf` transform.
3. **Key ROS topics (2 marks):** Subscribed: `/scan` (sensor_msgs/LaserScan), `/odom` (nav_msgs/Odometry), optional `/imu`. Published: `/map` (nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid), `/map_metadata`, `/entropy` (diagnostic).
4. **Key parameters (1 mark):** `~maxUrange`, `~particles` (default 30 → 100), `~map_update_interval`, `~linearUpdate`, `~angularUpdate`, `~delta` (map resolution = **0.05 m**).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Q.A(b):** Many students write "gmapping uses EKF-SLAM". This is **WRONG**. `gmapping` is a **Rao-Blackwellized particle filter** SLAM. Mixing these up will cost you a full mark on the parameter discussion. Likewise, the resolution is specified in **meters per cell**, not cells per meter — the default is `delta: 0.05`.

---

### **Question B** (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) Compare EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM, and Graph-SLAM in terms of state representation, computational complexity, scalability, and loop-closure handling. Use a comparison table. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the log-odds occupancy grid map update equation. Show how a cell's occupancy probability is updated with each new laser scan. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks

Present the **Analytical Comparison Matrix** from Section 3.3 verbatim in the answer script (4 marks). Add concise textual explanation (3 marks):

- **EKF-SLAM:** Single Gaussian over the joint state $x_t \oplus m$. Elegant but $O(N^2)$ — does not scale beyond ~200 landmarks.
- **FastSLAM:** Decouples pose estimation from landmark estimation; $M$ particles × $N$ independent 2D EKFs, total $O(M \log N)$ via a tree-based landmark data structure.
- **Graph-SLAM:** Stores only pose nodes and constraint edges. Loop closures are added as additional edges; optimization is sparse and uses iterative solvers (Gauss-Newton / Levenberg-Marquardt) over libraries like g2o or GTSAM.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks

1. **Define the cell** $m_i$ and its occupancy probability $p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$ (1 mark).
2. **Apply Bayes' rule** to the cell update (1 mark).
3. **Define the odds ratio** $\text{odds}(x) = x / (1-x)$ and the log-odds $l = \log(\text{odds})$ (1 mark).
4. **Derive the additive form** $l_{t,i} = l_{t-1,i} + \text{inv\_sensor}(x_t, z_t) - l_0$ (3 marks).
5. **Explain the threshold rule** (1 mark): $l < 0$ → free; $l > 0$ → occupied; $l = 0$ → unknown.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Q.B(b):** Students often forget to mention the **inverse sensor model** term explicitly. Without it, the equation is just a tautology and the examiner will deduct 1–2 marks. Also, do not confuse the **log-odds prior** $l_0$ with the **posterior** $l_t$ — they play different roles in the equation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Checklist — KTU PERUT525 / Module 4 / SLAM**

- **SLAM =** joint posterior $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.
- **Two-step recursion:** prediction (integral, uncertainty ↑) + update (multiplication, uncertainty ↓).
- **EKF-SLAM:** $O(N^2)$, single Gaussian, poor scalability.
- **FastSLAM:** $O(M \log N)$, particles × independent landmark EKFs, used in `gmapping`.
- **Graph-SLAM:** pose graph + nonlinear least squares, used in `cartographer` and `slam_toolbox`.
- **Log-odds update:** $l_{t,i} = l_{t-1,i} + \text{inv\_sensor}(x_t, z_t) - l_0$.
- **Loop closure** = global constraint; *the* step that turns drift into a consistent map.
- **ROS packages to know:** `gmapping` (PF), `hector_slam` (scan-match EKF), `cartographer` (graph), `slam_toolbox` (lifelong), `RTAB-Map` (visual), `ORB-SLAM3` (visual).
- **Key ROS topics:** `/scan` (LaserScan), `/odom` (Odometry), `/map` (OccupancyGrid), `/tf` (transforms).
- **Map resolution standard:** **0.05 m/cell** (parameter `~delta` in `gmapping`).
- **Thrun's theorem:** landmark estimates become fully correlated as $N \to \infty$.
- **Visual SLAM sensors:** monocular / stereo / RGB-D cameras; ORB features + Bag-of-Words place recognition (DBoW2).
- **Direct vs. feature-based:** LSD-SLAM is direct (photometric); ORB-SLAM is feature-based.
- **Examination mantra:** *If asked to derive, show all four lines of the Bayes recursion. If asked to compare, use a table. If asked to configure, list the ROS topics, the message types, and the resolution parameter.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: rosbag and its Functionalities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT525 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** rosbag and its Functionalities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — rosbag and its Functionalities

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**rosbag** is the official ROS (Robot Operating System) command-line utility suite designed for **recording, replaying, inspecting, manipulating, and migrating** ROS message streams published on the ROS topic-based communication backbone. Internally, a *bag file* (typically `.bag`) is a **binary structured file** (a serialized BAG format based on the ROS message serialization protocol) that stores timestamped messages along with their topic names, message types, connection headers, and metadata required for lossless reconstruction of the original ROS topic graph at the time of recording.

Formally, rosbag enables:
- **Time-synchronized capture** of asynchronous, multi-topic message traffic
- **Deterministic replay** for debugging, testing, and regression analysis
- **Offline data analytics** for SLAM, perception, and control pipelines

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** rosbag is the de-facto ROS data archival and reproducibility tool. Mastery of `record`, `play`, `info`, `compress`, `decompress`, `filter`, and `fix` subcommands is **mandatory** for Module 4 examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **flight data recorder (Black Box)** of an aircraft. The black box:
1. **Records** every sensor reading (altitude, speed, pilot commands) during the flight.
2. **Stores** the data in a tamper-proof, timestamped log.
3. **Allows playback** later on the ground for investigation, training, or accident reconstruction.

rosbag does the **exact same job** for a robot:

| Black Box Analogy | rosbag Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Sensors on the aircraft | ROS Nodes publishing on topics |
| Continuous flight recording | `rosbag record` capturing messages |
| Black Box storage unit | `.bag` file (binary log) |
| Crash investigators replaying | `rosbag play` reproducing the recorded scenario |
| Investigator's report metadata | `rosbag info` summary |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** While the robot is running, you can "tap into" every topic without modifying any node — rosbag works as a *passive eavesdropper* on the ROS communication graph. This non-intrusive design is what makes it invaluable for production robotics.

## 1.3 The `.bag` File Format — Conceptual View

A rosbag `.bag` file is conceptually organized as:

- **Version Header** — File format version (e.g., `version: 2.0`)
- **Chunk Records** — Contiguous blocks of compressed/uncompressed message data
- **Connection Records** — Mapping between connection IDs and (topic, message type, MD5)
- **Message Records** — Actual serialized messages with timestamps
- **Index Records** — Optional time-offset index for fast random-access seeking

> [!TIP]
> The default storage mode is `bag` (uncompressed). For large-scale datasets (multi-GB), ROS supports `compressed` mode where individual chunks are zlib-compressed on the fly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Core rosbag Subcommands — Functional Breakdown

### 2.1.1 `rosbag record` — Message Acquisition

Captures messages from one or more topics into a bag file.

**Logical operation:**

$$
\text{Topic}_i(t) \xrightarrow{\text{Subscribe}} \text{Recorder} \xrightarrow{\text{Serialize + Timestamp}} \text{Bag File}
$$

**Key options:**
- `-a` / `--all` : Record **ALL** topics currently advertised
- `-O <name>` : Specify output bag filename
- `-o <prefix>` : Use prefix; suffix is timestamp automatically
- `--split` / `--max-splits` : Split recording into multiple files of fixed size/duration
- `-j` : Enable compressed mode (compressed chunks)
- `--duration=<seconds>` : Stop recording after specified duration
- `-x <regex>` : Exclude topics matching regex pattern

### 2.1.2 `rosbag play` — Deterministic Replay

Reproduces the recorded message stream onto the live ROS topic graph.

**Logical operation:**

$$
\text{Bag File} \xrightarrow{\text{Deserialize}} \text{Publisher Nodes} \xrightarrow{\text{Advertise Topics}} \text{Subscribers}
$$

**Key options:**
- `-r <factor>` : Play at speed multiplier (e.g., `-r 2.0` = 2× speed)
- `-l` / `--loop` : Loop playback indefinitely
- `--start <sec>` / `--end <sec>` : Trim playback to time window
- `--topics <list>` : Play only specified topics
- `--pause` : Start in paused state (step-through debugging)

### 2.1.3 `rosbag info` — Metadata Extraction

Prints YAML-style summary: path, version, duration, start/end times, message count, compression, topics list with types and frequencies.

### 2.1.4 `rosbag compress` / `rosbag decompress`

In-place conversion between **uncompressed** and **compressed** (zlib) chunk storage. Useful for storage-constrained environments (edge robots, embedded systems).

### 2.1.5 `rosbag filter`

Filters messages based on a Python expression, writing the matching subset to a new bag. Common use cases:
- Strip noisy sensor data
- Extract specific time windows
- Remove privacy-sensitive fields (e.g., camera images)

### 2.1.6 `rosbag fix`

Migrates a bag file whose message definitions have changed in the current ROS environment. Re-records bag content against current message-type definitions, fixing connection mismatches.

### 2.1.7 `rosbag reindex`

Rebuilds the internal index for a bag file whose index was corrupted or stripped, restoring fast random-access seek.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Property Sheet

| Property / Command | Behavior | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| `record -a` | Records all topics | Full system capture |
| `record -O demo.bag` | Records into named file | Predictable filename |
| `play -r 0.5` | Half-speed playback | Slow-motion debugging |
| `play --clock` | Publishes `/clock` for sim time | Synchronized replay with simulation |
| `info demo.bag` | Outputs YAML summary | Quick metadata check |
| `compress` | Reduces size via zlib | Storage-constrained transfer |
| `filter expr` | Python-based message filter | Data curation |
| `fix` | Repairs outdated message types | Cross-ROS-distro replay |

**Storage Relationship:**

$$
S_{\text{compressed}} \approx S_{\text{raw}} \times (1 - \rho_{\text{zlib}})
$$

where typical $\rho_{\text{zlib}}$ for sensor data is **30%–60%** for image topics and **60%–85%** for IMU/odometry (highly compressible).

> [!NOTE]
> **Replay Fidelity Principle:** rosbag replay preserves the **original inter-message timing** by default. Without `-r`, playback occurs at the recorded wall-clock rate, enabling time-aware algorithms (e.g., EKF, MPC) to behave identically to live operation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Operational Derivations & Analytical Workflow

## 3.1 Derivation of the Recording Workflow

Let us derive the canonical rosbag recording session step-by-step, expressing every transitional state.

**Step 1 — Topic Discovery Phase**

$$
\text{roscore (Master)} \xleftrightarrow{\text{XMLRPC}} \text{Recorder Node}
$$

The recorder queries the ROS Master via **XMLRPC** to obtain the currently advertised topics list:

$$
\mathcal{T} = \{T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n\}
$$

For each selected topic $T_i$, the Master returns the publishing node's URI.

**Step 2 — Subscription Establishment**

For each $T_i \in \mathcal{T}_{\text{selected}}$, the recorder requests a **TCP/IP** subscriber handshake:

$$
\text{Recorder} \xrightarrow{\text{TCPROS handshake}} \text{Publisher of } T_i
$$

After successful handshake, a **persistent TCP connection** is established for streaming serialized message data.

**Step 3 — Message Reception Loop**

Each incoming message $m_{i,k}$ on topic $T_i$ at time $\tau_{i,k}$ is processed:

$$
(m_{i,k}, T_i, \tau_{i,k}, \text{msg\_type}_i, \text{connection\_id}_i) \longrightarrow \text{In-memory chunk buffer}
$$

The chunk buffer flushes to disk when:
- Chunk size threshold exceeded (default **~256 KB**), OR
- Recording session ends (Ctrl-C / timeout).

**Step 4 — Index Finalization**

On session termination, the recorder writes the **trailer block** containing chunk offsets and time-bound metadata, enabling `play` and `info` to function.

## 3.2 Derivation of Replay Timing

The replay engine restores timing based on two fields: `time` (record time) and `header.stamp` (message-internal timestamp).

**Header stamp preservation:**

$$
\text{Header.stamp}_{\text{replayed}} = \text{Header.stamp}_{\text{recorded}}
$$

This dual-timestamp model is critical for **ROS Time abstraction**:

$$
t_{\text{replay}} - t_{\text{record}} = \Delta t_{\text{wall-clock offset}}
$$

When replaying with `--clock`:

$$
\text{Recorder publishes } t_{\text{replay}} \text{ on } /clock
$$

Subscribers configured with `use_sim_time:=true` then consume:

$$
t_{\text{now}}^{\text{(sim)}} \leftarrow t_{\text{replay}}
$$

**Step-by-step playback loop (algorithmic derivation):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Input: } \text{Bag file } B \text{ with sorted records } \{r_1, r_2, \dots, r_N\} \\
&\textbf{Output: } \text{Replayed topic stream} \\
&\text{1. Load chunk index } \mathcal{I}_B \text{ via seek-table} \\
&\text{2. Set } t_{\text{prev}} \leftarrow r_1.\tau \\
&\text{3. For each } r_k \text{ in chronological order:} \\
&\quad \text{4. } \Delta \tau \leftarrow r_k.\tau - t_{\text{prev}} \\
&\quad \text{5. Sleep } \Delta \tau \text{ (scaled by } 1/r \text{ if } -r \text{ used)} \\
&\quad \text{6. Publish } r_k.\text{msg} \text{ on topic } r_k.T \text{ with preserved header stamp} \\
&\quad \text{7. } t_{\text{prev}} \leftarrow r_k.\tau \\
&\text{8. End (or loop if } -l \text{ specified)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Compression Trade-Off Matrix

| Data Modality | Typical Raw Rate | Compressed Rate | Space Savings | CPU Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Camera (RGB 640×480 @ 30 Hz) | ~27 MB/s | ~10–15 MB/s | ~50% | Low |
| LiDAR PointCloud | ~20 MB/s | ~12–18 MB/s | ~10–40% | Moderate |
| IMU / Odometry | ~50 KB/s | ~5–10 KB/s | ~80% | Negligible |
| TF Transforms | ~10 KB/s | ~1–2 KB/s | ~85% | Negligible |

**Engineering decision rule:**

$$
\text{If } C_{\text{disk}} < C_{\text{cpu}} \cdot \rho_{\text{saving}} \cdot t_{\text{mission}} \Rightarrow \text{use compression}
$$

where $C_{\text{disk}}$ is the marginal cost of storage and $C_{\text{cpu}}$ is the marginal CPU cost of zlib compression.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Valuation Insight:** When asked to justify compression, frame your answer around the **trade-off between CPU cycles and storage footprint**. Examiners reward students who articulate the cost asymmetry rather than just stating "compression saves space."

## 3.4 Filtering — Analytical Expression

A filter takes the form:

$$
\mathcal{F}(m_k, T_k, \tau_k) = \begin{cases} \text{retain} & \text{if } \varphi(m_k, T_k, \tau_k) = \text{True} \\ \text{discard} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

where $\varphi$ is a Python expression evaluated per message. Example: retain only messages whose topic starts with `/sensors/lidar/`:

$$
\varphi(m, T, \tau) := T.\text{startswith}(\text{'/sensors/lidar/'})
$$

The filtered stream is then re-bagged:

$$
B_{\text{out}} = \{r_k \in B \mid \varphi(m_k, T_k, \tau_k) = \text{True}\}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ROS Topic Graph with rosbag Recorder (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Camera Node] -->|sensor_msgs/Image| topicCam[/camera/image_raw/]
    node2[LiDAR Node] -->|sensor_msgs/PointCloud2| topicLid[/scan/]
    node3[IMU Node] -->|sensor_msgs/Imu| topicImu[/imu/data/]
    node4[Robot State Publisher] -->|tf2_msgs/TFMessage| topicTf[/tf/]

    topicCam --> nodeRec[rosbag Recorder]
    topicLid --> nodeRec
    topicImu --> nodeRec
    topicTf --> nodeRec

    nodeRec -->|serialize + chunk| nodeFile[(.bag file on disk)]

    style nodeRec fill:#FFD966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeFile fill:#A4C2F4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Replay Architecture — Playback into Live Graph

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeBag[(Recorded .bag file)] --> nodeReader[Bag Reader]
    nodeReader -->|deserialize| nodeSched[Time Sequencer]
    nodeSched -->|publish /cmd_vel| nodePub1[Republished Cmd_Vel]
    nodeSched -->|publish /scan| nodePub2[Republished Scan]
    nodeSched -->|publish /tf| nodePub3[Republished TF]
    nodeSched -->|publish /clock| nodeClock[Clock Publisher]

    nodePub1 --> nodeSub1[Robot Controller Node]
    nodePub2 --> nodeSub2[SLAM Node]
    nodePub3 --> nodeSub3[Localization Node]
    nodeClock --> nodeSub1
    nodeClock --> nodeSub2

    style nodeSched fill:#B6D7A8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeClock fill:#EA9999,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 End-to-End rosbag Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Live Robot Session] -->|rosbag record -a| nodeB[.bag Archive]
    nodeB -->|rosbag info| nodeC[Metadata Inspection]
    nodeB -->|rosbag compress| nodeD[Compressed Bag]
    nodeB -->|rosbag filter| nodeE[Filtered Bag]
    nodeB -->|rosbag fix| nodeF[Migrated Bag]
    nodeB -->|rosbag play -r 1.0| nodeG[Replay Session]
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG -->|rosbag play --clock| nodeH[Simulation Sync]

    style nodeB fill:#F9CB9C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeG fill:#C9DAF8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 rosbag Subcommand Functional Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Acquire
        nodeRecCmd[record]
    end
    subgraph Consume
        nodePlayCmd[play]
        nodeInfoCmd[info]
    end
    subgraph Transform
        nodeCompressCmd[compress]
        nodeDecompCmd[decompress]
        nodeFilterCmd[filter]
        nodeFixCmd[fix]
        nodeReindexCmd[reindex]
    end

    nodeRecCmd --> nodeFile[(.bag)]
    nodeFile --> nodePlayCmd
    nodeFile --> nodeInfoCmd
    nodeFile --> nodeCompressCmd
    nodeFile --> nodeDecompCmd
    nodeFile --> nodeFilterCmd
    nodeFile --> nodeFixCmd
    nodeFile --> nodeReindexCmd

    style nodeFile fill:#FFE599,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define rosbag. List any FOUR subcommands provided by the rosbag command-line tool.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** rosbag is a set of command-line tools for **recording** and **playing back** ROS topics. It allows capturing message data published on any ROS topic into a binary `.bag` file and reproducing the same topic stream later for offline analysis, debugging, or simulation.

**Four Subcommands (2 marks — ½ each):**
1. `record` — Records messages from specified topics into a bag file.
2. `play` — Replays recorded messages onto the live ROS graph.
3. `info` — Displays metadata such as duration, message count, and topic list of a bag file.
4. `compress` — Converts an uncompressed bag to compressed format to reduce size.

*(Acceptable alternatives: `decompress`, `filter`, `fix`, `reindex`)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the difference between `rosbag play` and `rosbag record`? Mention the role of the `--clock` option.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `record` | `play` |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Captures live topic messages → writes to `.bag` | Reads `.bag` → republishes on topics |
| Operation | Subscribes to topics | Advertises topics and publishes |
| Use case | Data acquisition in the field | Offline replay, regression testing |

**`--clock` option (1 mark):** When specified during `play`, rosbag publishes the recorded timestamp on the `/clock` topic. Nodes configured with `use_sim_time:=true` then synchronize their internal clocks with the replayed time, enabling deterministic simulation-style replay.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of the rosbag recording pipeline. Discuss the role of the ROS Master, XMLRPC handshake, and TCPROS subscriber connections in the recording process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the rosbag playback workflow. Explain how timing is preserved during replay and the significance of `header.stamp` preservation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Architecture of Recording Pipeline (7 marks)

**1. ROS Master Lookup (1 mark)**
When `rosbag record` is invoked, the recorder node registers with the ROS Master and requests the list of currently advertised topics along with their publishing node URIs via **XMLRPC** (an HTTP-based RPC protocol used by the Master).

**2. Topic Filtering (1 mark)**
The user-supplied topic list (or `-a` flag for all topics) is intersected with the Master's topic registry to form the **subscription set** $\mathcal{T}_{\text{sub}}$.

**3. TCPROS Subscriber Handshake (2 marks)**
For each topic in $\mathcal{T}_{\text{sub}}$, the recorder:
- Connects to the publisher's URI over TCP.
- Sends a **TCPROS connection header** declaring the desired topic and message type.
- Receives a connection response confirming the topic, type, and MD5 sum.
- On success, the publisher begins streaming serialized messages over the established TCP socket.

**4. Message Serialization & Chunking (2 marks)**
Each received message is wrapped with:
- A monotonic timestamp $\tau$ (from the recorder's clock at reception)
- The connection ID (mapping to topic + type)
- A chunk header every $\sim 256$ KB of accumulated data

These chunks are flushed to disk sequentially.

**5. Trailer & Index (1 mark)**
On termination (Ctrl-C or duration expiry), the recorder writes the **chunk index** and **file footer**, allowing `info`, `play`, and `reindex` to function.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Playback Workflow (7 marks)

**1. Bag File Loading (1 mark)**
The player reads the bag header, identifies chunk boundaries, and loads the connection records mapping connection IDs → (topic, message type, MD5).

**2. Topic Advertisement (1 mark)**
For each topic in the bag, the player **advertises** that topic on the ROS Master and opens a publisher endpoint, just like any normal ROS node.

**3. Chronological Sequencing (2 marks)**
The player iterates records in **timestamp order**. Before publishing each message $m_k$, it computes:

$$
\Delta \tau = \tau_k - \tau_{k-1}
$$

and sleeps for $\Delta \tau$ (scaled by $1/r$ if `-r` is specified).

**4. Header.Stamp Preservation (2 marks)**
Crucially, the message's internal `header.stamp` is **NOT overwritten** with replay wall-clock time. It retains the **original recorded timestamp**, allowing algorithms that consume `header.stamp` (e.g., TF, sensor fusion) to behave identically to the live session.

**5. /clock Publication (1 mark)**
With `--clock`, the player publishes $\tau_k$ on `/clock`, allowing `use_sim_time:=true` nodes to advance their internal time consistently with the replayed data.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:**
> Students frequently write that `rosbag play` "changes the system time" — **incorrect**. rosbag only publishes synthetic time on `/clock` if `--clock` is supplied, AND only subscribed nodes with `use_sim_time:=true` honor it. The wall-clock of the host machine is unaffected. Failing to mention this nuance typically costs **1–2 marks**.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the rosbag commands `compress`, `decompress`, `filter`, and `fix`. For each, explain its purpose, typical use case, and any trade-offs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how rosbag replay integrates with simulation frameworks like Gazebo. Describe the role of `/clock` and `use_sim_time` parameter in achieving time-synchronized replay. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Compression, Filtering, Migration (7 marks)

**1. `rosbag compress` (1.5 marks)**
- **Purpose:** Converts uncompressed chunks into zlib-compressed chunks in-place or to a new file.
- **Use Case:** Reducing bag size for transmission or archival.
- **Trade-off:** CPU overhead during recording/replay vs disk savings. Image topics compress less efficiently than scalar streams.

**2. `rosbag decompress` (1.5 marks)**
- **Purpose:** Reverses compression, restoring raw chunk storage.
- **Use Case:** Compatibility with tools or ROS versions that don't support compressed bags.

**3. `rosbag filter` (2 marks)**
- **Purpose:** Applies a Python expression $\varphi(m, T, \tau)$ per message; retains only those satisfying the predicate.
- **Use Case:** Removing noisy or privacy-sensitive topics, extracting time windows, downsampling high-rate topics.
- **Trade-off:** Requires Python expertise; expression errors can silently drop data.

**4. `rosbag fix` (2 marks)**
- **Purpose:** Migrates bag files when message definitions have evolved between ROS distributions.
- **Use Case:** Replaying a bag recorded under `ROS Melodic` against a system running `ROS Noetic` after message types changed.
- **Trade-off:** Requires the original `.msg` files to still be available; irreversible changes must be made to a copy, never the original.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Integration with Gazebo (7 marks)

**1. Background (1 mark)**
Gazebo and rosbag replay operate on the **same ROS topic graph**, making them inherently compatible. A recorded bag can supply Gazebo-equivalent sensor stimuli to a downstream algorithm (e.g., a perception stack) running in lieu of the actual simulator.

**2. The Time Problem (2 marks)**
During live operation, the robot's wall-clock drives `ros::Time::now()`. During replay, the **recorded time** is semantically what matters — not the host machine's clock. Without coordination, downstream nodes would see a temporal mismatch.

**3. `/clock` and `use_sim_time` Mechanism (3 marks)**
- Setting `use_sim_time:=true` on a node causes `ros::Time::now()` to return the value published on `/clock`.
- During `rosbag play --clock`, the player publishes each message's recorded timestamp on `/clock`.
- Result: every node in the graph sees a **coherent simulated timeline** advancing in lockstep with the replayed messages.

**4. Practical Workflow (1 mark)**

$$
\underbrace{\text{rosbag play --clock demo.bag}}_{\text{time source}} \longrightarrow \underbrace{\text{perception\_node\_with\_use\_sim\_time}}_{\text{consumer}} \longrightarrow \text{outputs logged to new bag}
$$

This pattern is the foundation of **reproducible robotics experiments** — the same `.bag` always produces the same algorithmic output.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:**
> A frequent error is stating that `rosbag play --clock` "overrides the system clock." It does **not** touch wall-clock time; it only publishes on `/clock` and requires consumers to opt-in via `use_sim_time:=true`. Mark this distinction explicitly to score full marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **rosbag** = ROS's official recording, playback, and manipulation toolset.
- **`.bag` file** = binary archive of timestamped, typed ROS messages with optional zlib compression.
- **Core subcommands:** `record`, `play`, `info`, `compress`, `decompress`, `filter`, `fix`, `reindex`.
- **`record -a`** captures all topics; **`-O`** sets a fixed output name; **`-o`** adds a timestamped suffix.
- **`play -r`** adjusts replay speed multiplier; **`--clock`** publishes synthetic time on `/clock`.
- **`header.stamp`** is **preserved verbatim** during replay — critical for time-aware algorithms.
- **`use_sim_time:=true`** makes a node consume `/clock` instead of wall-clock time, enabling simulation-synchronized replay.
- **Compression trade-off:** save disk space at the cost of CPU cycles; zlib gains ~50–85% on scalar streams, less on raw images.
- **`filter`** uses Python expressions per-message — useful for data curation but error-prone if expressions are buggy.
- **`fix`** migrates bags across ROS message-definition versions — requires original `.msg` sources.
- **Reproducibility principle:** rosbag is the cornerstone of deterministic robotics experimentation — the same bag always yields the same algorithmic behavior when replayed correctly.
- **Time abstraction:** rosbag operates on the ROS `Time` and `Duration` types; never confuse `/clock` time with host wall-clock time.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When a question mentions "offline debugging" or "reproducibility," your first answer should always invoke rosbag. When it mentions "time synchronization" or "simulation replay," always mention `--clock` together with `use_sim_time`. These two phrases are **high-frequency examiner triggers**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recording and Playing Back Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT525 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Recording and Playing Back Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recording and Playing Back Data in ROS** refers to the process of capturing live message streams published on ROS topics into a persistent file (a **bag file** with `.bag` extension) using the **`rosbag`** toolset, and subsequently republishing those exact messages onto the live ROS network at a later time for purposes such as **reproducible debugging, algorithm benchmarking, regression testing, and offline data analysis**.

In the official ROS architecture, this functionality is delivered by the package **`rosbag`** (also called the *Bag Utility*). A **bag file** is a binary log file that stores serialized ROS messages along with their associated **topic names, message types, timestamps, and connection headers**, allowing perfect reconstruction of the original topic graph at playback time.

The core sub-tools that constitute the recording and playback ecosystem are:

| Tool | Function |
|---|---|
| `rosbag record` | Subscribes to topics and writes messages to a `.bag` file |
| `rosbag play` | Reads a `.bag` file and republishes messages on the live network |
| `rosbag info` | Displays metadata, topic list, message count, and duration of a bag file |
| `rosbag compress` | Recompresses a bag file to a different compression format |
| `rosbag check` | Verifies that a bag file is not corrupted |

The conceptual equivalence in ROS 2 (relevant to the KTU 2024 scheme's forward-looking syllabus) is the `ros2 bag` command-line tool, which performs analogous recording and playback operations using the **MCAP** and **SQLite3** storage formats.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Black Box" of a Robot**
> Think of `rosbag` as the **black box flight recorder** of an aeroplane — but instead of recording cockpit voice and flight parameters, it records everything the robot "hears, sees, and decides" through its ROS topics.

Imagine a self-driving car going through a busy intersection. Hundreds of sensors are firing every millisecond — LIDAR scans, camera frames, GPS fixes, steering commands, and velocity readings. Reproducing this exact traffic scenario in the lab is impossible (a real pedestrian will not stand in the same place twice). So we **record** the entire sensor and command stream into a bag file. Later, back in the lab, we **play back** that file, and the robot's algorithms behave as if the car were really driving through that intersection — but now it is a controlled, repeatable, and analysable environment.

**Another simple analogy:** A bag file is like a **movie recording** of a live concert. You cannot pause the original concert to study the drummer's hand movement, but you can play back the recording frame-by-frame, in slow motion, or even skip to a specific timestamp. `rosbag` gives you the same ability over robot data streams.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics

- **Storage Format:** `.bag` (binary, optionally BZ2-compressed)
- **Default Sample Rate:** Records at the **publish rate of the topic** (no fixed sampling — it is event-driven)
- **Standard Time Format:** UNIX epoch time in **nanoseconds** (as per the `rospy.Time` standard)
- **Maximum Practical Recording Duration:** Limited by disk I/O bandwidth; typical sustained write rates of **20–200 MB/s** on SSD
- **Default Bag Split Size:** **1024 MB** (1 GB) per file, configurable via `-b` flag
- **ROS 2 Native Format:** **MCAP** (preferred) and **SQLite3** (legacy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PERUT525 Module 4):** Students are expected to understand bag recording, selective topic recording, playback control (rate adjustment, looping, pause), and inspection of bag metadata. Practical lab sessions typically involve recording a `/turtle` simulation session and replaying it to verify deterministic behaviour.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The recording and playback pipeline can be decomposed into **five logical stages**:

### Stage 1 — Topic Discovery and Subscription
When the user invokes `rosbag record /topic1 /topic2`, the tool:
1. Contacts the **ROS Master** (ROS 1) / **DDS Discovery** (ROS 2) to find publishers of the requested topics.
2. Establishes a **subscriber connection** for each topic with a queue size (default **100 messages**) to absorb bursts.
3. Reads the message type definition from the master's parameter server (ROS 1) or via topic type introspection (ROS 2).

### Stage 2 — Message Serialization and Time-stamping
Every received message is wrapped in a **bag record** containing:
- **Connection header** (topic name, MD5 sum, message definition, caller ID)
- **Message header** (sequence number, timestamp from `ros::Time::now()`)
- **Serialized message payload** (using the standard ROS binary serialization)

> The timestamp used is the **header.stamp** of the message if available, otherwise the **receive time** at the recorder.

### Stage 3 — Persistent Storage
- Data is buffered in memory and flushed to disk using **mmap()** for low-latency writes.
- File rotation occurs at the split size threshold (default **1024 MiB**), appending a numeric suffix (`_0`, `_1`, …).
- Compression is applied at the chunk level using **BZ2** by default.

### Stage 4 — File Structure (On-Disk Layout)
A bag file is organized as:
- **File header record** (version, size)
- One **connection record** per unique (topic, type) pair
- **Chunk records** containing time-ordered serialized messages
- **Index records** (optional, generated by `rosbag reindex`) for fast seeking

### Stage 5 — Playback Reconstruction
During `rosbag play`:
1. The tool opens the bag file, rebuilds the connection map, and advertises each topic on the live ROS network as a **publisher**.
2. It then sequentially replays messages in timestamp order, publishing each one through the standard ROS transport layer.
3. **Clock simulation:** By default, `rosbag play` publishes a new time to the `/clock` topic (with `--clock` flag in ROS 1) so that time-dependent nodes (`use_sim_time := true`) experience the original temporal sequence.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Command Cheat Sheet

| Command | Purpose | Key Flags |
|---|---|---|
| `rosbag record -a` | Record ALL topics currently advertised | `-a` all topics |
| `rosbag record /topic1 /topic2` | Record specific topics | `-O name.bag` output name |
| `rosbag record -O session /lidar /cmd_vel` | Custom filename `session.bag` | `-O` prefix override |
| `rosbag play data.bag` | Replay a bag file at original speed | `-r 2.0` 2x speed |
| `rosbag play -l data.bag` | Loop playback indefinitely | `-l` loop |
| `rosbag play -s 30 data.bag` | Start from 30 seconds in | `-s` start time (sec) |
| `rosbag play -u 10 data.bag` | Play for 10 seconds only | `-u` duration (sec) |
| `rosbag info data.bag` | Print metadata summary | `--freq` topic frequency |
| `rosbag compress data.bag` | Compress to `.bag.bz2` | `--bz2` or `--lz4` |
| `rosbag filter input.bag out.bag "topic == '/cmd_vel'"` | Filter messages by Python expression | `filter` subcommand |

## 2.3 The "Why" — Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Deterministic Regression Testing:** Reproduce a field-failure scenario byte-for-byte in CI/CD pipelines.
- **Algorithm Benchmarking:** Compare SLAM or perception algorithms on identical input data.
- **Dataset Curation:** Build large-scale autonomous driving datasets (e.g., KITTI, nuScenes rely on similar logged streams).
- **Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) Testing:** Replay real sensor data into a simulated or modified robot.
- **Forensic Debugging:** When a robot misbehaves in the field, the bag file is the audit trail.

## 2.4 Underlying Engineering Principles

1. **Publisher-Subscriber Decoupling** — Recording is transparent; nodes do not know they are being recorded. This is enabled by ROS's **anonymous publish/subscribe** middleware.
2. **Lossless Serialization** — Messages are stored using the same MD5-summed binary format used for transport, guaranteeing zero information loss.
3. **Time Fidelity** — The original publish-time stamps are preserved so that algorithms depending on temporal causality (e.g., control loops) behave identically.
4. **Asynchronous I/O** — The recorder uses a dedicated write thread to avoid blocking the subscription callback queue.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Required Disk Storage for a Bag Recording

For any topic being recorded, the storage requirement can be analytically estimated before a long field deployment. Let us derive the closed-form expression.

### 3.1.1 Define the Variables

Let:
