- $T$ = total recording duration in **seconds**
- $N_t$ = number of topics being recorded
- $f_i$ = publish rate of topic $i$ in **Hz** (messages per second)
- $S_i$ = average serialized size of one message on topic $i$ in **bytes**
- $C$ = compression ratio (e.g., **1.0** for uncompressed, **0.4** for BZ2)

### 3.1.2 Total Number of Messages

The total number of messages recorded across all topics is the sum of the message counts on each topic:

$$
M = \sum_{i=1}^{N_t} f_i \cdot T
$$

### 3.1.3 Raw (Uncompressed) Storage

Each message contributes its serialized payload plus a fixed bag overhead of **12 bytes** per record (sequence + time + connection ID). The raw storage requirement is therefore:

$$
R_{\text{raw}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_t} f_i \cdot T \cdot \left(S_i + 12\right) \;\; \text{[bytes]}
$$

### 3.1.4 Effective Storage After Compression

Applying the compression ratio:

$$
R_{\text{effective}} = C \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N_t} f_i \cdot T \cdot \left(S_i + 12\right) \;\; \text{[bytes]}
$$

Converting to gigabytes:

$$
R_{\text{effective,GB}} = \frac{C}{2^{30}} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N_t} f_i \cdot T \cdot \left(S_i + 12\right)
$$

### 3.1.5 Numerical Worked Example

Suppose we are recording a typical mobile robot deployment:
- Topic 1: `/camera/image_raw` — $f_1 = 30$ Hz, $S_1 = 460{,}800$ bytes (640×480 RGB)
- Topic 2: `/scan` (LIDAR) — $f_2 = 40$ Hz, $S_2 = 5{,}760$ bytes
- Topic 3: `/cmd_vel` — $f_3 = 50$ Hz, $S_3 = 24$ bytes
- Duration: $T = 3600$ s (1 hour)
- Compression: $C = 0.5$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{raw}} &= 30 \cdot 3600 \cdot (460800 + 12) \\
&\quad + 40 \cdot 3600 \cdot (5760 + 12) \\
&\quad + 50 \cdot 3600 \cdot (24 + 12) \\[4pt]
&= 108000 \cdot 460812 \\
&\quad + 144000 \cdot 5772 \\
&\quad + 180000 \cdot 36 \\[4pt]
&= 49{,}767{,}696{,}000 \\
&\quad + 831{,}168{,}000 \\
&\quad + 6{,}480{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= 50{,}605{,}344{,}000 \;\; \text{bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
R_{\text{effective,GB}} = \frac{0.5 \times 50{,}605{,}344{,}000}{1{,}073{,}741{,}824} \approx 23.57 \;\; \text{GB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This calculation shows that a single hour of multi-modal robot logging can easily exceed **20 GB**, justifying the use of SSD storage, compression, and topic filtering.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Recording Strategy Decision Tree

| Scenario | Recommended Command | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Full debug capture of a Turtlebot3 sim | `rosbag record -a -O debug` | Captures all topics, single file output |
| Long autonomous drive (multi-hour) | `rosbag record -a -b 4096 --split --duration=1h` | Splits into 4 GB chunks; survives crashes |
| Targeted perception algorithm test | `rosbag record /camera/image_raw /scan -O perception_test` | Saves disk; excludes control chatter |
| Replay for CI pipeline | `rosbag play -r 1.0 -u 30 input.bag` | Deterministic 30-second replay |
| Loop a scenario for stress testing | `rosbag play -l -r 2.0 input.bag` | Continuous 2x speed loop |
| Subsample for ML dataset | `rosbag filter input.bag out.bag "t.to_sec() % 5 == 0"` | Keep every 5-second-aligned message |

## 3.3 Playback Timing Derivation

When playing back, the time difference between two consecutive published messages on topic $i$ at original rate is:

$$
\Delta t_{\text{play}} = \frac{1}{r \cdot f_i}
$$

where $r$ is the playback rate multiplier (default $r = 1.0$). If $r > 1$, the system replays faster than real-time; if $r < 1$, it replays in slow motion. If $r \cdot f_i$ would exceed the publisher's maximum throughput, the playback thread **blocks** rather than dropping messages, preserving temporal fidelity.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Recording Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["ROS Master / DDS Discovery"] --> node2["Publisher Nodes on Topics /turtle1/cmd_vel, /turtle1/pose"]
    node2 --> node3["Network Transport Layer TCP/UDP"]
    node3 --> node4["rosbag record process"]
    node4 --> node5["In-Memory Message Buffer Queue Size 100"]
    node5 --> node6["Serialization Engine MD5 + Binary Encoding"]
    node6 --> node7["mmap Write to Disk .bag file"]
    node7 --> node8["Optional BZ2 Compression at Chunk Level"]
    node8 --> node9["Persistent Bag File session.bag on SSD"]
```

## 4.2 Playback Workflow with Clock Simulation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["session.bag file on disk"] --> nodeB["rosbag play process"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Chunk Index Reader"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Message Deserializer"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Live Publisher on original topic"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["Clock Simulator publishing to /clock"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Time-aware nodes use_sim_time = true"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["Subscriber Nodes identical to original session"]
```

## 4.3 Topic Filtering and Selective Recording Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP["Robot Sensors and Actuators"] --> nodeM["ROS Topic Multiplexer Master"]
    nodeM --> nodeT1["Topic /lidar"]
    nodeM --> nodeT2["Topic /camera/image_raw"]
    nodeM --> nodeT3["Topic /cmd_vel"]
    nodeM --> nodeT4["Topic /diagnostics"]
    nodeT1 --> nodeR["rosbag record with filter /lidar /camera/image_raw"]
    nodeT2 --> nodeR
    nodeT3 -.X EXCLUDED .-> nodeR
    nodeT4 -.X EXCLUDED .-> nodeR
    nodeR --> nodeF["Filtered Bag File selective.bag"]
```

## 4.4 Bag File Internal Binary Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeF["session.bag file"] --> nodeH["File Header Record version 2.0"]
    nodeH --> nodeC1["Connection Record topic /scan type sensor_msgs/LaserScan"]
    nodeH --> nodeC2["Connection Record topic /cmd_vel type geometry_msgs/Twist"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeCH1["Chunk 0 multiple message records"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeCH1
    nodeCH1 --> nodeCH2["Chunk 1 next batch"]
    nodeCH2 --> nodeCH3["Chunk N final chunk"]
    nodeCH3 --> nodeIDX["Optional Index Block for fast seek"]
```

## 4.5 End-to-End Engineering Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Field Deployment Real Robot"] --> B["rosbag record -a -O mission1.bag"]
    B --> C["Lab / Server Storage"]
    C --> D["rosbag info mission1.bag"]
    D --> E["rosbag play -r 1.0 mission1.bag"]
    E --> F["Algorithm Under Test"]
    F --> G["Performance Metrics and Logs"]
    G --> H["Regression Test Report"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is a bag file in ROS? List any two `rosbag` sub-commands used for data recording and playback.**

**Model Answer:**
A **bag file** is a binary log file in ROS used to **store recorded message data** from one or more topics for later playback and analysis. It preserves the **topic name, message type, timestamp, and serialized payload** of every message, enabling lossless reconstruction of the original ROS topic graph.

Two important `rosbag` sub-commands are:
1. **`rosbag record`** — subscribes to specified topics and writes the incoming message stream to a `.bag` file on disk.
2. **`rosbag play`** — reads a previously recorded `.bag` file and republishes the stored messages onto the live ROS network as if they were being produced in real time.

*(Word count: ~75 — appropriate for 3-mark KTU short answer)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between `rosbag record -a` and `rosbag record /topic1 /topic2`. In which situation would you prefer each?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `rosbag record -a` | `rosbag record /topic1 /topic2` |
|---|---|---|
| Topics captured | **All** topics currently advertised on the ROS master | **Only** the explicitly named topics |
| Disk usage | High — captures everything including chatter | Low — targeted, efficient |
| Use case | **Full-system debugging**, post-mortem analysis of a real robot field run | **Focused data collection** for a specific algorithm (e.g., only LIDAR + camera) |
| Risk | May capture irrelevant high-rate topics (e.g., diagnostics) flooding disk | Risk of missing a critical topic that was not enumerated |

`rosbag record -a` is preferred when reproducing an unknown field failure; the selective form is preferred for **machine-learning dataset construction** and **storage-constrained embedded deployments**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Many students **forget to mention the use of the `-O` flag** when naming the output bag file. This results in loss of one mark.
> - When explaining playback, students often **omit the `--clock` flag** and lose marks on time-synchronization questions.
> - In command syntax, **the leading `rosbag` keyword is mandatory** — writing just `record -a` is treated as a syntax error by the examiner.
> - For Part B sub-questions of 7 marks, examiners expect **both a conceptual explanation AND the exact command syntax**. A purely theoretical answer without commands scores at most **4 of 7 marks**.

---

### Question Choice A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the working of `rosbag record` command. Describe how selective topic recording and file splitting are achieved.**

**Model Answer:**

**Working of `rosbag record`:**
The `rosbag record` tool operates as a **subscriber-only node** that joins the ROS publish/subscribe graph, listens to user-specified topics, serializes each received message, and writes the serialized stream to a `.bag` file on persistent storage.

**Step-by-step operation:**
1. The tool contacts the ROS Master to discover publishers of the requested topics.
2. It establishes subscription callbacks with a queue size of **100 messages** (to absorb bursts without dropping).
3. On each callback, the message is **time-stamped** (using `header.stamp` if present, else receive time) and pushed to a memory buffer.
4. A dedicated **write thread** flushes batches of messages to disk using `mmap()` for low-latency I/O.
5. If the file exceeds the **split size** (default **1024 MiB**), a new file is opened with a numeric suffix.

**Selective recording syntax:**

$$
\texttt{rosbag\ record\ -O\ session\ /scan\ /cmd\_vel\ /imu}
$$

**File splitting syntax:**

$$
\texttt{rosbag\ record\ -a\ -b\ 2048\ --split\ --duration=30m\ /lidar}
$$

| Flag | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `-O` | Output file prefix |
| `-a` | Record all topics |
| `-b 2048` | Split every 2048 MiB |
| `--split` | Enable splitting |
| `--duration=30m` | Create a new chunk every 30 minutes |

*(Award 7 marks: 3 for explanation, 2 for diagram credit, 2 for command syntax)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Discuss the various flags available with `rosbag play` for controlling playback rate, start time, looping, and clock simulation. Why is clock simulation critical for nodes using `use_sim_time`?**

**Model Answer:**

**Key `rosbag play` flags:**

| Flag | Example | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| `-r <factor>` | `-r 2.0` | Playback at 2× the recorded speed |
| `-s <seconds>` | `-s 30` | Skip the first 30 seconds of the bag |
| `-u <seconds>` | `-u 60` | Stop playback after 60 seconds |
| `-l` | `-l` | Loop the bag continuously |
| `--clock` | `--clock` | Publish to `/clock` topic for time-aware nodes |
| `--topics` | `--topics /scan` | Replay only specific topics from the bag |
| `-q` | `-q` | Quiet mode — suppress per-message logging |

**Clock simulation rationale:**
Many ROS nodes — especially those running SLAM, navigation, or sensor fusion — depend on a global clock for time-stamping, TF interpolation, and control loop synchronization. When a bag is replayed, the **wall-clock time of the computer is no longer meaningful** for the recorded scenario. By passing `--clock`, `rosbag play` advertises a publisher on the `/clock` topic and broadcasts the original recorded timestamps. Nodes with `<param name="use_sim_time">true</param>` in their launch file subscribe to `/clock` and **synchronize their internal time to the playback**, ensuring that algorithms which rely on time differences (e.g., `$dt = t_{\text{now}} - t_{\text{prev}}$) yield identical results to the original recording.

Without `--clock`, time-aware nodes would use `ros::Time::now()` (wall-clock) and produce **non-deterministic results**, defeating the purpose of reproducible playback.

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: What is the function of `rosbag info`? With an example output, explain the metadata it provides and how a developer would use it before initiating playback.**

**Model Answer:**

`rosbag info` is a diagnostic command that **inspects the contents of a `.bag` file** without playing it back. It prints a human-readable summary of the bag's metadata, which is essential for a developer to:
- Confirm that the recording captured the intended topics.
- Verify the recording duration and message count.
- Identify the exact message types for downstream code.
- Detect any corrupt or truncated chunks.

**Example invocation:**

$$
\texttt{rosbag\ info\ /data/mission1.bag}
$$

**Example output (representative):**

```
path:        /data/mission1.bag
version:     2.0
duration:    1h15m32s (4532.7s)
start:       Jan 15 2024 09:30:12.45
end:         Jan 15 2024 10:45:44.72
size:        4.3 GB
messages:    8,742,116
compression: bz2
chunks:      7

types:
    sensor_msgs/LaserScan    [90c7ef2dc6890d24c442c471f89d5707]
    geometry_msgs/Twist      [9f1957fa248bd4dc0e5d1f6b1c87c0be]
    sensor_msgs/Image        [060021388200f6f0f447d0fcd9c64743]

topics:
    /scan            142,330 msgs  : sensor_msgs/LaserScan
    /cmd_vel         226,635 msgs  : geometry_msgs/Twist
    /camera/image_raw  723,451 msgs : sensor_msgs/Image
```

**Developer workflow before playback:**
1. Run `rosbag info` to confirm presence of `/scan` and `/camera/image_raw`.
2. Note `duration` to decide whether to use `-u` flag for partial playback.
3. Identify `types` to ensure corresponding message packages are installed.
4. Check `size` and disk space on the playback machine.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain the concept of the bag file format in ROS. Discuss its on-disk structure (file header, connection records, chunk records, index). Why is the MD5 checksum important for message integrity?**

**Model Answer:**

**On-Disk Bag File Structure:**

A ROS bag file is a **versioned binary log** organized in a strict sequential layout:

1. **File Header Record** (first record of the file)
   - Contains the **bag format version** (e.g., `2.0`).
   - Total file size for verification.

2. **Connection Records** (one per unique (topic, type) pair)
   - Declares the **topic name**, **message type string**, **MD5 sum**, **message definition text**, and **caller ID**.
   - Allows the player to advertise publishers with identical type contracts as the original recording.

3. **Chunk Records** (data-bearing blocks)
   - Each chunk contains a **time-ordered batch** of serialized messages.
   - Optionally compressed using **BZ2** at the chunk level.
   - Default chunk size is configured by the recorder; typical size is a few MB.

4. **Index Records** (optional, generated by `rosbag reindex`)
   - Stores a **time-to-offset map** enabling **O(log n) seeking** to a specific timestamp.
   - Without an index, playback must scan linearly from file start.

**Importance of the MD5 Checksum:**
The MD5 sum in the connection record is a **128-bit cryptographic hash of the message definition text**. Its purpose is **message contract verification**:
- The publisher and subscriber compute the MD5 of the message type they agree on.
- If both sides compute the **same MD5**, the field names, types, and order are guaranteed to be byte-compatible.
- During playback, the player includes the MD5 in its connection header; subscribers can therefore **reject malformed messages** or detect version mismatches.
- This protects against silent data corruption if a custom message definition is changed after the recording was made.

**Structural Block Diagram:**

```
+--------------+
| File Header  |  (version, size)
+--------------+
| Connection 1 |  (topic /scan, MD5, type, caller)
+--------------+
| Connection 2 |  (topic /cmd_vel, MD5, type, caller)
+--------------+
| Chunk 0      |  (N serialized messages, BZ2 optional)
+--------------+
| Chunk 1      |
+--------------+
|   ...        |
+--------------+
| Index Block  |  (optional: timestamp → byte offset)
+--------------+
```

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Bag file** = binary log (`.bag`) of ROS messages preserving topic, type, timestamp, and payload.
- **`rosbag record`** is a subscriber node; **`rosbag play`** is a publisher node. Both are **transparent** to the rest of the system.
- **Flags to remember:**
  - `-a` → record all topics
  - `-O <name>` → specify output filename prefix
  - `-b <MB>` → split file size threshold (default **1024 MiB**)
  - `--split` + `--duration=<m>` → time-based chunking
  - `-r <factor>` → playback speed multiplier
  - `-l` → loop playback indefinitely
  - `-s <sec>` → skip initial seconds; `-u <sec>` → play only N seconds
  - `--clock` → publish to `/clock` for `use_sim_time` nodes
- **`rosbag info`** provides path, version, duration, start/end, size, message count, compression type, types, and per-topic message counts.
- **On-disk layout:** File Header → Connection Records → Chunk Records → optional Index Block.
- **MD5 sum** in connection record guarantees **type compatibility** between publisher and subscriber.
- **Compression:** Default is **BZ2** (`.bag.bz2`); ROS 2 also supports **LZ4**.
- **Storage estimate formula:**

$$
R_{\text{effective,GB}} = \frac{C}{2^{30}} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N_t} f_i \cdot T \cdot \left(S_i + 12\right)
$$

- **ROS 2 equivalent** is `ros2 bag record` / `ros2 bag play` using the **MCAP** format (recommended) or **SQLite3** (legacy).
- **Use `rosbag filter`** with a Python expression to extract subsets of a bag for ML datasets.
- **Always set `use_sim_time := true`** in the launch file of any node that must synchronize to a `--clock` playback.
- **Never confuse `rosbag record` with `rosbag play`** during the exam — they are functionally inverse operations.
- **Playback is real-time by default** (rate factor = 1.0); use `-r 0.5` for slow-motion, `-r 2.0` for accelerated.
- **Topic names with leading `/`** are absolute; without leading `/` they are resolved relative to the node's namespace.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Techniques for Building Maps from Sensor Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT525 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Techniques for Building Maps from Sensor Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mapping** in mobile robotics is the computational process of constructing a spatial representation of an unknown environment using sensor measurements (such as LiDAR, sonar, depth cameras, odometry, and IMU data) collected as a robot traverses the space. When the robot's pose (position and orientation) is also unknown during this process, the combined problem is termed **Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Robot Operating System (ROS)*, mapping techniques are formally classified into three principal categories based on the resulting spatial representation:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 4)**
> Mapping is the process of transforming a stream of raw sensor measurements $\{z_{1:t}\}$ and odometry data $\{u_{1:t}\}$ into an estimate of the environment $m$ and (optionally) the robot trajectory $x_{1:t}$. The output may be a **metric map**, a **topological map**, or a **hybrid metric-topological map**.

| Map Type | Representation | Best Use Case |
|----------|---------------|---------------|
| Occupancy Grid | 2D / 3D grid of probabilistic cells | Indoor structured environments |
| Feature Map | Sparse list of geometric landmarks | Outdoor, structured landmarks |
| Topological Map | Graph of nodes (places) and edges (paths) | Large-scale navigation |
| Point Cloud Map | Dense set of 3D points | 3D reconstruction, SLAM |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Dark-Room Explorer

Imagine you wake up inside a completely dark room. You cannot see anything, but you have:
- A **flashlight** (range sensor / LiDAR) that tells you how far the nearest obstacle is in any direction you point it.
- A **pedometer + compass** (wheel odometry + IMU) that estimates how far and in which direction you have walked.

Every time you take a step, you swing the flashlight and record a distance reading. As you walk, you sketch the outline of the room on a piece of paper. After many steps, the sketch becomes your **map**. The challenge: each step introduces uncertainty about your **current location**, which corrupts the placement of new measurements onto the map. This coupled uncertainty — *where am I?* and *what does the world look like?* — is exactly the **SLAM problem**.

> [!NOTE]
> In ROS, the canonical packages implementing these techniques include `gmapping` (2D occupancy grid SLAM), `cartographer` (Google's graph-SLAM), `hector_slam` (scan-matching), `rgbdslam` (visual SLAM), and `Octomap` (3D volumetric mapping). These nodes subscribe to `/scan`, `/odom`, `/tf`, and `/map` topics to perform real-time map building.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

The following quantitative metrics are mandated for KTU-level analytical answers:

- **Map Resolution (r):** typically **0.05 m** (5 cm) per cell for indoor navigation.
- **Log-odds range:** $l \in [- \infty, +\infty]$, practically clamped to $\vert l \vert \leq l_{\max} = \pm 5$.
- **Probabilistic Occupancy Range:** $P(m_i) \in [0, 1]$, where $P(m_i) = 0.5$ denotes *unknown*.
- **Standard LiDAR FOV:** **360°** (2D) or **±15° to ±30°** (3D Velodyne-style).
- **Sensor Update Frequency:** typical **10–40 Hz** for LiDAR, **30–60 Hz** for cameras.
- **Wheel Encoder Resolution:** ~**$\pm 0.1°$** angular, ~**0.001 m** linear.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mapping Problem — Mathematical Formulation

Given a sequence of sensor measurements $z_{1:t} = \{z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_t\}$ and control inputs (odometry) $u_{1:t} = \{u_1, u_2, \ldots, u_t\}$, the mapping problem estimates the posterior probability over maps:

$$
p(m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})
$$

If the robot trajectory $x_{1:t}$ is also unknown, the full SLAM posterior is:

$$
p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A central **independence assumption** in grid-based mapping is that the map can be decomposed into a product of independent cell occupancies:
> $$ p(m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \prod_{i} p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) $$
> This factorisation, proposed by Moravec and Elfes (1985), makes the problem tractable by reducing it to N independent binary estimation problems.

## 2.2 Bayesian Update of Occupancy Grid Cells

For a single cell $m_i$, the binary occupancy estimate is updated recursively using **Bayes' rule with a static-world assumption**:

$$
p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \, p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1})}
$$

Because binary occupancy ($m_i \in \{0, 1\}$) admits the log-odds transformation, updates become additive:

$$
l_{t,i} = l_{t-1,i} + \ln \frac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)} - l_0
$$

where $l_0 = \ln \frac{p(m_i = 1)}{p(m_i = 0)} = \ln \frac{0.5}{0.5} = 0$.

## 2.3 The Inverse Sensor Model

For a 2D laser range finder, the **inverse sensor model** $p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)$ answers: *"Given that I observed a range reading $z_t$ from pose $x_t$, what is the probability that cell $m_i$ is occupied?"*

The classical Moravec–Elfes model assigns three zones along each laser ray:

| Zone along ray | Condition | $p(\text{occupied})$ | $p(\text{free})$ |
|----------------|-----------|----------------------|------------------|
| **Before hit** (ray passage) | $d < z_t - \alpha / 2$ | $0.5$ | **0.9** |
| **Hit region** | $\vert d - z_t \vert < \alpha / 2$ | **0.7** | $0.5$ |
| **Beyond hit** (shadow) | $d > z_t + \beta / 2$ | $0.5$ | $0.5$ |
| **Mixed / uncertain** | otherwise | linear interpolation | linear interpolation |

where $d$ is the Euclidean distance from $x_t$ to cell $m_i$, $\alpha$ is the **cell resolution** ($r$), and $\beta$ is the **beam width factor**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $p(m \mid z_{1:t}) = \prod_i p(m_i \mid z_{1:t})$ | Map factorisation into independent cells | Dimensionless probability |
| 2 | $l_t = \ln \frac{p}{1-p}$ | Log-odds transform | $l \in [-\infty, +\infty]$ |
| 3 | $p = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{l}}$ | Inverse log-odds (sigmoid) | $p \in [0, 1]$ |
| 4 | $l_t = l_{t-1} + \text{inv\_model}(z_t, x_t, m_i) - l_0$ | Additive Bayesian update | Clamped to $\vert l \vert \leq l_{\max}$ |
| 5 | $d_i = \sqrt{(x_{cell} - x_{robot})^2 + (y_{cell} - y_{robot})^2}$ | Euclidean ray–cell distance | **meters (m)** |
| 6 | $\theta_i = \text{atan2}(y_{cell} - y_{robot}, x_{cell} - x_{robot})$ | Bearing angle to cell | **radians (rad)** |
| 7 | $\Delta\theta = \theta_i - \theta_{ray}$ | Angular offset from beam centre | **radians (rad)** |
| 8 | $z_{\max} = $ range sensor max reading | Sensor clipping threshold | **~30 m** typical |
| 9 | $r = \frac{L_{map}}{N_{cells}}$ | Map resolution | **m/cell**, e.g. **0.05 m** |
| 10 | $H(m) = -\sum_i p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Map information entropy | **bits**, quality metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Whenever a question mentions "log-odds update" or "additive update", the answer MUST include the clamping operation $\text{clip}(l_t, -l_{\max}, +l_{\max})$ to prevent numerical underflow/overflow during long SLAM sessions.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Mapping techniques are the bedrock of:
- **Autonomous Vehicles (AV):** Google/Waymo uses LiDAR-based occupancy grids for drivable-space detection.
- **Warehouse Robotics (AMR):** Amazon Kiva robots build 2D grid maps for navigation.
- **Domestic Vacuum Robots:** iRobot Roomba uses topological + occupancy grids.
- **Search and Rescue (SAR):** 3D Octomap reconstruction of collapsed buildings via drone-mounted depth sensors.
- **AR/VR:** Visual SLAM (ORB-SLAM, LSD-SLAM) for inside-out tracking in headsets.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Log-Odds Bayesian Update for Occupancy Grids

### Starting Equation (Bayes' Theorem)

$$
p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \, p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}
$$

### Step 1 — Express the Evidence Term

The denominator is the total probability of seeing $z_t$, which (over $m_i \in \{0, 1\}$) is:

$$
p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1}) = p(z_t \mid m_i=1, x_t) \, p(m_i=1 \mid z_{1:t-1}) + p(z_t \mid m_i=0, x_t) \, p(m_i=0 \mid z_{1:t-1})
$$

### Step 2 — Take the Odds Ratio

Define the **odds** as $\text{Odds}(m_i) = \frac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)}$. Then the posterior odds are:

$$
\frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})} = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i=1, x_t)}{p(z_t \mid m_i=0, x_t)} \cdot \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1})}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Logarithm

Taking the natural log converts the multiplicative update into an **additive** update:

$$
\ln \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})} = \ln \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1})} + \ln \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i=1, x_t)}{p(z_t \mid m_i=0, x_t)}
$$

### Step 4 — Define the Log-Odds Notation

Let $l_{t,i} = \ln \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t})}$ and $l_0 = \ln \frac{p_0}{1 - p_0}$ (the prior log-odds). Then:

$$
l_{t,i} = l_{t-1,i} + \underbrace{\ln \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i=1, x_t)}{p(z_t \mid m_i=0, x_t)}}_{\text{inverse sensor model contribution}} - l_0
$$

### Step 5 — Clamp to Prevent Overflow

$$
l_{t,i} = \text{clip}\!\left(l_{t-1,i} + \Delta l_{sensor} - l_0, \; -l_{\max}, \; +l_{\max}\right), \quad l_{\max} \approx 5.0
$$

### Step 6 — Recover the Probability (Inverse Log-Odds)

$$
p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \exp(l_{t,i})}
$$

This sigmoid inverse is what ROS nodes like `gmapping` use to populate the 2D `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` message, where each cell stores an integer in $[0, 100]$ representing $100 \times p(\text{occupied})$, with **-1** denoting unknown.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Inverse Sensor Model for a 2D Range Finder

For each ray $k$ at time $t$ with measured range $z_t^k$ and angle $\theta_t^k$, the robot pose is $x_t = (x, y, \theta)$. For a candidate cell at world coordinates $(c_x, c_y)$:

### Step 1 — Convert Cell to Robot Frame

$$
\begin{aligned}
dx &= c_x - x \\
dy &= c_y - y \\
d &= \sqrt{dx^2 + dy^2} \\
\phi &= \text{atan2}(dy, dx) - \theta
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Angular Discretisation Check

The cell is swept by ray $k$ only if its angular bin matches:

$$
k_{cell} = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{\phi}{\Delta\phi}\right) = k
$$

where $\Delta\phi$ is the angular resolution of the scan (typically **0.5°** for a Hokuyo / **0.25°** for a Velodyne).

### Step 3 — Apply the Three-Zone Inverse Model

$$
p(m_i = 1 \mid z_t^k, x_t) =
\begin{cases}
0.5 & \text{if } d > z_t^k + \beta / 2 \;\; \text{(shadow zone)} \\
0.5 + 0.5 \cdot \frac{z_t^k - d}{\beta} & \text{if } z_t^k - \beta / 2 < d \leq z_t^k + \beta / 2 \;\; \text{(hit zone)} \\
0.5 - 0.5 \cdot \frac{d}{z_t^k} & \text{if } 0 < d \leq z_t^k - \beta / 2 \;\; \text{(free zone)} \\
0.5 & \text{if } d = 0 \;\; \text{(robot footprint)}
\end{cases}
$$

where $\beta$ is the **beam thickness / cell resolution** parameter.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Information-Theoretic Map Quality (Optional Advanced)

For examiner-level depth, derive the **map entropy** as a measure of mapping completeness:

$$
H(m) = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ p_i \log_2 p_i + (1 - p_i) \log_2 (1 - p_i) \right]
$$

- $H = 0$ bits/cell $\Rightarrow$ completely known (either $p_i = 0$ or $p_i = 1$).
- $H = 1$ bit/cell $\Rightarrow$ maximally uncertain ($p_i = 0.5$).
- Total entropy of a 20 m × 20 m map at $r = 0.05$ m = 160,000 cells $\Rightarrow$ max $H = 160,000$ bits.

This metric is used in **active SLAM** to decide *where the robot should explore next* (information gain).

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Technique | Sensor | Map Type | Strength | Weakness | ROS Package |
|-----------|--------|----------|----------|----------|-------------|
| Occupancy Grid SLAM | 2D LiDAR | 2D grid | Robust, simple | Memory grows $O(N^2)$ | `gmapping` |
| Hector SLAM | 2D LiDAR (no odom) | 2D grid | Works without odometry | Sensitive to noise | `hector_slam` |
| Cartographer | 2D/3D LiDAR | 2D/3D grid | Loop closure | High compute | `cartographer_ros` |
| ORB-SLAM2/3 | Mono/Stereo camera | Sparse feature | Low cost | Lighting dependent | `orbslam2` |
| RTAB-Map | RGB-D / Stereo | 3D point cloud + grid | Loop closure + memory mgmt | Memory-bound | `rtabmap_ros` |
| Octomap | 3D LiDAR / Depth | 3D voxel | Probabilistic 3D | Heavy memory | `octomap_server` |
| Karto SLAM | 2D LiDAR | 2D grid | Graph-based | Scan-match sensitive | `karto_slam` |
| ICP / NDT | 3D point cloud | Point cloud | High precision | Needs good init | `pcl_ros` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a ROS-Based Mapping Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Sensor Driver Node] --> nodeB[Sensor Msgs Scan Cloud]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Scan Matching Node]
    nodeD[Wheel Odometry Node] --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeE[Pose Graph Node]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Occupancy Grid Map Node]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Map Server Topic Map]
    nodeF --> nodeH[TF Broadcaster Map to Odom]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Navigation Stack AMCL Planner]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Velocity Commands to Robot]
    nodeE --> nodeK[Loop Closure Detector]
    nodeK --> nodeE
```

## 4.2 Occupancy Grid Update Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start New Scan z_t] --> nodeB[Transform Scan to World Frame using x_t]
    nodeB --> nodeC{For each ray k in scan}
    nodeC --> nodeD[Compute ray endpoint using z_t and theta_t]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Bresenham line traversal along ray]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Mark cells before hit as FREE]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Mark endpoint cell as OCCUPIED]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Update log odds l_t for each cell]
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI[Clamp l_t to plus or minus l_max]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Convert l_t to probability p_t via sigmoid]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Publish OccupancyGrid message]
    nodeK --> nodeL[More rays?]
    nodeL -- Yes --> nodeC
    nodeL -- No --> nodeM[End Update Cycle]
```

## 4.3 Mapping Technique Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Mapping Techniques] --> nodeB[Metric Maps]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Topological Maps]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Hybrid Maps]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Occupancy Grid 2D]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Voxel Grid 3D]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Feature Map Landmarks]
    nodeB --> nodeH[Point Cloud Map]
    nodeC --> nodeI[Place Graph Nodes]
    nodeC --> nodeJ[Edge Connectivity Edges]
    nodeD --> nodeK[Metric Local plus Topological Global]
    nodeE --> nodeL[Example ROS gmapping]
    nodeF --> nodeM[Example ROS Octomap]
    nodeG --> nodeN[Example ROS ORB SLAM]
    nodeH --> nodeO[Example ROS RTAB Map]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Each block represents a ROS node or algorithm stage. Arrows denote the direction of message flow via topics (`/scan`, `/odom`, `/tf`, `/map`). Loop closure arrows in diagram 4.1 indicate **bidirectional graph optimization** between the pose graph and loop closure detector — this is the essence of modern graph-SLAM systems like `cartographer`.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
*Explain the difference between metric and topological maps in robot mapping. Give one example use case for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Metric Map (1.5 marks):** Stores geometric properties of the environment in a global coordinate frame, e.g., occupancy grid, point cloud, or feature map. Each cell/landmark has an absolute $(x, y)$ coordinate. **Example:** Indoor office floor plan built by `gmapping`.
- **Topological Map (1.5 marks):** Stores the environment as a graph of *places* (nodes) connected by *paths* (edges), without geometric detail. Suitable for large-scale navigation where only connectivity matters. **Example:** A building floor map where nodes are rooms and edges are corridors, as used in `wifi_graph_slam`.

### Question A2
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
*What is the role of the inverse sensor model in occupancy grid mapping? Why is log-odds representation preferred over raw probability?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Inverse Sensor Model (1.5 marks):** It gives $p(m_i = 1 \mid z_t, x_t)$, the probability that a grid cell is occupied given the current sensor reading and robot pose. It transforms raw range measurements into per-cell probabilistic updates.
- **Log-Odds Advantage (1.5 marks):** (1) Converts the multiplicative Bayesian update into an **additive** operation, computationally cheap. (2) Allows **clamping** to prevent numerical underflow/overflow. (3) Symmetric representation around zero (unknown state).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 4 Choice A]`**

**(a)** Derive the log-odds update rule for occupancy grid mapping starting from Bayes' theorem. Show how the update becomes additive. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the architecture of the `gmapping` ROS package. Explain the role of `slam_gmapping` node, its inputs (`/scan`, `/tf`, `/odom`), and the algorithm it uses (Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Bayes' Theorem (1 mark):**

$$
p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \, p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}
$$

**Step 2 — Evidence Expansion (1 mark):** Denominator expanded over binary occupancy.

**Step 3 — Odds Ratio (1.5 marks):**

$$
\frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t})} = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i=1, x_t)}{p(z_t \mid m_i=0, x_t)} \cdot \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1})}
$$

**Step 4 — Logarithm Application (1.5 marks):**

$$
l_{t,i} = l_{t-1,i} + \ln \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i=1, x_t)}{p(z_t \mid m_i=0, x_t)} - l_0
$$

**Step 5 — Additive Interpretation (1 mark):** The update is *additive* — new information from $z_t$ is summed onto the prior log-odds $l_{t-1,i}$, making it $O(1)$ per cell per scan.

**Step 6 — Clamping and Inverse (1 mark):** Clamp to $\vert l \vert \leq l_{\max} \approx 5$, then recover $p$ via sigmoid:

$$
p = \frac{1}{1 + \exp(-l)}
$$

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Architecture (3 marks):** `gmapping` is launched via `roslaunch gmapping slam_gmapping_pr2.launch`. The `slam_gmapping` node subscribes to:
- `/scan` (sensor_msgs/LaserScan) — 2D LiDAR data.
- `/tf` (tf2_msgs/TFMessage) — coordinate transforms between `odom`, `base_link`, `base_laser`.
- (Optional) initial pose from `/initialpose` topic.

**Algorithm — Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter (RBPF) (4 marks):**
- Each particle $k$ maintains its own map $m^{[k]}$ and trajectory $x_{1:t}^{[k]}$.
- Particles are weighted by the likelihood $p(z_t \mid x_t^{[k]}, m^{[k]})$ of the current scan.
- At each step, particles are **resampled** (low-weight particles discarded, high-weight duplicated) to prevent particle depletion.
- The best map (from highest-weight particle) is published on `/map`.

Outputs: `/map` (nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid), `/map_metadata`, and updated `/tf` (`map → odom`).

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 4 Choice B]`**

**(a)** Compare occupancy grid mapping, feature-based mapping, and topological mapping. Tabulate the pros, cons, and computational complexity of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of the `cartographer` ROS package. How does it achieve global consistency via loop closure? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

| Criteria | Occupancy Grid | Feature-Based | Topological |
|----------|---------------|---------------|-------------|
| **Representation** | 2D/3D grid of probabilities | List of geometric landmarks | Graph of nodes and edges |
| **Memory** (1 mark) | $O(N^2)$ or $O(N^3)$ — high | $O(K)$, $K \ll N$ — low | $O(N)$ — very low |
| **Computation** (1 mark) | $O(N \cdot R)$ per scan, $R$ = rays | $O(K)$ matching per scan | $O(1)$ lookup |
| **Precision** (1 mark) | High (per-cell) | High at landmarks, sparse elsewhere | Low geometric detail |
| **Best For** (1 mark) | Indoor structured | Outdoor landmark-rich | Large-scale floorplans |
| **ROS Example** (1 mark) | `gmapping`, `cartographer` | `orbslam2`, `aruco` | `wifi_graph_slam` |
| **Weakness** (1 mark) | Memory grows with area | Needs detectable features | No obstacle avoidance info |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Hybrid maps combine metric + topological to balance precision and scalability.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Cartographer Architecture (3 marks):** Google `cartographer_ros` runs three main nodes:
- **Cartographer Node:** Subscribes to `/scan`, `/points2`, `/imu`, `/odom`. Performs **local SLAM** (scan-to-scan matching via Ceres-based non-linear optimization) and produces submap fragments.
- **Global SLAM Node:** Performs **scan-to-map matching** and detects **loop closures** by matching current scans against all accumulated submaps.
- **Map Builder Node:** Publishes `/map`, `/submap_list`, and trajectory data.

**Local SLAM (2 marks):**
- Inserts each scan into the most recent submap using **pixel-accurate scan matching** (Ceres solver minimizes residuals between scan points and map occupancy).
- Submaps are rotated grids of size $7 \times 7$ m updated as the robot moves.

**Global SLAM & Loop Closure (2 marks):**
- A **pose graph** stores all submap poses as nodes.
- **SPA (Sparse Pose Adjustment)** optimizes the graph whenever a loop closure is detected (current scan matches an old submap).
- This **back-propagates corrections** through the entire trajectory, eliminating accumulated drift and achieving **global consistency**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse `gmapping` with `cartographer`.** `gmapping` uses **particle filters** (RBPF); `cartographer` uses **graph-SLAM with Ceres optimization**. Mixing up algorithms costs **3 marks**.
> 2. **Always show the additive log-odds update with the clamping step.** Skipping the clamp means losing **1 mark** for incomplete numerical analysis.
> 3. **Mention `/tf` and topic names explicitly** in architecture answers. Vague descriptions like *"sends data to other nodes"* will be penalised **2 marks**.
> 4. **In derivation questions, every algebraic step must be justified in words.** Simply writing equations without explaining the Bayes-to-log-odds transition loses **2 marks**.
> 5. **Confusing `map → odom` vs `odom → base_link` TF frames** is a frequent error. Remember: `gmapping` publishes `map → odom`; the robot's own odometry publishes `odom → base_link`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Mapping Techniques**

- **Mapping** = estimating the environment $m$ from sensor/odometry streams.
- **SLAM** = simultaneous estimation of map $m$ AND robot trajectory $x_{1:t}$.
- **Three map types:** Metric (grids, features, point clouds), Topological (graph), Hybrid.
- **Bayes' theorem** is the foundation; the **factorisation assumption** makes grid SLAM tractable.
- **Log-odds update:** $l_t = l_{t-1} + \Delta l_{sensor} - l_0$, clamped to $\vert l \vert \leq 5$.
- **Inverse sensor model:** Converts range readings into per-cell occupancy probabilities (3 zones: free, hit, shadow).
- **Bresenham's algorithm** is used for ray traversal in grid maps.
- **RBPF (gmapping)** uses particles; each particle carries its own map.
- **Graph SLAM (cartographer)** optimises a pose graph via Ceres solver + loop closure.
- **3D mapping** uses **Octomap** with probabilistic octree voxels.
- **Map quality metric:** information entropy $H(m) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ bits.
- **Standard resolution:** $r = 0.05$ m/cell for indoor; $r = 0.2$ m/cell for outdoor.
- **TF tree for SLAM:** `map → odom → base_link → sensors`.
- **Key ROS topics:** `/scan`, `/odom`, `/tf`, `/map`, `/initialpose`, `/submap_list`.
- **Standard KTU answer must include:** equations, diagrams, numerical examples, and ROS package references.
- **Memory caveat:** A 50 m × 50 m map at 5 cm resolution = 1,000,000 cells — needs pruning or Octomap compression for large-scale deployment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GMapping and Cartographer for Mapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT525 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS) > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** GMapping and Cartographer for Mapping # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) In the context of mobile robotics and the Robot Operating System (ROS), **Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)** is the computational problem of constructing or updating a map of an unknown environment while simultaneously keeping track of an agent's location within it. Within the ROS ecosystem, SLAM is realized through standardized `slam_gmapping` and `cartographer_ros` packages, which form the backbone of 2D laser-based mapping. ### **GMapping** **GMapping** is an open-source, laser-based, 2D **Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter (RBPF)** SLAM implementation originally developed by Grisetti, Stachniss, and Burgard. It produces an **Occupancy Grid Map** by combining the fast proposal distribution of scan-matching with a selective resampling strategy to reduce particle depletion. > [!NOTE] > **GMapping Core Identity:** A *particle-filter-based online SLAM* algorithm that maintains hundreds of candidate robot trajectories (particles), each carrying its own map estimate. It is highly regarded for its low computational footprint on small, indoor environments. ### **Cartographer** **Cartographer** is a Google-developed, open-source SLAM system that provides real-time 2D and 3D mapping across multiple sensor configurations (LIDAR, IMU, odometry). It uses **graph-based SLAM** with **submaps** and **Ceres-based scan matching** for front-end pose estimation, combined with sparse pose adjustment for back-end loop closure optimization. > [!NOTE] > **Cartographer Core Identity:** A *graph-optimization-based SLAM* algorithm that breaks the environment into overlapping local submaps and runs global optimization to correct accumulated drift, making it suitable for both small and large-scale mapping. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview) Imagine you are **blindfolded inside an unfamiliar multi-storey building** and you are handed a long rope with a bell at the end: - **The Blindfold (No prior map):** You cannot see the walls, so you must *build* a mental map while simultaneously figuring out where you are. - **The Bell + Rope (LIDAR):** Every time you swing the rope, the bell rings when it touches a wall. The time it takes for the echo tells you how far the wall is — this is your **laser scan**. - **The Notebooks (Particles vs. Submaps):** - **GMapping** hands you *200 blank notebooks*. Every time you step, you write the same map in a slightly different notebook based on a "what if I had stepped this way instead?" hypothesis. At every step, you cross out the notebooks that no longer match reality (resampling). Eventually, one notebook survives and becomes your map. - **Cartographer** hands you *one notebook with sticky pages (submaps)*. You sketch a small chunk of the building on each sticky page. After every few pages, you go back through the book and check if any sticky pages actually represent the *same* corridor viewed from two different times — if they do, you use that to **straighten the entire book's alignment** (loop closure). --- ## 1.3 Critical Metrics & Constants | Metric / Constant | Symbol | Standard Value (2D LIDAR) | |---|---|---| | Map resolution | $r$ | **0.05 m** (typical) | | Particle count (GMapping) | $N_p$ | **30 to 200** | | Submap size (Cartographer) | $N_{sm}$ | **90 to 200 scans** | | LIDAR scan frequency | $f_s$ | **10–40 Hz** | | Maximum usable range | $R_{max}$ | **~30 m** | | Default loop closure search window | $\Delta_{lc}$ | **3 m** / **0.5 rad** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always explicitly mention that both algorithms assume a **2D planar environment** with **odometry + laser scan** as the primary sensor suite. Failure to state this assumption costs 1 mark in viva questions. --- ## 1.4 KTU Syllabus Cross-Reference (Module 4 Mapping Stack) | Layer | GMapping | Cartographer | |---|---|---| | **Input Topics** | LaserScan, Odometry, TF | LaserScan, IMU, Odometry, TF | | **Output Topics** | `/map`, `/map_metadata` | `/map`, `/submap_list`, TF | | **Computational Paradigm** | Particle Filter (Monte Carlo) | Pose Graph Optimization | | **Loop Closure** | Approximate (via scan-match score) | Explicit (branch-and-bound search) | | **KTU 2024 Weightage** | ~ 4–6 marks | ~ 4–6 marks | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 GMapping: Theoretical Foundations ### 2.1.1 The Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter (RBPF) The RBPF factorizes the joint posterior $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t-1})$ into a localization posterior and an independent map posterior: $$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t-1}) \;=\; p(x_{1:t} \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t-1}) \cdot p(m \mid x_{1:t}, z_{1:t})$$ where: - $x_{1:t}$ is the trajectory of the robot from time $1$ to $t$ - $m$ is the occupancy grid map - $z_{1:t}$ are the LIDAR observations - $u_{1:t-1}$ are the odometry control inputs **Step-by-step logic:** - **Why factorize?** Because once the trajectory $x_{1:t}$ is known, the map $m$ can be computed analytically using the standard "trace" algorithm (each cell's occupancy log-odds updated by ray-casting). - **How is it done?** Each particle $i$ carries (a) a pose hypothesis $x_t^{(i)}$ and (b) its own private map $m^{(i)}$. Mapping becomes a particle-filtering problem. ### 2.1.2 The Improved Proposal Distribution Raw odometry is noisy; naive particle filters therefore require thousands of particles. GMapping uses a **scan-matched proposal**: $$x_t^{(i)} \;\sim\; p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{(i)}, m_{t-1}^{(i)}, z_t, u_{t-1})$$ i.e., the next pose is sampled from the most recent scan match **and** odometry, both centered around the previous pose. This drastically reduces the required number of particles. ### 2.1.3 Selective Resampling Resampling is performed **only when** the effective number of particles $N_{\text{eff}}$ drops below a threshold $N_t$: $$\hat{N}_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N_p} \left( w^{(i)} \right)^2}$$ **Why?** Unconditional resampling causes *particle depletion* (all particles collapse onto a single high-weight ancestor, erasing diversity). By skipping resampling when weights are still uniform, GMapping preserves trajectory diversity. ### 2.1.4 Occupancy Grid Map Update Each grid cell $m_{xy}$ stores the log-odds of occupancy: $$l_{xy}^{(t)} \;=\; l_{xy}^{(t-1)} \;+\; l_{\text{hit}} \;-\; l_{\text{miss}}$$ where $l_{\text{hit}}$ and $l_{\text{miss}}$ are the inverse sensor model log-odds (typically $\pm 0.85$ and $\mp 0.4$). --- ## 2.2 Cartographer: Theoretical Foundations ### 2.2.1 Pose Graph Representation The trajectory is modeled as a graph of poses $P = \{p_0, p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n\}$ connected by constraints: - **Odometry constraints:** Relative poses from wheel encoders. - **Scan-matching constraints:** Relative poses from consecutive laser scans. - **Loop closure constraints:** Relative poses between non-adjacent poses detected via branch-and-bound search. ### 2.2.2 Submap Construction A submap $\mathcal{S}_k$ is built from the first $N_{sm}$ scans aligned via **Ceres-based scan matching** (a nonlinear least-squares optimizer). Each scan inserts a node into $\mathcal{S}_k$ and adds a constraint to the global pose graph. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Submap vs. GMapping Map:** A GMapping map is *global and online*. A Cartographer submap is a *local, fixed-lag* buffer. Many submaps together constitute the global map. ### 2.2.3 Scan Matching Cost Function (Ceres) The optimal relative pose $\boldsymbol{\xi}^* = (\xi_x, \xi_y, \xi_\theta)$ is found by minimizing: $$\boldsymbol{\xi}^* \;=\; \underset{\boldsymbol{\xi}}{\arg\min} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \left[ 1 \;-\; M_{\text{smooth}}\left( T_{\boldsymbol{\xi}} \cdot h_k \right) \right]^2$$ where: - $T_{\boldsymbol{\xi}}$ is the 2D rigid-body transformation matrix - $h_k$ are the scan endpoints in the sensor frame - $M_{\text{smooth}}(\cdot)$ is the bilinear-interpolated probability of the submap grid being occupied - $K$ is the number of scan points ### 2.2.4 Loop Closure: Branch-and-Bound Search To detect loop closures efficiently, Cartographer performs a **branch-and-bound** search over the pose space: $$c^* \;=\; \underset{c \in W}{\max} \; \text{score}(c, \mathcal{S}_{\text{current}})$$ where the search window $W = W_x \times W_y \times W_\theta$ is discretized hierarchically (voxel-based coarse-to-fine). The score threshold is typically **0.55** for a valid loop closure. ### 2.2.5 Sparse Pose Adjustment (SPA) Once loop closures are added, the global pose graph is solved via a non-linear least-squares optimization: $$\underset{P}{\arg\min} \sum_{(i,j) \in \mathcal{C}} \rho\left( E^2\left( p_i, p_j; \boldsymbol{\zeta}_{ij} \right) \right)$$ where: - $\mathcal{C}$ is the set of all constraints (odometry + scans + loops) - $\boldsymbol{\zeta}_{ij}$ is the relative measurement between pose $i$ and pose $j$ - $E(\cdot)$ is the residual function - $\rho(\cdot)$ is a robust loss function (Huber or Cauchy) to reject outlier loop closures --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Equation | Typical Unit / Value | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | RBPF Factorization | $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z, u) = p(x \mid z, u) \cdot p(m \mid x, z)$ | Dimensionless posterior | | 2 | Effective Particle Count | $\hat{N}_{\text{eff}} = \left( \sum_{i} (w^{(i)})^2 \right)^{-1}$ | $\leq N_p$ | | 3 | Resampling Threshold | Resample iff $\hat{N}_{\text{eff}} < N_t$ | $N_t \approx N_p / 2$ | | 4 | Log-Odds Update | $l_{xy}^{(t)} = l_{xy}^{(t-1)} + l_{\text{hit}} - l_{\text{miss}}$ | Log-odds (dimensionless) | | 5 | Scan-Match Cost (Cartographer) | $\xi^* = \arg\min_{\xi} \sum_{k} [1 - M_{\text{smooth}}(T_{\xi} h_k)]^2$ | Score $\in [0,1]$ | | 6 | Loop Closure Score | $c^* = \max_{c \in W} \text{score}(c, \mathcal{S})$ | Threshold $\approx 0.55$ | | 7 | Sparse Pose Adjustment | $\min_P \sum_{(i,j)} \rho(E^2(p_i, p_j; \zeta_{ij}))$ | Weighted residual sum | | 8 | 2D Transform from $\xi$ | $T_{\xi} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\xi_\theta & -\sin\xi_\theta & \xi_x \\ \sin\xi_\theta & \cos\xi_\theta & \xi_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | SE(2) matrix | | 9 | Loop Window | $W = W_x \times W_y \times W_\theta$ | e.g. $5\text{ m} \times 5\text{ m} \times \pi$ | | 10 | IMU Weight (Cartographer) | $w_{\text{imu}} \in [0, 1]$ | Default: **1e2** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Warning:** When writing any equation involving absolute value or norm, NEVER use $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table cell. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\Vert x \Vert$ with proper LaTeX sizing. The same applies to any conditional $|.|$ notation in cells — always use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Warehouse Robotics (Amazon, Flipkart):** GMapping is widely deployed on **TurtleBot 2/3** and **KUKA YouBot** platforms for indoor aisle mapping due to its low CPU footprint (runs on a Raspberry Pi 4 at ~20 Hz). - **Autonomous Service Robots:** Cartographer is the de-facto SLAM for **Boston Dynamics Stretch**, **Google's Everyday Robots**, and most ROS 2 reference designs because of its loop closure robustness over long corridors. - **Disaster Response & Search & Rescue (SAR):** Cartographer's 3D mode (`cartographer_3d`) is used with **Velodyne VLP-16** for rubble-pile and tunnel mapping where loop closures are crucial. - **Self-Driving Cars (Early Prototypes):** Toyota, Honda, and Ford research fleets initially benchmarked Cartographer against LOAM and Hector SLAM for underground parking mapping. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Deriving the Log-Odds Occupancy Update for GMapping **Problem Statement:** Show that the iterative log-odds update $l_{xy}^{(t)} = l_{xy}^{(t-1)} + l_{\text{hit}} - l_{\text{miss}}$ is the maximum-likelihood estimator under the inverse sensor model. **Step 1: Define the inverse sensor model probability.** For a beam of length $r$ hitting cell $(x,y)$: $$\Pr(m_{xy} = \text{occupied} \mid r, x, y) \;=\; p_{\text{hit}}(r, x, y)$$ and for all cells along the ray **before** the hit: $$\Pr(m_{xy} = \text{occupied} \mid r, x, y) \;=\; p_{\text{miss}}(r, x, y)$$ **Step 2: Convert to log-odds form.** The log-odds (also called *information filter* form) is defined as: $$l \;=\; \log \frac{\Pr(\text{occupied})}{\Pr(\text{free})} \;=\; \log \frac{\Pr(\text{occ})}{1 - \Pr(\text{occ})}$$ **Step 3: Apply Bayes' Rule sequentially.** With prior $\Pr(m_{xy}^{(t-1)})$, posterior after one observation: $$\Pr(m_{xy}^{(t)} \mid z_t) \;=\; \frac{\Pr(z_t \mid m_{xy}^{(t)}) \cdot \Pr(m_{xy}^{(t-1)} \mid z_{1:t-1})}{\Pr(z_t)}$$ **Step 4: Take log and use the additive property of log-odds.** Since log-odds are additive for independent observations: $$\begin{aligned} l_{xy}^{(t)} &= \log \frac{\Pr(m_{xy}^{(t)} \mid z_t)}{\Pr(\neg m_{xy}^{(t)} \mid z_t)} \\ &= \log \frac{\Pr(z_t \mid m_{xy}^{(t)})}{\Pr(z_t \mid \neg m_{xy}^{(t)})} + \log \frac{\Pr(m_{xy}^{(t-1)} \mid z_{1:t-1})}{\Pr(\neg m_{xy}^{(t-1)} \mid z_{1:t-1})} \\ &= \log \frac{\Pr(z_t \mid m_{xy}^{(t)})}{\Pr(z_t \mid \neg m_{xy}^{(t)})} + l_{xy}^{(t-1)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5: Substitute the inverse model constants.** By definition: $$l_{\text{hit}} = \log \frac{\Pr(z_t \mid \text{occupied})}{\Pr(z_t \mid \text{free})}, \quad l_{\text{miss}} = \log \frac{\Pr(z_t \mid \text{free})}{\Pr(z_t \mid \text{occupied})} = -l_{\text{hit}}$$ Substituting and simplifying: $$\begin{aligned} l_{xy}^{(t)} &= l_{xy}^{(t-1)} + \begin{cases} + l_{\text{hit}} & \text{if hit} \\ - l_{\text{hit}} = l_{\text{miss}} & \text{if miss} \end{cases} \\ \therefore l_{xy}^{(t)} &= l_{xy}^{(t-1)} + l_{\text{hit}} - l_{\text{miss}} \end{aligned}$$ **Conclusion:** The GMapping occupancy update is mathematically equivalent to incremental log-odds accumulation, ensuring maximum-likelihood estimation under the assumption of independent observations. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Effective Sample Size and Resampling Criterion **Problem Statement:** Derive $\hat{N}_{\text{eff}}$ from the variance of importance weights. **Step 1: Importance weights for particles.** Each particle $i$ has a normalized weight $w^{(i)}$ satisfying $\sum_i w^{(i)} = 1$. The true posterior $p(x)$ is approximated by: $$p(x) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N_p} w^{(i)} \delta(x - x^{(i)})$$ **Step 2: Estimate variance of weights using the $\chi^2$ test.** The unbiased estimator of variance for normalized weights is: $$\widehat{\text{Var}}(w) \;=\; \frac{1}{N_p - 1} \sum_{i=1}^{N_p} \left( w^{(i)} - \frac{1}{N_p} \right)^2$$ **Step 3: Simplify to obtain the canonical form.** $$\begin{aligned} \widehat{\text{Var}}(w) &= \frac{1}{N_p - 1} \left[ \sum_{i} (w^{(i)})^2 - \frac{2}{N_p}\sum_i w^{(i)} + N_p \cdot \frac{1}{N_p^2} \right] \\ &= \frac{1}{N_p - 1} \left[ \sum_i (w^{(i)})^2 - \frac{1}{N_p} \right] \\ &\approx \frac{1}{N_p} \left[ \sum_i (w^{(i)})^2 - \frac{1}{N_p} \right] \quad \text{(for large } N_p) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4: Invert to obtain effective sample size.** The effective number of particles is the inverse of normalized weight variance: $$\begin{aligned} \hat{N}_{\text{eff}} &= \frac{1}{N_p \cdot \widehat{\text{Var}}(w)} \\ &= \frac{1}{N_p \cdot \frac{1}{N_p}\left[ \sum_i (w^{(i)})^2 - \frac{1}{N_p} \right]} \\ &\approx \frac{1}{\sum_i (w^{(i)})^2} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5: Apply the resampling rule.** GMapping resamples only when: $$\hat{N}_{\text{eff}} \;<\; N_t \quad \text{where} \quad N_t \;=\; \frac{N_p}{2}$$ This guarantees that the particle set always represents a non-degenerate posterior. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Scan-Match Residual for Cartographer **Problem Statement:** Minimize the dissimilarity between an incoming scan and the submap under rigid transform $\boldsymbol{\xi} = (\xi_x, \xi_y, \xi_\theta)$. **Step 1: Transform a scan point from sensor frame to submap frame.** Each scan endpoint $h_k = (h_{k,x}, h_{k,y}, 1)^T$ in homogeneous coordinates is transformed by: $$p_k \;=\; T_{\boldsymbol{\xi}} \cdot h_k$$ where $T_{\boldsymbol{\xi}}$ is the 2D SE(2) transform. **Step 2: Query the submap probability at $p_k$.** The submap is a discrete probability grid $M_{\text{smooth}}$ with bilinear interpolation: $$M_{\text{smooth}}(p_k) \;\in\; [0, 1]$$ **Step 3: Define the per-point residual.** $$r_k(\boldsymbol{\xi}) \;=\; 1 \;-\; M_{\text{smooth}}(T_{\boldsymbol{\xi}} \cdot h_k)$$ **Step 4: Aggregate into the total cost.** $$S(\boldsymbol{\xi}) \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{K} \left[ 1 \;-\; M_{\text{smooth}}(T_{\boldsymbol{\xi}} \cdot h_k) \right]^2$$ **Step 5: Set $\nabla S = 0$ for optimum.** For Ceres solver, this is solved iteratively via Levenberg–Marquardt (a hybrid of Gauss–Newton and gradient descent): $$\boldsymbol{\xi}^{(j+1)} = \boldsymbol{\xi}^{(j)} - \left( J^T J + \lambda I \right)^{-1} J^T r(\boldsymbol{\xi}^{(j)})$$ where $J$ is the Jacobian of $r(\boldsymbol{\xi})$ w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\xi}$, and $\lambda$ is the damping factor updated per iteration. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Attribute | GMapping | Cartographer | |---|---|---| | **Filter Type** | Particle Filter (RBPF) | Pose Graph Optimizer | | **Core Math Tool** | Monte Carlo Sampling | Ceres-based Least Squares | | **Map Type Produced** | Single Occupancy Grid | Multiple Submaps + Global Pose Graph | | **Loop Closure** | Implicit (particle weights) | Explicit (Branch-and-Bound Search) | | **Computational Cost** | $O(N_p \cdot K)$ per scan | $O(\lvert \mathcal{C} \rvert \cdot d)$ per graph solve | | **Memory Footprint** | $N_p \times \text{map-size}$ bytes | $N_{sm} \times \text{submap-size}$ bytes | | **Best Suited For** | Small, indoor, low-drift | Large, long-corridor, drift-heavy | | **Sensor Compatibility** | LIDAR + Odometry | LIDAR + IMU + Odometry + GPS | | **ROS Launch Package** | `slam_gmapping` | `cartographer_ros` | | **KTU 2024 Difficulty** | Moderate (MCQ + 14-mark) | Moderate (Theory + 14-mark) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 GMapping Data Flow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR node1["Laser Scanner
(LIDAR Sensor)"] --> nodeA["Scan Buffer
/scan topic"] node2["Wheel Encoders
(Odometry)"] --> nodeB["Odom Buffer
/odom topic"] nodeA --> nodeC["slam_gmapping
RBPF Core"] nodeB --> nodeC node3["Static TF
base_laser to base_link"] --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD["Particle Set
N_p = 30 to 200"] nodeD --> nodeE["Best Particle
Selection"] nodeE --> nodeF["Map Builder
Log-Odds Grid"] nodeF --> nodeG["OccupancyGrid
/map topic"] nodeC --> nodeH["TF Broadcaster
map to odom"] nodeH --> nodeI["Move Base
Navigation Stack"] ``` **Explanation:** - The **Laser Scanner** and **Wheel Encoders** feed raw data into the RBPF core. - The **RBPF core** runs the scan-matched proposal and selective resampling. - The **Best Particle** is chosen based on maximum weight. - The **Map Builder** accumulates log-odds into the final occupancy grid. - TF (`map → odom`) is broadcast to allow downstream navigation. --- ## 4.2 Cartographer Data Flow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB node1["Laser Scanner
(2D or 3D LIDAR)"] --> nodeA["Scan Sink
/scan"] node2["IMU
(Optional)"] --> nodeB["IMU Buffer
/imu"] node3["Odometry
(Wheel / Visual)"] --> nodeC["Odom Buffer
/odom"] nodeA --> nodeD["Global Trajectory Builder"] nodeB --> nodeD nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE["Local Submap
Insertion Loop"] nodeE --> nodeF["Submap Set
S_1, S_2, S_3 ..."] nodeE --> nodeG["Ceres Scan Matcher
Pose Optimization"] nodeG --> nodeE nodeF --> nodeH["Sparse Pose Graph
SPA Backend"] nodeI["Loop Closure
Branch and Bound Search"] --> nodeH nodeF --> nodeI nodeH --> nodeJ["Global Optimized
Trajectory"] nodeJ --> nodeK["TF Publisher
map to odom"] nodeJ --> nodeL["OccupancyGrid
/map topic"] ``` **Explanation:** - **Local Submap Loop (front-end):** Scans are inserted into active submaps via Ceres scan matching. - **Sparse Pose Graph (back-end):** Constraint graph is solved iteratively. - **Branch-and-Bound Search:** Detects loop closures by matching new scans against finished submaps. - **TF and `/map` outputs** are identical in interface to GMapping, enabling drop-in replacement. --- ## 4.3 Comparison of Internal State Evolution ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph gm["GMapping State"] g1["t=0: 200 particles"] --> g2["t=1: resample to 180"] g2 --> g3["t=2: resample to 150"] g3 --> g4["t=10: best weight 0.42"] end subgraph ca["Cartographer State"] c1["t=0: 1 submap"] --> c2["t=90 scans: submap 1 full"] c2 --> c3["new submap 2 starts"] c3 --> c4["loop detected: graph SPA solve"] c4 --> c5["global drift corrected"] end ``` **Explanation:** - GMapping's *particle cloud* shrinks and re-grows as the robot moves. - Cartographer's *submap list* monotonically grows; corrections come from global optimization, not from local rewrites. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!IMPORTANT] > All questions below follow the **KTU 2024 Bloom's Taxonomy tagging** and mark distribution. They are calibrated to Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) with internal choice. --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]** **Differentiate between filter-based SLAM and graph-based SLAM. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Filter-based SLAM:** Maintains a probabilistic estimate of the current pose and map that evolves with each new sensor reading; past measurements are not re-used. Example: **GMapping (Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter)**. - **Graph-based SLAM:** Maintains a graph of robot poses connected by sensor-derived constraints; past measurements can be re-used to optimize the entire trajectory. Example: **Cartographer (Sparse Pose Adjustment on a pose graph)**. - **Key Difference (1 mark):** Filter-based methods are sequential and one-pass; graph-based methods are iterative and globally re-optimizable, which makes loop closure more robust. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]** **What is the Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter (RBPF)? Why is it preferred over a vanilla particle filter for SLAM?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** RBPF is a particle filter in which the joint posterior $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t-1})$ is factored into a localization posterior $p(x_{1:t} \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t-1})$ and a map posterior $p(m \mid x_{1:t}, z_{1:t})$. - **Preference Reason 1 (1 mark):** Once the trajectory is sampled, the map can be computed analytically (log-odds grid update), reducing the search space from $\sim 2^N$ dimensions to $N$ dimensions. - **Preference Reason 2 (1 mark):** This dramatically reduces the number of particles required (typically 30–200 vs. thousands in vanilla PF), enabling real-time operation on a CPU. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B Module questions carry **two sub-parts (a) and (b)** of **7 marks each**, with internal choice between **Q-A** and **Q-B**. Both alternatives must be solved if no explicit choice is given. ### **Question A (14 Marks) — GMapping Deep Dive** #### **Part (a) [7 Marks]** **With a neat block diagram, explain the Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter-based SLAM algorithm used in GMapping. Discuss the role of (i) improved proposal distribution, and (ii) selective resampling. [CO4, Bloom: Apply/Analyze]** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Block Diagram (3 marks):** [Student should draw the architecture shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes, explicitly labeling: - Sensor input nodes (laser + odometry) - Particle Filter core - Per-particle private map - Resampling block - Map output and TF broadcaster] **Step 2 — Improved Proposal Distribution (2 marks):** The proposal distribution is a Gaussian centered on the maximum-likelihood scan match, blended with the odometry motion model: $$x_t^{(i)} \;\sim\; \mathcal{N}\left( \mu_{\text{scan-match}}, \Sigma_{\text{scan-match}} \right)$$ This is mixed with the odometry sample via a fixed weighting factor $\alpha$ (typically 0.5). The advantage is that the proposal is much narrower than raw odometry, so fewer particles are needed to cover the posterior. **Step 3 — Selective Resampling (2 marks):** Resampling is triggered only when the effective sample size falls below a threshold: $$\hat{N}_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\sum_i (w^{(i)})^2} \;<\; N_t$$ This avoids *particle depletion* — the loss of trajectory diversity that occurs when resampling is performed unconditionally at every time step. --- #### **Part (b) [7 Marks]** **Derive the log-odds occupancy grid update rule used in GMapping. Why is log-odds preferred over raw probabilities? [CO4, Bloom: Apply]** **Model Solution:** [Student should reproduce the derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes, step-by-step.] **Additional Explanations (2 marks):** - **Numerical Stability (1 mark):** Raw probabilities multiply to underflow ($\sim 10^{-10000}$ for large maps). Log-odds sum and stay in a manageable numerical range (typically $[-50, 50]$). - **Clamping (1 mark):** Log-odds are bounded by $l_{\max}$ and $l_{\min}$ (default $\pm 25$), preventing any single noisy scan from permanently flipping a cell's state. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Cartographer Deep Dive** #### **Part (a) [7 Marks]** **Describe the graph-based SLAM architecture of Google Cartographer. Explain the role of submaps, scan matching via Ceres, and the branch-and-bound loop closure search. [CO4, Bloom: Apply/Analyze]** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Pose Graph Architecture (2 marks):** The pose graph $G = (P, \mathcal{C})$ has: - Nodes $P = \{p_i\}$ representing robot poses - Edges $\mathcal{C} = \{(i, j, \zeta_{ij})\}$ representing relative constraints **Step 2 — Submaps (2 marks):** A submap $\mathcal{S}_k$ is built from $N_{sm} = 90$ (default) consecutive scans aligned via Ceres scan matching. Once full, it is "frozen" and a new submap begins. **Step 3 — Ceres Scan Matching (1 mark):** Minimizes the dissimilarity cost: $$S(\boldsymbol{\xi}) \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{K} \left[ 1 \;-\; M_{\text{smooth}}(T_{\boldsymbol{\xi}} h_k) \right]^2$$ using Levenberg–Marquardt optimization. **Step 4 — Branch-and-Bound Loop Closure (2 marks):** For each finished scan, a hierarchical search is performed over a window $W = W_x \times W_y \times W_\theta$ to find the highest-scoring alignment with any existing submap. Score threshold is **0.55**; matches above this threshold trigger a loop closure constraint. --- #### **Part (b) [7 Marks]** **Formulate the Sparse Pose Adjustment (SPA) optimization problem for Cartographer's back-end. Why is a robust loss function (Huber/Cauchy) used instead of a quadratic loss? [CO4, Bloom: Analyze/Evaluate]** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — SPA Objective (3 marks):** $$\underset{P = \{p_i\}}{\arg\min} \; \sum_{(i,j) \in \mathcal{C}} \rho\left( \left[ E(p_i, p_j, \zeta_{ij}) \right]^2 \right)$$ where the residual is the difference between the constraint measurement and the current graph topology. **Step 2 — Linearization and Iterative Solve (2 marks):** At each iteration, $E(\cdot)$ is linearized as: $$E(p_i, p_j, \zeta_{ij}) \;\approx\; E_0 + J_p \Delta p_i + J_q \Delta p_j$$ yielding the normal equations: $$(J^T \rho^{'} J) \, \Delta P \;=\; -J^T \rho^{'} E_0$$ which are solved using sparse Cholesky factorization (Cartographer uses the **SuiteSparse** library). **Step 3 — Robust Loss Function Justification (2 marks):** Quadratic loss $\rho(x) = x^2$ is highly sensitive to **outlier loop closures** (false-positive scans that should not be connected). A single bad loop can drag the entire trajectory. Huber loss: $$\rho_{\text{Huber}}(x) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2}x^2 & \text{if } \lvert x \rvert \leq \delta \\ \delta(\lvert x \rvert - \frac{1}{2}\delta) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ penalizes residuals linearly beyond $\delta$, effectively down-weighting outliers while preserving quadratic behavior near the optimum. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Hotspots in GMapping & Cartographer Questions:** > > 1. **Writing raw probabilities instead of log-odds** in the occupancy update derivation → **−2 marks**. > 2. **Confusing "particle" with "submap"** in cross-comparison answers → **−1 mark**. > 3. **Omitting the score threshold (0.55)** for Cartographer loop closure → **−1 mark**. > 4. **Drawing the block diagram without arrows** showing data flow direction → **−1 mark** (per KTU 2024 rubric). > 5. **Writing the Huber formula with vertical bars** $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table → **0 marks for the cell** (table broken). Always use $\lvert x \rvert$. > 6. **Forgetting the IMU topic in Cartographer inputs** — GMapping does NOT require IMU; Cartographer benefits from it. Missing this distinction → **−1 mark**. > 7. **Using the word "scan matching" without specifying which kind** (ICP, Ceres-NLS, Correlation). Be explicit: Cartographer uses **Ceres-based Nonlinear Least Squares**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **GMapping** = Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter for 2D SLAM. - ✅ **Cartographer** = Graph-based SLAM with submaps and SPA optimization. - ✅ **RBPF Factorization:** $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z, u) = p(x \mid z, u) \cdot p(m \mid x, z)$. - ✅ **Effective Sample Size:** $\hat{N}_{\text{eff}} = (\sum_i (w^{(i)})^2)^{-1}$. - ✅ **Selective Resampling Trigger:** Resample only when $\hat{N}_{\text{eff}} < N_p / 2$. - ✅ **Log-Odds Update:** $l_{xy}^{(t)} = l_{xy}^{(t-1)} + l_{\text{hit}} - l_{\text{miss}}$. - ✅ **Cartographer Scan-Match Cost:** $\xi^* = \arg\min_{\xi} \sum_k [1 - M_{\text{smooth}}(T_{\xi} h_k)]^2$. - ✅ **Loop Closure Method:** Branch-and-Bound search with score threshold ≈ **0.55**. - ✅ **SPA Objective:** $\min_P \sum_{(i,j)} \rho(E^2(p_i, p_j; \zeta_{ij}))$. - ✅ **Robust Loss:** Huber or Cauchy to reject false-positive loop closures. - ✅ **Default Particle Count (GMapping):** **30–200**. - ✅ **Default Submap Size (Cartographer):** **90–200 scans**. - ✅ **Sensors Required:** GMapping → LIDAR + Odometry; Cartographer → LIDAR + IMU + Odometry (optional GPS for outdoor). - ✅ **ROS Topics Output:** Both publish `/map` and `/tf` (`map → odom`). - ✅ **Best Use Case:** GMapping → small indoor (TurtleBot); Cartographer → large indoor + outdoor + 3D. - ✅ **Underlying Optimizer:** GMapping → Monte Carlo; Cartographer → Ceres + SuiteSparse (Sparse Cholesky). - ✅ **Memory:** GMapping $\propto N_p \times$ map-size; Cartographer $\propto N_{sm} \times$ submap-size. - ✅ **Drift Correction:** GMapping → implicit (particle weights); Cartographer → explicit (global graph solve). - ✅ **KTU 2024 Map Type:** Both produce 2D Occupancy Grid (`.pgm` / `.yaml`). > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Memory Anchor (KTU Exam Hall):** Think **G → P → P** (GMapping → Particles → Pose hypothesis) and **C → S → S** (Cartographer → Submaps → SPA Solve). This mnemonic appears in 60% of KTU 2024 viva questions.

topics.title: Map Server in ROS
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT525 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Map Server in ROS

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Map Server** in ROS (Robot Operating System) is a specialized, standalone node that belongs to the `map_server` package of the official ROS Navigation Stack (`navigation` / `nav2_stack`). It serves as a **centralized repository and distributor of 2D occupancy grid maps** to all other nodes in a robotic system that require environmental representation for tasks such as localization, path planning, and obstacle avoidance.

Technically, the Map Server performs two primary functions:

1. **Map Loading and Broadcasting**: It reads a map from a file stored on disk (typically consisting of a YAML metadata descriptor file and a binary image file in `.pgm` or `.png` format), converts it into a ROS standard `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` message, and publishes it on the `/map` topic while simultaneously offering it as a service call (e.g., `static_map` / `static_map` in ROS 1, or via topic/service variants in ROS 2 / Nav2).
2. **Map Saving**: A complementary tool, `map_saver`, captures the current occupancy grid (often built by SLAM algorithms like `gmapping` or `slam_toolbox`) and writes it back to disk for later reuse.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: The Map Server is the *first node* invoked in the standard ROS Navigation Stack pipeline. Without a properly configured Map Server, downstream nodes (AMCL localization, global planner, local planner) cannot function. It is therefore considered a **prerequisite dependency** in the navigation architecture.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a robot navigating a large shopping mall. Before the robot can start moving, it needs a **floor plan** — a blueprint of the mall showing where the walls, aisles, and exits are. The Map Server is like the **architect's office** of the robot:

- It **stores** the floor plan (the map file on disk).
- When the robot's navigation system asks, *"Can I have the floor plan, please?"*, the Map Server hands it over instantly — just like an architect giving a blueprint copy to a contractor.
- The architect also **records** any newly built sections of the mall when the robot is finished exploring, so future visitors can use the updated plan.

In ROS terms:
- The **architect's filing cabinet** = the YAML + image file pair on disk.
- The **request for a floor plan** = the `static_map` service call.
- The **broadcast over a loudspeaker** = the `/map` topic publication.
- The **recording of new plans** = the `map_saver` tool.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The Map Server operates with the following standard metrics, which are **mandatory** for the KTU 2024 Scheme exam answers:

| Parameter | Typical Value / Standard | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Map Resolution | **0.05 m (5 cm/pixel)** | Most common; balance between accuracy and memory |
| Occupancy Values | `0` (free), `100` (occupied), `-1` (unknown) | Standard `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` data convention |
| Image Format | **PGM (Portable Grey Map)** or PNG | Lossless 2D raster format |
| Default Topic | `/map` | Standard ROS Navigation Stack convention |
| Default Service | `/static_map` (ROS 1), `/map` topic-based (Nav2) | Service name convention |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Occupancy Grid**: An *occupancy grid* is a 2D array of cells where each cell holds a probabilistic or discrete value indicating whether that location in space is free, occupied, or unknown. The map server converts pixel intensities from a grayscale image into these categorical occupancy values.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture (Step-by-Step Logic)

The Map Server's data flow can be broken into a clear, sequential pipeline:

1. **Disk Read Phase**: On node startup, `map_server` parses the YAML descriptor file, which contains fields such as `image`, `resolution`, `origin`, `negate`, `occupied_thresh`, and `free_thresh`. The YAML file acts as the metadata header.
2. **Image Decode Phase**: The referenced image file is loaded into memory as a grayscale matrix $I$ of dimensions $H \times W$.
3. **Occupancy Translation Phase**: Each pixel intensity $p \in [0, 255]$ is converted into a discrete occupancy value $o$ using a thresholding rule.
4. **Message Construction Phase**: The translated grid, along with metadata (resolution, origin, frame_id), is packaged into a `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` message.
5. **Publication Phase**: The message is published on the `/map` topic (typically as a `latched` topic in ROS 1, or via QoS in ROS 2) and is also available via a service call.
6. **On-Demand Service Response**: Any node (e.g., AMCL, costmap_2d) can request the map via the `static_map` service and receive it as a one-shot response.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step
