The actual measurement $z_t^k$ is then compared to $z_t^{k*}$ through four components. **Four Components of the Beam Model:** 1. **Hit Model $P_{\text{hit}}$** — Correct range with measurement noise (Gaussian): $$P_{\text{hit}}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \begin{cases} \eta \exp\left(-\frac{(z_t^k - z_t^{k*})^2}{2\sigma_{\text{hit}}^2}\right) & 0 \le z_t^k \le z_{\max} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ 2. **Short Model $P_{\text{short}}$** — Unexpected objects cause shorter readings: $$P_{\text{short}}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \begin{cases} \eta \lambda_{\text{short}} e^{-\lambda_{\text{short}} z_t^k} & 0 \le z_t^k \le z_t^{k*} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ 3. **Max Model $P_{\text{max}}$** — When the beam hits no obstacle (returns $z_{\max}$): $$P_{\text{max}}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \begin{cases} 1 & z_t^k = z_{\max} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ 4. **Random Model $P_{\text{rand}}$** — Unexplained / spurious measurements: $$P_{\text{rand}}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{z_{\max}} & 0 \le z_t^k < z_{\max} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ **Combined Likelihood (with mixing weights $z_{\text{hit}} + z_{\text{short}} + z_{\text{max}} + z_{\text{rand}} = 1$):** $$P(z_t \mid x_t, m) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} \left[ z_{\text{hit}} P_{\text{hit}} + z_{\text{short}} P_{\text{short}} + z_{\text{max}} P_{\text{max}} + z_{\text{rand}} P_{\text{rand}} \right]$$ **Typical weights:** $z_{\text{hit}} = 0.95$, $z_{\text{short}} = 0.05$, $z_{\text{max}} = 0.0$ (or 0.05), $z_{\text{rand}} = 0.05$. --- #### Model Solution for Question B (b) The **kidnapped robot problem** arises when a fully-localized robot is suddenly teleported to an arbitrary new location. AMCL must (i) detect the inconsistency and (ii) recover localization. **Detection Mechanism — Average Likelihood Tracking:** AMCL maintains two exponentially-weighted averages of the average per-particle measurement probability: $$w_{\text{fast}} = w_{\text{fast}} + \alpha_{\text{fast}}\bigl(\text{avg}_i P(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m) - w_{\text{fast}}\bigr)$$ $$w_{\text{slow}} = w_{\text{slow}} + \alpha_{\text{slow}}\bigl(\text{avg}_i P(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m) - w_{\text{slow}}\bigr)$$ with $\alpha_{\text{fast}} \gg \alpha_{\text{slow}}$ (e.g., $0.1$ vs. $0.001$). **Behavior:** - During consistent tracking, both averages remain high and similar: $w_{\text{fast}} \approx w_{\text{slow}}$. - After kidnapping, the average likelihood collapses; $w_{\text{fast}}$ drops quickly, $w_{\text{slow}}$ drops slowly. **Injection Probability:** $$p_{\text{random}} = \max\left(0,\; 1 - \frac{w_{\text{fast}}}{w_{\text{slow}}}\right)$$ **Derivation sketch:** When tracking is consistent, $w_{\text{fast}} / w_{\text{slow}} \to 1$ → $p_{\text{random}} \to 0$ (no extra particles). When kidnapped, $w_{\text{fast}} \ll w_{\text{slow}}$ → $p_{\text{random}} \to 1$ (inject many random particles uniformly over free space). **Recovery:** The injected random particles eventually receive high weights from the sensor model, allowing the particle set to re-converge on the new true pose. The recovery time is bounded by the maximum number of injected particles and the KLD-sampling rate. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall / Valuation Warning:** > - Do **NOT confuse** $w_{\text{fast}}$ / $w_{\text{slow}}$ with the particle weights $w_t^{[i]}$ — they are different quantities. $w_t^{[i]}$ are per-particle; $w_{\text{fast}}$ / $w_{\text{slow}}$ are scalar averages over the entire particle set. > - Do **NOT forget to normalize** particle weights before resampling. Un-normalized weights silently break the resampler. > - When describing AMCL, students often skip the **KLD-sampling** part. This is a guaranteed 2-mark loss in 14-mark questions — always mention it. > - Beam model is **not** just a Gaussian — you must list all **four components** (hit, short, max, random) to get full credit. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Localization** = estimating pose $(x, y, \theta)$ from sensor data and a known map. - Three difficulty classes: **position tracking**, **global localization**, **kidnapped robot**. - Probabilistic localization is rooted in the **Bayes filter** (predict + correct recursion). - **Particle filter / MCL** represents the belief as a set of $M$ weighted samples. - Each particle undergoes: **sample from motion model → weight by sensor likelihood → resample if $N_{\text{eff}}$ low**. - **AMCL** = MCL with two key enhancements: **KLD-sampling** (adaptive particle count) and **random particle injection** (kidnapped-robot recovery). - The **measurement likelihood** is the **beam model** with hit, short, max, and random components. - The **KLD formula** bounds the KL divergence between the sample-based belief and the true posterior: $$M \approx \frac{k - 1}{2\epsilon}\left\{1 - \frac{2}{9(k-1)} + \sqrt{\frac{2}{9(k-1)}} z_{1-\delta}\right\}^3$$ - The **random injection probability** is: $$p_{\text{random}} = \max\left(0,\; 1 - \frac{w_{\text{fast}}}{w_{\text{slow}}}\right)$$ - The **effective sample size** trigger for resampling is: $$N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{M} (w_t^{[i]})^2}$$ - In **ROS**, AMCL subscribes to `/map`, `/scan`, `/odom`, `/initialpose` and publishes `tf` (`map → odom`), `/amcl_pose`, and `/particlecloud`. - **Default parameters**: `min_particles = 100`, `max_particles = 5000`, `kld_err = 0.05`, `update_min_d = 0.25 m`, `update_min_a = 0.2 rad`. - AMCL is the **2D localization standard** in both ROS 1 (`amcl` package) and ROS 2 (`nav2_amcl` package).

topics.title: Implementing Localization in ROS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT525 - ROBOT OPERATING SYSTEM (ROS) > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Implementing Localization in ROS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) **Localization** in ROS is the computational process by which a mobile robot estimates its pose (position $(x, y)$ and orientation $\theta$) within a known reference frame, typically a pre-built static map, by fusing **noisy odometry data** with **noisy exteroceptive sensor measurements** (e.g., 2D laser scans). In the KTU 2024 Scheme curriculum, this is realized through the **amcl** (Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization) node of the ROS Navigation Stack, which is a probabilistic implementation of the **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)** algorithm — a particle-filter-based variant of the recursive Bayes filter. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** Localization = Pose estimation problem solved by maintaining a *belief distribution* $bel(x_t)$ over all possible robot poses $x_t$ at time $t$, given the history of controls $u_{1:t}$ and observations $z_{1:t}$. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Blindfolded Tourist Imagine you are dropped blindfolded into a city you have a **map** of, but you do not know exactly where you are. - You can **feel the ground** under your feet — that is **odometry** (proprioceptive, drifts over time). - You can occasionally **remove the blindfold**, take a quick look at a landmark, and compare it to your map — that is a **sensor measurement** (e.g., LIDAR scan matching). - You keep a set of **"candidate locations"** (like pins on a Google Map), and every time you get new evidence, you **drop the unlikely ones** and **multiply the likely ones**. This is precisely how the **AMCL particle filter** works: each "pin" is a *particle* — a hypothesis about your pose. > [!NOTE] > **Key Vocabulary for KTU Viva:** > - **Pose**: $(x, y, \theta)$ — 2D position + heading angle. > - **Belief**: A probability distribution over all possible poses. > - **Particle**: A weighted sample from the belief distribution. > - **Global vs. Local Localization**: Finding the robot's pose *without* prior knowledge vs. *with* prior knowledge (kidnapped robot problem). ### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Highlighted) - **Particle Count**: Default in AMCL = **500 to 5000** particles. - **Update Rate**: AMCL publishes pose at **20 Hz** by default. - **Sensor Standard Deviations**: Typical LIDAR $\sigma_{range} = 0.05$ m, $\sigma_{angle} = 0.02$ rad. - **Frame Rate**: AMCL default laser update rate = **10 Hz**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Bayes Filter Localization is fundamentally a **recursive Bayesian estimation** problem. The robot must maintain the posterior: $$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$ where $x_t$ is the current pose, $z_{1:t}$ is the sequence of measurements, and $u_{1:t}$ is the sequence of control inputs. The filter has two alternating phases executed every timestep. ### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step **Phase 1 — Prediction (Motion Update)** Uses the **motion model** $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ to shift the belief forward in time using odometry. Because odometry is noisy, the predicted belief *spreads* (uncertainty grows). **Phase 2 — Measurement Update (Correction)** Uses the **sensor model** $p(z_t \mid x_t)$ to weigh each hypothesis. Particles whose pose would have produced a scan *similar to the actual scan* get higher weights; mismatched particles get low weights. **Phase 3 — Resampling** The KLD-sampling step in AMCL dynamically adjusts the number of active particles: more particles when the robot is "lost" (belief spread out), fewer particles when the robot is confident (belief concentrated). ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Symbol | Mathematical Expression | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | Belief posterior | $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$ | Bayes filter recursive form | unitless (probability) | | Motion model (odometry) | $x_t = x_{t-1} \oplus \Delta x_{odom}$ | Pose update via odometry delta | meters / radians | | Measurement model (beam) | $p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m)$ | Likelihood of a full scan | unitless | | Particle weight update | $w_t^{[i]} = w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$ | Importance weight for particle $i$ | unitless | | Normalization | $w_t^{[i]} = \dfrac{w_t^{[i]}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} w_t^{[j]}}$ | Weights must sum to 1 | unitless | | KLD-resampling bound | $N_{top} = \dfrac{\chi^2_{k-1, 1-\delta}}{2 \epsilon}$ | Upper bound on particle count | particles | | Effective sample size | $N_{eff} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (w^{[i]})^2}$ | Resample trigger | particles | | Pose dimension | $x = (x, y, \theta)^T$ | 2D pose vector | m, m, rad | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** This system is the backbone of warehouse robots (Amazon Kiva), autonomous forklifts, AGVs in factories, and the ROS `move_base` global planner. Without localization, the planner cannot convert "go to the kitchen" into a collision-free trajectory. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Recursive Bayes Filter We start from the definition of the belief and apply the **law of total probability** and **Bayes' theorem** step by step. **Step 1** — Express the belief as a conditional probability over the last pose: $$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$ **Step 2** — Apply Bayes' theorem treating $z_t$ as the new evidence and $z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}$ as the conditioning context: $$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$ **Step 3** — Apply the **Markov assumption**: given $x_t$, the current measurement $z_t$ is independent of past measurements and controls: $$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)$$ **Step 4** — This is the **measurement model** (likelihood). Substitute it back: $$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$ **Step 5** — Now marginalize (integrate) over the previous pose $x_{t-1}$ to obtain the **prediction step**: $$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$ **Step 6** — Recognize the integral's second factor as the prior belief $bel(x_{t-1})$: $$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1} = \overline{bel}(x_t)$$ **Step 7** — Combine Steps 4 and 6 into the **canonical Bayes filter equation**: $$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$ **Explanation of every term:** - $bel(x_{t-1})$ — what we believed at the previous timestep (input). - $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ — motion model: how the pose evolves under control $u_t$. - $\int \ldots dx_{t-1}$ — marginalization: average over *all* possible previous poses. - $p(z_t \mid x_t)$ — sensor model: how likely the actual measurement is at this hypothetical pose. - $\eta$ — normalization constant ensuring the result is a valid probability distribution. ### 3.2 Derivation of the Particle Weight Update (Importance Sampling) The Monte Carlo approximation replaces the continuous belief with a discrete set of $N$ weighted particles: $$bel(x_t) \approx \{(x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]}) : i = 1, \ldots, N\}$$ **Step 1** — Particles are drawn from a *proposal distribution* $\pi$ (in AMCL this is the odometry motion model $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$): $$x_t^{[i]} \sim \pi(x_t \mid x_{1:t-1}^{[i]}, z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$ **Step 2** — The importance weight corrects for the gap between the proposal $\pi$ and the true target distribution: $$w_t^{[i]} \propto w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot \dfrac{p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}) \, p(x_t^{[i]} \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t)}{\pi(x_t^{[i]} \mid x_{1:t-1}^{[i]}, z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})}$$ **Step 3** — Since AMCL uses the motion model as its proposal, $\pi = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$, the ratio simplifies to **1**: $$\dfrac{p(x_t^{[i]} \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t)}{\pi(x_t^{[i]} \mid x_{1:t-1}^{[i]}, z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})} = 1$$ **Step 4** — Therefore the weight update reduces to: $$w_t^{[i]} = w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$$ **Step 5** — Normalize so that all weights sum to 1: $$w_t^{[i]} = \dfrac{w_t^{[i]}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} w_t^{[j]}}$$ ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Complexity | Operation | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Frequency in AMCL | |---|---|---|---| | Particle propagation (motion) | $O(N)$ | $O(N)$ | Every odometry tick | | Scan likelihood evaluation | $O(N \cdot K)$ | $O(N)$ | Every laser scan | | Resampling (low-variance) | $O(N)$ | $O(N)$ | When $N_{eff} < N/2$ | | KLD-bounded resampling | $O(N \log N)$ | $O(N)$ | Adaptive node | | TF broadcast | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | Every cycle | Here $N$ is the particle count and $K$ is the number of laser beams per scan (typically $K = 360$ for a Hokuyo / $K = 1080$ for a Velodyne 2D slice). ## 4. (rigid mount)" --> LASER BASE --> WHEEL ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Focus Point:** The **map → odom** transform is the *output* of AMCL. The **odom → base_footprint** transform is produced by your robot's odometry source (e.g., `robot_pose_ekf` or `ros2_control` odometer). These two must **not** be published by the same node — that would defeat the purpose of localization. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]** *Define localization in the context of ROS. Differentiate between **global localization** and **local localization**.* **Model Answer:** Localization is the process by which a robot determines its pose $(x, y, \theta)$ in a given reference frame, typically a pre-built map, by fusing sensor data and odometry through a probabilistic estimator. | Aspect | Global Localization | Local Localization | |---|---|---| | Initial knowledge | No prior estimate of pose | Prior estimate available | | Belief shape | Multi-modal (many possible poses) | Uni-modal (concentrated) | | Typical problem | "Kidnapped robot" problem | Tracking known pose | | AMCL behavior | Uses **max_particles**, broad spread | Uses fewer particles, tight cluster | *(Self-test: mention that AMCL seamlessly handles both modes using KLD-adaptive resampling — **+1 mark**)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *With a neat block diagram, explain the role of the **amcl** node in the ROS Navigation Stack. List any **four** key parameters of amcl.** **Model Answer:** The **amcl** node is a probabilistic localization module implementing the Adaptive Monte Carlo algorithm. It subscribes to `/scan`, `/odom`, `/map`, and `/initialpose`, and publishes the **map → odom** TF transform and `/amcl_pose`. **Four key parameters** (any four of the following): 1. `min_particles` — minimum number of particles. 2. `max_particles` — upper bound on particle count. 3. `odom_model_type` — odometry noise model (`diff`, `diff-corrected`, `omni-corrected`). 4. `laser_z_hit` — weight of the "hit" likelihood in the beam model. 5. `update_min_d` / `update_min_a` — translational / rotational thresholds to trigger a filter update. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a)** Derive the recursive Bayes filter equation for localization starting from the definition of the belief $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$. Clearly state any assumptions made. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) algorithm** and the **Adaptive MCL (AMCL)** variant. Show how the particle weights are updated and why resampling is necessary. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** The derivation is given in detail in **Section 3.1** above. The student should write: 1. Start with $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ — **(1 mark)** 2. Apply Bayes' rule → split into measurement likelihood and prior — **(1 mark)** 3. State and apply the **Markov assumption**: $p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)$ — **(1 mark)** 4. Marginalize over $x_{t-1}$ to obtain prediction integral — **(2 marks)** 5. Recognize $bel(x_{t-1})$ inside the integral — **(1 mark)** 6. Final form: $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$ — **(1 mark)** **Model Solution for (b):** 1. **MCL idea**: Represent $bel(x_t)$ as a set of $N$ weighted particles $\{(x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]})\}_{i=1}^{N}$ — **(1 mark)** 2. **Algorithm steps**: - Sample $x_t^{[i]} \sim p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t)$ (prediction) — **(1 mark)** - Compute weight $w_t^{[i]} = w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$ — **(2 marks)** - Normalize weights — **(1 mark)** - Resample (e.g., low-variance resampler) — **(1 mark)** 3. **AMCL enhancement**: KLD-sampling — particle count $N$ is dynamically bounded by $N_{top} = \chi^2_{k-1, 1-\delta} / (2\epsilon)$ — **(1 mark)** 4. **Why resampling**: Prevents **particle depletion** (a few particles carrying all the weight, the rest becoming useless) — **(1 mark)** --- #### **Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2023 — Alternative Module Choice]** **(a)** Describe the **odometry motion model** and the **likelihood-field sensor model** used in AMCL. Compare them with the older beam-based model. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **TF tree** required for a properly configured ROS localization system. Indicate which node is responsible for publishing each transform. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** 1. **Odometry motion model** uses the relative pose displacement $\Delta x_{odom} = (\delta_{rot1}, \delta_{trans}, \delta_{rot2})$ between two successive odometry readings — **(2 marks)** 2. Noise parameters $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \alpha_3, \alpha_4$ scale the variance of each component — **(1 mark)** 3. **Likelihood-field model**: pre-computes a 2D distance map from the static map; for each beam endpoint, the likelihood is $\exp(-d^2 / 2\sigma^2)$ where $d$ is the distance to the nearest obstacle — **(2 marks)** 4. Comparison table: | Feature | Beam model | Likelihood field | |---|---|---| | Computation per beam | Ray-cast — slow | Lookup — fast | | Robust to small map errors | Poor | **Excellent** | | Memory | Low | Higher (grid) | | Default in AMCL | No | **Yes** | **(1 mark)** for the table. **Model Solution for (b):** The diagram is shown in **Section 4.3**. Student must draw: - `map → odom` (published by **amcl**) — **(2 marks)** - `odom → base_footprint` (published by **robot's odometry node** / `ros2_control`) — **(2 marks)** - `base_footprint → base_laser` (published by **static_transform_publisher**) — **(1 mark)** - Brief explanation of why `map → odom` is the *correction* and `odom → base_footprint` is the *dead-reckoning* path — **(2 marks)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing `map → odom` and `odom → base_link`**: Many students believe AMCL publishes both. It only publishes `map → odom`. > 2. **Forgetting the $\eta$ normalizer**: The Bayes filter derivation is incomplete without the normalization constant. > 3. **Wrong Markov assumption statement**: It applies to the *measurement*, not the *motion*. > 4. **Mixing up `kld_err` and `kld_z`**: The first is $\epsilon$ (error tolerance); the second is $1-\delta$ (confidence). > 5. **Using `odom_alpha5` without omni model**: It is only valid for `omni-corrected` odometry. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Localization = pose estimation in a known map.** - **AMCL** = Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization, the standard ROS node. - The belief is a **probability distribution** $bel(x_t)$, approximated by weighted particles. - The **Bayes filter** has two phases: **prediction** (motion) and **update** (measurement). - The recursive formula: $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$. - **Particle weight update**: $w_t^{[i]} \propto w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$. - **KLD-sampling** dynamically resizes the particle set: more particles when lost, fewer when confident. - **TF tree** has three critical links: `map → odom` (AMCL), `odom → base_footprint` (odometry), `base_footprint → base_laser` (static). - Default AMCL particle range: **500 to 5000**. - Sensor model: prefer **likelihood field** over beam model for speed and robustness. - Odometry model: typical noise parameters $\alpha_1 = \alpha_2 = \alpha_3 = \alpha_4 = 0.2$ for a TurtleBot-class robot.
